Tafseer:
Alif Lam Meem: This is an independent sentence. According to the general rules of the Arabic language, the subject (Mubtada) is omitted here. If it were made apparent, the complete sentence would be: 'Hadha Alif Lam Meem' (This is Alif, Lam, Meem). We have clarified this omission in the translation.
These and all such letters that appear at the beginning of various Surahs are called 'Huroof-e-Muqatta'at' (Disjoined Letters) because they are read separately, letter by letter.
In whichever Surah they appear, they are placed right at the beginning, just like names appear at the start of books, sections, and chapters. This indicates that they are the names of these Surahs. The Quran has further clarified them as names by pointing to them using demonstrative pronouns like 'Dhalika' and 'Tilka' in various places. Hadith traditions also confirm that they are names.
Although not all Surahs named with these letters became exclusively known by them (some became famous by other names), some are indeed widely known by these very names, such as Ta-Ha, Ya-Sin, Qaf, and Nun, etc.
It is very difficult to state anything definitive about the meanings of these names. Therefore, a question might arise in someone's mind: The Quran claims to be a completely clear book, containing nothing like a riddle or an enigma; then why has it given Surahs names whose meanings no one knows?
The answer to this question is that, as far as these letters are concerned, they were not alien to the Arabs; rather, they were well-acquainted with their usage. Given this familiarity, naming the Surahs of the Quran with these letters does not contradict the Quran's claim of being a clear book. However, the question does arise whether forming names from such letters was in accordance with Arab taste. The greatest testimony that it was according to Arab taste is that the Quran adopted this method of naming. If this method of naming had been completely unfamiliar to the Arabs, they would have definitely frowned upon it and used these letters as an excuse to say: 'Who can accept the claim of a clear book when even the names of its Surahs cannot be understood by anyone?'
The Arabs raised many objections against the Quran, and the Quran has recorded all these objections, but it has not mentioned any such objection from them, which clearly shows that there was no unfamiliarity for them in these names.
Furthermore, those familiar with Arab traditions and literature know that the Arabs were not only familiar with such names, but they themselves used to give similar names to persons, things, horses, flags, swords, and even odes and sermons. These names were based on single letters as well as compound letters. It was not even necessary for there to be a pre-existing semantic relationship between the noun and the named entity; the name itself indicated that it was coined for that entity.
And it is a completely obvious fact that once it is known about a thing that it is a name, then the question of its meaning does not arise at all, because the main purpose of a name is to associate the named entity with that name, not its meaning. At least from the perspective of understanding the Quran, investigating the meanings of these names has no special importance. It is just that since these names were given by Allah, one naturally assumes they must have been given based on some relevance. This assumption naturally creates a curiosity in the disposition. Based on this curiosity, many of our past scholars pondered over these names and tried to discover their meanings. Although their search did not yield any particular benefit, in our view, their effort in itself was not wrong, and if we too reflect upon them, our effort will not be wrong either. If any reality is revealed through this effort, it will add to our knowledge, and if nothing is found, we will attribute it to the limitation of our knowledge and the fathomless nature of the Quran. We will certainly not form the opinion that these names are meaningless.
This realization of the limitation of one's knowledge and the fathomless nature of the Quran is a great knowledge in itself. This realization opens many closed doors of knowledge and spiritual insight. If the very first letter of the Quran becomes the key to this great revelation, this too will be one of the many miracles of the Quran. It is the perfection of this book that the letter whose secret could not be unlocked by anyone became a guiding light to unveil thousands of hidden mysteries through the inquiry it generated.
The opinions expressed by our past scholars regarding these letters do not, in our view, rest on a solid foundation; therefore, mentioning them here would not be of much use. However, I will briefly present here the opinion of Ustadh Imam Maulana Hamiduddin Farahi (may Allah have mercy on him). Although this does not entirely solve the fundamental issue, it certainly seems to open a path toward its resolution. It is quite possible that with the clue provided by the Maulana, others might discover more useful milestones, and thus the steps of research may gradually advance further.
Those familiar with the history of the Arabic script know that Arabic letters were derived from Hebrew, and these Hebrew letters were derived from the letters prevalent in ancient Arabia. Regarding these letters of ancient Arabia, Ustadh Imam's research is that, unlike English and Hindi letters, they did not merely represent sounds, but like Chinese characters, they also indicated meanings and objects. And the meanings or objects they indicated were generally written in the shape and form of those very things. The Maulana's research is that these are the same letters that the ancient Egyptians adopted, and after amending and reforming them according to their concepts, gave them the shape of the hieroglyphic script, traces of which exist in the inscriptions of the Pyramids of Egypt.
Although the knowledge of the meanings of these letters has now been lost, the meanings of some letters are still known, and their writing style still retains some reflection of their ancient form. For example, regarding 'Alif', it is known that it meant a 'cow' and is written in the shape of a cow's head. 'Ba' is pronounced 'Bait' in Hebrew, and its meaning is also 'Bait' (house). The Hebrew pronunciation of 'Jeem' is 'Jameel', which means 'Jamal' (camel). 'Ta' meant 'snake' and was also written somewhat in the shape of a snake. 'Meem' indicated a 'water wave' and its shape was also made resembling a wave.
In support of his theory, the Maulana presents Surah 'Nun'. The letter 'Nun' is still used in its ancient meaning. It means 'fish', and in the Surah named after it, Prophet Jonah (peace be upon him) is mentioned by the title 'Sahib al-Hoot' (Companion of the Fish). Presenting this name, the Maulana states that the mind naturally goes to the fact that this Surah was named 'Nun' (N) precisely because it narrates the incident of Sahib al-Hoot, Prophet Jonah (peace be upon him), who was swallowed by a fish. Then what is the surprise if the letters at the beginning of some other Surahs have also come due to some relationship between their ancient meanings and the themes of the Surahs?
The names of some other Surahs of the Quran also support the Maulana's theory. For instance, the meaning of the letter 'Ta', as I mentioned above, was a snake, and its written form also resembled the shape of a snake. Now look at Surah Ta-Ha in the Quran, which begins with 'Ta'. In it, after a brief introduction, the story of Prophet Moses (peace be upon him) and his staff turning into a snake is narrated. Similarly, Ta-Sin-Meem, Ta-Sin, etc., also begin with 'Ta', and they too mention the miracle of Prophet Moses's staff taking the form of a snake.
Regarding 'Alif', we have already stated that it was written in the shape of a cow's head and also meant a cow. Its second meaning was the One God. Now, if you look at the Quran, you will find that in Surah Al-Baqarah, whose name begins with Alif, the story of the slaughter of a cow is narrated. Other Surahs whose names begin with Alif seem to share the theme of Monotheism (Tawheed). This theme is discussed in them with special emphasis. This aspect of these names is also particularly noteworthy: Surahs with similar names have similar themes, and in some Surahs, even the style of expression is similar.
As I have mentioned, I have presented this theory of the Maulana merely with the thought that it opens a scholarly path for pondering over the Huroof-e-Muqatta'at. In my view, its status is still no more than a theory. Until the meanings of all the letters are researched and the relationship between these names and the Surahs named after them becomes clear from every angle, it will not be correct to rely on it as more than a theory. This is merely a hint for the appreciators of Quranic sciences; those who have the courage for further research and inquiry should try their luck on this path. Perhaps Allah will ease this difficulty through this route.

Dhalika: Grammarians say that 'Dhalika' (That) is used to point to something far, and 'Hadha' (This) is used to point to something near. This generally leads people to the misconception that if one has to point to a distant object, 'Dhalika' will be used, and if to a nearby object, 'Hadha' will be used. However, by 'near' and 'far', grammarians do not mean physical distance; they mean that if one is pointing to something that is already in the knowledge of the addressee or has already been mentioned in the conversation, 'Dhalika' is used. And if pointing to something that is about to be mentioned, 'Hadha' is used. Native speakers use these two demonstratives in this way, and if they ever use them contrary to this general rule, it is keeping a point of rhetoric (Balaghat) in mind. For example, if 'Hadha' is used to point to a previously mentioned thing, the purpose would be to bring the thing present before the eyes. Similarly, if 'Dhalika' is used instead of 'Hadha', the purpose is generally to express that the status of what is being pointed to is too exalted to be brought and stood in front.
Here, the pointing of 'Dhalika' is towards the name of the Surah that has already been mentioned, and the intended meaning is to state that this 'Alif Lam Meem' is a part of the Glorious Quran. There are numerous precedents for such pointing in the Quran. For example:
Ha-Meem, 'Ayn-Sin-Qaf. Thus does Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise, reveal to you and to those before you. (Ash-Shura 1-3)
Ta-Sin. These are the verses of the Quran and a clear Book. (An-Naml)
Al-Kitab: The word 'Kitab' (Book) has been used in five different meanings in the Holy Quran:
1. The written decree of destiny (Taqdeer). For example:
'If not for a decree (Kitab) from Allah that preceded, you would have been touched for what you took by a great punishment.' (Al-Anfal - 68)
2. Allah's register in which everything is recorded. For example:
'And with Us is a retaining record (Kitab).' (Qaf - 4)
3. A letter or message. For example:
'Indeed, there has been dropped to me a noble letter (Kitab).' (An-Naml - 29)
4. Commands and Laws (Shariah). For example:
'And teaches them the Book (Shariah) and wisdom.' (Al-Jumu'ah - 2)
5. The revealed speech of Allah. In this meaning, this word is used for the Divine Book, referring both to a specific part of the Divine Book and to its entirety.
For the meaning of the entirety, the precedent is this verse of Al-A'raf:
'And those who hold fast to the Book and establish prayer...' (Al-A'raf - 170)
For the second meaning (a part), the precedent is this verse of Surah Al-Imran:
'Do you not consider those who were given a portion of the Book? They are invited to the Book of Allah that it should arbitrate between them...' (Al-Imran - 23)
Just as a word becomes specific to one of its higher and superior meanings among its various meanings, similarly, the word 'Kitab' began to be spoken specifically for the Divine Book. This usage has been known since ancient times. The Jews used to call each of the prophets' scriptures a 'Sefer', which means a book. Christian translators named these books the 'Bible', which also means 'book' in Greek. Similarly, the word 'Scripture' was used for these scrolls, which means 'book' in Latin. In short, using the word 'Kitab' for the Book of Allah is not a new usage. This usage, as is clear, is very ancient. The Quran also used this word in this meaning, and through its usages, made this meaning so clear that its addressees began to understand it effortlessly.
La raiba feeh: 'Raib' means doubt. 'There is no doubt in it' means there is no doubt in it being a Divine Book or a revealed book. This sentence is not a predicate of the first sentence, but an emphasis of it. 'Dhalika Al-Kitab' means 'This is the Divine Book.' After that, this emphasis highlights the same reality with more force: that there is no room for any doubt or suspicion regarding it being a Divine Book.
If this meaning is not taken, then there is no suitable context left for this phrase here. The precedents in the Holy Quran also support this meaning. For example, just a few verses later in this very Surah, it is stated:
'And if you are in doubt about what We have sent down upon Our Servant, then produce a surah the like thereof...' (Al-Baqarah - 23)
'Alif, Lam, Meem. The revelation of the Book, about which there is no doubt, is from the Lord of the worlds.' (As-Sajdah 1-2)
'Ha, Meem. The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Knowing.' (Al-Mu'min 1-2)
Generally, people understand it to mean that there is nothing in this book that can be doubted. Although this is a reality in itself—there is nothing in the Quran that has any room for doubt or suspicion—in our view, this is not the meaning of this sentence. There are several reasons for this.
Firstly, the Quranic precedents we have presented contradict this meaning. Secondly, doubt and suspicion are not attributes of a book, but attributes of a person's mind. A person with a crooked mind can find some crookedness even in the most straightforward matter; therefore, there is no special benefit in stating this. Thirdly, the question of doubt and suspicion actually arises concerning a claim. Here, the claim is that this is a Divine Book. Therefore, if there is a need to negate doubt, it is regarding this claim, not regarding the book itself. Fourthly, negating doubt regarding a book does not add any special grandeur to the book, because such a negation of doubt can also be made regarding a book of mathematics or Euclidean geometry. Fifthly, the main confusion of the initial addressees of the Quran was not that some statements of the Quran seemed doubtful to them; rather, their main confusion was that this book was being presented as revealed by Allah, and they were not ready to accept it as a book revealed by Allah. Sixthly, even if doubt regarding the book itself were negated, it would not yield any special benefit because the issue of it being from God would still remain doubtful. However, if its being from God becomes indubitable, then its being above every kind of doubt and suspicion is automatically proven.
Huda: The word 'Huda' (Guidance) has been used in several meanings in both the Arabic language and the Holy Quran. The meanings for which precedents exist within the Quran itself are as follows:
1. Inner light and insight. For example:
'And those who are guided - He increases them in guidance (inner insight).' (Muhammad - 17)
2. Evidence, proof, and a signpost. For example:
'...or find at the fire some guidance (a sign showing the way).' (Ta-Ha - 10)
'...without knowledge, without guidance, and without an enlightening book.' (Al-Hajj - 8)
3. A straight and clear path. For example:
'...without knowledge and without guidance and without an enlightening book.' (Al-Hajj - 8) [Note: The Tafseer text repeats Al-Hajj 8 here contextually for 'path/way']. From here, this word is used in the meaning of a method and Shariah. Examples of this meaning also exist in the Quran. For example:
'So from their guidance take an example.' (Al-An'am - 90)
'Indeed, the [true] guidance is the guidance of Allah (the Shariah of Allah).' (Al-Imran - 73)
4. The act of guiding. For example:
'Not upon you is their guidance, but Allah guides whom He wills.' (Al-Baqarah - 272)
It is obvious that in the Holy Quran, 'Huda' encompasses all four of these meanings.
Lil-Muttaqeen: The letter 'Lam' (for) here carries the meaning of deriving benefit. That is, only those people will derive benefit from this book who are 'Muttaqi' (God-fearing/pious). Just as the sun shines for everyone, but only those benefit from it who have eyes and who open those eyes to see. Similarly, this book was revealed for the guidance of everyone, but since in reality, only those people will benefit from it who have the fear of God in them, it is said that it is a guidance for the Muttaqeen. The word Muttaqi is from 'Ittiqa'. The word Ittiqa has been used in several meanings in the Holy Quran. We clarify this with examples:
1. To avoid something that poses a threat of harm. For example:
'Then how can you fear (avoid), if you disbelieve, a Day that will make the children white-haired?' (Al-Muzzammil - 17)
2. To be apprehensive of the manifestation of a calamity. For example:
'And fear a trial which will not strike those who have wronged among you exclusively...' (Al-Anfal - 25)
3. To constantly tremble and fear the Holy Lord, who has mercy on His grateful and loyal servants, who dislikes disbelief and disobedience, and who is aware of every hidden and apparent thing.
'And those who feared their Lord will be driven to Paradise in groups...' (Az-Zumar - 73)
4. Its fourth meaning encompasses all three mentioned above. That is, to keep away from sin due to the fear of its evil consequences and the wrath of God. When this word is used without an object, it generally means this, and this very thing is termed as Taqwa.
'And if you believe and fear Him (adopt Taqwa), then for you is a great reward.' (Al-Imran - 179)
In light of this explanation, a Muttaqi will be a person in whose heart the greatness of God and the fear of His wrath are deeply embedded, and who is fully conscious of the consequences of sins. Although in Taqwa, regarding actions, the aspect of state and condition, and the aspect of abstention (omission) as opposed to commission, are more prominent—and from this perspective, one can say that negation dominates affirmation in it—yet, because it is a proof of the health of the heart, and if the heart is healthy, everything is healthy, therefore, both knowledge and action spring from its sources.

Yu'minoona bil-ghaib (They believe in the unseen): 'Iman' (belief/faith) comes from 'Amn' (peace/security). The root meaning of Iman is to grant peace/security. If it is followed by the preposition 'Lam', it means to affirm and accept, and if it comes with 'Bi' (as in 'Bial-Ghaib'), it means to have certainty, trust, and conviction. The true spirit of this word is certainty, trust, and belief. That which is found with the characteristics of certainty, awe (Khashyat), reliance (Tawakkul), and conviction is called Iman. A person who believes in Allah, His signs, His commands, and hands over everything to Him, becoming completely satisfied and pleased with His decrees, is a Mu'min (believer).
When this word is used with its object, it specifically means to believe in that very thing which is mentioned as its object. But if it comes without an object, it can encompass all those things which the Quran has commanded to believe in, or which contextual evidence indicates.
The word 'Ghaib' (unseen) has appeared in the Holy Quran in the following meanings:
1. That which is hidden from our eyes. Its opposite word is 'Shahadah' (the seen/witnessed). One of Allah's attributes is that He is the 'Knower of the unseen and the witnessed'. This means He is aware of those things which are hidden from our eyes, and also aware of those things which are before us.
2. A thing for which man has no source of knowing. The Quran quotes the Prophet (PBUH) saying:
'And if I knew the unseen, I could have acquired much wealth...' (Al-A'raf - 188)
3. A place that is not in front of a person, or a direction that is not determined.
'That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you, [O Muhammad]. And you were not with them when they put together their plan...' (Yusuf - 102)
4. The use of this word in the meaning of a 'secret' is also common. For example, in praise of righteous wives, it is stated: 'Hafithatun lil-ghaib' (They guard the unseen/secrets).
Regarding the 'Bi' in 'Bil-Ghaib', there can be two opinions.
One is that it should be taken in the meaning of an adverbial phrase (Zarf), meaning 'they believe while being in the unseen (while the reality is hidden)'. There are multiple examples of this meaning in the Quran. For example:
'Who fear their Lord unseen, while they are of the Hour apprehensive.' (Al-Anbiya - 49)
'You can only warn those who fear their Lord unseen and have established prayer.' (Fatir - 18)
In this case, 'Yu'minoona' (they believe) will remain general. And all those things will fall under it which are necessary to believe in. The meaning of the verse would be that, in order to believe, they do not wait to observe all realities with their eyes; rather, they believe in all the things demanded of them without physical observation, based solely on the testimony of intellect and nature, and the invitation of the Prophet (PBUH). Among the predecessors, Rabi' bin Anas adopted this interpretation, and we have also preferred this in the translation.
The second opinion can be that it is considered the preposition of connection (Silah), and 'Bil-Ghaib' is considered the object of 'Yu'minoona'. Although this is the majority opinion, and linguistically there is no flaw in it, we do not find this opinion very strong due to the following reasons:
The first reason is that in this case, faith becomes restricted only to the unseen. Except for the unseen, all other things which are necessary to believe in fall outside the circle of faith. On the contrary, in the first case, all those things come into the circle of faith which are necessary to believe in, the details of which the Quran has explained in other places.
The second reason is that while the word 'Ghaib' may apply to all those things necessary to believe in, it certainly does not apply to the Prophet and the Book. The question arises: why have these two things, which are the most necessary to believe in after Allah, been excluded from faith here?
The third reason is that the word 'Ghaib' is not even used for Allah. 'Ghaib' is not among the names of Allah. In other words, this would mean that Allah is also not included in the components of faith here. If Allah is also not included in the components of faith, then under 'belief in the unseen', it only becomes necessary to believe in the Hereafter and the angels, or at most, the events of the future. Why restrict the circle of faith to such an extent?
The fourth reason is that the scholars who take this second interpretation say that 'Ghaib' refers to the states of the Hereafter. If the states of the Hereafter are meant, then the mention of the Hereafter is coming independently in the same sequence just ahead. It is said:
'Wa bil-akhirati hum yuqinoon' - And of the Hereafter they are certain [in faith].
After all, what was the need to repeat the same thing in the same sequence?
The fifth reason is that the first interpretation brings out a very great reality, which the second interpretation entirely lacks. That is, the only faith or awe (Khashyat) that is reliable is the one born of insight and Taqwa. The faith or fear that is born after the consequences of sins appear in front of one's eyes has no value before God. Regarding those who believed upon seeing God's punishment, it is stated:
'Then is it that when it has [actually] occurred you will believe in it? Now? And you were [once] for it impatient.' (Yunus - 51)
One thing that can be said against the concept of adverbial usage is that wherever the preposition 'Bi' has come with the word 'Iman' in the Quran, it has never come in the meaning of an adverb. But this point does not hold much importance because, in response, an equally weighty point can be made that wherever the word 'Bil-Ghaib' has appeared in the Quran, it has always appeared as an adverb, and never as an object. Therefore, as far as the precedents of the Quran are concerned, they are more prominent in favor of the adverbial meaning.
Yuqeemoona As-Salah (They establish prayer): 'Iqamah' means to stand something up or to straighten it in such a way that no crookedness remains in it. It is said 'they establish the prayer', it is not said 'they read the prayer'. By using the word 'establish' for prayer, the Quran has drawn attention to several realities all at once.
The first thing this word draws attention to is sincerity in prayer, meaning the prayer should be offered only for Allah, and no one else should be associated with it. The concept of 'straightening' inherent in it cannot be fulfilled until it is offered with complete singular focus for Allah alone. This reality is explained in clear words in another place:
'And direct your faces toward Him at every place of prostration and invoke Him, sincere to Him in religion.' (Al-A'raf - 29)
From here, the point also emerged that during prayer, the face should be towards the Qiblah, because that is the center of monotheism and sincerity.
The second thing this word points to is to fully fix the heart on the true purpose of prayer. The true purpose of prayer is humility and submissiveness (Khushu and Khudu) in the remembrance of Allah. If a person offers prayer while being heedless of this, this is not 'establishing' the prayer, but merely going through the motions. The Quran has drawn attention to this reality in certain places as well. For example:
'And establish prayer for My remembrance.' (Ta-Ha - 14)
In another place, it is stated:
'Certainly will the believers have succeeded: They who are during their prayer humbly submissive.' (Al-Mu'minun 1-2)
The third thing is that the prayer should be performed without any addition or subtraction, according to the method prescribed by Allah. Thus, it is stated:
'But when you are secure, then remember Allah [in prayer], as He has taught you...' (Al-Baqarah - 239)
Straightening the rows of prayer and accurately performing the physical postures (Arkan) of prayer are also included in this. For this reason, it is mentioned in a Hadith that:
'Straightening the rows is part of the establishment of prayer.'
The fourth thing is the strict observance of the times of prayer. It is stated:
'Establish prayer at the decline of the sun [from its meridian] until the darkness of the night and [also] the Quran of dawn.' (Al-Isra - 78)
This same thing is referred to in other places as 'guarding the prayers'. 'Maintain with care the [obligatory] prayers...' (Al-Baqarah - 238)
The fifth thing is to remain steadfast in prayer, as stated:
'Those who are constant in their prayer.' (Al-Ma'arij - 23)
The sixth thing is the establishment and arrangement of Friday (Jumu'ah) and congregational (Jama'ah) prayers. Especially when it is attributed to the Ummah or the Imam (leader), it clearly means the establishment and arrangement of Jumu'ah and Jama'ah. For instance, observe:
'[And they are] those who, if We give them authority in the land, establish prayer and give Zakah and enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong.' (Al-Hajj - 41)
The prayer of Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him), in which he stated the mission of his progeny, is recorded in these words:
'Our Lord, I have settled some of my descendants in an uncultivated valley near Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may establish prayer.' (Ibrahim - 37)
The word 'Salah' originally in the lexicon means to turn towards something. From there, this word was used in the meaning of bowing (Ruku), and then in the meanings of reverence, entreaty, and supplication. The research of Ustadh Imam Maulana Hamiduddin Farahi (may Allah have mercy on him) is that this word has been used in the meaning of worship since ancient times. It was used in Chaldean for supplication and entreaty, and in Hebrew for bowing and prayer. In the Quran, this word is used as a specific term, which the Quran has explained, and the Sunnah has fully detailed it. Furthermore, the verbal and practical consensus (Tawatur) of the Ummah has perfectly preserved its form, structure, and timings. If there is any difference in any of its parts, it is merely of a secondary/subsidiary nature, which does not affect the core reality.

Wa bil-akhirati hum yuqinoon (And of the Hereafter they are certain [in faith]): The Hereafter (Akhirah) refers to the abode of the Hereafter or the life of the Hereafter. Here, instead of 'Iman' (belief), the word 'Iqaan' (certainty) has been used for the Hereafter. There is a slight difference between Iman and Iqaan which should be understood. Iman means to affirm and accept. Its opposite is Kufr (disbelief), denial, and rejection. Iqaan means to have absolute certainty. Its opposite is conjecture and doubt. Just as it is not necessary for a person to have Iman in something to have certainty about it, similarly, to have Iman in something, having absolute certainty is not a prerequisite. It is possible that a person's faith is based merely on predominant probability, and gradually, leaving the stage of probability, he reaches the stage of certainty, and thus his faith is perfected. Here, the mention of Iqaan comes after some well-known practical manifestations of Iman, which hints at the fact that those who possess the aforementioned qualities are, in reality, the ones who have absolute certainty in the Hereafter.

Ala hudan (Upon guidance): The various meanings of 'Huda' have been explained above. Here, from the mentioned meanings, both the meaning of 'the light of insight' and 'the straight path' can be taken. Whichever of these two meanings is taken, the interpretation of the verse fits perfectly, and it is supported by Arabic lexicon and Quranic usages.
Al-Muflihoon (The successful): The core essence of this word is expansion and revelation, and it refers to the attainment of goals and that success which, although achieved after patience-testing and grueling struggle, leaves those who worked hard completely overjoyed when attained, and all measures of their expectations fall short of measuring it.

Innal-ladheena kafaroo (Indeed, those who disbelieve): In the original lexicon, the meaning of 'Kufr' is to cover and to hide. In the Quran, this word has been used as the opposite of 'Shukr' (gratitude) as well as the opposite of 'Iman' (belief). In the first case, it means ingratitude and denial of blessings. In the second case, it means denial/disbelief. If you reflect, you will find that the root essence of the word is present within both these meanings.
In the Holy Quran, this word has been used both absolutely (without an object) and with its object. Where it is used with an object, it definitively means the disbelief and denial of that specific object. But where it is used in an absolute form without an object, it is generally used in the meaning of denying all those things which are necessary to believe in; however, in some places, it is also used in the meaning of ingratitude and denial of blessings, which is determined by the context and situation.
The context demands that 'al-ladheena kafaroo' here refers to a specific group of deniers. The reason is that certain specific characteristics of these people are also mentioned here. For example, that it is the same for them whether you warn them or not, that they are not going to believe, that Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and hearing, and there is a covering over their vision. Obviously, this was not the condition of all the disbelievers; there were many among them who were initially deniers and opponents but later embraced Islam. Because of this, it is self-evident that a specific group is meant here. However, the question arises: who are the people of this group?
In our view, this refers to the leaders and chiefs of the Quraish, the People of the Book, and the hypocrites, to whom the truth of the Quran and the Prophet (PBUH) had become completely clear, but despite this clarity, they were opposing it out of pure stubbornness, obstinacy, egoism, jealousy, and arrogance. Some reasons for this specification are as follows:
The first reason is that the preceding segment described the group that was to believe in the Quran. There, while interpreting 'Hudan lil-muttaqeen al-ladheena yu'minoona bil-ghaib', we explained that it refers to all those people of sound nature and God-fearing individuals among the People of the Book and the children of Ishmael whose conscience was alive, whose capabilities were intact, and whose hearts were awake. In contrast to them, the aforementioned verses describe the group that is not going to believe. This contrast itself is proof that this refers to those among the Quraish and the People of the Book whom worldliness, jealousy, and egoism had made completely blind and deaf, whose nature had been distorted, and who had entirely been deprived of all capabilities of accepting the truth.
The second reason is that the characteristics of this group which the Quran has described here without naming them, are exactly the same characteristics described in other places either explicitly with names or with such clear contextual indicators that the group is automatically identified. If one tries to clarify the brevity of this verse keeping those places in mind, one reaches the same conclusion that we have reached. That is, it should be understood as referring to the chiefs and leaders of the polytheists, the Jews, and the hypocrites who had fully realized the truth that the Quran's call is true, but despite this, they were using all their might to oppose it. Here we quote a few verses that support our view.
'Whoever disbelieves in Allah after his belief... except for one who is forced [to renounce his religion] while his heart is secure in faith. But those who [willingly] open their breasts to disbelief, upon them is wrath from Allah, and for them is a great punishment. That is because they preferred the worldly life over the Hereafter and that Allah does not guide the disbelieving people. Those are the ones over whose hearts and hearing and vision Allah has sealed, and it is those who are the heedless.' (An-Nahl: 106-108)
In this verse, it is completely clear that those who, after believing or after the truth has become clear to them, choose the path of disbelief purely out of worldliness, incur the wrath of Allah, for them is a great punishment, God does not open the path of faith for them, and a seal is set upon their hearts, hearing, and vision. Obviously, if anyone could be its true subjects, it could only be the chiefs of the Quraish, the scholars of the Jews, and the hypocrites, or those who adopt their ways.
In another place, regarding the opponents and enemies of all the prophets, it is stated:
'Those cities - We relate to you, [O Muhammad], some of their news. And certainly did their messengers come to them with clear proofs, but they were not to believe in that which they had denied before. Thus does Allah seal the hearts of the disbelievers.' (Al-A'raf: 101)
Specifically regarding the Jews, it is stated:
'And [We cursed them] for their breaking of the covenant and their disbelief in the signs of Allah and their killing of the prophets without right and their saying, "Our hearts are wrapped". Rather, Allah has sealed them because of their disbelief, so they believe not, except for a few.' (An-Nisa: 155)
Similarly, regarding the hypocrites, these words are mentioned:
'That is because they believed, and then they disbelieved; so their hearts were sealed over, and they do not understand.' (Al-Munafiqun: 3)
From these explicit statements of the Quran, it is perfectly clear that in the verses under discussion, 'al-ladheena kafaroo' points to a specific group. But this group is neither exclusively the polytheists nor, in a limited sense, the People of the Book; rather, it encompasses those individuals from both the polytheist and the People of the Book groups who were at the forefront of opposing the truth after having recognized it well.
The statements narrated from the predecessors (Salaf) in the interpretation of this verse also support our view. According to Ibn Abbas (RA), it refers to those stubborn people among the People of the Book who had denied all the prophecies present in their scriptures regarding the Prophet (PBUH), and thus they broke the covenant that Allah had taken from them concerning the final Prophet. According to Rabi' bin Anas, it refers to the leaders of the various factions who were at the forefront of opposing Islam. Both these statements are very close to each other; the only difference, if any, is that the interpretation of Rabi' bin Anas is relatively more comprehensive and broader. Quranic precedents support this, which is why we have adopted it.
A-andhartahum (Whether you warn them [or not]): 'Indhar' means to warn, to alert, and to inform of danger. The invitation and preaching of the Prophets (peace be upon them) is, on one hand, based on highly concrete psychological and universal proofs. On the other hand, it also contains the aspect of 'Indhar' (warning) and 'Tabsheer' (glad tidings). The concept of 'Tabsheer' gives the glad tidings of that triumph, salvation, success, and prosperity which is achieved in both this world and the Hereafter by accepting the Prophet's invitation and adopting the straight path shown by him. The concept of 'Indhar' is to warn of those dangers and perils which the deniers of the Prophet will inevitably face in both this world and the Hereafter. In normal circumstances, the Prophets (peace be upon them) perform both these duties. But where stubborn and obstinate people stand in opposition, whose opposition is not based on any misunderstanding but purely on jealousy and enmity, there naturally the aspect of warning (Indhar) becomes dominant in the Prophet's invitation, because the circumstances at that time demand it. This is why your [the Prophet's] work here has been expressed only with the word 'Indhar'. Because the verse under discussion, as has been clarified, relates to those opponents and enemies who were opposing the Prophet (PBUH) not due to some misunderstanding, but knowingly, being aware that you (PBUH) are the true Prophet and the Quran is the Book of Allah.
Whether it is warning or glad tidings, the essence of both is to inform about the natural consequences that are latent in faith or disbelief. Just as a physician informs his patient under treatment about the benefits of medicine and dietary restrictions, and the consequences of indiscretion and negligence towards the disease, similarly, the Prophet also informs his people about the benefits and consequences of accepting or rejecting his invitation. 
Some people, being ignorant of this reality of 'Indhar' (warning), raise an objection against religion that it tries to intimidate people by narrating imaginary threats and does not appeal to human intellect. These objectors are generally unaware of two things. First, they are unaware that the Quran's invitation is not based solely on warnings and glad tidings; rather, it possesses highly robust psychological and rational arguments within itself. Warning and glad tidings are just one aspect of its invitation. The second thing they are unaware of is the value and significance of moral and faith-based values. These people know that eating arsenic kills a person, but this reality is beyond their comprehension that disbelief, hypocrisy, and lying also destroy a human being. Since the Prophet is well aware of the fruits and consequences of moral values, he warns people about them, and he warns them in a style of expression that befits his knowledge and certainty. This is the very thing the Holy Quran expresses with the word 'Indhar'.

The Meaning of 'Khatm': 'Khatamalla' - The word 'Khatm' in the Arabic language means to place a stamp/seal on wax, clay, or any similar substance. From here, this word began to be used for sealing a letter and closing the mouth of something in such a way that neither can anything enter it nor can anything come out of it. In the Holy Quran, in some places, when Allah attributes an action to Himself, the intention is not to attribute the action itself directly to Him, but to attribute that law or that 'Sunnah' (Divine practice) to Himself under which that action occurs. Since this law is established by Allah Himself, the action that occurs under this law is sometimes attributed to the Maker of the law. This style of expressing meaning is found in almost every language. There are abundant examples of this in the Arabic language and the Holy Quran as well. According to this style, Allah has attributed the act of sealing the hearts to Himself here, but the purpose is to attribute the 'Sunnah of Allah' to Himself, which He has established for guidance and misguidance, and under which this act of sealing the hearts takes place. As for the question of what this 'Sunnah of Allah' is, we will explain it further below.
'Ala Sam'ihim' (Over their hearing): A question might arise in someone's mind: why is the word 'Sam'' (hearing) used here in the singular, while the words for hearts (Quloob) and eyes (Absar) are used in the plural? The harmony of speech would demand that this also be used in the plural, i.e., 'Isma''? In my view, the answer is that this relates to the usage pattern of native speakers. In the Quran, this word has been used in about 20-22 places, and mostly it is used alongside Quloob, Af'idah (hearts), and Absar (eyes), but everywhere it is used only in its singular form; it is never used in the plural form anywhere. Obviously, the Holy Quran is a standard text linguistically as well, so it must be acknowledged that the eloquent Arabs used this word in this very way in this context.
7. The Reality of the Sealing of Hearts and the Divine Law Regarding It:
Regarding the sealing of the hearts mentioned here, two things should be understood very well. The first is that this sealing does not mean a physical seal, but a metaphorical/spiritual one. As far as seeing, hearing, and understanding apparent physical things are concerned, these people saw, heard, and understood them; but people of this disposition limit all their faculties and capabilities of understanding to the superficial appearances and tangibles of the world. As for the realities hidden behind these superficial appearances, neither do they pay attention to them themselves, nor do they lend an ear to anyone else trying to draw their attention to them. Their absorption in the world and worldly adornments increases so much that there is no room left within them to pay attention to anything else. They expend their intelligence and sagacity on this single objective. The result is that their intellect becomes very sharp in measuring the length and breadth of the earth and the heavens, but when it comes to spiritual values and realities, it is utterly blunt. This situation corrupts their taste so much that they only like those things that feed this corrupted taste. They feel repelled by anything that discourages it, no matter how rational it may be. This very situation has been described here with the term 'sealing of the hearts'. 
The second thing is that this 'sealing of the hearts' does not mean that Allah created these people from their mothers' wombs with stamps on their hearts; rather, it means that through their own evil deeds, they corrupted themselves to such an extent that their hearts became deprived of the ability to hear and understand the Prophet's words. As far as Allah is concerned, He has created every human being with good capabilities, granted them the ability to distinguish between good and evil, and simultaneously ingrained in them the taste to love good and hate evil. After adorning man with these innate capabilities, He left him free to choose either the path of good or the path of evil. Moving forward, it is this chosen good or evil that plays the primary role in developing or corrupting his innate capabilities. If a person chooses the path of goodness and virtue, his natural capabilities flourish, and he is granted the Divine grace (Tawfeeq) from Allah to progress further in the path of goodness. And if, following the desires of his self, he sets out on the path of evil, his heart gradually begins to take on the color of evil, until this color dominates him so much that not a trace of goodness is left within him. This is the stage where, under the law of Allah, a seal is set upon a person's heart, and his disposition becomes so corrupted that all his interest remains solely in evil deeds; let alone doing good deeds, he even feels repulsed by hearing good words. Thus, it is repeatedly stated in the Holy Quran that this seal on a person's heart is set as a retribution for his sins. Observe a few verses: 'Has it not become clear to those who inherited the earth after its [former] people that if We willed, We could afflict them for their sins? But We seal over their hearts so they do not hear.' (Al-A'raf: 100). In this verse, it is explicitly stated that the sealing of hearts occurs as a punishment for sins. In another place, it is stated: 'And certainly did their messengers come to them with clear proofs, but they were not to believe in that which they had denied before. Thus does Allah seal the hearts of the disbelievers. And We did not find for most of them any covenant; but indeed, We found most of them defiantly disobedient.' (Al-A'raf: 101-102). That is, they were already well-practiced in violating Allah's covenant and His commands. For this reason, when their messengers came to them with the verses of Allah and His signs, they cared nothing for them either. For those who become so audacious and stubborn in denying the truth, Allah seals their hearts, causing their intellect to be completely wiped out. This is stated with even greater clarity regarding the Jews: 'And [We cursed them] for their breaking of the covenant and their disbelief in the signs of Allah and their killing of the prophets without right and their saying, "Our hearts are wrapped" [i.e., sealed]. Rather, Allah has sealed them because of their disbelief, so they believe not, except for a few.' (An-Nisa: 155). 
From the above verses, one reality that becomes clear is that Allah does not send anyone from their mother's womb with a sealed heart; rather, whosoever's heart gets sealed, it happens as a natural consequence of their own sins. The second reality that becomes clear is that not every level of sin is what results in a person's heart being sealed; rather, when an individual or a group, while recognizing the truth as truth and acting completely against the testimony of their own conscience, opposes it purely out of stubbornness, selfish motives, and obstinacy, and remains adamant in this opposition, then its effect is that their heart is sealed, and they are deprived of the ability to think and understand correctly. The third reality that becomes clear is that the heart being sealed in this manner and being deprived of the faculties of hearing and sight is a punishment from Allah, which descends upon an individual or group in this world in retribution for their ingratitude towards His blessings, and the natural consequence of this very punishment is the 'great punishment' which such people will suffer in the life after this one. Therefore, the phrase 'Wa lahum adhabun azeem' (And for them is a great punishment) at the end of the verse under discussion is actually a statement of that natural consequence of the sealing of the hearts which will manifest in the Hereafter.
The reality of the sealing of the hearts that we have explained is the very reality clarified by the Ahadith (prophetic traditions) as well. To avoid excessive length, we will suffice with just one Hadith here: 'When a believer commits a sin, a black spot appears on his heart. If he repents, desists from the sin, and seeks forgiveness from Allah, his heart is polished clean. But if his sins increase until the blackness covers his entire heart, then that is the 'Ran' (rust/covering) which Allah the Exalted has mentioned: 'No! Rather, the stain has covered their hearts of that which they were earning'.' (Ibn Kathir, referencing Tirmidhi). For the righteous predecessors (Salaf Saliheen) as well, this is the reality of the sealing of the hearts. Ibn Kathir has narrated on the authority of Al-A'mash, who said that Mujahid once explained to us that the Salaf (Companions) considered the heart to be like the palm of a hand. When a person indulges in a sin, (he explained by folding one finger) the heart contracts like this. Then when he commits another sin, (folding another finger, he said) the heart clenches further like this. Similarly, he folded a third finger, until one after another he folded all the fingers. Then he said that when the heart is clenched like this by the dominance of sins, a seal is set upon it. Mujahid mentioned that the Salaf (Companions) considered this very thing to be the 'Ran' mentioned in the verse 'Kalla bal rana ala quloobihim...'.
Once the actual reality of the sealing of the hearts has become clear, we have no need to delve into the debate of Predestination vs. Free Will (Jabr wa Ikhtiyar) which raged between the Ash'arites and the Mu'tazilites, and into which these gentlemen unnecessarily dragged this verse as well. The Holy Quran is neither in favor of the strict predestination claimed by the Ash'arites, nor in favor of the absolute free will championed by the Mu'tazilites; rather, the truth lies between these two. But this is not the appropriate place for the details of this issue. We will only state a few principled points here, which, God willing, will suffice for those who reflect on this issue with a purely scholarly mind, above all kinds of prejudice. These principled points are as follows:
1. From the very origin of nature, Allah has created every human being upon a good nature, granted him the distinction between good and evil, and given him the freedom to choose whichever of them he wishes to choose. After that, his becoming good or bad depends on his own attitude and Divine grace (Tawfeeq). If he tries to choose the path of goodness, Allah grants him the Tawfeeq for goodness, and if he wants to tread the path of evil, Allah, if He wills, leaves him to go down the path of evil as well.
2. For the things for which Allah will hold humans accountable or for which He will reward them, He has also granted humans the freedom of choice and will. Those who do not possess this choice and will, Allah has exempted them from accountability as well. This choice and will are not inherent to humans but are granted by Allah, and the human uses it under the Will (Mashiyat) of Allah alone. Under His Will and Wisdom, Allah may not allow the intention of any human to be fulfilled; however, if under some wisdom He does not allow a person's good intention to be fulfilled, He does not deprive him of the reward for that goodness. Similarly, if He does not allow a person's evil scheme to reach completion, it does not mean that He will necessarily exempt him from its consequence in the Hereafter.
3. Wherever the absolute Will of Allah is mentioned in the Holy Quran, it solely means that no one else can stop or alter His Will. It does not mean that His Will is not bound by any justice and wisdom at all. Allah is Just and Wise; none of His actions are devoid of justice and wisdom. Therefore, wherever He has mentioned His Will, it should be understood under this very law of justice and wisdom under which He has chosen to run the order of this world. It is completely incorrect to imagine that He would Himself break, by the force of His Will, the very 'Sunnah' He Himself initiated and the law of justice He Himself chose. For example, when Allah says that He guides whom He wills and misguides whom He wills, it does not mean that He has set absolutely no rule of justice and wisdom for guidance and misguidance; rather, it means that this guidance and misguidance occur according to the Sunnah that He has established for guidance and misguidance, and no one else is capable of breaking or altering this Sunnah.
4. In the Holy Quran, Allah has attributed certain actions to Himself, but the actual intent of these, as we hinted above, is not the attribution of the actions themselves, but the attribution of the rules and laws under which those actions occur. Since those rules and regulations are established by Allah Himself, in some places Allah has also attributed the actions occurring under them to Himself. For example, it is said, 'So when they deviated, Allah caused their hearts to deviate.' Or it is said, 'And We will turn away their hearts and their eyes.' In such places, the principle under which the action occurs is also generally stated in the Holy Quran. For example, a statement is made like 'Allah does not misguide except the defiantly disobedient.' The meaning of these hints is that the reader should turn their attention to the actual reality and not fall into any misconception from the apparent words.
5. Allah's eternal, everlasting, and all-encompassing knowledge does not negate any of the 'Sunnahs' (practices/laws) established by Allah. There is no doubt that He knows from eternity about every person whether they will choose the path of guidance or misguidance, but alongside this, He also knows that they will choose guidance or misguidance in accordance with the very Sunnah of Allah that He has established for guidance and misguidance. Whoever keeps these principled points in mind will, God willing, automatically emerge from many of the confusions regarding predestination and free will which are not created by the Holy Quran, but by the hair-splitting of the scholastic theologians (Mutakallimeen).
8. The Main Objective of the Collection of Verses 6-7:
The main objective of these verses is not just to inform the Prophet (PBUH) that the people of a certain group are not going to believe whether you warn them or not, but these two verses (6-7) are unveiling a few highly important realities. We will point to some of these things here so that the actual teaching of these verses becomes clear. 
1. The first thing that is most evident in these verses is the comfort and consolation for the Prophet (PBUH), and a reprimand and threat for his opponents. The Prophet (PBUH) is told not to think that these people who are steadfast in their disbelief are doing so because there is some shortcoming in your warning and preaching, or that the word you are reciting is ineffective in some aspect. Neither is there any shortcoming in your warning and preaching, nor is there any defect or gap in this word; rather, the entire fault lies within the hearts of these people themselves. By continuously denying the truths of Allah's religion, they have now come under the strike of the Divine law, due to which all capabilities of receptivity have been stripped from their hearts, the capacity to listen to the truth from their ears, and the ability to take heed from their eyes. Now you should completely despair of their reform and salvation. If anything is left for them now, it is the punishment of Allah, which they will inevitably face.
2. The second reality that becomes clear is that the pathway for faith and guidance to enter a person is through their heart, intellect, ears, and eyes. If a person keeps them open, casts a glance of insight at the observations occurring every moment in the horizons (the universe) and within themselves; listens with complete attention to the words of God and the callers to the truth; and then, after pondering and reflecting on all these things, firmly grasps the realities they arrive at with honesty and integrity and makes them the amulet of their life—only then do they receive guidance. If they do not adopt this path and do not use these capabilities granted by nature, they may bang their head a hundred thousand times, but the door of faith and guidance cannot open for them.
3. The third reality that becomes clear is that a person's spiritual and intellectual progress and their perfection entirely depend on using the great capabilities of hearing, sight, and heart (Fu'ad) that Allah has granted them, for their correct purpose. If a person does not use them, or uses them but not for the high purpose for which they were actually granted, then Allah turns them into a curse (Wabaal). In the case of not using them, the form of their becoming a curse is that despite possessing everything, the person remains helpless and stranded in every field of thought and action. In the case of misuse, they become a curse in such a way that they cause the person to wander aimlessly in every valley and desert throughout their life, to the extent that even in this infinite space, they make them spin around; the only door they do not let them reach is the main door of salvation and success.

Min an-Naasi (From among the people): Although the word 'An-Naas' (the people) is general, the context is evidence that a specific group is meant here from this general term, and that group is the Jews. The reason for this specification is that only the Jews could be the ones among whom a faction could adopt the guise to which the Quran has pointed in these verses. The clarification of this brevity will come ahead under a separate heading.

Yukhadi'oon-Allaha (They seek to deceive Allah): 'Mukhad'ah' means to attempt to deceive, whether that deception succeeds or not. Here, both the word 'Mukhad'ah' and 'Khada'' (to deceive) are used. Where the word relates to Allah, 'Mukhad'ah' (attempt to deceive) is used, because a person might desire to deceive Allah due to his own foolishness, but obviously, he cannot actually deceive Him. On the contrary, for themselves, the word 'Khada'' is used, because whoever intends to deceive God certainly fails in his attempt, but he definitely puts himself into deception.
Wa ma yash'uroon (And they do not perceive): The word 'Shu'oor' (perception) is used for the cognition of a tangible/perceptible thing. The use of this word here points to the reality that although deceiving oneself in the attempt to deceive God is a perceptible thing, these self-deluded people, despite their presumption of cleverness and cunning, are so obtuse that they are not perceiving this reality, because its consequence has not yet appeared before them.

Fi quloobihim maradun (In their hearts is a disease): The word 'Marad' (disease) is generally used in two meanings in the Quran. One in the meaning of malice and envy, and the second in the meaning of hypocrisy. In the places where this word is used in the meaning of hypocrisy, it is clearly in the meaning of malice and envy, but in the places where it is used independently, either both meanings are combined within it, or the context determines one of its two meanings. Here, a clear contextual indicator exists that it means envy, because the group being described here will later be clarified as a faction from among the Jews themselves, and the envy the Jews held for the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and those who believed in him is known and famous. The Holy Quran has mentioned it in multiple places.
Fa zadahum-ullahu marada (So Allah increased them in disease): Here, the act of increasing the envy which Allah has attributed to Himself is, in reality, the attribution of the 'Sunnah' (Divine Law) under which this act takes place. Allah's Sunnah is that if a person, instead of making his chest a manifestation of faith and submission (Islam), wishes to keep it as a breeding ground for malice and envy, then similar circumstances and events unfold before him which water this very poisonous crop of his. The Jews were envious of the Muslims as to why Allah had bestowed the blessing of Islam upon them. The result was that the day-by-day progress of Islam and its blessings further added to the causes of their envy, and this increase continued relentlessly. Until this very thing completely destroyed them.

La tufsidoo fil-ard (Do not cause corruption on the earth): 'Fasad fil-ard' (Corruption on earth) is a Quranic term whose meaning is to corrupt or attempt to corrupt the system of truth which is based on the worship of the One God and obedience to His commands and laws, and the invitation to which the noble Prophets (peace be upon them) bring. The Quran's claim is that just as the cosmic order of this universe is sustained because the will of the One Omnipotent and Irresistible Lord operates within it—if anyone else's power and authority were to operate within it, it would be shattered to pieces in an instant—similarly, in its legislative order, if the permissibility or interference of anyone else's worship and obedience is acknowledged, its temperament is completely ruined. And this ruin destroys the entire system of civilization. For this reason, according to the Quran, every attempt that opens the door to this ruin falls under the ruling of 'Fasad fil-ard', even if this attempt is apparently made with the good intention of reform.
Kama aamana-nnaasu (As the people have believed): Here, 'An-Naas' (the people) refers to those Muslims who believed in the Prophet (PBUH).

Wa idha khalaw ila shayateenihim (And when they are alone with their devils): After 'Khalaw', the preposition 'ila' demands that a verb compatible with this preposition be considered omitted (Mahdhoof) here. We have tried to maintain this consideration in the translation.
The word 'Shaytan' is an intensive form (Mubalaghah) on the pattern of 'Fa'lan', derived from 'Shata-Yasheetu'. Its meanings include hasty, ill-tempered, easily enraged, and wicked & rebellious. There are those who possess these characteristics among the jinn, and also among humans. Here, this word is used for those Jewish leaders who were guiding this entire game of 'Fasad fil-ard' (corruption on earth).

Allahu yastahzi'u bihim wa yamudduhum fi tughyanihim (Allah mocks them and prolongs them in their transgression): 'Madd' means to give slack and to lengthen someone's rope. The meaning is that they are advancing in their rebellion, and Allah is lengthening their rope to establish the proof (Hujjah) against them so that when He seizes them, no excuse is left for them.
The 'mockery' Allah has mentioned doing with them is clarified by the words 'yamudduhum fi tughyanihim' (prolongs them in their transgression). These people were happy that they had succeeded in making fools of the Muslims and deceiving Allah. However, a person who, according to his own thinking, deceives the one showing the right path and adopts a wrong path, does not harm the one showing the path; rather, he involves himself in the calamity of aimless wandering. Now, it is merely his self-delusion and foolishness that he thinks he deceived the guide. In reality, he is the one who has been deceived.

Ishtarawud-dalalata (They purchased error): 'Ishtira' means to buy. When a person buys a thing by paying a price, he gives preference to that thing over what he sets as the price. From here, the meaning of 'giving preference' was born within this word, and in this meaning, this word has been used in various places in the Quran.
Who does this point to?
Two groups have been mentioned above. One, the people who believed; second, the people who are not going to believe. Following both these groups, this is the description of a third group which, although connected to the non-believing group, has a somewhat different temperament in terms of some of its characteristics. The question is, who are the people of this group? People generally have understood that this is the group of hypocrites. But a doubt arises here that the group generally understood by the term 'hypocrites' outwardly tried to keep itself included among the Muslims in every aspect, and their enmity with Islam and Muslims was hidden, which only manifested on specific occasions. But the characteristics the Quran has described for this group make it clear that these people were neither inwardly with the Muslims, nor were they verbally ready to express agreement with them. For example, these people claimed to believe in Allah and the Hereafter, but they were not ready to even verbally express belief in the Prophet (PBUH) and the Quran. Furthermore, they considered themselves superior to the Muslims. Thus, when they were asked that if they claim to have faith, they should believe like the Muslims do, they openly labeled the Muslims as fools. For this reason, considering them in the category of hypocrites in the general sense is not correct in our view. But if they did not belong to the category of hypocrites, then who are these people and to which category do they belong?
In our view, the answer to this question is that this too was a faction from within the Jews, but its role in opposing Islam was of a somewhat different nature from the character of the group mentioned earlier. The group mentioned above was not even ready to listen to the words of the Prophet (PBUH), but had descended into opposing him blindly and deafly. But this group wanted to oppose Islam under the guise of diplomatic expediency and reconciliation.
Reflecting deeply on the above verses, the mental background of this group that emerges is that, as far as the sentiment of enmity towards Islam is concerned, this group was in no way lesser than the former group. The feeling of superiority over others that existed among the Jews was also present to the fullest extent within these people. They were also afflicted with the jealousy the Jews harbored against the children of Ishmael due to the prophecy of the final Prophet appearing among them. In fact, the practical manifestation of this prophecy, the daily progress of Islam, and the extraordinary popularity of the Prophet (PBUH) among the Arabs had increased their jealousy much more. In all these matters, these people were partners with their fellow tribesmen. However, to stop the rising tide of Islam, they did not consider the policy of mere denial and stubbornness adopted by the group of Jews mentioned earlier to be correct. Instead, these people desired a sort of compromise between Judaism and Islam. Their desire was that Islam should remain in its place, and the status and distinction they themselves enjoyed as a religious group should also remain. Based on these verses, the form they had in mind seems to be that they desired the Muslims to consider them as believers and monotheists just like themselves, because as far as belief in Allah and the Hereafter is concerned, they claimed to believe in these two things as well. Their thought was that if the Muslims want to accept Muhammad (PBUH) as a Prophet and the book presented by him as a heavenly book, they may do so, but they should not insist on them (the Jews) accepting it. If the Muslims made the salvation of others dependent on accepting him as well, and declared whoever did not accept him as a denier of Allah and His Messenger, then according to them, this would create a severe state of hatred and conflict between the different religions and their followers in this country, and the atmosphere of religious tolerance that had existed in this country until now would be completely destroyed. Based on this very thought, these people also considered themselves to be reformers. That is, their thought was that if we are not accepting Muhammad (PBUH) and the Quran, this is not an attempt at corruption; rather, it is an attempt at pure reform, because this way we are stopping the chaos being created by this new Prophethood and this new invitation.

This parable is of a person who kindled a fire in a dark night to show light to the people. He did this work with great effort and great arrangement until his entire surroundings were illuminated. But the people for whom he bore this hardship did not appreciate this light at all. As a punishment for their lack of appreciation, Allah took away their light and left them in pitch-darkness where they could not even see their own hands. Then, an additional wrath on top of this darkness is that these people are deaf, dumb, and blind, and all these traits are present in them simultaneously. (1. Regarding 'deaf, dumb, blind', the insight of Ustadh Imam Maulana Farahi (may Allah have mercy on him) is worth mentioning here: if traits are described without a conjunction (harf-e-'ataf), it is proof that all these traits are present in the subject simultaneously). Because of this, neither can they hear the call of a caller, nor can they answer his call, nor can they seek guidance from any sign, symbol, or indication. For this reason, there is no possibility that they can turn back from the path they have set out on and adopt another path.
If you reflect, this parable applies perfectly to the group of Jews mentioned earlier, about whom Allah has informed that a seal has been set upon their hearts and their ears, and a covering has fallen over their eyes. Therefore, they are not going to believe now.
In the parable, the person kindling the fire points to Prophet Moses (peace be upon him). He lit the candle of guidance for his people, and this candle provided light for the whole nation, but not much time passed before the majority of the Children of Israel became disgusted with this light, in punishment of which Allah cursed them, and they were deprived of the capability to understand and accept words of guidance.
The details of this deprivation and wretchedness of the Children of Israel are described in the Torah and the Gospel as well, and it is also mentioned in various places in the Quran. Here, their very condition is presented in the form of a parable. This is a parable of a caravan whose all individuals are deaf, dumb, and blind; moreover, the night is dark, and in this dark night, this caravan is wandering lost. Neither do they hear anyone, nor can they call out to anyone, nor can they answer anyone, nor can they obtain guidance from any sign or light.

Meaning of 'Sayyib': The word 'Sayyib' is used for heavy rain and also for a forcefully pouring cloud. We have preferred the first meaning in our translation. The reason is that in the parable, as will be clear later, this word points to the Holy Quran, and the Quran has been compared to rain in various places within the Quran itself.
Meaning of 'Sama': The word 'Sama' is generally spoken for the blue roof we call the sky. In addition to this, it has also come in the meaning of a cloud, and for the vast and expansive space above our heads.
Although rain falls from the sky anyway, which makes the addition of the word 'Sama' (sky) seem somewhat unnecessary, this addition first brings the picture of the rain before the eyes, and such a picture is of great importance in any parable. Secondly, it subtly points to the heavenly nature of the Holy Quran, because this rain refers to the Quran itself, as we pointed out.
Meaning of 'Sawa'iq': 'Sawa'iq' is the plural of 'Sa'iqah'. Its meanings include thunder and a crack, and this word is also spoken for the lightning that strikes with a thunderclap.
This is a parable of a caravan that has been caught in rain in the darkness of the night. There is pitch darkness, it is raining heavily, and with the rain, there is thunder and lightning. The condition of the people of the caravan is that when it thunders, they put their fingers in their ears out of fear. When the lightning flashes, they walk a few steps in its light. When it vanishes, they stand still.
This parable is the picture of the second group of Jews, the mention of which begins with the verse 'Wa min-an-naasi...'. In this, the rain points to the Holy Quran. The darkness points to the difficulties of the path that those accepting the invitation of the Quran inevitably had to face. Thunder and lightning refer to those threats and warnings the Quran was issuing to its deniers, the brunt of which was particularly falling upon the Jews. The correct remedy for this group was to accept the invitation of the Quran, but entirely contrary to this, they thought the remedy was to not listen to the Quran's words at all. The parable depicts this situation as: 'These people thrust their fingers into their ears out of fear of death.' Obviously, this is a foolish tactic. If lightning is about to strike, can the tactic of thrusting your fingers in your ears be effective to save yourself? This is exactly like a person seeing a lion about to attack him, but he closes his eyes. By someone closing their eyes like this, the lion is not going to refrain from attacking. However, what will happen is that he will not see the lion's attack.
Similarly, this remedy for the warnings and threats of the Holy Quran—that they not be listened to—is a foolish remedy. This will make no difference to their reality; however, if anything happens, it is that they will occur when the person is completely heedless of them. It is famous about the ostrich that when it senses the danger of a storm, it hides its head in the sand. To escape a reality, this policy of this group of Jews was also no different from this policy of the ostrich.

'When the lightning flashes, they walk a few steps; when it vanishes, they stand still.' This is a picture of their bewilderment and distress which they were afflicted with after the revelation of the Quran. They could not understand at all how to confront the Quran. Its brilliance and radiance were dazzling to the eyes, and it was no longer possible for them to escape its snatching lightning. They were bewildered and helpless as to what to do. In this state of bewilderment and helplessness, if they saw any excuse forming, they tried to form it, but confronting a reality is not possible merely with rhetoric for very long. For this reason, when the fabricated excuse collapsed, they would then become bewildered and helpless, looking around for an escape. Thus, it was mentioned above that to deceive the Muslims, they used to say, 'If you claim to believe in God and the Hereafter, we also believe in God and the Hereafter.' But when they were challenged on this—that if you claim faith, why don't you simply believe like the Muslims do?—then out of desperation, they would start spewing venom against the Muslims and abusing them.

The Address of 'Ya Ayyuhan-Naas' to the Polytheists: Although the address 'Ya Ayyuhan-Naas' (O mankind) is apparently general, the addressees here, as mentioned above, are specifically the polytheists of Arabia. The reason for considering this address specific to the polytheists in our view is that the statement made after this, the style of argumentation adopted, and the demand made from the addressee—everything testifies that the main focus of the address here is indeed towards the polytheists. This address has come in the middle of the mention of the Jews as a parenthesis/aside.
'Ubudoo rabbakum (Worship your Lord): The research on the word 'worship' (Ibadah) has been covered in the Tafseer of Surah Al-Fatihah. Here, the purpose of 'Ubudoo rabbakum' is not merely to invite the polytheists to worship God, but that they accept this invitation to the worship of God that Muhammad (PBUH) is giving. The hidden reality of this statement is exactly what made it suitable to be connected with 'And if you are in doubt about what We have sent down upon Our Servant...'. Meaning: Accept the worship to which the Prophet (PBUH) is inviting, and if you have a doubt about this book—that it is something fabricated, not sent down by God—then you too produce a Surah like it.
Moreover, hidden within this is also the point that the worship of God you claim to perform is not truly the worship of God; the correct method of worshipping God is the one this book is inviting to.
Khalaqakum wal-ladheena min qablikum (Who created you and those before you): The addition of 'wal-ladheena min qablikum' (and those before you) along with 'Khalaqakum' is because the polytheists of Arabia did acknowledge that their Creator is Allah alone, but they had associated some of their ancestors with Divine attributes, standing them in the ranks of the Creator, and by making their idols, had started worshipping them. Here, the Quran, by including all their ancestors along with them among the general creations of Allah, also subtly drew attention to the fact that if you want to worship God, then not only should you submit to God by acknowledging yourself as a created and subjugated being, but also acknowledge those whom you have foolishly given the status of Creator as merely God's creation.
La'allakum tattaqoon (So that you may become righteous / protect yourselves): 'La'all' comes in various meanings, among which its use to express a possible and expected consequence of a thing is well-known and recognized. We have taken it in this meaning here, and in the context this word is used, these very meanings are correct in our view.
'Tattaqoon' can have two meanings here. One is that you acquire 'Taqwa' (piety/God-consciousness), and the second is that you save yourselves from God's wrath and His punishment. Here, there is a possibility of both meanings being correct, but we have preferred the second meaning. In this case, its object has to be considered omitted (Mahdhoof). The Quran itself has revealed this omitted object in the following verse. It is stated: 'Fattaqu-nnar-allati waqooduhan-naasu wal-hijarah' (Al-Baqarah - 24) (So fear the Fire, whose fuel is men and stones).

Fala taj'aloo lillahi andadan wa antum ta'lamoon (So do not set up equals to Allah while you know): 'Andad' is the plural of 'Nidd', which means equal, peer, rival, similar, and match.
The Arabs accepted all those fundamental premises related to the attributes of the Creator from which Monotheism (Tawheed) is self-evidently proven. However, despite this, they also believed in partners to God. Therefore, it is said that when you yourselves know this fact that it is God alone who created you, He alone created those before you, He alone laid out the bed of the earth for you, He alone stretched out the canopy of the sky, He alone sent down water from the sky, and He alone produced various kinds of fruits and crops for your sustenance, then why do you set up as partners to God those who have not done a single one of these things? Here, 'while you know' means that you accept and acknowledge all these things.

Meaning of 'Shaheed': Ud'oo shuhada'akum (Call your witnesses): 'Shaheed' refers to a leader, spokesman, and representative of a nation who represents and speaks for it on important occasions and stands up as its supporter. These supporters could be from among humans and, according to the belief of the Arabs, from among the jinn as well. In pre-Islamic Arabia (Jahiliyyah), poets and orators held great honor and prestige because these were the people who stood as protectors of national dignity on all important occasions. The Arab polytheists also held the belief that every poet is accompanied by a jinn who inspires him with poetry. Therefore, they held the same assumption about the Quran, that it was also a marvel of similar inspiration.
