Based on these very thoughts of theirs, a demand was made of them: if you consider the Quran to be something fabricated by a human or a jinn, then with the help of your supporters, produce just a single Surah like it. If these supporters of yours do not stand up to help you even on this critical occasion, when not only your ancestral religion but their own godhood is at stake, then understand that this Quran is Divine speech and all these gods and goddesses of yours are completely baseless. The Quran has explained this theme in other places as well. For example, it is stated:
'Say, "If mankind and the jinn gathered in order to produce the like of this Quran, they could not produce the like of it, even if they were to each other assistants."' (Al-Isra - 88)
In another place, this theme is further clarified:
'And call upon whoever you can besides Allah, if you should be truthful. And if they do not respond to you - then know that the Quran was revealed with the knowledge of Allah.' (Hud 13-14)
In kuntum sadiqeen (If you are truthful): 'If you are truthful' can have two meanings. One is: if you are truthful in the assumption you hold about the Quran. The second is: if you are truthful in your belief that you have other supporters and helpers besides God. If you truly have some supporters and helpers, then call them for help; what occasion could be more important than this to seek their help and for them to rise to help you?
My inclination is towards this second meaning. The reason for this is that it also encompasses the first meaning.

Waqoodu-han-naasu wal-hijarah (Whose fuel is men and stones): These words are describing the temperament of the Fire with which these deniers of the Quran have been threatened. From this, it is known that the preferred fuel for this Fire will, firstly, be those people within whom the material of disbelief and polytheism is present; it is from their bodies that this Fire will blaze in its true colors. And secondly, its fuel will be those stones that have been worshipped or are being worshipped as deities in the world, because due to this worship, some combustible material of polytheism is created within them as well, which is the favorite food of this Fire.
Although the word 'Al-Hijarah' (stones) is general, it is clear from the context that it refers to those very carved stones that are worshipped as gods and goddesses. Throwing them into Hell is not actually meant to punish them, but rather to increase the punishment of their worshippers. In this way, they will be shown what is becoming of those before whom they used to prostrate in the world and to whom they used to offer milk and sweets as a feast.
Insulting the symbols of disbelief is actually meant to insult the disbelief itself. The Holy Quran has itself clarified this entire reality in another place:
'Indeed, you [disbelievers] and what you worship other than Allah are the firewood of Hell. You will be coming to [enter] it. Had these [false deities] been [actual] gods, they would not have come to it, but all are eternal therein.' (Al-Anbiya 98-99)

Jannatin tajree min tahtihal-anhar (Gardens beneath which rivers flow): In a garden, the most captivating concept is that it is on an elevation and a river flows beneath it. The elevation adds to the charm of this scenery and protects it from the disasters of floods, etc., and the river flowing below guarantees its lushness. The parable of an elevated garden is also present in verse 265 of this Surah: 'Kamasali jannatin birabwatin...' (Like a garden on high ground...). In the verse under discussion, the words 'min tahtihal-anhar' (beneath which rivers flow) self-evidently clarify that these gardens will be on an elevation.
Qaloo hadhal-ladhi ruziqna min qablu (They will say, "This is what we were provided with before"): 'Saying' takes many forms. One saying is that which is heard. One saying is that which is done secretly. The Quran says:
'It is the same [to Him] concerning you whether one conceals [his] speech or one publicizes it...' (Ar-Ra'd - 10)
This word is also used for a mere gesture. For example:
'Then say [by gesture], "Indeed, I have vowed a fast to the Most Merciful, so I will not speak today to [any] man."' (Maryam - 26)
The hint that emerges from the language of circumstances and actions is also a form of saying. Similarly, this word is also used for the thoughts a person says in his heart. There are many examples of this in Arabic speech and the Holy Quran. For example, the state of the hypocrites is described in Surah Al-Ma'idah:
'They say, "We fear that a misfortune may strike us." But perhaps Allah will bring conquest or a decision from Him, and they will become, over what they have been concealing within themselves, regretful.' (Al-Ma'idah - 52)
In the mentioned verse, the thought in the hearts of these hypocrites has been expressed as 'saying', and then it is also clarified that this is the hidden matter of their hearts. Similarly, in the verse under discussion, 'Qaloo' (they will say) means they will think in their hearts that the blessings whose taste the Quran and the Prophet (PBUH) made them experience in the world through their glad tidings, those very blessings are now appearing before them in their true shape and form. This thought will arise in their hearts with a deep joy, a profound sense of success, and an enthusiastic feeling of gratitude and thanks. They will be happy that, Alhamdulillah, all the promises upon which they lived and died have proven true, and the blessings of the Paradise they are now enjoying, they had already taken a metaphorical tour of them in the world itself thanks to the Quran.
The word 'Rizq' (provision) in this segment is also worth pondering. This word is used in both the Arabic language and the Quran for both material provision and spiritual provision. One should not consider only food and drink items as provision; the true provision is the knowledge and insight that we have attained from the Quran and the Prophet (PBUH). For this reason, the Quran has expressed revelation with the word 'Rizq', and there is a saying of Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him) that man does not live by bread alone, but by the word that comes from God.
People have provided two interpretations of 'min qablu' (before). One is 'before this in the world', and the second is 'before this in this very Paradise'. My teacher Maulana Farahi (may Allah have mercy on him) tried to combine both these interpretations, but in my view, as I hinted above, this points to the world. The reasons for this will become clear moving forward.
Azwajun mutahharah (Purified spouses): 'Zawj' means a pair; a man is a pair for a woman, and a woman for a man. Nature itself has left a void within human beings that cannot be filled by any means other than this pair. Therefore, without it, the concept of any blessing is not complete for a human being. Thus, in Paradise as well, which is the expression of the perfection of blessings, its mention is present. Alongside this, the attribute 'Mutahharah' (purified) reveals the reality that they have been nurtured with great care and their purification (Tazkiyah) has been done so that they may be completely suitable for the companionship of the people of Paradise. This meaning emerges from the word 'Mutahharah', because 'Tatheer' (purification) means to groom and purify someone's habits, traits, nature, and temperament with special care and attention. In Surah Al-Ahzab, after teaching many necessary etiquettes to the household of the Prophet (PBUH), it is stated:
'Allah intends only to remove from you the impurity [of sin], O people of the [Prophet's] household, and to purify you with [extensive] purification.' (Al-Ahzab - 33)

The True Value of a Parable: Innallaha la yastahyee an yadriba mathalan (Indeed, Allah is not timid to present an example): 'Darb-e-mathal' means to explain a reality in the style of a parable. Until high realities and spiritual subtleties are dressed in the garment of a parable, they do not come into the grasp of common intellect; therefore, in teaching spiritual realities, this genre of speech holds great importance. Thus, it is abundantly found in the speech of Prophets and sages. This can be gauged by glancing at the Torah and the Gospel. The speech of Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him) is full of parables. There are also countless parables in the Ahadith of the Prophet (PBUH). Highly excellent examples of this genre of speech are also present in the Quran.
What is to be seen in a parable is only how beautifully the reality presented in it has been conveyed. There is not much debate about what the constituent elements of the parable are. Whatever thing can be useful in painting a picture of a reality before the eyes can be utilized in a parable, be it a fly, a mosquito, or a spider. The Holy Quran, while giving an example of the helplessness of the polytheists' deities, has said that if a fly were to snatch anything from these gods, they cannot even take it back from it. Similarly, the reliance they placed on partners and intercessors has been exemplified by the fragility of a spider's web. The Jews, being careless about the principles of religion while fussing over its minutiae, were compared by Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him) to straining out a gnat and swallowing a camel.
All these similes and parables are of the highest order in this respect: the realities presented in them become very well understood even by a common man through the medium of these parables. For this reason, appreciators of knowledge and insight greatly value these parables and derive immense benefit from them. But those who are enemies of knowledge and insight and slaves to the desires of their self are very irritated by these parables. The reason for this is that these parables unveil things for them which they do not wish to be unveiled against the desires of their self. When they want to vent this anger, it is not possible for them to directly attack the reality the parable is presenting, because it is so clear that saying anything against it would be tantamount to throwing dust at the sun. However, they will try to vent their anger against it by taking cover behind some element of the parable. For example, suppose a fly or a mosquito is mentioned in the parable, then no matter how illuminating the parable is, they will say, 'What a useless parable this is; if this is the word of God, could God only find a fly and a mosquito for a parable?'... In this way, they will try to throw dust in the eyes of their own conscience as well as in the eyes of others.
Meaning of 'Fisq': Wa ma yudillu bihee illal-fasiqeen (And He does not misguide by it except the defiantly disobedient): The root meaning of 'Fisq' is 'Khurooj' (to exit/deviate). From here, this word was used for exiting from the recognized good (Ma'roof) to the evil (Munkar), and from obedience to disobedience. In the Holy Quran, regarding Iblis, it is stated:
'He was of the jinn and deviated (Fasaqa) from the command of his Lord.' (Al-Kahf - 50)
There can be different degrees of deviating from the recognized good to the evil, and from obedience to disobedience. The evil can be small or large, and similarly, disobedience can be of a minor degree or of the degree of rebellion. Thus, in the Quran, this word has been used for everything from common evil acts to disbelief and rebellion; in fact, its usage has mostly been for those major disobediences with which faith cannot coexist. Therefore, this word should not be taken everywhere in the Quran in the lighter meaning in which our jurists (Fuqaha) and theologians (Mutakallimeen) have generally taken it.

The Importance of the Tie of Kinship: Wa yaqta'oona ma amarallahu bihee an yoosala (And sever that which Allah has ordered to be joined): 'And they sever that which Allah has ordered to be joined'. In our view, this refers to severing the ties of the womb (Silat ar-Rahm) and ties of kinship. After breaking the covenant with Allah, the second step a disobedient person takes is disregard for the rights of the womb (kinship), or imbalance and injustice in them. Since Islam has placed the foundation of all goodness, success, civilization, and society on Taqwa (God-consciousness) towards Allah and the respect for the ties of the womb, the person who frees himself from both these obligations will inevitably cause his every step to be a source of 'Fasad fil-ard' (corruption on earth). Therefore, here too, the inevitable result of breaking Allah's covenant and severing the ties of the womb is stated as 'Wa yufsidoona fil-ard' (and they cause corruption on the earth). In the Quran, the mention of these two things occurs together as if they are inseparable. For example:
'So would you perhaps, if you turned away, cause corruption on earth and sever your [ties of] relationship?' (Muhammad - 22)
Due to this very necessity of these two being together, Qatadah has taken it to mean the ties of the womb here, and Ibn Jarir has preferred this statement. Some people have kept it general and taken it to mean the severing of every thing that God has ordered to be joined. As far as the apparent words are concerned, this meaning cannot be declared wrong either, but the question is not merely of the apparent words, but of the style of expression of the Holy Quran. Wherever the Quran has adopted this style of expression, the context and situation are evidence that it has been adopted for the ties of the womb. The ambiguity in this style of expression actually highlights the greatness and importance of the ties of the womb: it is such a clear, evident, and well-known reality that even without naming it, every person knows and understands what it is that God has ordered to be joined, not severed. And from the perspective of the betterment and success of civilization and society, its importance is such that whoever severs it has virtually placed an axe on the very root of civilization and society.

A Specific Aspect of Kufr (Disbelief): Kayfa takfuroona billah (How can you disbelieve in Allah?): The research on the meaning of 'Kufr' has been explained in the eighth section. Here, attention is to be drawn to a specific aspect of this word, which is that this word has been used addressing those people who were not deniers of God, but only associated partners with Him. However, they were either deniers of the Day of Resurrection or at least considered it a very far-fetched and irrational concept. Addressing these people, the question is asked: How do you commit 'Kufr' in Allah? This shows that in the Quran, this word has been used in a broad sense. Just as the explicit denial of God is Kufr, similarly, believing in Him while negating His true attributes—such as Oneness, Power, and Knowledge, etc.—is also Kufr.

Meaning of 'Istawa', 'Taswiyah', and 'Sama': Thummas-tawa ilas-sama'i fasawwahunna sab'a samawat (Then He directed Himself to the heaven, and He made them seven heavens): 'Istawa' means to stand straight, and its use with the preposition 'ila' is proof that this word contains the meaning of turning attention towards, or a concept synonymous to it. The intent is to state that after creating the earth, Allah made the sky. This style of speech has been adopted merely to depict the situation. Here, the meaning of standing or turning attention should be taken in a way that befits the Majesty of Allah, Exalted is His Glory.
'Taswiyah' means to level something, to make it even, and to establish it with balance and equilibrium. As far as our eyes can see this blue canopy—whether with the naked eye or armed with instruments invented by science—they cannot find any rift in it. This very thing is stated as:
'You do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful any inconsistency. So return [your] vision [to the sky]; do you see any breaks? Then return [your] vision twice again. [Your] vision will return to you humbled while it is fatigued.' (Al-Mulk 3-4)
The word 'Sama' is from 'Sama-Yasmoo', which means height. This canopy, which appears stretched above us, the Quran draws our attention to its wonders and its marvels, and invites us to accept the clear conclusions to which they guide. The permanent principle of the Quran regarding the observations of this universe is that the Quran does not concern itself with things that are hidden from our common sight, or are based merely on conjecture and speculation, or can only be seen with the help of microscopes and telescopes. The reason is that much room for dispute and disagreement can arise in them. The Quran only wants to draw our attention to those realities wherein there is no room for dispute and disagreement for any fair-minded person. In drawing attention to the realities of the sky as well, the Quran has adopted this very approach. It has drawn attention to those things which only require drawing attention to be proven. However, it has hinted that these heavens are seven, so that man does not fall into the misconception that God's godhood is limited only to this visible roof and these shining stars; rather, it should be clear to him that for the gallop of his passion for investigation and research, there are more fields beyond these stars.

Wa idh qala rabbuka lil-mala'ikati (And when your Lord said to the angels): In the Arabic language, when speech begins with 'Idh' (when), it means that before it, a verb like 'think', 'imagine', 'remember', or something synonymous is omitted here. Generally, after it comes a reference to such an account or event that is either in the knowledge of the addressee or the speaker himself is so satisfied with its certainty that he can refer to it as a known and recognized reality. Here, although a reference is made to an event concerning Adam, the angels, and Iblis which occurred in the unseen world and whose knowledge no one but God possesses. However, the addressee here is firstly the Prophet (PBUH), for whom every word of revelation held the status of a factual event and an established reality. Secondly, the main direction of this narrative, as hinted above, is towards the Jews, and the Jews were aware of this event through the Torah, even though they had altered its original form significantly through interpolation.
Mala'ikah: This is the plural of 'Malak'. In the Arabic language, 'Alukah' means a message, and 'Malak', whose root is from 'Mala'ik', means a messenger and message-bearer. This word is specific to those spiritual message-bearers whom we call 'angels' (Farishtay) in our language. Angels are reliable intermediaries between Allah and His other creations. Due to their spirituality, they hold the utmost proximity and connection with Allah, and due to being creations, they also have an affinity and relationship with the creations. They have the capability to directly receive the lights and emanations descending from Allah, and they also possess the ability to transmit these lights and emanations to the servants of Allah. They bring revelation from Allah to His prophets and messengers, and they also execute His commands among His creations. The attributes described for them in the Quran are entirely the highly exalted and pure attributes of an intelligent, volitional, and conscious creation. Therefore, the thought of those who consider them to be merely abstract forces expressed by the word 'Mala'ikah' is not correct.
Innee ja'ilun fil-ardi khaleefah (Indeed, I will make upon the earth a successive authority/vicegerent): 'Khaleefah' refers to the one who takes someone's place after him to manage his affairs. For this reason, the question arises here: whose 'Khaleefah' did God express the intention of appointing on the earth? His own, or that of some predecessor creation residing on the earth? One opinion is that before humans, jinn inhabited the earth; when they caused corruption in it, Allah scattered and dispersed them, and handed over their succession to mankind. The second opinion is that Allah decided to appoint His own Khaleefah on the earth. Although the first opinion cannot be said to be completely baseless, nothing is found in the Quran, the Torah, or any reliable Hadith that proves that jinn ruled the earth before humans. If anything can be presented in its support, its status is no more than hints and allusions, and establishing the foundation of a reality merely on some hint or allusion is not correct in our view.
The second opinion seems stronger from various perspectives. The Holy Quran has pointed to many aspects of human excellence. For example, that Allah created all things for humans, commanded the angels to prostrate to Adam, and also stated about him that the trust which the heavens and the earth failed to bear was taken up by the human. All these things are in favor of the fact that Allah made the human His Khaleefah. However, despite all these arguments, a question arises regarding this opinion as well. That is, the need to appoint a Khaleefah arises for someone who is absent or non-present. God is never absent nor non-present; His rule has always been and will always be everywhere in the heavens and the earth. Then what is the meaning of His appointing someone as a Khaleefah?
In our view, this question is not of much importance. The reason for this is that the purpose of making a Khaleefah is that Allah will give him some powers regarding the management and administration of the earth, to see whether the human uses these powers according to God's will, or whether, after attaining the Khilafah, he becomes absolute and starts acting arbitrarily. This is akin to a deputy being appointed by the actual ruler, and the necessity of appointing this deputy was not that the actual ruler had to be absent or non-present, but rather, by giving some powers to this deputy, the intent was to test his obedience and loyalty.
Qaloo ataj'alu feeha man yufsidu feeha wa yasfikud-dimaa (They said, "Will You place upon it one who causes corruption therein and sheds blood?"): In the terminology of the Holy Quran, 'Fasad fil-ard' means that instead of running the administration of the earth according to the commands and laws of Allah, it is run arbitrarily, God's Shariah is disobeyed and one's own desires are followed, the will of the actual Ruler of the earth is ignored and one's own will is imposed. This thing in itself is 'Fasad fil-ard' and rebellion, whether it occurs with violence and insurgency, or peacefully under some thought and philosophy. The actual Ruler of this earth is Allah. Man's position in it is not that of the actual ruler, but that of the deputy of the actual Ruler. For this reason, the peace and justice of this earth depend on the fact that God's law alone runs in every corner of it. If God's law does not remain in any part of it, it means that rebellion has erupted in that part, and this is a danger for the entire earth.
Bloodshed is the natural consequence of 'Fasad fil-ard'. When God's law of justice does not remain, it will inevitably be replaced by the reign of human desires. The inevitable result of this will be that there will remain no guarantee for any person's life, property, or honor. Even if the corruptors of a specific piece of land hypothetically create a system in which they guarantee the protection of each other's lives and property mutually, they will create a form of security for themselves, but they will still remain a danger to others. Their example will be like a gang of robbers whose members have an agreement among themselves not to encroach upon each other's lives and property, but there will be nothing to protect the lives and property of those outside their gang from their transgressions. The guarantee of protection for the entire human world and the whole globe can only be provided by God's law, which protects the lives and properties of all, and binds all equally and grants freedom to all equally.
The angels expressed this apprehension regarding humans based on their being a 'Khaleefah', because hidden within the word Khaleefah is the fact that they will be delegated powers by Allah within a certain limit. The angels felt that using power is not an easy task; upon attaining it, a human can go astray, and the consequence of this straying can manifest in the form of unrest and corruption on the earth.
Wa nahnu nusabbihu bihamdika wa nuqaddisu laka (While we declare Your praise and sanctify You): The true essence of 'Tasbeeh' (glorification), lexically, is to lay oneself down in front of someone with humility and submission. Tasbeeh is done by words and also by actions. The meaning of Tasbeeh of God by action is to remain constantly bowed down in the execution of God's commands. This Tasbeeh is also done by those things in this universe that are inanimate and devoid of will. The human action that the Holy Quran has specifically termed as Tasbeeh is prayer (Salah), because prayer is the most perfect picture of submission, humility, and lowliness. Verbal Tasbeeh means to declare God's purity. That is, to declare God free from and above those things which are contrary to the Majesty of His Godhood. From this perspective, the negative aspect (negating flaws) is dominant in Tasbeeh, but when the condition of 'Hamd' (praise) is added to it, as is the case here, then along with 'Tanzeeh' (declaring purity), the concept of 'Ithbat' (affirmation) is also born in it. That is, along with declaring God free from attributes contrary to the Majesty of Godhood, also declaring Him characterized by those attributes for which He is worthy of praise and gratitude.
The meaning of 'Nuqaddisu laka' is that we declare Your purity, Your superiority, and Your holiness. In Tasbeeh, as stated, the concept of Tanzeeh is dominant, but the concept of Taqdees is to declare Allah characterized by all attributes of purity and holiness. The necessity of Taqdees along with Tasbeeh is because until there is an affirmation along with the negation, the right of Allah's recognition and His praise is not fulfilled.
After expressing the aforementioned apprehension regarding humans, the angels' reference to their own Tasbeeh and Taqdees was not because they wanted to express their own entitlement to the Khilafah in contrast to humans. Rather, their actual intent was to inquire about the wisdom and rationale of this decision of Allah. For this purpose, on one hand, they expressed the apprehension they saw latent in the Khilafah of humans, and on the other hand, they also made it clear that the creation of humans could not merely be for Tasbeeh and Taqdees, because 'we are already doing that work'.
Qala innee a'lamu ma la ta'lamoon (He said, "Indeed, I know that which you do not know"): In response to the aforementioned question of the angels, it was said that your sight does not encompass all aspects of this scheme. For this reason, this question has arisen in your minds. When the complete scheme comes before you, it will become clear to you that within it, there is also an arrangement for the prevention of the apprehension you have expressed.

Wa 'allama Adam-al-asmaa'a kullaha (And He taught Adam the names - all of them): Whose names did Allah teach Adam? There are three opinions in answer to this question. The first opinion is that it means the names of all things. The second opinion is that it means the names of the angels. And the third opinion is that it means the names of Adam's progeny.
As far as the second opinion is concerned, there is no evidence for it in the Quran, so it requires no further discussion. Regarding the first and third opinions, the third opinion appears stronger to us. The reasons are as follows:

First, contextual evidence suggests that the 'Alif Lam' (definite article) on 'Asmaa' (names) is for a specific, known entity (Al-Ahd). If it is considered as such, taking it to mean some general or unspecified names would not be correct.
Second, the pronouns and demonstratives used for it are entirely those used in the Arabic language not for inanimate objects, but specifically for beings endowed with intellect, perception, consciousness, and will. For example, it is said: 'Thumma 'aradahum 'alal-mala'ikah' (Then He presented them to the angels); 'Anbi'oonee bi-asmaa'i ha'ulaa'i' (Inform Me of the names of these); 'Ya Adamu anbi'hum bi-asmaa'ihim' (O Adam, inform them of their names); 'Falamma anba'ahum bi-asmaa'ihim' (So when he had informed them of their names).
Third, the context here is about convincing the angels. The angels held the assumption regarding Adam's progeny that upon receiving vicegerency (Khilafah), they would cause corruption in the earth and shed blood. The only way this assumption could be refuted was by showing them a vision of Adam's progeny, informing them of the prophets, messengers, reformers, martyrs, and the truthful ones who were to be born among them. This would make it clear to the angels that while there was a possibility of people arising among Adam's progeny who would misuse the authority granted by Allah, there would also arise people who would fulfill this responsibility and risk their lives to make others aware of their responsibilities.
These three points hold great significance. Even if weak objections can be raised against each individually, collectively they form a very strong argument that 'names' here refers to the names of Adam's progeny, particularly those who were to come to eradicate corruption and establish justice in the world.
As for the question of where Adam's progeny was when they were presented and their names revealed, the Quran itself provides the answer. It explicitly states that Allah brought forth all of Adam's progeny at once and made them testify to His Lordship:

'And [mention] when your Lord took from the children of Adam - from their loins - their descendants and made them testify of themselves, [saying to them], "Am I not your Lord?" They said, "Yes, we have testified."' (Al-A'raf - 172)

This shows that before sending them to the world, Allah gathered all of Adam's progeny in the unseen realm and took their pledge of His Lordship. It was likely during this grand assembly that Adam (AS) was taught their names, and they were presented to the angels, leading to the dialogue referenced here.
Anbi'oonee bi-asmaa'i ha'ulaa'i in kuntum sadiqeen (Inform Me of the names of these, if you are truthful): Meaning, if you are truthful in your assumption that Adam's progeny will cause corruption on earth upon receiving vicegerency, then tell Me the names of these people—who are they? Will they be the ones causing corruption or establishing peace and justice? The convincing aspect for the angels here is that an opinion regarding the behavior of Adam's progeny could only be formed if they had comprehensive knowledge of them. But since they lacked such knowledge, there was no justification for their negative assumption.

Subhanaka (Exalted are You): In the Holy Quran, this word is used on various occasions.
To declare Allah's purity and transcendence from inappropriate things and things contrary to His Majesty. For example:

'Exalted is Allah and high above what they associate with Him.' (Al-Qasas - 68)

On the occasion of supplication (Dua). For example:

'Their call therein will be, "Exalted are You, O Allah."' (Yunus - 10)

In the meaning of a command. For example:

'So exalted is Allah when you reach the evening and when you reach the morning.' (Ar-Rum - 17)

To deny something with astonishment. For example:

'Exalted are You! This is a great slander.' (An-Nur - 16)

Here, this word is used in its first meaning. The angels meant: Your Majesty is far too exalted for any action lacking wisdom and purpose to occur at Your hands. The doubt we expressed was merely the result of our limited knowledge; we only possess the knowledge You have bestowed upon us. The true treasure of knowledge and wisdom lies solely with You.

Alam aqul lakum innee a'lamu ghaybas-samawati (Did I not tell you that I know the unseen [aspects] of the heavens): Here, Allah refers to His statement previously mentioned in Ayah 30, 'Innee a'lamu ma la ta'lamoon' (I know that which you do not know). Initially, this was stated briefly, but once the angels were thoroughly convinced—and they did concede—the same point was reiterated with further clarity. This was to firmly establish the reality that the wisdom and purpose behind everything happening in the universe are known only to its Creator and Sustainer. Even the angels, despite their proximity to God, neither know these underlying wisdoms nor can they know them unless God reveals them. Therefore, if an act of the Almighty appears to lack wisdom or purpose, a person should attribute it to the deficiency of their own knowledge rather than making God a target of objection or succumbing to doubts. Instead, like the angels, one should confess: 'Subhanaka la 'ilma lana illa ma 'allamtana, innaka antal-'aleemul-hakeem' (Exalted are You; we have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed, it is You who is the Knowing, the Wise). No action of the All-Knowing and All-Wise God is devoid of wisdom, but comprehending the wisdom behind all His actions is not possible for angels, jinn, or humans.
Furthermore, the statement 'Wa a'lamu ma tubdoona wa ma kuntum taktumoon' (And I know what you reveal and what you have concealed) means: I understood both your question and the underlying reason that gave rise to it. The reason was that you were unaware of the hidden aspects of the scheme of Adam's vicegerency. You wanted them revealed to you. For this purpose, you formulated your question by pointing to the negative aspects of the scheme that were apparent to you, so that its positive aspects could be disclosed to you. Consequently, your desire was fulfilled by showing you Adam's progeny and informing you of their names. Allah states this as a favor to the angels, letting them know that both the overt and covert intents of their question had been answered. There is no aspect of reprimand for the angels in this.

Wa idh qulna lil-mala'ikatis-judoo li-Adama (And [mention] when We said to the angels, "Prostrate before Adam"): The word 'Sajdah' in the Arabic language implies bowing down. There can be different degrees of bowing. Bowing one's head in reverence before someone is also a form of Sajdah, and placing one's forehead and nose on the ground is also Sajdah. In previous religions, this form of reverence (prostration of respect) was permissible for entities other than Allah, but generally, its limit was similar to what we call 'Ruku' (bowing from the waist). Such reverential prostration was common among the Children of Israel, and its form, as determined from various places in the Torah, resembles Ruku. Islam restricted this form of extreme reverence solely to the Lord of Honor. The reason is that Islam is God's final and perfect religion. To fully highlight the reality of monotheism (Tawheed), it has reserved all forms of ultimate reverence and submission exclusively for God, leaving no loophole for polytheism (Shirk) to enter.
Commanding the angels to prostrate to Adam did not involve any aspect of Shirk. Firstly, because this prostration was in obedience to God's command, so it was essentially a prostration to God. Secondly, as mentioned, prostration was declared a symbol of Shirk only in Islam. Before Islam, its significance was nothing more than a method of showing reverence. When it was said, 'Prostrate to Adam,' it simply meant, 'Show reverence to Adam'; it carried no meaning beyond that.
Why were the angels commanded to show reverence to Adam? In our view, this was a test of the angels' obedience and servitude by Allah. A true test is always in something that is difficult for one's ego (Nafs). Since the angels were created from light (Noor) and were created to glorify and sanctify God, the command to bow before an Adam made of dust was a major trial for them. However, the angels well understood the reality that true honor and exaltation do not lie in being created from light or fire, but in unquestioning obedience to the Creator's commands. Hence, they passed this test perfectly.
Undoubtedly, this prostration of the angels highlights an aspect of Adam's greatness. But the primary objective here is not to display Adam's greatness, but to demonstrate the angels' servitude and obedience. It serves to show that those who are obedient and submissive to God do not strut around in the pride of lineage and origin like Iblis (Satan). Instead, they consider every such thing as a grace and favor from God, and the realization of this grace instills in them humility and servitude rather than arrogance and pride.
Contextually, this is a lesson for the Children of Israel who, in the matter of the unlettered Prophet (PBUH), adopted the path of Satan by falling into the trap of racial and lineage pride, rather than adopting the angelic approach of submission.
In my view, there is no solid foundation for the opinion of those who consider the angels' reverence for Adam to be a result of Adam's intellectual superiority. Just as Adam (AS) acquired the knowledge of the names of his progeny through God's teaching, the angels acquired it through Adam's teaching. What aspect of Adam's superiority is there in this that warrants the command for angels to prostrate to him? Furthermore, there is no strong proof that the angels were commanded to prostrate to Adam only *after* Adam informed them of the names. Undoubtedly, the mention of the prostration command comes after the mention of teaching the names here, but this mere sequence is not enough to prove that the former was the result of the latter. Firstly, the narrative of the prostration command begins with the word 'Idh' (when/remember when), which provides strong contextual evidence that it is an independent event; it is not necessary that it occurred immediately after the first event. Secondly, other places in the Quran indicate that the command for angels to prostrate to Adam was given not only before the demonstration of his intellectual superiority but even before his physical creation. For example:

'And [mention, O Muhammad], when your Lord said to the angels, "I will create a human being out of clay from an altered black mud. And when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My [created] soul, then fall down to him in prostration." So the angels prostrated - all of them entirely, Except Iblis, he refused to be with those who prostrated.' (Al-Hijr: 28-31)

There are other verses in the Quran with this theme, clearly showing that the command to prostrate to Adam was given before his creation. These verses also implicitly suggest that the actual purpose of this prostration was to test the angels' obedience and loyalty. This is why the creation of Adam from altered black mud is specifically highlighted here, to clarify the testing aspect for the angels. We have already pointed out the reality that a test always involves something that is difficult for the ego. It was a severe trial for the angels, despite being creatures of light, to prostrate before an earthly Adam created from foul mud. But they knew well that the thing that grants honor and nobility is actual obedience to God, not being created from light or fire. Therefore, despite the severity of the test, they passed it. Iblis, however, failed this test due to his arrogance.
Someone might think that in some verses, the mention of prostration comes after Adam's creation and fashioning. There is no doubt that in some verses the sequence of the theme is like this, but reflecting on such instances reveals that Allah's intention is to describe His blessings upon mankind, not to establish a chronological timeline of events.
Illa Iblees (Except Iblis): Iblis is on the pattern of 'If'eel' from 'Ablasa'. 'Ablasa' means to be sorrowful, to deny, and to despair. Iblis is actually the title of the jinn who refused to prostrate to Adam. The Holy Quran explicitly states that he was from the jinn. In Surah Al-Kahf, it is stated:

'And [mention] when We said to the angels, "Prostrate to Adam," and they prostrated, except for Iblis. He was of the jinn and departed from the command of his Lord.'

The Holy Quran mentions three creations as accountable beings: Angels, Jinn, and Mankind. Satan is not an independent creation. Those among jinn and humans who adopt the path of disobedience to God are included among the progeny and allies of Iblis. Such jinn and humans were not created upon misguidance; they were created upon the same nature (Fitrah) upon which Allah has blessed all jinn and humans with the gift of free will. Because of this, those among them who choose the path of misguidance for themselves, Allah leaves them to walk on that very path. Allah has detailed this Sunnah (practice) of His in various places in the Quran. We will explain its different aspects on appropriate occasions.
A point might bother some people here: since the command to prostrate was given to the angels and not the jinn, why was Iblis, who was a jinn, deemed deserving of a curse for not prostrating? The answer is that the primary difference between jinn and angels is in terms of characteristics and attributes; in terms of their creation, jinn are not far removed from angels (angels created from light, jinn from fire). Therefore, it seems that by way of 'Taghleeb' (incorporation/generalization), the jinn were also included in this command to prostrate, but their misguided member, Iblis, refused. This opinion has also been expressed by some of our past commentators, and I find it strong. (1. See the words of Qadi Baidawi (RA): 'Or the jinn were also commanded along with the angels, but mentioning the angels made it unnecessary to mention them, because when it is known that the superiors are commanded to humble themselves before someone and seek closeness to him, it is known that the inferiors are also commanded to do so. And the pronoun in 'fasajadoo' (so they prostrated) refers to both groups').

Wa la taqraba hadhihish-shajarah (And do not approach this tree): The word 'Shajarah' (tree) is prefixed with 'Alif Lam' (the definite article), which makes it clear that as far as Adam and Eve (peace be upon them) were concerned, this tree was pointed out to them with specific determination. As for the question of what kind of tree it was, neither the Quran nor any authentic Hadith provides the answer. Therefore, trying to determine it is a futile effort. In our view, the correct stance on this matter is that of Imam Ibn Jarir. He states: 'We cannot say with certainty what kind of tree this was because we find no evidence for its specification in either the Quran or the Hadith; after all, if someone claims something, on what authority do they do so?'
In our view, there is not even much need to identify this tree. The main point the Quran wants to convey here is that just as Allah tested the loyalty and obedience of the angels and jinn by commanding them to prostrate to Adam (AS), He tested the obedience and loyalty of Adam (AS) by forbidding him one of the trees in Paradise. In that Paradise full of blessings, there was only one tree from which Adam (AS) was stopped from benefiting. But human nature is such that one becomes most greedy for the very thing one is forbidden from. Consequently, Iblis exploited this very weakness of Adam (AS) and started convincing him that the secret to eternal life and an everlasting kingdom lay solely in the fruits of this tree from which he had been deprived. The result was that Adam (AS) fell for Satan's deception and ate the fruit of that tree. However, after committing this mistake, he did not persist stubbornly in his error like Satan, but felt remorseful and repented.
A completely similar situation is before us in this world. Every blessing of this earth is permissible for us. There are only a handful of things that God has forbidden us from, yet the state of many among us is that, due to Satan's whisperings, they believe the secret to all our progress and success in the world lies hidden precisely within those few things God has forbidden. And the tragedy is that, after disobeying, instead of feeling remorse and repenting like our forefather, they act arrogantly and stubbornly like Iblis.
In the Torah, this tree is called the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. This is interesting but, in our view, incorrect. It is likely that the People of the Torah initially adopted this as an interpretation because the consequence of eating the fruit of this tree was described as Adam and Eve becoming naked. Initially, it might have appeared as an interpretation, but later, being appealing, it replaced the original text through the backdoor of interpolation. The Quran does mention that after eating the fruit of this tree, Adam (AS) became naked, but the Quran does not imply that this nakedness was the result of intellect and consciousness suddenly awakening within them. Rather, it is clear that the consequence of the wrong they did to themselves by disobeying God was that they were stripped of their heavenly garments.
If Adam (AS) was so devoid of consciousness before eating the fruit of this tree that he had no sense of his own nakedness, then putting him through a test at that time, and that too at the hands of a cunning enemy like Iblis, seems completely irrational. Before this test, it was essential for him to have enough understanding to comprehend his own good and evil in the face of Satan's whisperings. If he lacked this understanding, his falling into Satan's trap was completely obvious, and it is entirely contrary to God's justice that He would place him against Satan and then penalize him for his slip.

Ihbitu ba'dukum liba'din 'aduww (Go down, [all of you], as enemies to one another): According to Ibn Abbas (RA) and some other interpreters, this address of 'Ihbitu' (Go down) is directed at Adam, Eve, and Iblis; whereas according to Ibn Zayd, it is directed at Adam, Eve, and their progeny. In our view, the correct interpretation among these is that of Ibn Abbas (RA).
One reason for this is that the statement 'you will be enemies to one another' finds its natural basis between Adam and Iblis, not between Adam and Eve. The natural bond between Adam and Eve is one of affection and love; similarly, the natural bond among the children of Adam is essentially one of brotherhood and love. If the seed of enmity and hostility is sown among them, it is due to the efforts of Satan, and it thrives on his corruptions. Human nature itself does not possess much capacity to nurture this seed of corruption. The natural enmity between Satan and Adam is also mentioned in several places in the Quran.

'So We said, "O Adam, indeed this is an enemy to you and to your wife. Then let him not remove you from Paradise so you would suffer."' (Ta-Ha - 117)

'Then will you take him and his descendants as allies other than Me while they are enemies to you?' (Al-Kahf - 50)

If many among the children of Adam establish friendship with Iblis and his progeny, it is not because their natural relationship is actually one of friendship. The natural relationship between them is one of enmity and should remain one of enmity, as hinted in the verse from Al-Kahf above. However, due to their foolishness and lack of foresight, many people mistake their enemies for their friends, and by becoming their tools, destroy themselves.
The second reason is that in other places in the Quran, it is explicitly stated that just as Adam (AS) was commanded to leave Paradise, Iblis was also given this command in the exact same words. In Surah Al-A'raf, it is stated:

'[Allah] said, "Descend from it, for it is not for you to be arrogant therein. So get out; indeed, you are of the debased."' (Al-A'raf - 13)

The third reason is that in some places, the word 'Jamee'an' (all together) is added to this command for emphasis. For example, in Surah Ta-Ha it is 'Ihbitaa minha jamee'an' (Descend from it all together); later in this very Surah, it says 'Qulnah-bitu minha jamee'an'. If this address is considered to be solely for Adam and Eve, then the word 'Jamee'an' becomes somewhat redundant. And if, to make it meaningful, it is assumed that this command was for Adam, Eve, and their progeny, it would be a stretch. Because up to this stage, if anything has been mentioned regarding Adam's progeny, it is only that they were made to acknowledge God's Lordship and were shown to Adam and the angels. It would be difficult to find any evidence in the Quran to support the claim that Adam's progeny was physically present with Adam in Paradise and were expelled from it due to their father's sin.
As for the argument that in some places in the Quran the dual form (Muthanna) is used, and this is a clear proof that the address is only to Adam and Eve—in our view, this argument is also not very weighty. Undoubtedly, the dual form is used in some places. For example:

'[Allah] said, "Descend from it all [of you] - your descendants being enemies to one another. And if there should come to you guidance from Me - then whoever follows My guidance will neither go astray [in the world] nor suffer [in the Hereafter]."' (Ta-Ha - 123)

However, this dual form is not used for Adam and Eve; rather, the context proves that it is addressing Adam and Iblis as two separate parties and factions. And the command regarding following guidance here is just as applicable to the human race as it is to the jinn race.

Fatalaqqaa Adamu min rabbihi kalimatin fataba 'alayhi (Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance): 'Tawbah' means to return. When it is followed by the preposition 'Ala', it points to the fact that the theme of mercy is hidden within it.
The word 'Talaqqaa' (received) indicates that these words of repentance descended upon Adam (AS) from Allah. These words are referenced elsewhere in the Quran:

'They said, "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers."' (Al-A'raf - 23)

Adam's (AS) restlessness for repentance and the inspiration of the words of repentance into his heart reveal the Sunnah (practice) of Allah concerning repentance. This Sunnah is that when a servant commits a sin, a feeling of remorse, shame, and an urge to return to Allah automatically arises within him. This feeling is a demand of his nature, and it continues to arise unless the person repeatedly insists on sins and completely crushes this feeling. For this very specific task, Allah has endowed humans with the 'Nafs Lawwamah' (the self-reproaching soul). Further details regarding this will be discussed at appropriate places.

Qulnah-bitu minha jamee'an (We said, "Go down from it, all of you"): These words are repeated twice: once after the slip of Adam (AS), and then again after his repentance. After the slip, it is mentioned to state the consequence of the slip, and after the repentance, to state the wisdom behind this test. It means that Allah now wants to test you by sending you to the world so that a distinction can be made between your good and evil. Those who pass this test will be the inheritors of Paradise, and those who fail this test will be deprived of Paradise.
Fa-imma ya'tiyannakum minnee hudan (And when guidance comes to you from Me): This is the first promise from Allah to establish the chain of Prophethood and Messengership for Adam (AS) and his progeny. Adam's (AS) slip revealed the weakness of human nature and human intellect, which proves man's need for the guidance of divine revelation and the support of the Prophets (peace be upon them). Consequently, observing this human weakness, Allah promised as a means of comfort and consolation that He Himself would send light from His side for human guidance. Those who value this light will have no fear nor will they grieve.
The words 'no fear and no grief' are specific to describing Paradise in the Holy Quran. If you reflect, you will find that these words are highly comprehensive for describing Paradise. Fear is regarding an impending future danger, and grief is for a past or present loss. A place where there is neither any grief of the past nor any danger of the future can only be Paradise.

Wal-ladheena kafaroo wa kadh-dhaboo bi-ayaatina (And those who disbelieve and deny Our signs): This verse is in contrast to the preceding verse. The preceding verse described the reward of those who follow the guidance sent down by Allah. This verse describes the fate of those who deny the signs (Ayat) of Allah.
The word 'Ayat' is the plural of 'Ayah'. The root meaning of Ayah is a sign or mark.
In the Holy Quran, this word is also used for those arguments and signs that are present in every corner of the heavens, the earth, the horizons, and the self, which testify to God's power, His wisdom, His oneness, and His law of reward and punishment.
It is also used for those miracles that were manifested through the noble Prophets (peace be upon them) or which the disbelievers kept demanding.
It is also used for the verses of the Holy Quran, of which the Surahs of the Quran are composed. The use of this word for the verses of the Quran is proof of the reality that they are not baseless commands; rather, each verse holds the status of an argument, a testimony, a proof, and a demonstration.

Ya Banee Isra'eel (O Children of Israel): Israel is the title of Prophet Jacob (Yaqub, AS). Jewish scholars explain its meaning as 'the champion of God'. This interpretation is likely heavily influenced by the narrative the Jews inserted into the Torah about Jacob (AS) wrestling with God.
Ustadh Imam Maulana Hamiduddin Farahi (may Allah have mercy on him) was also proficient in the Hebrew language. According to his research, this word is composed of two parts: 'Isra' and 'El'. In his research, 'Isra' means servant (Abd), and 'El' in Hebrew is famous for meaning God (Ilah). Thus, according to the Maulana, Israel means 'Abdullah', i.e., the servant of Allah.
The kind of ingenuity the Jews showed in determining the etymology of 'Israel', they also showed in determining the etymology of the name Yaqub (AS). According to them, Prophet Yaqub was named Yaqub because he was born holding the heel (Aqib) of his brother Esau. According to Ustadh Imam, his explanation is completely different from the Jews' explanation. In light of the Quran's statements, he explains the reason for Prophet Yaqub (AS) getting the name Yaqub is that Allah had given Prophet Abraham (AS) the glad tidings of his birth following Prophet Isaac (Ishaq, AS) (Yaqub meaning 'the one who follows').
Udhkuroo ni'matiyal-latee an'amtu 'alaykum (Remember My favor which I have bestowed upon you): Remember. This is an invitation to the Children of Israel in a tone of reproach. It means: remember, because you have completely forgotten, and the favors I bestowed upon you, you have assumed them to be the result of your personal and familial entitlement.
The favors and blessings from Allah that are alluded to here by the word 'Ni'mah' (favor/blessing) are detailed in various places in the Quran. We quote a few verses here to clarify this brevity.
In the next section (Ruku) of this very Surah, it is stated:

'O Children of Israel, remember My favor which I have bestowed upon you and that I preferred you over the worlds.' (Al-Baqarah - 47)

This verse refers to the favor Allah bestowed upon the Children of Israel in the form of world leadership and Imamate.
Then, in Surah Al-Ma'idah, it is stated:

'And remember the favor of Allah upon you and His covenant with which He bound you...' (Al-Ma'idah - 7)

This verse points to the favor Allah bestowed upon the Children of Israel by giving them His Shariah (Law). This Shariah held the status of a covenant and treaty between Allah and them, as Allah took a pledge from them to abide by His Shariah, and in return for this obedience, He guaranteed them success and salvation in this world and the Hereafter.
Later in Al-Ma'idah, this favor is further elaborated in these words:

'And [mention, O Muhammad], when Moses said to his people, "O my people, remember the favor of Allah upon you when He appointed among you prophets and made you possessors and gave you that which He had not given anyone among the worlds."' (Al-Ma'idah - 20)

These verses fully clarify the brief mention in the verse under discussion. Another thing to note in this verse is that first it says 'My favor' and then adds 'which I bestowed upon you'. This emphasis is because the root of all the deviations of the Children of Israel, as will become clear later, was precisely this: the greatness they attained purely out of Allah's grace and mercy, they considered to be a miracle of their own merit and entitlement, and a natural right of their lineage and race. Here, the words 'Ni'mati' (My favor) and 'An'amtu alaykum' (I bestowed upon you) aim to reform this very mindset, and this will gradually unfold further.
Wa awfoo bi'ahdee oofi bi'ahdikum (And fulfill My covenant; I will fulfill your covenant): 'Covenant' generally refers to the entire Shariah, because the Shariah essentially holds the status of a treaty between the servants and God. This treaty is a very great favor from Allah because Allah is the Creator and Master of all the heavens and the earth; no one has the stature that the King of all the heavens and the earth should make a treaty with them. Yet, if He makes a treaty with someone, it means He is granting them a very high honor from His side. But here, alongside this general treaty, it also points to the specific covenant taken from the Children of Israel regarding the Prophet (PBUH). This covenant is mentioned in the Torah and hinted at in the Quran. In Deuteronomy 18:15-19, it says:

'The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your fellow Israelites. You must listen to him... I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their fellow Israelites, and I will put my words in his mouth. He will tell them everything I command him. I myself will call to account anyone who does not listen to my words that the prophet speaks in my name.'

There is a reference to this covenant in the Holy Quran. In response to the prayer for mercy that Prophet Moses (AS) made for the Children of Israel, Allah said:

'...but My mercy encompasses all things. So I will decree it [especially] for those who fear Me and give Zakah and those who believe in Our verses - Those who follow the Messenger, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written in what they have of the Torah and the Gospel, who enjoins upon them what is right and forbids them what is wrong and makes lawful for them the good things and prohibits for them the evil and relieves them of their burden and the shackles which were upon them. So they who have believed in him, honored him, supported him and followed the light which was sent down with him - it is those who will be the successful.' (Al-A'raf - 156-157)

This verse clarifies the responsibility placed on the Children of Israel in the covenant Allah took from them regarding the Prophet (PBUH), and the promises Allah made to them in return for fulfilling this responsibility.
Wa iyyaya farhaboon (And be afraid of [only] Me): The state of trembling and shivering that overcomes the heart due to the conception of someone's greatness and majesty is called 'Rahbah' in the Arabic language. As we clarified while interpreting 'Iyyaka na'budu', if the object of a verb is placed before the verb, it is a way of emphasizing and stressing it. Furthermore, if 'Fa' (so/then) precedes the verb, it is evidence of further emphasis. Similarly, if a pronoun also follows the verb, it further clarifies this aspect. Based on this, 'Wa iyyaya farhaboon' means, 'So, fear only Me'.
'Fear only Me' here means that in fulfilling the demands of My covenant, the conception of My greatness and majesty should dominate over your other interests and your other fears. You fear that if you accept the invitation of the Final Prophet, your leadership and dominance will end, the unlettered people will gain superiority over you, your common people will stand up against you as enemies, and the doors to the benefits you have been reaping from them so far will close. However, these are not the things to fear. You should only fear Me, in whose power of control is everything, and who, while taking the covenant from you, raised the mountain over your heads like a canopy.

Wa aaminoo bimaa anzaltu musaddiqan lima ma'akum (And believe in what I have sent down confirming that which is [already] with you): 'Musaddiqan lima ma'akum' (confirming that which is with you), meaning it is proving true the prophecy present in the Torah regarding the advent of the Final Prophet (PBUH) and the characteristics of this advent. The intent is that, if you use your intellect, the Holy Quran and this Prophet are not things for you to be irritated by, but things to be highly revered, because your honor has been elevated the most by their appearance. The prophecies about them existed in your scriptures, and these prophecies were awaiting the appearance of their true subject. Now, the appearance of this Book and this Prophet has presented their subject to the world, thereby granting your book a certificate of validation. Therefore, you should be the first to step forward to believe in it.
The second aspect of this confirmation is that, as far as the Torah or Gospel being heavenly scriptures is concerned, the Holy Quran openly confirms their heavenly origin. It confirms the prophethood and messengership of those who brought them in highly unambiguous words, and it fundamentally confirms their teachings as well. If the Quran refutes anything, it only refutes those things that have been wrongly included in these scriptures or whose original form has been distorted through interpolation. Thus, if you reflect, you will find that as far as the original Torah is concerned, the Holy Quran has been revealed as a witness to its truth; it does not belie it, but rather exonerates it from those things that belie it.
Wa la takoonoo awwala kafirin bihi (And do not be the first to disbelieve in it): If the 'Mudaf Ilayh' (the second part of a possessive construction) of 'Af'al' (superlative pattern) is an indefinite singular noun, it acts in the sense of 'Tamyeez' (specification), but if it is annexed to a definite noun (Ma'rifah), then in that case the 'Mudaf Ilayh' will be plural. For example:

'Say, "If the Most Merciful had a son, then I would be the first of [His] worshippers."' (Az-Zukhruf - 81)

Both 'Awwala kafirin' and 'Awwala al-kafireen' are used in their respective contexts. Ustadh Imam Maulana Hamiduddin Farahi (may Allah have mercy on him) points out a subtle difference. When 'Awwala kafirin' is used, it does not concern itself with whether anyone else besides him exists who disbelieves or not. In the second form, the meaning would be that he is the first person among the group of disbelievers.
The word 'Kufr', as we have clarified, is used in the meaning of denying the truth, and also in the sense of ingratitude for a blessing. Here, this word seems to encompass both meanings because a covenant had already been taken from them to believe in the Quran, so its being the truth was very clear to them; therefore, this was a denial of a great truth. Furthermore, the Holy Quran was revealed as a very great blessing for them. Upon believing in it, there were promises of eternal blessings from Allah for them; therefore, turning away from it was actually a very grave ingratitude for a blessing.
'Do not be the first to disbelieve in it' does not mean that once others disbelieve, it will become permissible for you to disbelieve. Rather, it means that since this Quran has been revealed confirming your book, and a covenant was taken from you to believe in it even before its revelation, it was expected from you first and foremost to accept it and believe in it. But it is a strange situation that others, despite being unfamiliar with it, race to believe in it, while you, being familiar with it beforehand, race in the path of opposing it.
In such contexts, regarding the qualifier attached to them, according to Ustadh Imam (RA), its purpose is merely to highlight the heinousness of the situation. The main connection is actually with the verb itself; the qualifier is simply added to bring forth the situation that is implicit in committing it. For example, the Holy Quran commands:

'Do not consume usury, doubled and multiplied...' (Al-Imran - 130)

In this verse, it does not mean that if the form of compound interest does not arise, then usury is permissible. Rather, the purpose of presenting this situation is to bring the repulsive nature of the actual act to the forefront.
Similarly, after the segment under discussion, it is stated, 'Wa la tashtaroo bi-ayatee thamanan qaleelan' (And do not exchange My signs for a small price). This does not mean that if you get a good price, you can sell them. Rather, the prohibition here also relates to the actual act. That is, the thing forbidden is selling religion (Deen). But the qualifier 'for a small price' also clarifies the reality that this business of selling religion is being done in a highly despicable manner because even if the entire world is obtained in exchange for the verses of Allah, it is still a worthless commodity.
It is possible that a question may arise in some people's minds here: in denying the Quran, the Quraish preceded the Jews, so why did the Quran place the blame for being the first on the Jews? The answer is that this is being said here to the Jews as a nation, and in contrast here are the unlettered Arabs as a nation, whether they are Adnani or Qahtani. There is no doubt that the Quraish preceded in denying the Quran. However, the nature of the Quraish's denial was not such that the entire Quraish stood up just to deny it. Among them were deniers of the Quran, and also those ready to sacrifice their lives for the Quran. But the case of the Children of Israel was completely different. They stood up collectively as a nation to deny and oppose the Quran and the Holy Prophet (PBUH), and remained adamant in this opposition until their last breath. This was despite the fact that, being the inheritors of the divine religion and the custodians of the prophecies regarding the Final Prophet (PBUH), they were supposed to hold the status of 'Awwalal-Mu'mineen' (the first to believe) compared to the unlettered Arabs.
Wa la tashtaroo bi-ayatee thamanan qaleelan (And do not exchange My signs for a small price): Meaning, do not sacrifice the Torah and its commands and guidance for your religious (worldly) interests and benefits. This is a comprehensive style of expression which hints at all the covenant-breaking the Jews were guilty of, the details of which are coming later in this Surah. In the covenant Allah took from the Jews previously, three things were particularly prominent: first, that they would firmly stand by the Shariah of the Torah; second, that they would believe in the Quran which would be revealed confirming the prophecies present in the Torah; third, that they would testify before the creation regarding the book granted to them, and not conceal any part of it.
Here, when it was said 'do not exchange My signs for a small price', it was effectively said in other words: do not ruin all the covenants you have made with God for the sake of your worldly affairs.
The Holy Quran has also used this style in other places to express the concept of breaking a covenant. For example, it is stated:

'Indeed, We sent down the Torah, in which was guidance and light. The prophets who submitted [to Allah] judged by it for the Jews, as did the rabbis and scholars by that with which they were entrusted of the Scripture of Allah, and they were witnesses thereto. So do not fear the people but fear Me, and do not exchange My verses for a small price. And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed - then it is those who are the disbelievers.' (Al-Ma'idah - 44)

If you look at the context and placement of 'Wa la tashtaroo bi-ayatee thamanan qaleelan' in this verse, it clearly means: do not break Allah's covenant, which He took from you in the Torah, for your worldly interests. No matter how much importance these interests hold in your eyes, they are absolutely insignificant compared to God's covenants, commands, and verses.
The addressees of this segment are both the common people and the elite of the Jews. The common people because, although they outwardly believed in the Torah, all their religiosity was merely formal and customary. They had sacrificed the true Shariah for the desires of their flesh. The elite because they had either thrown the veil of interpretation (Ta'weel) over the prophecies regarding the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and the Holy Quran present in their scriptures, or used the scissors of interpolation (Tahreef) on them. There were two motivators for this interpretation and interpolation: one, the feeling of jealousy against the Children of Ishmael; second, the fear that if the actual truth were revealed, the common people would rebel, and the honor and leadership they currently enjoyed would be jeopardized.
Wa iyyaya fattaqoon (And fear [only] Me): The research on 'Ittiqa' and 'Taqwa' has already been explained during the interpretation of 'Hudan lil-muttaqeen'. In the verse above, it was stated 'Wa iyyaya farhaboon' (And be afraid of [only] Me). Here, it is stated 'Wa iyyaya fattaqoon' (And fear [only] Me). Then, in an upcoming verse, the word 'Khushu' (humility) will appear. Rahbah, Taqwa, Khushu—all are different manifestations of the same reality. The trembling and shivering that overcomes the heart due to the conception of someone's greatness and majesty is 'Rahbah'. The state of helplessness, humility, lowliness, and submission that arises in the heart for the possessor of greatness and majesty due to this trembling and shivering, and the feeling of indigence (Faqr) instead of independence, and devotion (Ikhbat) instead of arrogance that emerges in the disposition, is 'Khushu'. Similarly, the restlessness that arises in the disposition to avoid the wrath of this possessor of greatness and majesty, to refrain from violating His established limits, and to exercise caution and avoidance in breaching His commands and verses—which keeps a person awake and alert everywhere, in private and public—is 'Taqwa'.
The segment 'Fear only Me' contains two realities simultaneously. First, do not consider Me to be a very soft entity and become careless of My grasp and My wrath. Those who fail to appreciate My blessing, trample My covenant, and consider My verses as commodities for buying and selling—when My wrath descends upon them, it breaks their backs, and at that time there is no one who can stand up to save them from My wrath.
The second reality, created here by placing the object first, is that you fear that if you reveal the actual truth, your common people will rebel, your leadership and religious authority will be in danger, the Children of Ishmael will rise above you, and your other worldly interests will be harmed. However, none of these things are to be feared or avoided. If there is anything truly to be feared, it is solely My wrath, because no one can provide refuge from it. On the other hand, if I will, I can protect those who fear My wrath from every danger.

Wa la talbisul-haqqa bil-batili (And do not mix the truth with falsehood): 'Labisath-thawb' means he wore the clothes. 'Labisal-amra 'alayh' means he confused the matter for him. 'Labasahum' would mean he mixed them with one another or made them clash with each other. In the Quran, it says 'Aw yalbisakum shiya'an' (Or confuse you [so you become] sects / make you clash with each other).
'Labsus-shay'i bish-shay'i' means mixing and confusing one thing with another. In the verse under discussion, the meaning of covering the truth with falsehood can also be taken. Both these meanings are implicit in the root meaning of 'Labs', and both apply here. The Quran has used this style elsewhere as well. For example, it is stated:

'They who believe and do not mix their belief with injustice - those will have security, and they are [rightly] guided.' (Al-An'am - 82)

In the verse under discussion, there is a reference to the action of the Jews that they inserted their own opinions and innovations into the Torah, thereby mixing the truth revealed by Allah with the falsehood they introduced. Pointing to this, the Holy Quran states:

'So woe to those who write the "scripture" with their own hands, then say, "This is from Allah," in order to exchange it for a small price. Woe to them for what their hands have written and woe to them for what they earn.' (Al-Baqarah - 79)

The Jews made all kinds of alterations in the Torah to cover the truth. They added some things to it on their own, removed some things from it, and changed some things. The purpose of all these alterations was to cover up the facts mentioned in the Torah regarding the sacrifice of Prophet Abraham (AS), his place of sacrifice, and his Qiblah (direction of prayer), which pointed towards the advent of the Final Prophet (PBUH). Since the Jews disliked from the bottom of their hearts that any sign of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) should appear from the Torah, they tried to conceal all these matters. (1. Hints to some of these matters will come later in this Surah. Those seeking more details should read the treatise 'Dhabeeh' by Ustadh Imam Maulana Hamiduddin Farahi RA.)
Wa taktumul-haqqa wa antum ta'lamoon (And conceal the truth while you know [it]): There is no particular lexical difficulty in this segment; however, interpreters have differed regarding the grammatical inflection (I'rab) of 'Wa taktumoo'. Some people consider 'An' (that) to be hidden here, which is why they place 'Wa taktumoo' in the accusative state (Nasb). Others consider it a conjunction (Ataf) to the preceding verb, thus placing it in the jussive state (Jazm). Ustadh Imam Maulana Hamiduddin Farahi (RA) prefers the conjunction form. He says that not repeating the particle 'La' (do not) here highlights the reality that both these statements refer to a single reality. The second statement after the first merely acts as an explanation and a clarification. The main purpose of the Jews' attempt to mix truth and falsehood was precisely to conceal the truth. The thing they were forbidden from in the Torah was this very concealment of the truth. But the apparent method they adopted to conceal this truth was to mix truth and falsehood. Therefore, the Quran first forbade them from mixing truth and falsehood, and then forbade them from that concealment of truth which was, in fact, the real goal and purpose of all this effort to confuse truth and falsehood.
Ustadh Imam (RA) interprets the verses 'Wa la ta'kuloo amwalakum baynakum bil-batili wa tudloo biha ilal-hukkami' and 'La takhoonullaha war-rasoola wa takhoonoo amanatikum' based on this very principle. Their details will come in their respective places.
The full research on the word 'Haqq' (Truth) is coming later in this Surah. Here, it is clear from the context that 'Haqq' refers to those realities that were made clear in the Torah, and which the Quran has now made even clearer by its support and confirmation. These realities mostly related to the signs of the Final Prophet; as hinted above, the Jews were particularly interested in putting a veil over these signs.

Wa aqeemus-salata wa atuz-zakata war-ka'oo ma'ar-raki'een (And establish prayer and give Zakah and bow with those who bow [in worship and obedience]): The complete research on establishing prayer (Iqamat as-Salah) has already been explained at the beginning. There is no need to repeat it here.
Zakah: The word Zakah is from 'Zaka-Yazku', which means to be pure. In Arabic, a 'pure soul' (Nafs Zakiyyah) is a soul that is pure and clean from sins. The second meaning within this root is to grow and develop. 'Zaka-az-Zar'u' would mean 'the crop grew and thrived'. The concept of both purity and growth is found in Zakah, because Zakah purifies both the soul and wealth, and it also brings blessings and increase to wealth. Certain verses of the Holy Quran point to this reality.
For example, it is stated:

'Take, [O, Muhammad], from their wealth a charity by which you purify them and cause them increase...' (At-Tawbah - 103)

In another place, it is stated:

'And whatever you give for interest to increase within the wealth of people will not increase with Allah. But what you give in Zakah, desiring the countenance of Allah - those are the multipliers.' (Ar-Rum - 39)

The word Zakah initially continued to be used for all types of spending in the way of Allah (Infaq fi sabilillah), and its meaning was the same as the word 'Sadaqah' (charity). But later, the usages in the Quran and Hadith restricted it to those specific fixed amounts of spending that Allah and the Messenger (PBUH) have made obligatory for the poor and needy under all circumstances.
Ruku (Bowing): means to bend forward, to show humility, and to become lowly out of poverty and submissiveness. In the Holy Quran, it refers to prayer because it is one of the most important pillars (Arkan) of prayer. Along with this, the condition 'ma'ar-raki'een' (with those who bow) highlights the importance and emphasis of congregational prayer (Jama'ah). Although the concept of congregational prayer is also present in the words 'Aqeemus-salah' (establish the prayer), due to the specific conditions of the addressees, this theme has been stated separately in explicit words.
The addressees of this command to establish prayer, give Zakah, and bow with those who bow are, as is clear from the context, the Jews. And the reference is to both their common people and their elite. The covenant of Allah referenced above, its fundamental commands were precisely these, and the Jews had completely abandoned them. Here, the Holy Quran drew the attention of the Jews to the revival of these commands, and also hinted that they had completely eradicated these fundamental commands of the Divine covenant. However, it only hinted; it did not state this explicitly so that they would not get entangled in debate and refutation.
Regarding the Jews, it must remain clear that they had practically abolished the commands of prayer and Zakah, etc. As far as prayer is concerned, its command does not even exist in their scriptures at all; in fact, one of their sects even believes that Prophet Moses (AS) never gave the command for this, and it is merely an innovation of later people.
Although they did not deny Zakah, their scholars and priests designated themselves as its recipients instead of the poor and needy. Thus, the Book of Leviticus, which details the rights and duties of the priests, offerings, and sacrifices, is completely empty of any mention of the poor and needy. The tithe of the harvest, the ransom for the firstborn, and all kinds of offerings have been reserved for the priests in it. In this way, instead of the poor and destitute being the true rightful recipients of Zakah, the scholars and priests became the recipients. The Holy Quran clarified the command of the Divine Shariah in both the matters of prayer and Zakah, and reproached the Jews for their excesses in highly explicit words.
Regarding prayer, the Holy Quran clarified that the very first thing made obligatory upon the Jews was indeed prayer. After the teaching of Monotheism, Moses (AS) was given the command of prayer first. Thus, it was commanded:

'Indeed, I am Allah. There is no deity except Me, so worship Me and establish prayer for My remembrance.' (Ta-Ha - 14)

In another place, it is stated:
