'And We inspired to Moses and his brother, "Settle your people in Egypt in houses and make your houses [facing the] Qiblah and establish prayer..."' (Yunus - 87)

These verses clearly show that the communal organization of the Jews was first established through congregational prayer, but later they completely abolished its importance.
In the light of the words 'War-ka'oo ma'ar-raki'een' (And bow with those who bow), Ustadh Imam (RA) has pointed to two realities.
First, the reality that here the Jews were commanded to bow (do Ruku) because they had completely abandoned bowing. (1. According to Ustadh Imam (RA), the Jews considered prostration obligatory upon themselves only once a year, and even for that, their scholars had given the permission that if a person standing rests his forehead against a wall or pillar, this is sufficient to fulfill the obligation. It is not known what the source of Maulana's statement is, but the frequent characterization of the Jews as 'stiff-necked' in the Torah is well explained in light of this behavior.)
Second, towards the importance of arranging congregational prayer. This is because the leaders were commanded to join the poor and common people in prayers and stand side by side with them. The first thing that destroys prayer is precisely this abandonment of the congregation. The wealthy begin to consider it below their dignity to attend mosques with the common people. The result is that the respect for prayer diminishes, and mosque attendance becomes specific only to the poor. Due to this very importance of congregational prayer, Allah also emphasized to Mary (Maryam AS) the observance of the congregation:

'O Mary, be devoutly obedient to your Lord and prostrate and bow with those who bow [in prayer].' (Al-Imran - 43)

Birr (Righteousness/Piety): In the Arabic language, 'Birr' comes in the meaning of fulfilling a covenant, loyalty, and fulfilling rights. Rights include all types of rights, fundamental and real ones too. For example, obedience to God, obedience to parents, and sympathy for creation. Then moving forward, it also includes those rights that arise from verbal commitments, agreements, and treaties. For this reason, this word encompasses all forms of benevolence and goodness, and is also synonymous with 'Adl' (justice). Based on its usages, this word is the opposite of 'Ithm' (violation of rights/sin), 'Uqooq' (disobedience to parents), 'Ghadr' (treachery/disloyalty), and 'Zulm' (oppression/injustice). 'Barr' and 'Baarr' are adjectives used from it. For example, one would say 'Barrun bi-walidihi' (he is obedient to his father). 'Barra bil-qasam' means he fulfilled his oath. In the Holy Quran, in praise of Prophet John (Yahya AS), it is mentioned:

'And [he was] devout, and dutiful to his parents, and he was not a disobedient tyrant.' (Maryam - 13-14)

In another place, it is stated:

'Never will you attain the good [reward/Birr] until you spend [in the way of Allah] from that which you love.' (Al-Imran - 92)

In praise of Allah the Exalted:

'Indeed, it is He who is the Beneficent [Al-Barr], the Merciful.' (At-Tur - 28)

Also, it is stated:

'And cooperate in righteousness [Birr] and piety, but do not cooperate in sin and aggression.' (Al-Ma'idah - 2)

From this detail, the reality becomes clear that the word 'Birr', from one perspective, encompasses all deeds of virtue and goodness, but in its specific sense, it relates to the fulfillment of rights and duties.
The addressees of this verse are the scholars and elites of the Jews. The final segment, 'Wa antum tatloonal-kitab' (while you recite the Scripture), very clearly supports our view. These scholars and elites are being addressed and told: You vehemently preach to the common people to fulfill rights and duties, but while preaching this, you completely forget yourselves. You advise people to hand over their wealth to you, but you never think of the rights of God and the poor that are due upon you; rather, you devour the wealth given by the people. You have imposed your obedience upon others with full authority, to the extent that you have become their lords, but you yourselves are completely free from the obedience and submission to God. By ruining prayer and Zakah, you have completely destroyed the entire religion. Prophet Jesus (AS) also pointed to this condition of the Jewish scholars in highly eloquent words:

'And he said, "Woe to you as well, experts in the law, because you load people down with burdens they can hardly carry, and you yourselves will not lift one finger to help them."' (Luke 11:46)

Reflect on how much conformity there is between these words of the Gospel and the aforementioned words of the Holy Quran.
Wa antum tatloonal-kitab (while you recite the Scripture): Meaning, you are the scholars of the religion and Shariah, and you know that logically and textually, the responsibilities of the Shariah upon you are far greater compared to others.

The research on the word 'Salah' has already been explained during the interpretation of Al-Baqarah verse 3, so here we will suffice with the research on the word 'Sabr' (Patience).
The root meaning of the word 'Sabr' is to stop/restrain—that is, to save the self (Nafs) from panic, despair, and discouragement, and keep it firm on its stance. In the Holy Quran, this very reality has taken a much purer form. That is, in the Quran, its meaning is generally that a servant remains steadfast on Allah's covenant with complete peace of heart, believes in His promises, and does not give the weight of a blade of grass to whatever difficulties he has to face in this path.
People generally understand the meaning of 'Sabr' as helplessness and destitution, but in the Arabic lexicon and Quranic usages, this is not its meaning. Ustadh Imam (RA), in his Tafseer of Surah Wal-Asr, refutes this common notion in light of Arabic speech in the following words:

'But it must be remembered that according to the Arabs, Sabr is not some kind of helplessness and submission which is the practice of the powerless and the destitute; rather, it is the foundation of resolve and strength. Its usage is frequent in Arabic speech, and all its usages shed light on this very meaning. Hatim Tai says:

And how many terrifying rivers of death and destruction are there, over which there are bridges of swords.

We showed steadfastness against all their calamities and hardships with our swords. Until they cooled down.

There is a couplet by Asbagh:

O offspring of noble chiefs and those who are steadfast against hardships.

Zuhair bin Abi Sulma has said:

Riding thoroughbred horses, being sons-in-law to kings, and showing steadfastness in trenches where others would lose heart.'

The Holy Quran itself has also clarified the actual meaning of Sabr. Thus, it is stated: 'Was-sabireena fil-ba'saa'i wad-darraa'i wa heenal-ba's' (Al-Baqarah 177) 'And those who are patient in poverty and hardship and during battle.' In this verse, three occasions for Sabr are mentioned: poverty, illness, and war. If you reflect, these three are the sources of all calamities and hardships.
Earlier, the things that the Children of Israel were commanded to do or forbidden from in order to renew the Divine covenant—adopting them or refraining from them is extremely difficult for the ego. Therefore, the prescription that can make this difficult task easy is also provided. This prescription consists of two parts: Sabr (Patience/Steadfastness) and Salah (Prayer). By adopting these two things, this steep climb becomes easy for the ego. Sabr relates to character and conduct, and Salah relates to acts of worship. If a person does not have the trait of remaining firm on the truth despite difficulties and obstacles, he cannot bring any great work to completion in the world. But the trait of remaining firm on a correct stance despite difficulties and obstacles is not easily born within a person; rather, it is born through spiritual exercise (Riyadat), the method of which is Salah. If a person resolves to walk on a right path and sets out on it, and simultaneously constantly remembers his Lord and keeps seeking help from Him (the best form of which is Salah), then the strength of his resolve increases a thousandfold. Even the most difficult situation cannot cause his firm feet to stumble. If the delicacy of the circumstances causes a person's feet to falter, his connection with Allah, established through Salah, saves him from falling. From this detail, it is known that the command for Sabr given here is because without developing this attribute, no nation can remain firm on Allah's covenant. And the command for Salah is given because this is the means and source for developing Sabr, advancing it, and taking it to the level of perfection. Since we will discuss this issue in detail later during the contemplation of these verses, we suffice with just a hint here. Ustadh Imam (RA) has pointed to another aspect here. Although we have not adopted it, it is highly subtle. We will explain it later.
Wa innaha lakabeeratun illa 'alal-khashi'een (And indeed, it is difficult except for the humbly submissive): In this segment, the very first question that arises is: what does the pronoun 'ha' (it) in 'innaha' refer to? According to Mujahid, it refers to Salah. Imam Ibn Jarir (RA) has preferred this very opinion. The meaning would be that prayer is very heavy on the ego. Only those people can bear this heavy burden who have the fear of God in them and whose hearts remain bowed before God at all times out of fear of accountability in the Hereafter. The second opinion is that it refers to the guidance and advice mentioned in the previous sentence. Ustadh Imam (RA) is in favor of the first opinion, and he has presented several arguments in its support. According to him, the three reasons for ignoring 'Sabr' here and mentioning only the heaviness and difficulty of 'Salah' are: First, the difficulty and heaviness of Sabr were completely obvious; therefore, there was no need to mention it. He presents the verse 'Wasta'eenoo bis-sabri was-salah innallaha ma'as-sabireen' (Seek help through patience and prayer. Indeed, Allah is with the patient. Al-Baqarah 153) as an example, pointing out that it says Allah is with the patient, not that He is with those who pray. The reason is that attaining Allah's companionship in prayer is so obvious that it needed no mention. Similarly, since the hardship required for Sabr was obvious, it was not mentioned; only Salah was mentioned. Second, Sabr is among the necessary prerequisites of Salah. Only those people can remain steadfast on prayer who possess the trait of Sabr. Keeping this characteristic of Salah in mind, if you reflect, it becomes clear that when it is said that prayer is a heavy and difficult thing, it automatically hints at the aspect of it being heavy and difficult because it requires Sabr. This hint eliminated the need to explicitly mention Sabr. Third, since it is obvious that Sabr is a tough thing, commanding it while expressing its toughness would feel burdensome to the addressee's disposition; therefore, its toughness was not referenced. Only the toughness of Salah was referenced, which appears to be an easy thing.
Although these points are highly subtle and bring to light some very important aspects of the verses under discussion, my own inclination is towards the second opinion. That is, the pronoun 'ha' refers, in my view, to the instruction of seeking help through Sabr and Salah mentioned in the preceding segment. Many examples of this style of expression can be found in the Arabic language and the Holy Quran. We present a few examples from the Holy Quran. It states:

'But those who had been given knowledge said, "Woe to you! The reward of Allah is better for he who believes and does righteousness. And none are granted it except the patient."' (Al-Qasas - 80)

Meaning, the rank of faith and righteous deeds is not granted except to those who are patient.
In another place, it is stated:

'And not equal are the good deed and the bad. Repel [evil] by that [deed] which is better; and thereupon the one whom between you and him is enmity [will become] as though he was a devoted friend. But none is granted it except those who are patient, and none is granted it except one having a great portion [of good].' (Fussilat - 34-35)

Allama Ibn Kathir (RA) states in the interpretation of the aforementioned verses: 'Meaning, this advice/guidance is not granted except to those who are patient, and 'Wa ma yulaqqaha' means that this guidance is not given or inspired except to those who are very fortunate.'
The benefit of adopting this opinion is that the pronoun 'ha' does not relate solely to prayer, but to both patience and prayer. This aligns with both the rules of the Arabic language and the actual reality, because in truth, both these things are difficult for the ego. No one can dispute the difficulty of patience. Prayer too, with the condition of continuity and punctuality, becomes such a strict thing that only those granted Divine success (Tawfeeq) can fulfill it.
Lakabeeratun: 'Kabeerah' here means heavy, weighty, and difficult. In other places in the Holy Quran, this word is used in this meaning. 'Wa in kanat lakabeeratan illa 'alal-ladheena hadallah' (And indeed, it is a great/difficult thing, except for those whom Allah has guided. Al-Baqarah 143). 'Wa in kana kabura 'alayka i'raduhum' (And if their evasion is difficult for you [to bear]... Al-An'am 35).
Illa 'alal-khashi'een (except for the humbly submissive): The actual reality of Khushu is lowliness, humility, and submission. If a voice is low, this word is used for it; if a gaze is lowered, it is used for it. If a camel's hump sinks due to emaciation, this word is also used for it. Regarding the advice given above to seek help through patience and prayer to remain steadfast on the Divine covenant, it is stated that this path is easy only for those who possess Khushu, who fear God, who are free from the disease of pride and arrogance, and whose hearts constantly remain apprehensive with the thought of accountability before God. Those people cannot walk this path whose chests are empty of the fear of God, who are afflicted with the arrogance of national and racial pride, and who are more concerned with maintaining the prestige of their wealth and leadership than with God and the Hereafter. This Khushu is the foundation of both patience and prayer. Patience here means, as explained above, remaining firm on God's covenant despite all kinds of calamities, hardships, persecution, and disdain. And this can only be done by a person whose heart is overcome by such awe and greatness of God that in comparison to it, every calamity and humiliation seems insignificant to him. Similarly, regarding prayer, it is clear to every person of knowledge that its very foundation rests on Khushu and Khudu (humility and submissiveness). Thus, this reality has been clarified in more than one place in the Holy Quran. For example:

'Certainly will the believers have succeeded: They who are during their prayer humbly submissive (Khashi'oon).' (Al-Mu'minun - 1-2)

In another place:

'...and they used to supplicate Us in hope and fear, and they were to Us humbly submissive (Khashi'een).' (Al-Anbiya - 90)

The opinion a person forms about something without seeing it is called 'Zann'. Since there is generally no certainty on such an opinion, the word 'Zann' has become somewhat synonymous with doubt. Thus, in the Arabic language and the Holy Quran, this word has been used extensively in this meaning. There is a famous couplet by Tarafa:

'I know for a fact, it is not merely a Zann (guess),
That when a man's cousin is humiliated, he himself is humiliated.'

Similarly, in the Holy Quran:

'We only assume an assumption [Zann], and we are not convinced.' (Al-Jathiyah - 32)

However, the opinion formed about an unseen thing is not necessarily doubtful. Often, this opinion is based on certainty, but the word 'Zann' is also used for it. This usage of Zann is in accordance with its general meaning; the concept of doubt is not implicit in it. There is a couplet by Aws bin Hajar:

'That intelligent one, if he even makes an assumption (Zann) about you,
It seems as if he does it after seeing and hearing.'

Duraid bin Simmah says:

'So I told them to be certain (Zannoo) of two thousand heavily armed riders,
Whose leaders would be wearing armor of fine links.'

This is a further praise of the 'Khashi'een' (the humbly submissive), that they possess the Zann (certainty) of being resurrected after death and meeting their Lord; they are not heedless and unconcerned about the Hereafter.
This point in the praise of the Khashi'een sheds light on their inner self. It clarifies that the state of helplessness, destitution, lowliness, and humility that overcomes them is because the fear of the Hereafter and the dread of appearing before God permeates their hearts.
In using the word 'Zann' to express this inner state of the Khashi'een, there is a special beauty: this word is sufficient to express a state ranging from apprehension and predominant probability to certainty and absoluteness. The matter of the Hereafter is such an important issue that it is not necessary for a person to prepare for it only when he achieves absolute certainty about it; rather, even the apprehension and predominant probability of it is enough for a person to prepare for it. A massive dam, the breaking of which poses the danger of submerging an entire city, demands our attention not only when the water creates cracks in its walls, but due to the terrifying apprehension of its breaking, its maintenance is prioritized even when it appears completely secure. When a human's foresight is like this in the matter of a small danger, then regarding the life after death and the matter of the Hereafter—which relates to an eternal life—why should he become so insensitive and dull as to close his eyes to all its signs and symptoms, and not feel the need to prepare for it until he is completely certain of it?
Wa annahum ilayhi raji'oon (And that to Him they will return): The words 'And that to Him they will return' simultaneously reveal two realities: Tawheed (Monotheism) and Tafweed (Submission/Delegation).
The aspect of Tawheed is that they firmly believe that in the Hereafter, all matters will be presented solely before the One God who has no partner. He alone will give reward and punishment, and whatever He gives, He will give with complete justice and fairness. No one else will have the power to influence His decisions or save anyone from His wrath. This theme is created by placing 'ilayhi' (to Him) first. This reference to Tawheed was necessary here because if even a trace of the belief in Shirk (polytheism) remains in the heart, the belief in meeting God becomes completely lifeless. This is because a polytheist believes that, firstly, God will not lay a hand on him out of regard for His partners, and if He does, His partners will save him through their efforts and intercession.
The aspect of Tafweed is that those who remain steadfast on the covenant of servitude to Allah cheerfully accept whatever difficulties and persecutions they face, because they have the confidence that the One in whose path they are enduring all this, they are advancing towards Him with every step. And when the One who is sought is ahead, then what care is there for all the noise and uproar left behind?

'What sorrow is it if the whole creation is against me,
It is enough if the One God is for me.'

The explanation of the word 'Ni'mah' (favor) has already been given above. Here, 'Wa annee faddaltukum 'alal-'alameen' (And that I preferred you over the worlds) is conjoined to it. This mention of the specific after the general clarifies the brevity present in the word 'Ni'mah'. This preference refers to the position of guiding and leading nations, for which Allah chose the Children of Israel in a specific era. Any preference that fulfills the responsibility of a position remains with it; but if it abandons it, it is not only deprived of the preference granted to it, but as a retribution for its ingratitude, it also encounters disgrace. Here, the Children of Israel are reminded that the preference you are proud of was a gift from God. If you wish to retain it, remain firm on God's covenant and fulfill its rights. By leaving God's covenant, you cannot fulfill this preference.
The selection of the Children of Israel for the guidance and leadership of nations is mentioned elsewhere in the Holy Quran as well. For example, it is stated:

'And We certainly chose them by knowledge over the worlds.' (Ad-Dukhan - 32)

Here, the words 'Ala 'ilm' (by knowledge) also hint that this selection was not a blind choice, selecting whoever came to hand. Rather, this act was done by a Possessor of Knowledge and Insight, who can determine through His knowledge and insight when they are worthy of this position and when they are not.

'Jaza 'anhu' means, he paid on his behalf, or he sufficed on his behalf. 'La tajzee nafsun 'an nafsin shay'an' (No soul will suffice for another soul at all) means that no person will be of any use to another. The responsibility that falls on one, no one else will be able to fulfill on their behalf. This theme is expressed in the Holy Quran in various styles, for example:

'And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another.' (Al-An'am - 164)

'And fear a Day when no father will avail his son, nor will a son avail his father at all.' (Luqman - 33)

On that Day, everyone will be consumed with concern for their own self. 'For every man, that Day, will be a matter adequate for him.' (Abasa - 37)
'Shafa'ah' (Intercession) is from 'Shafa'a'. 'Shafa'a-ash-shay'a' means he added a similar thing to it to make it a pair. 'Shafa'a lifulanin' or 'Shafa'a feehi' means that a person adds his support or recommendation to someone's words or request to reinforce it.
'Adl' means justice. It is stated:

'...that you judge with justice.' (An-Nisa - 58)

Then from here, this word was used in the meaning of equal and equivalent. It is stated:

'...or the equivalent (Adl) of that in fasting.' (Al-Ma'idah - 95)

It is also used in the meaning of ransom (Fidyah), because a ransom is considered equivalent to the one being ransomed.
In 'La yuqbalu minha shafa'atun wa la yu'khadhu minha 'adlun wa la hum yunsaroon' (nor will intercession be accepted from it, nor will compensation be taken from it, nor will they be aided), the Arabic language style is observed where apparently the consequence (Lazim) of a thing is negated, but the actual intent is to negate the cause (Malzoom). The poet Imru' al-Qais, in one of his couplets, praised a desert path saying 'La yuhtada bimanarihi' (It cannot be navigated by its minarets/milestones). Obviously, by this expression, his intent is to say that there are absolutely no minarets or milestones present in this desert for guidance. In the same style, the intent here is to state that on that Day, neither will there be an intercessor for them, nor will any intercession be accepted, nor will anyone have compensation to give, nor will compensation be taken from anyone, nor will they have supporters and helpers, nor will anyone be able to support and help them. This very reality is expressed in other words as follows:

'So there will not benefit them the intercession of [any] intercessors.' (Al-Muddaththir - 48)

And then these words are quoted from the tongues of the dwellers of Hell:

'So now we have no intercessors, And not a devoted friend.' (Ash-Shu'ara - 100-101)

The arrogance the Children of Israel had for being the offspring of Prophets like Abraham (AS), Isaac (AS), and Jacob (AS), due to which they fell into the misconception that their relation to and intercession from these elders would be sufficient for their salvation—this verse cuts the root of their illusion. It reminds them that the real thing that will be of use before God is adherence to the Divine covenant, faith, and righteous deeds. Do not remain heedless of this by relying merely on castles in the air.

Aala Fir'awn (The people of Pharaoh). 'Aal' does not solely mean someone's children/offspring; rather, this word encompasses family, children, nation, tribe, followers, and helpers.
There is a couplet by Nabighah Dhubyani:

'From the tribe (Aal) of Mayyah, departing in the evening or morning,
Hastening, some with provisions and some unprovisioned.'

In Surah Al-Mu'min verse 45, it is stated:

'...and the people of Pharaoh (Aala Fir'awn) were enveloped by the worst of punishment.'

'And We certainly seized the people of Pharaoh (Aala Fir'awn) with years of famine and a deficiency in fruits...' (Al-A'raf - 130)

The punishment mentioned in these verses obviously came upon Pharaoh and his entire nation, not just his children; in fact, his children are not even mentioned anywhere. Furthermore, as far as evidence goes, it proves that he was childless. The Torah does mention that the one who had Moses (AS) pulled from the river in his childhood was Pharaoh's daughter. However, the Quran corrected this error as well, stating that it was not his daughter, but his wife. Thus it states:

'And the wife of Pharaoh said, "[He will be] a comfort of the eye for me and for you. Do not kill him; perhaps he may benefit us, or we may adopt him as a son." And they perceived not.' (Al-Qasas - 9)

'Sawm' means to put a burden or load on someone. One would say 'Samahu zulman' and 'Samahu khasfan'—he made him taste oppression or humiliation.

Yudhabbihoona abnaa'akum wa yastahyoona nisaa'akum
(They were slaughtering your sons and keeping your women alive.)

This is the detail of the punishment of oppression and humiliation in which the Children of Israel were afflicted at the hands of the Pharaonic people. Although various kinds of oppression were inflicted upon the Children of Israel in Egypt, and they faced countless types of humiliations, the details of which exist in their history, here only two things are mentioned as a sample. From these samples, one can gauge the extent of the torment the Children of Israel were subjected to there.
The reasons for the killing of sons and its nature will be detailed at a suitable place. However, a point of rhetoric should be noted: when mentioning the slaughter of boys, the word 'Abnaa'akum' (your sons) is used to evoke the emotion of paternal love; and when mentioning keeping the girls alive, the word 'Nisaa'akum' (your women) is used because this expression was more effective in stirring honor and protective jealousy (Ghayrah).
Wa fee dhalikum balaa'un min rabbikum 'azeem (And in that was a great trial from your Lord). The severity of this trial is hinted at here so that they could grasp the magnitude of the salvation they achieved—what a massive tribulation it was from which their Lord freed them. If He had not freed them, no other power could have freed them from this torment.

Faraqna bikumul-bahr (We parted the sea for you): The translation of this would be 'We took you along and crossed by parting the sea.' It means that just as someone carries another in their arms across a river, We brought you across in the same manner.
Wa antum tanzuroon (while you were looking on): Meaning, after your salvation, you saw with your own eyes the spectacle of Pharaoh and his drowning while standing on the shore.
Two things should be kept in mind regarding the events of the history of the Children of Israel being pointed out here.
First, all these events are highly important and famous events in the history of the Children of Israel, known to every child among them. Therefore, there was no need to detail them; mere hints were sufficient.
Second, the Children of Israel during the time of the revelation of the Quran not only accepted these events as facts of their history but took pride in them. For this reason, the Quran has presented these events to them as if they occurred with them personally. This eloquent style is highly effective and useful from the perspective of establishing the proof (Itmam-e-Hujjah).

This points to the promise Allah made to Prophet Moses (AS) to give His commands and guidance after leaving Egypt and crossing the sea, and for this purpose, He called him to Mount Sinai. This forty-day period was for the spiritual and internal preparation necessary to bear the immense burden of the Divine Book. Initially, this promise was for thirty days, but Prophet Moses (AS) arrived earlier than the appointed time. Due to his haste, Allah's wisdom of nurturing required that this period be extended from 30 days to 40 days. In the aforementioned verse, this entire period has been aggregated. In Surah Al-A'raf, its detail is recorded as follows:

'And We made an appointment with Moses for thirty nights and perfected them by [the addition of] ten; so the term of his Lord was completed as forty nights.' (Al-A'raf - 142)

Thummat-takhadhtumul-'ijla mim ba'dihi wa antum zalimoon (Then you took the calf [for worship] after him, while you were wrongdoers): Meaning, after Moses went to the mountain, you made a metallic calf and began worshipping it. The details of this event are present in the Book of Exodus, chapter 32, but the Jews, true to their habit, implicated Prophet Aaron (Harun AS) in it as well, which the Quran refuted on another occasion:

'When the people saw that Moses was so long in coming down from the mountain, they gathered around Aaron and said, "Come, make us gods who will go before us. As for this fellow Moses who brought us up out of Egypt, we don't know what has happened to him."... Then the Lord said to Moses, "Go down, because your people, whom you brought up out of Egypt, have become corrupt. They have been quick to turn away from what I commanded them and have made themselves an idol cast in the shape of a calf. They have bowed down to it and sacrificed to it and have said, 'These are your gods, Israel, who brought you up out of Egypt.' I have seen these people," the Lord said to Moses, "and they are a stiff-necked people. Now leave me alone so that my anger may burn against them and that I may destroy them."' (Exodus 32:1-10)

Wa antum zalimoon (while you were wrongdoers): Meaning, by committing this calf-worship, you inflicted a great wrong upon your own souls. Thus, just two verses later, the Quran itself clarifies this:

'O my people, indeed you have wronged yourselves by your taking of the calf [for worship].' (Al-Baqarah - 54)

The essence of 'Zulm' (wrongdoing/oppression) is to violate rights. By committing Shirk (polytheism), a person severely degrades his own soul, because despite being the vicegerent of God and the noblest of all creations, he makes a creation similar to himself, or even inferior to himself, his god. What greater violation of the rights of one's own soul could there be?

Furqan means the thing that distinguishes between truth and falsehood. Here, the 'Waw' (and) is for explanation and elaboration. Meaning, by referring to the book of Torah itself with the word 'Furqan', it has highlighted another aspect of it. In the Holy Quran, the expression 'Furqan' is used for both the Quran and the Torah. For example:

'And We had already given Moses and Aaron the Furqan (criterion).' (Al-Anbiya - 48)

Similarly, regarding the Holy Quran:

'Blessed is He who sent down the Furqan (criterion) upon His Servant...' (Al-Furqan - 1)

Using the word Furqan to describe these books keeps several aspects in view. First, it presents the details of all commands and guidance. Second, it distinguishes between truth and falsehood, and the permissible (Halal) and the prohibited (Haram). Third, they are completely clear in their intent and objective. Fourth, through them, man attains the wisdom that provides light to recognize good and evil in all the ups and downs of life.
The Quran has also termed the Battle of Badr as 'Furqan', because it too thoroughly laid bare the distinction between truth and falsehood.

The meaning of 'Bari' (Maker/Creator) is very similar to the meaning of 'Khaliq' (Creator). In the Holy Quran, the attributes of Allah are mentioned together in one place: 'Huwallahul-Khaliqul-Bari'ul-Musawwir' (He is Allah, the Creator, the Inventor, the Fashioner). 'Khalq' means to design the blueprint of a thing; 'Bari'' means to bring it into existence and set it right; 'Tasweer' means to complete its form. From this perspective, although there is a very fine distinction in the lexical meaning of 'Khaliq' and 'Bari'', in general usage, both are used interchangeably.
Faqtuloo anfusakum (So kill yourselves): 'So kill yourselves' does not mean that you should run your own swords across your own necks. Rather, it means that from every tribe, those people who remained aloof from this tribulation of Shirk and calf-worship should, with their own hands, strike the necks of those from their respective tribes who opened the door to this tribulation of apostasy for the nation. There were several great wisdoms in giving this command.

First, in this way, this repentance took on a collective form. It was as if the collective conscience of the Children of Israel excised from within themselves those who had insulted Allah's covenant of Monotheism.
Second, it made the true greatness of Monotheism and the true abhorrence of Shirk completely clear. It meant that Shirk is such an evil that if a man's left hand commits it, it is obligatory upon his right hand to chop off his left hand. In this matter, he should not allow any appeasement or tolerance to interfere, nor should he consider any kinship or relationship.
Third, if the righteous of every tribe and family raise their swords against the wicked of their own tribes, it will not incite familial and tribal prejudice; rather, the purification of the Children of Israel will be achieved without any fear of further tribulation.

Reading the Torah also reveals almost the same thing. Thus, it says in the Book of Exodus:

'Moses saw that the people were running wild and that Aaron had let them get out of control and so become a laughingstock to their enemies (1). So he stood at the entrance to the camp and said, "Whoever is for the Lord (i.e., firm on the covenant of Monotheism), come to me." And all the Levites rallied to him. Then he said to them, "This is what the Lord, the God of Israel, says: 'Each man strap a sword to his side. Go back and forth through the camp from one end to the other, each killing his brother and friend and neighbor.'" The Levites did as Moses commanded, and that day about three thousand of the people died. Then Moses said, "You have been set apart to the Lord today (i.e., renew the covenant of Monotheism), for you were against your own sons and brothers, and he has blessed you this day."' (Exodus 32:25-29)

Although the Torah's account suggests that Prophet Moses (AS) appointed only the Levites to the task of killing the apostates, the latter part of the quoted excerpt itself testifies that the true reality of the matter is what we have pointed out: the monotheists of every tribe were appointed to this task to strike the necks of the apostates of their respective tribes, so that this would be a testimony to the enhanced faith of the believers, and people would learn the lesson that Shirk is such a grave sin that in this matter, a father will not forgive his son, nor a son his father.
In Islamic history, you may remember, a similar type of advice was given by Umar (RA) regarding the prisoners of Badr.
From this command, one point emerges that for the acceptance of repentance, complete disavowal of the original sin is necessary. The second point is that an evil that spreads in society due to the negligence of its responsible figures, its expiation has to be paid by everyone; without this, this crime is not forgiven by Allah. The third point is that the punishment for apostasy was execution in the Shariah of Prophet Moses (AS) as well.
Dhalikum khayrun lakum 'inda bari'ikum (That is best for you in the sight of your Creator): Meaning, apparently this might seem to you like a great injustice and a huge national loss to cut off such a large portion of the nation from the national body, but in the sight of your Creator, cutting off this portion is exactly where the blessings of your religion and the world lie. If, out of the fervor of familial emotions and national love, you consider it better to keep this corrupt portion attached to your national existence, then remember that its corruption will corrupt your entire national existence. If those who oppose the principles of a group built on principles and beliefs cling to it merely based on racial ties, that entire group will inevitably be destroyed.

(1) We will refute at an appropriate place the kind of additions the Jews made in the Torah to defame Prophet Aaron (AS).

'We will not believe you until we see God with our own eyes.' The Children of Israel were such patients of doubt that they simply could not believe that Allah actually spoke to Prophet Moses (AS). Therefore, when Moses (AS) would tell them that the Lord commands this, they would say, 'When God speaks to you, He should speak to us too, and we should see Him with our own eyes; without this, how can we accept the truth of your word?'
As far as the desire to see Allah is concerned, this desire is not a blameworthy one. Prophet Moses (AS) also had this desire. But there is a huge difference between this desire being for the expansion of the chest and peace of heart, and it being made an excuse for denial and rejection. Prophet Moses's (AS) desire was similar to how Prophet Abraham (AS) wanted to see how Allah gives life to the dead, so that he could attain complete peace of heart regarding the Hereafter. Therefore, Allah did not reproach Prophet Moses (AS), but only said that you cannot see My Essence with these mortal eyes; you can only see My Attributes. The Holy Quran details this as follows:

'And when Moses arrived at Our appointed time and his Lord spoke to him, he said, "My Lord, show me [Yourself] that I may look at You." [Allah] said, "You will not see Me, but look at the mountain; if it should remain in place, then you will see Me." But when his Lord appeared to the mountain, He rendered it level, and Moses fell unconscious. And when he awoke, he said, "Exalted are You! I have repented to You, and I am the first of the believers."' (Al-A'raf - 143)

In contrast, the demand of the people of the Children of Israel was merely a manifestation of their uncertainty and skeptical mindset, and they displayed this at every step despite seeing Allah's exceedingly clear signs. For this reason, wrath descended upon them.
This wrath is described here with the words 'fa'akhadhatkumus-sa'iqah' (so the thunderbolt struck you), and in Surah Al-A'raf 155 with the words 'falamma akhadhathumur-rajfah' (So when the earthquake struck them). We have already explained the word 'Sa'iqah' in the seventeenth section. It means thunder and crack, and is also spoken for the lightning that strikes with a thunderclap. 'Rajfah' means earthquake. There is no contradiction in the seemingly two different words the Quran has used in two places for the same event. These are two different effects of the same incident appearing simultaneously. It seems that when Allah cast His manifestation on the mountain to let Prophet Moses (AS) observe, just as the mountain shattered, similarly, when His manifestation appeared upon the demand of the Children of Israel, it manifested in the form of a 'Sa'iqah' (thunderbolt) which cast an earthquake into the entire mountain, and these people fell down thunderstruck.

The state that overcame those seventy leaders who went to the mountain with Prophet Moses (AS) on this occasion (1) due to this thunderbolt and earthquake, the Holy Quran has termed it as 'Mawt' (death). This death can mean actual death, and metaphorically, unconsciousness as well. In the Arabic language, the word 'Mawt' is also used metaphorically for sleep and unconsciousness (2). Thus, the famous supplication recited after waking from sleep, recorded in Hadith, contains these words: 'Alhamdu lillahil-ladhee ahyana ba'da ma amatana wa ilayhin-nushoor' (Praise be to Allah who gave us life after having given us death, and to Him is the return). Similarly, the word 'Ba'th' (resurrection) is also used for waking the Companions of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf) from their sleep.
Although the Children of Israel, due to their rebellion, deserved not to be raised again, Allah, out of His special grace, granted them further respite. Their Prophet also prayed for them with great compassion on this occasion, which Allah accepted. It is referenced in Surah Al-A'raf as follows:

'And Moses chose from his people seventy men for Our appointment. And when the earthquake seized them, he said, "My Lord, if You had willed, You could have destroyed them before and me [as well]. Would You destroy us for what the foolish among us have done? This is not but Your trial by which You send astray whom You will and guide whom You will. You are our Protector, so forgive us and have mercy upon us; and You are the best of forgivers."' (Al-A'raf - 155)

(1) It is clear from the Holy Quran that this relates to the occasion when, after the incident of calf-worship, Prophet Moses (AS) took seventy chosen men of his nation to the mountain with the purpose of seeking forgiveness for his people and involving these leaders of his nation in this task.
(2) It is mentioned in Lisan al-Arab: 'Mata ar-rajulu wa hama wa hwama idha nama... Al-mawtu as-sukoon wa kullu ma sakana faqad mata...' (The man 'died' means he slept... death is stillness, and whatever becomes still has 'died'...). And in the Hadith of the supplication of waking up: 'Praise be to Allah who gave us life after giving us death...' Sleep is called death because intellect and movement cease with it, metaphorically and as a simile, not literally. It is said that 'Mawt' in Arab speech is applied to stillness; it is said 'matatir-reeh' (the wind died), meaning it became still. From this comes sleep, per Allah's saying 'and those that do not die in their sleep.' It has been said that sleep is light death, and death is heavy sleep... 'Mawtah' is a kind of madness and epilepsy that afflicts a person; when he recovers, his intellect returns like a sleeper. And stillness. And 'Mawtah' is swooning.

This is a description of the favors Allah bestowed upon the Children of Israel in the Sinai desert to save them from the calamity of the scorching sun and starvation.
The root meaning of 'Mann' is grace and favor, but here it refers to that special food which Allah, out of His special grace, provided for the Children of Israel in the Sinai desert, for which they had to neither plow nor bear the hardships of sowing and irrigation. Its details are described as follows:

'That evening quail came and covered the camp, and in the morning there was a layer of dew around the camp. When the dew was gone, thin flakes like frost on the ground appeared on the desert floor. When the Israelites saw it, they said to each other, "What is it?" (Man huwa?) For they did not know what it was. Moses said to them, "It is the bread the Lord has given you to eat..." Each morning everyone gathered as much as they needed, and when the sun grew hot, it melted away.' (Exodus 16:13-21)

From this, it appears that something like dew dropped onto the ground and froze like grains of frost. It was possible to gather it before the sun's heat intensified. After the heat intensified, these grains would melt. Since this blessing, as mentioned, was obtained without bearing any trouble or hardship, and was obtained in such a barren desert where the means and resources for providing food were non-existent, it was named 'Mann' (it should be clear that both Arabic and Hebrew are closely related languages).
This explanation for the naming of 'Mann' seems reasonable, but the aforementioned excerpt from the Torah suggests that when the Children of Israel saw this strange and wonderful thing, the question arose among them: 'Man huwa?' (What is it?). In response to this very question of theirs, it was named 'Mann'. In our view, this etymology is merely an invention of the Jews' poor taste. Neither the word supports it, nor does sound intellect accept it.
When Prophet Moses (AS) referred to this thing as 'bread', it does not mean it was actually some kind of bread, but 'bread' here implies food. This word was used extensively in ancient scriptures to express the concept of food. In our language too, this word is used in this sense.
Salwa: Like 'Mann', 'Salwa' also entered Arabic through the People of the Book, and the Arabs have used it in their poetry as well. This word is used for those birds which Allah sent for the Children of Israel in the Sinai desert. They were similar to quails, and like quails, hunting them was very easy. Their details in Exodus are as follows:

'The whole Israelite community set out from Elim and came to the Desert of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after they had come out of Egypt. In the desert the whole community grumbled against Moses and Aaron. The Israelites said to them, "If only we had died by the Lord's hand in Egypt! There we sat around pots of meat and ate all the food we wanted, but you have brought us out into this desert to starve this entire assembly to death." ... The Lord said to Moses, "I have heard the grumbling of the Israelites. Tell them, 'At twilight you will eat meat, and in the morning you will be filled with bread. Then you will know that I am the Lord your God.'" That evening quail came and covered the camp.' (Exodus 16:1-13)

Kuloo min tayyibati ma razaqnakum (Eat of the good things We have provided you): On such occasions, our commentators generally consider the word 'Qulna' (We said) to be omitted. Meaning, We bestowed these things upon them and said, 'Eat of the good things We have provided.' In our view, leaving the word 'said' omitted on such occasions holds a special eloquence. It means that every blessing of Allah, by its very form and structure—or in other words, by its circumstantial language—also extends this invitation: 'Benefit from this Divine blessing and be grateful to your Sustainer.' These hints have been made explicit in some places in the Holy Quran, and in some places, like here, they have been left hidden. Those who possess an intellect capable of understanding the hints of the blessings spread throughout this universe understand these hints very well.
Here, the context of the speech makes it clear that the Children of Israel did not recognize the right of these great blessings of Allah. Instead of becoming grateful upon receiving these blessings, they remained unappreciative of them and disobedient to God. Since this is clear from the context, it was not explicitly stated in words; instead, it was replaced by the statement: 'And they wronged Us not, but they were [only] wronging themselves.' This sentence clarifies the attitude of the Children of Israel regarding these blessings, and it also clarifies that those who are ungrateful for any of God's blessings do not harm God in the least, but only harm themselves. This final point, instead of being addressed directly to the Jews like the previous points, is stated in the third person, turning away from them, which expresses the speaker's disgust towards them.

The root meaning of 'Qaryah' in the lexicon is a gathering place. In Arabic, one would say 'Qaryal-maa'a fil-hawd' (he gathered water in the cistern). From here, this word was used to mean a settlement, because it is a gathering place for people. From the usages of this word, it is clearly known that it is not used only for small villages, but also for large cities and central populated areas.
Here, this 'Qaryah' can refer to a city in the land of Palestine, because the description given later with the words 'fakuloo minha haythu shi'tum raghadan' (and eat from it wherever you will in abundance) can apply to a city in that land. It is possible that it refers to Jericho (Ariha). This is the opinion of Ibn Abbas (RA) and Ibn Zayd. This was the first city in the Palestinian region to come under the control of the Children of Israel.
Udkhulul-baba sujjadan (Enter the gate bowing humbly): The root meaning of 'Sajdah' is to bow the head. There can be different degrees of this bowing. Its perfect form is placing the forehead on the ground, which we adopt in prayer. Amr bin Kulthum has taken this very perfect meaning in his famous boastful couplet:

'When a toddler of our tribe reaches the age of weaning,
The greatest tyrants fall down in prostration before him.'

Here in the verse, it only means to bow the head. The context is proof of this.
By 'Al-Bab' (the gate), some people have taken it to mean the gate of the city. Others have taken it to mean the gate of the Tabernacle (Tent of Meeting) (1). I prefer this second opinion. The advice to enter the gates of a conquered city humbly, though a valuable advice, might be suitable for a nation that is brave and powerful. The condition of the Children of Israel was such that when Prophet Moses (AS) commanded them in the Desert of Paran to march upon Palestine, their hearts sank, and they plainly replied that there are tyrannical and powerful people in this land, we are not ready to fight them; you and your God go and fight, and when the territory is cleared of those tyrants, we will enter. For such people, the advice not to enter the city gates with conqueror's pride but humbly and with bowed heads seems somewhat unnecessary. Therefore, our view is that 'gate' here refers to the gate of the Tabernacle. The purpose is to state that they were commanded to enter this city, fully and freely benefit from its fertility and lushness, and humbly present themselves at the Tabernacle to thank God and seek forgiveness for their sins. But just as they were ungrateful for every blessing and violated every guidance, they were ungrateful for this blessing and guidance as well.
Qooloo hittatun (And say, 'Relieve us of our burdens'): The word 'Hittah' stands in place of a sentence. For example, in the Holy Quran, it is stated: 'Yaqooloona ta'atun' (They say, 'Obedience') (An-Nisa: 81). For this reason, the subject (Mubtada) must be considered omitted here. Zamakhshari has fully clarified it like this: 'Mas'alatuna hittatun' (Our request is Hittah). 'Hittah' is from 'Hatt', which means to sweep. Here it means the sweeping away of sins. Since Arabic and Hebrew share close roots, it is presumed that this root was also used in Hebrew for sweeping and forgiving, and it was among their words for seeking forgiveness (Istighfar) and repentance. From there it transferred into Arabic.
Muhsineen (The doers of good): In Arabic, 'Ahsana ila fulanin' would mean he did a favor to so-and-so, and 'Ahsanash-shay'a' would mean he did the thing very well. Because of this, the word 'Muhsin' is used in Arabic for both one who does a favor and one who performs an action excellently. The context indicates that here this word is used in this second meaning. I could not think of a beautiful single word for it in Urdu. Therefore, in the translation, an attempt has been made just to convey the meaning.
(1) See Tafseer Allama Abi Al-Su'ud.

Meaning, the word they were instructed to use for supplication, they replaced it with a word having a completely different meaning. Some people think that here it does not mean changing the words, but changing the attitude. This is the view of Abu Muslim Al-Isfahani from the earlier scholars, but the words of the Quran do not support this view. When the word 'Baddala' (changed) comes with its two objects, as it is here—even though one is omitted—it means substituting one thing for another. Furthermore, when it is explicitly stated here that the wrongdoers substituted the word that was said to them with another word that was not said to them, then taking it to mean only a change in attitude and action is a clear deviation from the Quran.
In our view, this is not merely a reference to a change in attitude and action; rather, the words of the Quran indicate that some wretched individuals among the Children of Israel had replaced the word 'Hittah' with a word having a completely different meaning. As for the question of what word they replaced it with, there is no explicit mention in the Quran. Various statements are narrated from the interpreters, none of which can be stated with certainty.
I sometimes think that just as the Christians changed the meaning of the phrases of their Lord's Prayer (equivalent to Surah Al-Fatihah), a similar change was made by the Children of Israel in the meaning of this prayer of theirs. In the Lord's Prayer of the Christians found in Luke 11:1-4, these words appear: 'Give us each day our daily bread'. Obviously, this is completely deviated from the meaning of the original prayer. The original prayer would have been: 'Grant us the spirit of guidance that leads to the straight path' (1). But since the Hebrew word for bread is used for both spiritual food and material bread, this common word must have been used to express guidance. Later, in translations, the spirit of guidance vanished, and only the bread remained. A similar kind of change was made by the Children of Israel in the words of their prayer, which completely changed the original spirit of the prayer.
Fa-anzalna 'alal-ladheena zalamoo rijzan minas-sama'i (So We sent down upon those who wronged a punishment from the sky): 'Rijz' and 'Rijs' are both two forms of the same word. Their original meaning is agitation and trembling. From here they were used for filth and impurity because seeing filth and impurity creates a kind of agitation and shudder in the disposition. Then they were used for torment/punishment because torment also creates a kind of agitation and trembling in hearts. The addition of 'minas-sama'i' (from the sky) points to the fact that the nature of this calamity was completely different from common calamities. The aspect of Divine wrath was very prominent in it. In some places in the Torah, this specific nature is explained as follows:

'If these men die a natural death and suffer the fate of all mankind, then the Lord has not sent me. But if the Lord brings about something totally new, and the earth opens its mouth and swallows them, with everything that belongs to them, and they go down alive into the realm of the dead, then you will know that these men have treated the Lord with contempt.' (Numbers 16:29-30)

The Quran has expressed this specific nature of the aforementioned punishment with the word 'minas-sama'i', just as we refer to a terrifying disaster as 'heavenly wrath'.
As for the question of what this punishment was specifically related to the 'Qaryah' (city) mentioned here, answering this question is difficult. However, reading the Torah reveals that during this journey, the Children of Israel severely disobeyed Allah multiple times, and as retribution for these disobediences, they fell victim to various plagues. For example, when the Children of Israel were in Shittim (which was a city right next to the land of Palestine), these people committed immorality with Moabite women. At their invitation, these people began participating in their polytheistic sacrifices, and thus indirectly began worshipping their deity 'Baal of Peor'. As punishment, Allah sent a severe plague upon them in which twenty-four thousand of their people perished.
In the Book of Numbers, chapter 33, it is also mentioned that Prophet Moses (AS) gave this instruction to the Children of Israel in the plains of Moab: When you cross the Jordan and enter the land of Canaan, you must drive out all the polytheists from there, smash their carved stones and cast idols, and demolish their high places. If you violate this, then remember that whatever I have told you to do to them, I will do exactly that to you. It seems the Children of Israel, true to their habit, violated this command of the Prophet as well, as retribution for which a plague similar to the one in Shittim came upon them.

(1) See Maulana Farahi's (RA) Tafseer of Surah Al-Fatihah.

It is known from the Torah that Prophet Moses (AS) made this supplication for water in the Desert of Zin. It is stated in the Book of Numbers, chapter 20:

'In the first month the whole Israelite community arrived at the Desert of Zin, and they stayed at Kadesh... Now there was no water for the community, and the people gathered in opposition to Moses and Aaron. They quarreled with Moses and said, "If only we had died when our brothers fell dead before the Lord! Why did you bring the Lord's community into this wilderness, that we and our livestock should die here? Why did you bring us up out of Egypt to this terrible place? It has no grain or figs, grapevines or pomegranates. And there is no water to drink!" Moses and Aaron went from the assembly to the entrance to the tent of meeting and fell facedown, and the glory of the Lord appeared to them. The Lord said to Moses, "Take the staff, and you and your brother Aaron gather the assembly together. Speak to that rock before their eyes and it will pour out its water. You will bring water out of the rock for the community so they and their livestock can drink." So Moses took the staff from the Lord's presence, just as he commanded him. He and Aaron gathered the assembly together in front of the rock and Moses said to them, "Listen, you rebels, must we bring you water out of this rock?" Then Moses raised his arm and struck the rock twice with his staff. Water gushed out, and the community and their livestock drank.' (Numbers 20:1-11)

Qad 'alima kullu unasin mashrabahum (Every people knew its watering place): 'Qad 'alima' meaning knew, determined. Since twelve springs gushed forth from the rock and the tribes of the Children of Israel were also twelve, every tribe determined their separate watering places, and there remained no fear of any quarrel breaking out over taking water. If water had not been provided in abundance, swords would have remained drawn among these people every day over getting a drink of water in this desert. Therefore, this event was not merely a great miracle, but also a great favor.
Kuloo washraboo... (Eat and drink...): Just as after mentioning the blessing of Manna and Quail, it was stated in verse 57:

'Eat of the good things We have provided you.'

Similarly, after referencing this provision of water, it was stated:

'Eat and drink from the provision of Allah, and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption.'

This is the statement of the 'right' of this great blessing mentioned above. Every blessing of Allah that we receive, through its circumstantial language, also reminds us of the right that falls upon us due to benefiting from it. This is an expression of that right, and if human nature had not been distorted by the darkness of ingratitude, it could not deny this right. This is the very voice of his nature that divine revelation is sounding in his ears.
This point is also worth noting here that after Manna and Quail, only the word 'Kuloo' (Eat) was used, because up to that time, only food had been abundantly provided. When water was also provided with the same abundance and plenty, 'Washraboo' (and drink) was added along with 'Kuloo'.

The word 'Baql' (herbs/vegetables) is a general term for all types of greens and vegetables.
'Qiththa' means cucumbers.
'Foom' and 'Thoom' refer to the same thing, meaning garlic. The Arabs sometimes interchange the letter 'Thaa' (ث) with 'Faa' (ف). For example, they change 'Aathoor' to 'Aafoor' and 'Athafee' to 'Athafee'. This word is so famous for garlic that there is no room to interpret it as bread, wheat, or grain, etc. The Quran should always be interpreted according to the well-known meanings of words.
The demand of the Children of Israel hinted at in this verse is mentioned in Chapter 11 of the Book of Numbers in the Torah as follows:

'The rabble with them began to crave other food, and again the Israelites started wailing and said, "If only we had meat to eat! We remember the fish we ate in Egypt at no cost—also the cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions and garlic. But now we have lost our appetite; we never see anything but this manna!"' (Numbers 11:4-6)

Qala atastabdiloonal-ladhee huwa adna bil-ladhee huwa khayr (He said, "Would you exchange what is better for what is less?"): 'Adna' is from 'Dana'at' (lowness/inferiority), meaning, do you want to exchange a superior food for an inferior and lowly food? Your Sustainer has provided this food of Manna and Quail for you, and you are receiving it in this desert in a state where you are completely free from the humiliation of slavery to the Pharaonic people and obedience to polytheism and disbelief. The dry and bland food obtained with freedom is a thousand times better than the sweet dish of slavery and humiliation. But it is your misfortune that you are such slaves to taste that, in pursuit of it, you place no value on this freedom, wherein no one's rule remains over you except God's.
In this attitude of the Children of Israel, there is a great lesson for those Muslim nations who put their blessing of freedom at risk in pursuit of the accessories and diversions of civilization, and fail to realize the disgusting evils of humiliation attached to the worldly pleasures they have thus acquired. From this passage of the Quran, the reality becomes clear that if a person's conscience is alive, he does not seek the pleasure of eating in the variety of the dining spread, but in the freedom of conscience and will. If he possesses this, even dry bread provides him with all kinds of blessings.
Ihbitu misran (Go down to [any] settlement): The original meaning of 'Habata' is to fall, and in usage, it is also used for a traveler alighting at a destination. For example, one would say 'Habatnal-wadi' (we entered the valley). From here, the idiom 'Ihbitu misran' became current, and the word 'Huboot' began to be used as a synonym for 'Nuzool' (descending/alighting). The reason for this usage is probably that when a traveler intends to stay at a place, he dismounts from his mount there.
On this specific occasion, there is also appropriateness in this word because the things the Children of Israel had demanded could only be found in a flat, low-lying, and fertile area.
'Misran' refers to a city. It cannot refer to the country of Egypt. 'Misr' has been used for the country of Egypt in several places in the Quran, but everywhere it has been used as a non-declinable (Ghayr Munsarif) noun [i.e., without Tanween]. Only in this verse has it appeared in a declinable form (Munsarif) [with Tanween: Misran]. Because of this, it necessarily comes in the general meaning of a city. However, there might be this aspect of eloquence in specifically using the word 'Misr' for a city here: through this word, they are reminded of the humiliations and sufferings they had endured in Egypt, and the intent is to hint that if you cannot live without these tasty foods, then to get them you will have to give your necks to the yoke of some 'Egypt'. Because a nation that cannot develop patience and steadfastness within itself for a high ideal cannot save itself from humiliation.
Wa duribat 'alayhimudh-dhillatu wal-maskanatu wa baa'oo bighadabim-minallah (And they were covered with humiliation and poverty and returned with anger from Allah): 'Maskanah' means helplessness, destitution, lack of courage, and wretchedness. The expression 'humiliation and lack of courage were struck upon them' reveals the reality that just as wet mud is plastered onto a wall, similarly, due to their continuous ingratitude and lack of appreciation for Divine signs, humiliation and destitution were plastered upon them. The result was that they became very soft fodder for their enemies; no resolve or courage remained within them to face situations and dangers.
Wa baa'oo bighadabim-minallah (And they returned with anger from Allah) means that the opportunities Allah had provided for them to achieve success and high rank, due to their lack of courage and unworthiness, they returned from them carrying God's curse and wrath.
Dhalika bi-annahum... Dhalika bima 'asaw wa kanoo ya'tadoon (That was because they... That was because they disobeyed and used to transgress): This states the cause of humiliation and destitution being plastered upon them, that it is not the result of a single sin of theirs, but their entire history is a series of intoxications and disobediences. Due to their nature of rebellion and transgression, they continuously denied the signs of Allah and murdered His prophets. Therefore, their assumption that they are God's favorites and beloved, and that no one can dislodge them from this status, is a completely baseless arrogance. They are, in fact, outcasts from God's court due to their own misdeeds.
Among the Prophets (peace be upon them) whose murder at the hands of the Jews is known from the Jews' own history, the first name is that of Prophet Zechariah (Zakariyya AS), who was stoned to death right inside the temple, between the sanctuary and the altar, by the order of the Judean King Joash.
After that, we find the name of Prophet John (Yahya AS), who was executed by the order of Herod, the ruler of Judea, and the king presented his head on a platter as a gift to his mistress.
Then comes the name of our master, Jesus (Isa AS), whom the Jews, according to their own assumption, crucified, although Allah saved the Holy Prophet (PBUH) from their evil.
Here, along with the mention of the killing of the Prophets (peace be upon them), the condition of 'Bighayril-Haqq' (without right/unjustly) is also attached. The purpose is to highlight the gravity of their crime. Because killing a soul is in itself the greatest crime in human society. This crime becomes even more severe if it is committed against Prophets and reformers. Then, its severity increases further if this crime is committed without any justification. Here, the Quran has gathered all these gravities in this crime of the Jews.

'Haad', 'Yahood', 'Haudan' means to return and to repent. In the Holy Quran, the prayer of Prophet Moses (AS) is recorded in these words:

'And decree for us in this world [that which is] good and [also] in the Hereafter; indeed, we have turned (hudna) back to You.' (Al-A'raf - 156)

Later, 'Haada' and 'Tahawwada' were used in the meaning of becoming a Jew, and this usage is not according to the general rule of the Arabic language, similar to how 'Tanassara' is used in the meaning of becoming a Christian.
This is the actual reality of this word, but some opponents of Islam have raised the objection that the Quran has used this word incorrectly. Their claim is that the word 'Yahood' (Jews) is not from the root 'Haud'; rather, it is attributed to Judah (Yahudha), who was the fourth son of Prophet Jacob (Yaqub AS). Due to this objection, research on this word is necessary. We insert here a relevant excerpt from the research Maulana Farahi (RA) presents on this word in his book 'Mufradat al-Quran'. Discussing the etymology of this word, Maulana (RA) writes:

'We will discuss the etymology of this word here so that it becomes clear that those who have raised this objection against the Holy Quran have neither understood the Holy Quran nor have they understood their own scriptures. When the Holy Quran used this word, it did not invent it on its own, but used a commonly used word in the Arabic language. The Arabs had been using the verb 'Haada Yahoodu' in the meaning of becoming a Jew, and when the Holy Quran used the word 'Hudna', it did not use it to explain the etymology of the word 'Yahood', but used it in its original meaning, i.e., to repent and return. However, it is certain that there is a point of eloquence in the specific use of this word. That is, it draws the attention of the Jews to a reality that they had completely forgotten. The explanation of this will follow.'

'The ignorance of their own scriptures they have demonstrated with this objection will become clear from the details we are presenting below.'

'Judah was the fourth of the twelve sons of Prophet Jacob (AS) from whom the twelve tribes of the Children of Israel emerged. In the time of Joshua, the conquered territory was divided among these people, and in this division, all the southern territory starting from Jerusalem came to the share of the descendants of Judah. Prophet David (Dawud AS) was from this tribe. During his time, the entire kingdom of the Children of Israel came under their control, which added great glory to this tribe. After him, his heir was his son, Prophet Solomon (Sulayman AS), who built the Temple in his capital. This further increased the greatness of the descendants of Judah.'

'After Prophet Solomon (AS), differences arose among them, and this entire nation split into two parts. One part was named Judah, and the other Children of Israel. The names of the remaining tribes became completely unknown after this. In later history, only two names appear: Judah and Israel. Then, when these people fell into the captivity of the Chaldeans, the word 'Yahood' (Jews) began to be used as a common name for all the Children of Israel. This shows that they did not consider any difference between Judah and Yahood.'

'In the etymology of the word Judah, the Jews have fallen into a great misunderstanding. They think this word is composed of 'Yahu' and 'Dha'. 'Yahu' means Allah and 'Dha' means 'this' (Hadha). Since names combined with 'Yahu' exist among them in this manner, they fell into this misconception, and they could not understand the text present in the Book of Genesis regarding the reason for naming Judah. The text of the Book of Genesis is as follows:

"She (Leah, wife of Jacob AS) conceived again, and when she gave birth to a son she said, 'This time I will praise the Lord.' So she named him Judah." (Genesis 29:35)

From this, the Jews understood that this word points to this event and the word 'Yahood', whereas this word points to the praise of Allah. The words bear this interpretation, and the following points support it. First, just as the meaning of the names of Prophet Jacob's (AS) sons is hinted at in connection with the mention of their birth, it is also hinted at in the place where Prophet Jacob (AS) prayed for blessings for them. For example, regarding birth, it is stated in Genesis 30:19-20:

"Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son. Then Leah said, 'God has presented me with a precious gift. This time my husband will treat me with honor, because I have borne him six sons.' So she named him Zebulun."

Then in the same book, in connection with the prayer of blessing, these words appear:

"Zebulun will live by the seashore..." (Genesis 49:13)

Reflect, on both these occasions, a hint towards the meaning of dwelling/settling is present.

Similarly, the prayer mentioned in this book regarding Judah contains these words:

"Judah, your brothers will praise you;
your hand will be on the neck of your enemies;
your father's sons will bow down to you." (Genesis 49:8)

From this, it is clear that in the naming of Judah, the concept of praise and obedience is actually intended. And the word Judah is not composed of 'Yahu' and 'Dha'; rather, it is a single word, and its root is 'Haud'.

Second, the common name used for them since their captivity by the Chaldeans is 'Yahood' and 'Yahudi'. Evidence for this is present in Ezra, Esther, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Daniel, and the Gospel, to the extent that this name became common on the tongues of the masses and the elite. If the original name was Yahudha, as these people claim, then its attribution should have been 'Yahudhi', with a 'Dhal' (ذ), not with a 'Dal' (د).

Third, only a word that is appropriate to be joined with the word 'Yahu' can be joined with it. The word 'Dha' is not such an appropriate word to be joined with it to name a creation. Because the resulting meaning, 'This is Allah', is obviously a highly detestable thing to use for a creation.

From this detail, the reality becomes clear that the Holy Quran, according to its general rule, warned the Jews here of a mistake of theirs and clarified that the word 'Yahudha' to which they attribute themselves, its actual root is 'Haud', and in this there is a subtle hint that their name demands that they 'return' to Allah.' (1)

Nasara (Christians): Regarding the word 'Nasara', Ustadh Imam Maulana Hamiduddin Farahi (RA) has stated the following research in his book 'Mufradat al-Quran':

'Nasara is the plural of Nasran, just as Nadama is the plural of Nadman. Initially, this was the name of the Nasara, and their early followers liked this name, but the later ones, contrary to their predecessors, considered it derogatory to themselves. The detail of this brevity is that the Nasara in the later period divided into two sects. One sect followed the rightful successor Simon (Peter), and he named himself Nasara. People of this group believed in the Holy Prophet (PBUH) after his advent. This is the group the Quran has praised in various places. For example, 'wa latajidannahum aqrabahum mawaddatan lil-ladheena aamanul-ladheena qaloo inna nasara' (Al-Ma'idah: 82) (and you will find the nearest of them in affection to the believers those who say, "We are Christians (Nasara)"). In this verse, it is explicit that the group praised by the Quran is the one that named itself Nasara.

Their second sect followed the innovator Paul. Present-day Christians belong to this sect. For these people, the word Nasara is a derogatory term. In their view, it is an attribution to a village that was a very insignificant village. Thus, in John chapter 1, it says:
"Philip found Nathanael and told him, 'We have found the one Moses wrote about in the Law, and about whom the prophets also wrote—Jesus of Nazareth, the son of Joseph.' 'Nazareth! Can anything good come from there?' Nathanael asked." This shows the arrogance of this group. If Nazareth is indeed the birthplace of Prophet Jesus (AS), then what aspect of contempt is there in being attributed to it? Especially since these people also claim that Nazareth is the birthplace of Prophet Jesus (AS) and that he will be called by the title Nazarene. Thus, in Matthew 2:23, it says:
"and he went and lived in a town called Nazareth. So was fulfilled what was said through the prophets, that he would be called a Nazarene."

Some opponents of the Quran have made this word an excuse for objecting to the Quran as well. Their objection is that since the Quran did not know this etymology, it assumed Nasara is derived from 'Nusrat' (help) and mentioned them from this perspective in this verse of Surah As-Saff: 'Wa idh qala 'Eesab-nu Maryama lil-hawariyyeena man ansaree ilallah qal-al-hawariyyoona nahnu ansarullah' (And [mention] when Jesus, the son of Mary, said to the disciples, "Who are my supporters for Allah?" The disciples said, "We are supporters of Allah"). In our view, this objection of these critics is based on complete ignorance of the verse's meaning. Here, the Holy Quran has not stated the etymology of Nasara, but has stated a factual event. The most that can be derived from this verse is that it contains a subtle allusion that those who are named Nasara should be supporters of the truth, because the hint of this is present within their own name. Such subtle allusions are abundantly present in the speech of the Prophets (peace be upon them). Thus, Prophet Jesus (AS) said to Simon, whose title was Cephas (Peter/Rock): "And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church." (Matthew 16:18)' (2)

Sabi'een (Sabians): Regarding the Sabians, various statements are narrated from the interpreters. According to Mujahid and Hasan, these people were not followers of any specific religion but were somewhere between Judaism and Magianism. According to these scholars, the animal slaughtered by them is forbidden (Haram). Ibn Zayd's statement is that they were followers of a specific religion and lived in the Mosul peninsula; their belief was monotheism, but neither did they follow any Prophet or Book, nor did they have any specific system of Shariah practices. Qatadah says that these people worshipped angels, prayed facing the Qiblah, and recited the Psalms (Zabur). According to Abu Al-Aliyah and Sufyan, these people were a sect from among the People of the Book.

Maulana Farahi (RA) says that these statements appear contradictory on the surface, but in reality, there is no contradiction in them. There is no doubt that initially these people were on the true religion, but later they deviated from the true religion and fell into the worship of angels and stars. This is exactly like how the descendants of Prophet Ishmael were initially on the religion of Prophet Abraham (AS), but later fell into polytheism and idol worship. This thought of Maulana Farahi (RA) is supported by the verse of the Holy Quran under discussion, because the manner in which the Quran has mentioned this group makes it completely clear that these people were initially on the true religion, and later fell into innovations and misguidance. Maulana's (RA) conjecture is that it seems the act of prayer (Salah) was very prominent among these people. Thus, due to this commonality, the polytheists used to call the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and his companions 'Sabi'een'.

Regarding the reason for their name, Maulana's (RA) thought is that since 'Saba'a' means to rise (like a star), it is possible that these people were named this due to their expertise in astronomy and knowledge of the stars.

Since followers of this religion no longer exist anywhere, nor is there any authentic history of them available, it is difficult to say anything about them with certainty, but during the time of the revelation of the Holy Quran, it seems their existence as a sect was quite well-known.

(1) For all this detail, see Maulana Farahi's (RA) research on the word 'Hadoo' in Mufradat al-Quran.
(2) For this detail, see Maulana Farahi's (RA) research on the word Nasara in Mufradat al-Quran.

'Mawthiq' and 'Meethaq' mean a covenant and pledge. The spirit of this word is trust and firmness, which is why it is specifically used for a covenant that is made for an important matter with full consciousness and a full sense of responsibility, and whose loyalty has been emphatically expressed and acknowledged. Here, it refers to the covenant taken from the Children of Israel regarding adherence to the Torah. The Divine Shariah is a treaty between God and His servants, which is why it can be termed a 'Meethaq'. In Surah Al-A'raf, this Meethaq is referenced as follows:

'Was not the covenant of the Scripture taken from them that they would not say about Allah except the truth, and they studied what was in it? And the home of the Hereafter is better for those who fear Allah, so will you not use reason? But those who hold fast to the Book and establish prayer - indeed, We will not allow to be lost the reward of the reformers. And [mention] when We raised the mountain above them as if it was a dark cloud and they were certain that it would fall upon them, [and Allah said], "Take what We have given you with determination and remember what is in it that you might fear Allah."' (Al-A'raf: 169-171)

It is explicitly stated in both the Holy Quran and the Torah that this treaty was taken from the leaders of the Children of Israel at the foot of the mountain, and at that time, by the command of Allah, a severe earthquake shook the mountain.
