If during an earthquake a person is sitting in the shadow of a high wall or at the foot of a mountain, it will seem as if the mountain or wall is hanging over his head like a canopy and is about to fall on him. The Quran has described this state as raising the Mount (Tur) over their heads.
Suspending this mountain over their heads was not to force the Children of Israel into the treaty, meaning that if they did not make this treaty, they would be crushed by this mountain. Accepting or not accepting a treaty is a matter of choice. In matters of religion, Allah the Exalted has not approved of coercion and compulsion. What happened was merely a demonstration of Allah's power and His majesty, so that the Children of Israel would remember that the God with whom they are entering into a treaty is not a weak and powerless being; rather, His power is boundless. Just as His rewards in both this world and the Hereafter are limitless in the case of abiding by the treaty, similarly, His wrath is also limitless in the case of its violation. He can suspend a massive mountain like Tur over their heads and crush them with it.
Khudhoo ma ataynakum biquwwatin wadh-kuroo ma feehi la'allakum tattaqoon (Take what We have given you with determination and remember what is in it that you might fear Allah): Meaning, take this Torah, which is a great gift from Allah to you, with full firmness and resolve, and uphold its commands and guidance with complete steadfastness and perseverance through all stages of life. Its commands are both lenient and strict, and its responsibility exists in times of ease and in times of hardship. Therefore, its right cannot be fulfilled with weak hands and loose intentions; rather, it requires strength and resolve.
Wadh-kuroo ma feehi (and remember what is in it): 'What is in it' refers to the commands and guidance, and especially those warnings and threats related to the consequences of violating this Meethaq that were conveyed to the Children of Israel. In the Torah, they were informed in great detail that if they remained firm on this covenant, they would receive God's grace from both the earth and the sky, and if they unappreciated it, its punishment in both this world and the Hereafter would be very severe.
La'allakum tattaqoon (that you might fear Allah/protect yourselves): This can also mean 'so that you may adopt Taqwa (piety)', because the primary purpose of the Divine Book is to indicate the path of Taqwa. But in light of the context and flow, we have taken it to mean saving oneself from the wrath and anger of God. Our mind has gone towards this because before this, as we mentioned, they were specifically advised to remember the warnings and threats that were narrated in the Torah regarding the consequences of violating the Divine Meethaq, and the purpose of narrating them was precisely that they remain safe from God's wrath and anger in their future lives.

'Then you turned away after that.' This address is obviously to the Children of Israel of the time of the Quran's revelation, while this act of turning away was committed by their predecessors, and these people were not the initiators in this path but the followers of their predecessors. This style of address very eloquently points to the reality that if the successors (Akhlaf) are walking exactly in the footsteps of their predecessors (Aslaf) in matters of misguidance or guidance, then their history and the history of their predecessors are essentially one and the same. The actions of the predecessors will be seamlessly attributed to the successors, and the history of the successors' misguidance will begin from the time their predecessors initiated that evil in their society.
We have translated 'mim ba'di dhalika' as 'after all that'. The reason for this is that from the usages of this style in the Arabic language and the Quran, it becomes clear that when 'Dhalika' (that) is used in this way, it makes a comprehensive reference to the entire preceding narrative. Meaning, after making the covenant to abide by the Torah, seeing the majesty of God, and becoming well-aware of all the warnings and threats, your predecessors turned their faces away from this covenant, and in this matter, you exactly followed their ways.
The phrase 'Falawla fadlullahi 'alaykum...' (And if not for the favor of Allah upon you...) hints that your deeds and actions have been such from the very beginning that Allah would have cast you away, but it is purely His grace and His mercy that He did not do so; rather, He gave you respite until today. The right of His grace and mercy is that you become grateful to Him and correct your ways, but you, conversely, are proud of these ways of yours. And afflicted with this pride, you want to waste this last opportunity as well, after which no opportunity will remain for you to reform your condition.

This is an example of the breaking of the covenant briefly mentioned in the verse above. For the Children of Israel, the day of Sabbath (Saturday) was specified for worship. On this day, working, hunting, and recreation were forbidden to them. But to free themselves from these restrictions of the Divine Shariah, they invented many legal loopholes (Hiyal). So much so that they opened many ways even for hunting and recreation, etc. This verse points to these kinds of actions of theirs, and since these things were well-known among them, the Quran pointed to it as a known and recognized fact.
Faqulna lahum koonoo qiradatan khasi'een (So We said to them, "Be apes, despised"), is a sentence of curse and reproach. Meaning, as retribution for this crime, Allah cursed those people who, in following their selfish desires, ruined the sanctity of the Sabbath.
Interpreters have differed on whether, as a result of this curse, their physical appearance also became like that of monkeys, or if this metamorphosis (Maskh) was only intellectual and spiritual. But in our view, this difference is not a very significant one. The difference in form and appearance between a human and a monkey is not very large. The main difference lies in intellect and will. The main characteristic of a human is that before fulfilling any desire of his ego, he first sees whether fulfilling this desire is permissible or not, and if it is permissible, what are the legal and moral limits and restrictions for it? In contrast, there is no barrier of moral limits and restrictions between a monkey's desire and its action; whatever its ego desires, it immediately acts upon it. If this becomes the condition of any human or human group in following the desires of their ego, then no meaningful difference remains between them and a monkey. Only a slight physical difference remains, which only lasts until intellectual and moral decline reaches its ultimate limit. When this decline reaches its ultimate limit, this slight physical difference also eventually vanishes.

'Nakal' means an exemplary punishment or a warning example. Here, the reference is to the settlement whose people committed those unlawful audacities to ruin the sanctity of the Sabbath, which were pointed out in the verse above. In the Holy Quran, pronouns have been used for settlements and places in this manner in more than one instance. The meaning is that as a result of Allah's curse, this settlement was made a warning example for the settlements before it, behind it, and around it, seeing which those possessing intellect and the fear of God could take heed.
From the Quran, it is known that this settlement was on the coast of the sea. From this, it can be surmised that the people of this settlement had made great progress in trade and civilization, but as retribution for this curse, such a decline fell upon them that their outward and inward states were completely disfigured, and they became a cautionary tale for surrounding settlements and future generations.

This is the second example of the Jews breaking their covenant, and to describe this example, the Quran has adopted the method of dividing a single matter into two parts. One part clarifies how the mindset of the Children of Israel had been evasive and escapist from the very beginning in the matter of accepting the Divine Shariah. The second part makes the reality clear that even when they did accept something after a thousand excuses and arguments, they did not execute it correctly, but sought ways to evade that command.
To clarify this, the Quran has selected an incident of 'Qasamah' (oath-taking over a murder) from the history of the Children of Israel. From the hints in the Holy Quran, the situation of the incident appears to be that a person was murdered among the Children of Israel, and his killers could not be traced. Prophet Moses (AS), according to the law of his Shariah, commanded the people of the area where the murder took place to sacrifice a cow and swear oaths over it. Firstly, these people hesitated and argued in accepting this command—what kind of cow should it be, what should its color be, how old should it be, etc., etc. But even when they somehow slaughtered the cow with great difficulty, it seems they swore a false oath.
The fact that the method of Qasamah existed in the Mosaic Shariah is proven by the following verses from the Book of Deuteronomy:

'If a man is found slain, lying in a field in the land the Lord your God is giving you to possess, and it is not known who killed him, your elders and judges shall go out and measure the distance from the body to the neighboring towns. Then the elders of the town nearest the body shall take a heifer that has never been worked and has never worn a yoke and lead it down to a valley that has not been plowed or planted and where there is a flowing stream. There in the valley they are to break the heifer's neck. The Levitical priests shall step forward, for the Lord your God has chosen them to minister and to pronounce blessings in the name of the Lord and to decide all cases of dispute and assault. Then all the elders of the town nearest the body shall wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken in the valley, and they shall declare: "Our hands did not shed this blood, nor did our eyes see it done."' (Deuteronomy 21:1-7)

Qaloo atattakhidhuna huzuwa (They said, "Do you take us in ridicule?"): The Children of Israel considered this command of Prophet Moses (AS) a joke. They could not understand how this measure could be an effective way to trace the killer. However, where all paths to finding a clue are closed, if there could be any final measure, it was to gather the prominent people around the place of the murder and take oaths from them. And to give the oath the utmost color of respect and sanctity, this oath should be taken over a sacrificed animal. In the matter of treaties and oaths, it has been a custom since ancient times that they were generally performed in front of the deity/place of worship so that the parties would refrain from lying and hypocrisy. In some situations, this method was also adopted where the blood of the sacrificed animal was sprinkled on those taking the oath. It is possible that this form was also adopted in the case of Qasamah among the Children of Israel, although there is no hint of it in the reference above. Maulana Farahi (RA) has mentioned some forms of this type of oath in his book 'Im'an fi Aqsam al-Quran'.
Qala a'oodhu billahi an akoona minal-jahileen (He said, "I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant"): The word 'Jahl' (ignorance/foolishness) comes as the opposite of 'Ilm' (knowledge) as well as the opposite of 'Hilm' (forbearance/seriousness). Here it is the opposite of Hilm. The meaning is: I seek God's refuge from saying anything as a joke or mockery in the matter of Allah's religion. Whatever I am saying is God's command, and in it lies goodness and blessing for you. I have not fabricated this on my own.

The question the Children of Israel asked after the command to sacrifice the cow was merely born of their corrupt disposition. In reality, this question did not arise. If there had been soundness in their disposition, they could have slaughtered any average cow and complied with Allah's command. Since Allah knew the mindset hidden behind this question, He gave them the answer that was sufficient to remove their doubt—that is, the cow should be of medium age—but along with it, He also commanded them to execute the given order without further ado. Do not seek ways to evade the Shariah by asking such questions, nor narrow the vastness of the religion for yourselves.

Among the colors of a cow, golden and yellow are the most appealing colors. Arab poets, due to this appeal, also use this attribute for their beloved. The word 'Faqa'' is used for the depth and brightness of this very color.
Although the previous question was also unnecessary, once an age limit was set, there was absolutely no room left for any question regarding the cow. But after that, they asked a question regarding the color. In response, Allah specified a cow of the color that is considered the most beautifully colored and desirable. Obviously, in response to the question, the directive had to be for the most desirable color. But it is through such questions that the Children of Israel deprived themselves of the expansiveness and concessions of the Divine Shariah, turning it into a collection of burdens and shackles.

Even after the clarification of the color, the questioners were not satisfied. When they sought further clarification, the instruction came that the cow should not be trained; it should not have been used for plowing the fields or watering them. Furthermore, the instruction was that it must be of a solid color, without the mixture of any other color.
In this way, after having various restrictions and conditions piled upon themselves, they said, "Yes, now the matter is completely clear."
The word 'Haqq' (truth/reality) has been used in several meanings in the Holy Quran:
For something whose occurrence is absolute. The Day of Judgment is called 'Haqq' in this sense.
For something that is morally obligatory. Justice is called 'Haqq' in this respect.
For something that holds the status of a decisive word between dispute and disagreement. Calling the Holy Quran 'Haqq' also has this aspect.
This word is also used in the Holy Quran for the concept of ultimate end and purpose. The creation of the heavens and the earth is called 'Bil-Haqq' in this sense.
Something that is completely clear and evident in its manifestation is also called 'Haqq'.
In the verse under discussion, the word 'Haqq' is used in this last meaning.
With this final question, the words 'Wa inna in sha'allahu lamuhtadoon' (And indeed we, if Allah wills, will be guided [to it]) escaped their tongues. These words reflect their inner state. It shows that after successive questions, the absurdity of their own questions had become clear even to themselves. Thus, perhaps it was the blessing of this realization that this phrase escaped their lips, and by the blessing of this phrase, they were granted the success (Tawfeeq) to comply with this command. Otherwise, given the mindset they were demonstrating, there was no expectation that they would ever comply with this command.

Even after the clarification of the color, the questioners were not satisfied. When they sought further clarification, the instruction came that the cow should not be trained; it should not have been used for plowing the fields or watering them. Furthermore, the instruction was that it must be of a solid color, without the mixture of any other color.
In this way, after having various restrictions and conditions piled upon themselves, they said, "Yes, now the matter is completely clear."
The word 'Haqq' (truth/reality) has been used in several meanings in the Holy Quran:
For something whose occurrence is absolute. The Day of Judgment is called 'Haqq' in this sense.
For something that is morally obligatory. Justice is called 'Haqq' in this respect.
For something that holds the status of a decisive word between dispute and disagreement. Calling the Holy Quran 'Haqq' also has this aspect.
This word is also used in the Holy Quran for the concept of ultimate end and purpose. The creation of the heavens and the earth is called 'Bil-Haqq' in this sense.
Something that is completely clear and evident in its manifestation is also called 'Haqq'.
In the verse under discussion, the word 'Haqq' is used in this last meaning.

'Dar'un' means to repel and to throw. From this is 'Tadara'tum', which by the rule of assimilation (Idgham) has become 'Iddara'tum'. This means to blame one another.
Wallahu mukhrijun ma kuntum taktumoon (And Allah would bring out that which you were concealing): Here it is as a parenthetical sentence (Jumla Mu'tarida). The segment following it, 'Faqulnad-riboohu biba'diha' (So We said, "Strike the slain man with part of it"), is connected to 'faddara'tum feeha'. The meaning of this parenthetical sentence is that by murdering someone, you might try to hide it in the world by playing the blame game with one another, but remember that if you hide something in the world, it will not remain hidden forever. Rather, one day Allah will surely expose all that you are hiding.
This states the actual purpose of the command to sacrifice the cow from 'Wa idh qataltum' (And [remember] when you slew). It was clarified above what the mindset of the Children of Israel—who sit today as the sole monopolists of the Divine Shariah—was doing when it came to accepting this Shariah. How they kept making various kinds of excuses at every step in accepting it. Now, in the portion onwards from 'Wa idh qataltum nafsan', it is being clarified for what purpose this command to sacrifice the cow was given, and what attitude they adopted in this matter.

'Strike him with a part of it'. Generally, the interpreters have taken this to mean that the murdered person should be touched with a piece of the cow's meat, which will bring him back to life, and he will name his killer. Although there is no harm in taking this meaning—nothing is far-fetched for Allah's perfect power—in connection with 'Qasamah', I sometimes think that this might point towards taking an oath. Meaning, sprinkle the blood of the sacrificed cow on the murdered person and take an oath from those nearby. Instead of detailing the incident, just a hint to it might have been considered sufficient because the purpose here is not to narrate the incident, but merely to remind the Children of Israel of an incident from their history.
If this meaning is taken, the segment 'kadhalika yuhyillahul-mawta' (Thus does Allah bring the dead to life) would be an answer to what the Children of Israel said upon hearing the command to sacrifice the cow: 'Atattakhidhuna huzuwa' (Do you take us in ridicule?). Meaning, to you this command is a joke, but if you act upon it with its true spirit and show honesty in this sacrifice and oath, this is the way to trace the killers and exact retribution (Qisas) from them, in which there is life for everyone.
The fact that there is life for everyone in Qisas is clearly stated in the Holy Quran:

'And there is for you in legal retribution [saving of] life, O you [people] of understanding...' (Al-Baqarah - 179)

In the Torah as well, the murder of one was considered the murder of all, and the retribution for one was considered life for all. In the Holy Quran, this command is referenced as follows:

'Because of that, We decreed upon the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land - it is as if he had slain mankind entirely. And whoever saves one - it is as if he had saved mankind entirely.' (Al-Ma'idah - 32)

From this verse, it is clear that this philosophy of the law of Qisas was so evident to the Children of Israel that not exacting Qisas is death for all, and exacting Qisas is life for all.
In this case, 'wa yureekum ayatihi' (and He shows you His signs) would be connected to the parenthetical sentence. Meaning, Allah is showing you signs that whatever you hide, none of it will remain covered and hidden; rather, everything will be exposed. This would be a hint towards those things which the Jews tried to hide and for which they made various kinds of interpolations in the religion, but now they were being exposed one by one through the Quran and the Holy Prophet (PBUH). This was an open sign that attempting to hide anything from God is a futile effort; one day He will lift the veil from all secrets.

'Then your hearts became hardened after that'. Here, the use of 'Thumma' (Then) suggests that the result of such stubborn arguments and evasion on your part in matters of religion was that your hearts became hard. Although it is not explicit here, the context is proof that just as the Children of Israel created many arguments in complying with the command to slaughter the cow, similarly, even after its slaughter, they did not maintain the proper respect for this sacrifice, but tried to hide the killer by swearing false oaths. When evasion, stubborn argumentation, and furthermore, audacity and audacity are added to a crime, the hearts of such criminals, according to the law of God, become hard like stone, after which the capacity for righteousness and piety to grow within them is completely extinguished.
Fahiya kal-hijarati aw ashaddu qaswah (So they were like stones, or even harder). This style of speech is similar to what is stated elsewhere:

'Those are like livestock; rather, they are more astray.' (Al-A'raf - 179)

This is not merely a stylistic exaggeration but an absolute expression of reality. The hardness in inanimate objects, etc., does not deprive them of the capabilities endowed in them by nature. In contrast, if a human corrupts himself, his corruption gradually, according to Divine law, deprives him of all those capabilities endowed to him by nature. A stone, even if it gets harder and harder, remains a stone. If nature has placed the capability of springs of water flowing through its veins, this thing remains within it despite its hardness. Conversely, if a human's heart becomes hard due to some moral disease, all the springs of his heart completely dry up. This is why nothing in the world can match the corruption of a corrupted human, no matter how corrupted that thing might be.
The statement here that 'there are some stones from which rivers burst forth, and there are some of them that split open and water comes out, and there are some of them that fall down for fear of Allah'—these are hints towards those very innate capabilities of stones that nature has endowed them with, and which remain in any case.
It should be kept in mind here that this is not merely some poetic style of expression with no connection to the world of events; rather, it is an allusion to those observations that had passed right before the eyes of the Children of Israel in their desert life. They had seen with their own eyes twelve springs bursting forth simultaneously from a rock, and a part of Mount Tur shattering from the Divine manifestation. But despite seeing all this, the hardness of their hearts was such that they did not soften after seeing any sign. Then what room is left to doubt that the hardness of their hearts exceeded even the hardness of stones and rocks?
Wa mallahu bighafilin 'amma ta'maloon (And Allah is not unaware of what you do). Meaning, one should spin tales of their honor and sanctity, their greatness and nobility, and their innocence only to someone whose deeds are hidden, and before someone who is unaware and ignorant. What is the point of such claims and arrogance before the One who is aware of everything!

In the middle of the address to the Jews, this is a transition (Iltifat) towards the Muslims, similar to the transition towards the Holy Prophet (PBUH) in verses 6-7, and towards the Children of Ishmael in verses 21-29. This transition has two purposes. First, to reassure the Muslims so they do not become disheartened by the opposition of the Children of Israel, nor be surprised that these educated people and scholars of religion and Shariah are opposing this call. People whose minds are so crooked that even after hearing a command from the tongue of their Prophet and clearly understanding its intent, they continued to create crookedness in it and kept twisting it in a direction completely contrary to its intent—as you heard in the matter of the command to sacrifice the cow—do you expect from such crooked-minded people or their followers that they will try to understand your straightforward words in a straightforward manner?
Second, to inform the Muslims about some of the behind-the-scenes actions of these Jews, so that the naive Muslims who were deceived by their fraudulent claims of faith and began to hold a good opinion of them, or were desirous of increasing ties with them, would be warned that all of this is deceit; there is not a trace of truth in it.
Yasma'oona kalamallahi thumma yuharrifoonahu mim ba'di ma 'aqaloohu wa hum ya'lamoon (They hear the words of Allah, then they distort it after they had understood it while they were knowing): 'Harrafash-shay'a 'an wajhihi' means to turn something away from its correct orientation towards another direction. From this comes 'Harrafal-qawla' or 'Harrafal-kalama', which means altering a statement or speech. This alteration can take several forms. For example:
Intentionally interpreting a statement in a way completely contrary to the speaker's intent.
Making such a change in the pronunciation and recitation of a word that makes the word something entirely different. For example, distorting 'Marwah' to 'Morah' or 'Mariya' etc.
Adding or removing something from a text or speech in a way that completely messes up the original intent. For example, the Jews altered the incident of Prophet Abraham's (AS) migration in such a way that his connection with the Kaaba could not be proven.
Providing a translation of an ambiguous word that is completely contrary to the context. For example, the Hebrew word 'Ibn' was translated as 'son', whereas it also means 'servant' and 'slave'.
A statement's meaning might be completely clear, but raising such questions about it that make this clear statement ambiguous or throw it in a completely different direction.
The People of the Book committed all these types of interpolations (Tahreef), and the Quran holds them guilty of all of them. God willing, details of each will appear in this Book with necessary arguments at their appropriate places. Here, one should only briefly keep its various forms in mind, and the fact that the term 'Tahreef' is applied to Tahreef only when it is done intentionally and knowingly. The Holy Quran has attached the condition 'mim ba'di ma 'aqaloohu wa hum ya'lamoon' (after they had understood it while they were knowing). It is this knowledge and consciousness that actually makes Tahreef a grave crime, and as punishment for this crime, the perpetrators of this crime are completely deprived of the light of knowledge bestowed by Allah.

'And when they meet those who believe, they say, "We have believed"'. Meaning, Muslims are not the sole monopolists of religion and faith; we also have faith. By this statement, their intent, as we have explained while interpreting verses 8-9, was merely to deceive the Muslims. They deceived the Muslims with the apparent words of this statement so that the Muslims would start trusting them. In their own minds, they took it to mean that they do believe in their Prophets and their scriptures anyway—what else is faith called? The Quran has warned the Muslims here not to fall into the trap of these people's deceptive attacks and pin good hopes on them, because there is a vast difference between what they say in private and in public. Vis-a-vis, they claim 'Aamanna' (We believe), but when they are in their exclusive gatherings, they severely hold each other to account. If, in the fervor of showing tolerance, any such thing that is in favor of Islam escapes the tongue of one of them by mistake in front of you, they strictly catch it in their gatherings, saying: 'Do you disclose to the Muslims those things regarding the Final Prophet (PBUH) and Islam which God has disclosed only to you through His scriptures? And you do not realize that the Muslims can present these very statements of yours as testimony and argument against you on the Day of Judgment.' (1)

(1) At this point, the Tafseer of verses 8-14 of this Surah in Tadabbur-e-Quran should be read. The same theme has been expressed in both places with a slight variation.

Generally, this sentence is universal: Allah knows every manifest and hidden thing. But here the context of the speech points very subtly to the fact that these people, while playing tricks with the Muslims, do not consider that God is also well aware of the reality of this 'Aamanna' (We believe) which they show. And He also knows very well the reprimands and reproaches they give to each other in their exclusive gatherings for revealing secrets to the Muslims. The meaning is that these foolish people can hide themselves under the cloak of outward appearances in front of the Muslims, but what trick have they devised to hide themselves from that God who is present everywhere, in their solitude and in their public life, and to whom everything apparent and hidden is clear!

'Ummiyoon' is the plural of 'Ummi', which means someone unfamiliar with writing, reading, and formal education. Here it refers to the unlettered common masses of the Jews.
Mentioning them separately implies here that the group mentioned in the preceding verses with the words 'Fareequm-minhum' (A party of them) refers to the educated and clever people among the Jews. The actions described of them can also only be of the clever and educated. Obviously, distorting the words of Allah and trying to deceive the Muslims cannot be the work of the ignorant masses. It can only be the work of the cunning and the clever. Therefore, after mentioning them and making the Muslims despair of them, now the common masses of the Jews are mentioned. It is clarified that Muslims should not expect the acceptance of truth from them either, because just as the first group is involved in mischief and trickery, this second group is similarly involved in false hopes and illusions.
Their disease is described as 'La ya'lamoonal-kitaba illa amaniyy' (They do not know the Scripture except [as] wishful thinking). 'Amaniyy' is the plural of 'Umniyyah', which means hope, desire, and wish. It means that these people are completely unaware of the actual reality of their Book—what it teaches them and what it does not teach. There are just some desires and wishes in their minds which, although completely baseless and unreal, have become deeply ingrained in them due to the wrong teachings of their scholars. They consider their Book to be a collection of these very desires of theirs. In their view, their Book does not impose any responsibility upon them, but was revealed solely to grant a certificate of validation to their desires. The Holy Quran has referenced some of these kinds of desires of theirs. For example:

'And they say, "Never will the Fire touch us, except for a few days."' (Al-Baqarah - 80)

'And they say, "None will enter Paradise except one who is a Jew or a Christian." That is [merely] their wishful thinking.' (Al-Baqarah - 111)

'Say, [O Muhammad], "If the home of the Hereafter with Allah is for you alone and not the [other] people, then wish for death, if you should be truthful."' (Al-Baqarah - 94)

'But the Jews and the Christians say, "We are the children of Allah and His beloved."' (Al-Ma'idah - 18)

These are just a few mentioned as examples of their desires. Obviously, people involved in such illusions and dreaming such delicious dreams—upon whom there is no burden of responsibility, save the performance of a few rituals, and for whom all rights are secured with God—how could they become believers in this Quran, which wanted to wake them up from these delicious dreams and stand them before the realities of life and its actual responsibilities.
'Wa in hum illa yazunnoon' (And they do not do except guess) means that all these desires of theirs are merely the product of their own and their scholars' minds; they have not even a remote connection to the actual reality.

While explaining the word 'Kitab' at the beginning of this Surah, we mentioned that this word is used in the Quran for the commands and laws of the Shariah, among other meanings. Here, it refers to those fatwas (religious edicts) and rulings which the Jewish scholars used to issue without any Shariah basis, purely for their worldly interests and to keep the masses happy, claiming that this is the command of Allah and His Messenger.
'Writing with their own hands' means that there was no foundation or authority for these fatwas within the Divine Book; they were merely their own freewheeling and fabricated fatwas, yet they attributed them to God and His Shariah. It was because of such fatwas that their common people became heedless of the true responsibilities of the Shariah and fell into the illusions hinted at in the preceding verses. Through this very route, things that had nothing to do with religion were mixed into their religion.
Liyashtaroo bihi thamanan qaleelan (in order to exchange it for a small price): 'Small' because they committed this act of selling religion purely for their worldly interests, and even the greatest worldly benefit is ultimately small if it is acquired by selling religion.
Fawaylul-lahum mimma katabat aydeehim wa waylul-lahum mimma yaksiboon (So woe to them for what their hands have written and woe to them for what they earn): Woe to them because of what their hands have written, and also because of what they earn in exchange for it. Meaning, in the Hereafter, both these things will separately become causes of ruin and destruction for them. Their inventing Shariah from their own minds is a cause of destruction, and acquiring worldly benefits in exchange for it is also a cause of destruction!

'And they say, "Never will the Fire touch us, except for a few days."' This is an example of the false hopes that were referenced above. The Jews did not believe in eternal punishment in Hell for themselves under any circumstances. Instead of considering Paradise and Hell as the result of deeds and based on deeds, they assumed that since they are God's chosen community, regardless of what their deeds might be, firstly, they would not be sent to Hell at all; and if they were sent, they would serve a minor sentence and then be returned to Paradise. This illusion of theirs made both their masses and their elite completely indifferent to the responsibilities of the Shariah. In the matter of salvation, all their reliance rested on their group affiliation rather than on deeds and beliefs. And unfortunately, we Muslims are also involved in a somewhat similar misunderstanding.
Am taqooloona 'alallahi ma la ta'lamoon (Or do you say about Allah that which you do not know?): 'That which you do not know', meaning for which there is no evidence in your Book; you have just fabricated a thing on your own and attributed it to your God, even though a covenant was taken from you that you would not attribute anything to God except the truth.

'Was not the covenant of the Scripture taken from them that they would not say about Allah except the truth...' (Al-A'raf: 169)

This is a refutation of the Jews' illusion mentioned above. Meaning, Paradise and Hell are not connected to familial or group affiliations, but entirely to deeds. A person who commits an evil deed and that evil completely surrounds him, for him is eternity in the Fire, regardless of which group he belongs to. In contrast, a person who remains firm on the path of faith and righteous deeds, for him is eternity in Paradise, regardless of which family he belongs to.
Just as at the end of the first sequence of discourse in this Surah, the verse 'Innal-ladheena aamanoo wal-ladheena hadoo...' (Indeed, those who believed and those who were Jews...) appeared, similarly at the end of this second sequence of discourse, this verse 'Bala man kasaba...' (Yes, whoever earns...) has appeared. The placement and purpose of both these verses are completely identical; therefore, an effort should be made to understand them in light of each other. Further explanation of this will follow at an appropriate place.

This is a reference to the initial covenant taken from the Children of Israel regarding avoiding Shirk, treating parents well, fulfilling the rights of relatives, orphans, and the needy, and establishing prayer and Zakah. In it, 'La ta'budoona illallah' (Do not worship except Allah) is mentioned first. Meaning, you shall not worship anyone other than Allah. Although this sentence is apparently in the form of a declarative statement (Khabar), in meaning it is an imperative prohibition (Nahi). Because of this, conjoining the subsequent imperative sentences to it is appropriate.
Wa bil-walidayni ihsanan (And to parents do good): After stating the right of Allah, immediately mentioning the right of parents is proof that after God, if there is any greatest right upon a human, it is only that of the parents and no one else's. However, this right only demands 'Ihsan' (good treatment), not worship. From this, it clearly emerges that when Allah the Exalted did not permit associating parents in worship—whose rank is the highest after God—then what to say of others.
Wa dhil-qurba (And to relatives): This can also be placed under 'Ihsanan' (good treatment) mentioned for the parents, or another appropriate omitted verb can be assumed for it. Precedents for both these forms exist in the Holy Quran. For example, in one place it is stated:

'Worship Allah and associate nothing with Him, and to parents do good, and to relatives, orphans, the needy...' (An-Nisa - 36)

In this verse, 'Wa dhil-qurba' is placed under 'Ihsanan'. But in another place, it is stated:

'And your Lord has decreed that you not worship except Him, and to parents, good treatment... And give the relative his right, and [also] the poor and the traveler...' (Al-Isra - 23, 26)

Here, separate verbs are used: 'Ihsanan' (good treatment) for parents, and 'Aati' (give the right) for the relative, the poor, and the traveler. Combining both these instances brings out the point that 'Ihsan' is, in reality, the name for fulfilling rights. If rights are not fulfilled, the obligation of 'Ihsan' cannot be fulfilled by mere empty words.
Wal-yatama wal-masakeen (And orphans and the needy): In the sequence of rights, the mention of parents, then relatives, and immediately after them orphans and the needy, highlights the importance orphans and the needy hold in Islamic society and the Islamic system. In the Islamic system, upon every capable person, after the rights of his parents and relatives, are the rights of the orphans and the needy. Without fulfilling them, no person can absolve himself of the responsibilities imposed by Islam.
We have not used the word 'rights' for these entitlements on our own merely as a metaphor; rather, the Holy Quran itself has used this word, and in the Islamic system, their protection is ensured specifically as rights.
Wa qooloo lin-nasi husnan (And speak to people good [words]): The first is the general meaning derived from its apparent words. From this perspective, every word of goodness, nobility, advice, and counsel will fall under it, the teaching and preaching of which Muslims have been instructed to do on every occasion. In our view, there is no harm in keeping it general. Our interpreters have indeed kept it general. However, this exact statement, with slight word variations, has also been made in other places in the Holy Quran within this very context. Gathering all those verses reveals that in the context where these words appear here, they also have a specific meaning of which a student of the Quran should not remain unaware. We will gather all the synonymous verses here to try and clarify this specific meaning.
In Surah An-Nisa, while clarifying some responsibilities of the guardians regarding orphans, it is stated:

'And do not give the weak-minded your property, which Allah has made a means of sustenance for you, but provide for them with it and clothe them and speak to them words of appropriate kindness.' (An-Nisa - 5)

In this same Surah An-Nisa, in another place, it is stated:

'And when [other] relatives and orphans and the needy are present at the [time of] division, then provide for them [something] out of the estate and speak to them words of appropriate kindness. And let those [executors and guardians] fear [injustice] as if they [themselves] had left weak offspring behind and feared for them. So let them fear Allah and speak words of appropriate justice.' (An-Nisa - 8-9)

Further in Surah Al-Baqarah, it is:

'Those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah and then do not follow up what they have spent with reminders [of it] or [other] injury will have their reward with their Lord, and there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve. Kind speech and forgiveness are better than charity followed by injury. And Allah is Free of need and Forbearing.' (Al-Baqarah - 262-263)

Specifically regarding spending (Infaq), it is stated in Surah Al-Isra:

'And if you [must] turn away from the needy awaiting mercy from your Lord which you expect, then speak to them a gentle word.' (Al-Isra - 28)

Reflecting upon all these verses reveals that the phrase 'wa qooloo lin-nasi husnan' here says the same thing regarding orphans, the needy, and travelers that is said in the verses above sometimes with the words 'wa qooloo lahum qawlan ma'roofan' (and speak to them words of appropriate kindness), sometimes with 'wal-yaqooloo qawlan sadeedan' (and let them speak words of appropriate justice), and 'qawlun ma'roofun wa maghfiratun' (Kind speech and forgiveness), etc.
On one hand, the Holy Quran has emphasized good treatment and fulfillment of rights regarding parents, relatives, orphans, and the needy. On the other hand, it has instructed that one should speak to them in a noble and dignified manner. If there is anger in the heart against them, it should be controlled, and their mistakes and shortcomings should be overlooked. The reason for this is that without this character, no person can properly fulfill their rights and duties. Often, a person falls short in respecting their dignity due to their weak condition, which further wounds their already injured hearts. Sometimes a person holds a grudge against them, which manifests in a harsher tone when finding them helpless and dependent. In some situations, the attitude of these needy people themselves takes on an unpleasant form, and this too becomes a cause for harsh words from a person. The Quran has forbidden all these things and instructed speaking to them in a good manner, declaring a word of comfort and consolation to be better than that charity which is accompanied by harsh words, insult, and injury. This is what is expressed here with the words 'wa qooloo lin-nasi husnan'. Although the words are general, the context of the speech and the structure are proof that the meaning is this.
Wa aqeemus-salata wa atuz-zakata (And establish prayer and give Zakah): The mention of establishing prayer and giving Zakah here is in the nature of summarizing after detailing. Meaning, these two things encompass all the aforementioned points. The instructions given above to worship Allah alone, and to treat relatives, the needy, and orphans well—all these virtues are bound together by establishing prayer and giving Zakah. Therefore, after mentioning the parts, these fundamental things are also mentioned. This automatically makes it clear that if you establish prayer and keep giving Zakah, it will be easy for you to perform the aforementioned virtues; but if you ruin prayer and Zakah, then you will ruin everything.
Thumma tawallaytum illa qaleelam-minkum wa antum mu'ridoon (Then you turned away, except a few of you, and you were refusing): Then you turned your backs, except for a few among you, and you are a people given to turning away. This is the point for which the Meethaq (covenant) was recalled above to clarify. Meaning, this covenant which you tied with such solemnity, you completely broke it to pieces. Only a few among you emerged who could remain firm on it.
The Holy Quran has expressed their breaking of the covenant here first in the form of a verb (tawallaytum - you turned away), and then by saying 'wa antum mu'ridoon' (and you were refusing), it has mentioned it as their permanent attribute. This is to clarify that this is not a crime committed by them accidentally; rather, this aversion and deviation has become a characteristic of their national temperament. The temperamental characteristic of theirs that the Holy Quran points to was also mentioned by Prophet Moses (AS). Repeatedly rebuking the Children of Israel for their disobediences, he said that you are a highly rebellious and stiff-necked people.
Here, this reality of the discourse structure should not be lost from mind: the Children of Israel are being reminded of this breaking of the covenant to strike a blow against their arrogance that they considered themselves to be the sole custodians of the Divine Book, the bearers of God's Shariah, and the exclusive monopolists of all worldly and otherworldly blessings of Allah. Because of this, neither did they believe in the need for a new Prophethood and Messengership, nor were they ready to believe in any Prophethood and Messengership outside their circle. These people are being reminded in this verse, and in the verses following it, how they shredded the covenant and pledge Allah took from them, of which they are so proud.

This is pointing to another covenant taken from the Children of Israel. This covenant was that they would neither shed each other's blood nor attempt to exile their brothers from their homes. But, as the details follow, they ruthlessly trampled upon this covenant as well.
To highlight the importance of this covenant, it is stated: 'Thumma aqrartum wa antum tashhadoon' (Then you acknowledged [it], and you are witnesses). This can have two meanings, and both clarify its importance and the heinousness of its violation. One meaning, as generally taken by commentators, is: you acknowledge this covenant and you are witnesses to it even today, because its mention exists in the Torah. The second meaning could be: you acknowledged this covenant, and you were present with Prophet Moses (AS) at the time of this acknowledgment.
This reality should be kept in mind here that Prophet Moses (AS) always informed the Children of Israel of Allah's commands in front of the entire assembly, and then took a pledge of obedience and adherence to these commands from the entire assembly. This was so that through this collective acknowledgment, the sense of its adherence would gain full importance among the people, and generation after generation, the tradition would remain alive among them that 'we acknowledged this covenant as a collective body at such-and-such place'. Here, the Quran has reminded the Children of Israel of its revelation period: the ancestors whose traditions you take pride in, when the acknowledgment of their entire assembly exists in your Book, how can you deny its responsibility?

Meaning, the covenant you tied with such solemnity, now you yourselves are breaking it with such zeal. The Holy Quran has described the form of this breaking here: you conspire against your own brothers with their enemies, and then becoming their helpers, you get your brothers exiled from their settlements. In this way, after having them humiliated and disgraced, when they come to you as captives in the hands of enemies, you pay ransom to free them to display your love for the community and nationalism, saying that this is the command of the Torah. However, just as there is a command in the Torah to free them by paying ransom, there is also the prohibition against exiling your brothers from their settlements.
Studying the history of the Children of Israel reveals that after the kingdoms of Judah and Israel were established separately, such events occurred frequently among the Children of Israel. Rivalries existed between the two kingdoms, and the easy recipe for taking revenge on one another was to incite opposing forces and launch attacks on the rival through them. And when, after massacre and plunder, they were captured by enemies and sought help, they would free them to exert the influence of national sympathy and well-wishing on the masses.
The conditions of the Jews settled in Arabia during the time of the Quran's revelation were similar. Their various branches had established alliances with different branches of the Ansar. For example, Banu Qaynuqa and Banu Nadir were allies of the Khazraj, and Banu Qurayza were allies of the Aws. Tribal wars constantly raged between the Aws and Khazraj. In these wars, the Jews would also participate alongside their respective allies, becoming the cause of the murder of their own brothers and their exile. But alongside this fratricide, to show off their religiosity, they would also do this: when their religious brothers were captured by enemies, they would pay ransom to free them, saying it is the command of the Torah. (1)
On one hand, this opposition to God's religion, and on the other hand, this display of religiosity is blatant hypocrisy. Its clear meaning is that whatever part of the Divine Book aligns with one's desires should be accepted, and whatever goes against one's desires should be rejected. Such an arbitrary faith is not acceptable to Allah. Those who adopt this attitude in the matter of Divine Shariah, Allah disgraces them in the world, and such people will also be deserving of severe punishment in the Hereafter.

(1) While reciting these verses, one should also keep an eye on the conspiracies that Muslim governments and Muslim groups continue to hatch against each other. In this matter, they go to such an extent that they feel no shame even in colluding with the enemies of Islam and Muslims.

Here, 'Ishtira' does not mean buying or selling, but merely giving preference. When a person buys a thing by paying a price, he gives it preference over the price. Lisan al-Arab has quoted Abu Ishaq's statement regarding the verse 'Ula'ikal-ladheenash-tarawud-dalalata' (Those are the ones who have purchased error), stating: 'Here buying and selling are not meant; rather, the intent is merely to express that just as a buyer gives his wealth to acquire his desired and coveted item, similarly, these disbelievers have grasped misguidance as their coveted and beloved item.' Arabs, on every occasion when one thing is abandoned and another is chosen, will say 'Ishtarahu' (he bought it), meaning he preferred it. The Holy Quran has used this word in this meaning in more than one place.
La yukhaffafu 'anhumul-'adhabu wa la hum yunsaroon (The punishment will not be lightened for them, nor will they be aided): Neither will their punishment be lightened, nor will any help reach them. Meaning, neither will any leniency be shown to them from within, nor will they be able to get any help from outside. After getting caught in this eternal punishment of Allah, all doors of hope will be closed for them.

This points to the arrangement Allah made to continuously remind them of the aforementioned covenant, that Allah sent Prophet Moses (AS) with 'Bayyinaat' (clear signs). 'Bayyinaat' refers to the miracles given to Prophet Jesus (AS), which were so clear that only a stubborn person could doubt their being from God. But the Jews, instead of acknowledging these clear miracles as the result of Divine support and the grace of the Holy Spirit (Ruh al-Qudus), attributed them—God forbid—to satanic manipulation. Their claim was that Prophet Jesus (AS) performed these miracles with the help of Beelzebul, the prince of demons. Refuting this very accusation of the Jews, the Holy Quran repeatedly says about Prophet Jesus (AS): 'Ayyadnahu biroohil-qudus' (We supported him with the Holy Spirit). Meaning, the miracles that emanated from him are the result of the support of the Holy Spirit, not the help of any devil or jinn, as the Jews believe.
This accusation by the Jews is mentioned repeatedly in the Gospel. And the response Prophet Jesus (AS) gave to this accusation of theirs is also recorded. We present an excerpt from Matthew here, which fully supports the thought we presented above. In Matthew Chapter 12, it is stated:

'Then they brought him a demon-possessed man who was blind and mute, and Jesus healed him, so that he could both talk and see. All the people were astonished and said, "Could this be the Son of David?" But when the Pharisees heard this, they said, "It is only by Beelzebul, the prince of demons, that this fellow drives out demons." Jesus knew their thoughts and said to them, "Every kingdom divided against itself will be ruined, and every city or household divided against itself will not stand. If Satan drives out Satan, he is divided against himself. How then can his kingdom stand? And if I drive out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your people drive them out? So then, they will be your judges. But if it is by the Spirit of God that I drive out demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you. Or again, how can anyone enter a strong man's house and carry off his possessions unless he first ties up the strong man? Then he can plunder his house. Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters. And so I tell you, every kind of sin and slander can be forgiven, but blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven. Anyone who speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but anyone who speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, either in this age or in the age to come. Make a tree good and its fruit will be good, or make a tree bad and its fruit will be bad, for a tree is recognized by its fruit."' (Matthew 12:22-33)

Keeping this background in mind, if you reflect upon the words 'Wa atayna 'Eesab-na Maryamal-bayyinati wa ayyadnahu biroohil-qudus' (And We gave Jesus, the Son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Holy Spirit), the actual emphasis of the verse will be understood—what is being affirmed and what is being refuted in it. As far as the support of the Holy Spirit is concerned, it is attained by every Prophet, and the miracles that emanate from a Prophet are the result of this very support. But this was repeatedly declared in the case of Prophet Jesus (AS) because the Jews leveled the aforementioned accusation against him. The Holy Spirit refers to the pure spirit that comes from God, and in Hebrew, it refers to Gabriel (Jibril).

This statement from the Jews could be as a lame excuse, or as an expression of arrogance. In the first case, it would mean: These things that the Prophet presents simply do not sink into our hearts. If they are from God, then everything is in God's power. Why doesn't He just open our hearts to these things?
In the second case, it would mean: Our hearts and minds are not made for such absurd things; that is why they do not penetrate our hearts in any way. If there were even a little rationality in them, who could surpass us in accepting rational things?
Precedents and evidence from the Holy Quran exist in support of both these meanings, but we prefer the second meaning here. The reason is that after this statement of theirs, Allah saying 'Bal la'anahumullahu bikufrihim' (Rather, Allah has cursed them for their disbelief) clearly supports this second meaning. Meaning, due to their arrogance and pride, they think that the words of the Prophet (PBUH) are such that they cannot sink into the heart of any rational person, whereas the reality is completely contrary to this. These words are highly rational and deeply touching, but due to these people's disbelief, their stubbornness, and obstinacy, Allah has cursed their hearts. Because of this, there is no capability left in them to accept these rational things.

'Kitab' (Book) here refers to the Holy Quran, which was revealed confirming the prophecies regarding it that were present in the scriptures of the Jews. From this perspective, the very first and greatest favor of the Quran was upon the Jews themselves, that it proved true many things from their scriptures. The right of this favor was that they should have stepped forward first and welcomed this esteemed Book with open arms. But instead of accepting it, due to stubbornness and jealousy, they took the lead in opposing it. The reality of calling the Quran a confirmer of previous scriptures has been clarified in the Tafseer of verse 41 of this very Surah.
Since prophecies about the Quran and the Final Prophet (PBUH) were present in the Jews' scriptures, they were waiting for the manifestation of these prophecies with great intensity. They hoped that when this promised Prophet appeared, the days of their wretchedness and misery would end, and through him, Allah would grant them victory over all their enemies. They even used to pray for this victory. But it is a strange misfortune that when this prophecy was fulfilled, the one they were waiting for had arrived, and his deeds also proved that he is the one whose signs were described in the previous scriptures—and the Jews recognized him very well—they denied him purely out of stubbornness and jealousy. Prophet Jesus (AS) illustrated this attitude of the Jews in the Parable of the Ten Virgins, which is narrated in Matthew chapter 25.

Here 'Ishtira' means to sell and exchange. Exactly this theme has appeared in this manner in another place in this Surah:

'And wretched is that for which they sold themselves, if they only knew.' (Al-Baqarah: 102)

Lexicographers generally count this word among the 'Azdad' (words having opposite meanings). Imam Raghib has given a very reasonable explanation for it being among the Azdad. He says:

'When goods are exchanged for goods, it is correct to consider each of the two parties as both a buyer and a seller. From this perspective, the words for buying (Bay') and selling (Shira') are used interchangeably.'

In light of this research, the translation of the aforementioned verse would be: (How wretched is that for which they exchanged their souls). Meaning, becoming indifferent to the concern of their own salvation and success, they became involved in stubborn opposition against others, and to spite others, they cut off their own noses.
An yakfuroo bima anzalallahu baghyan an yunazzilallahu min fadlihi 'ala man yasha'u min 'ibadihi (That they disbelieve in what Allah has revealed, out of envy that Allah should send down His grace upon whom He wills of His servants): This explains what these people chose. That is, instead of believing in the Book revealed by Allah and His sent Prophet, they chose the path of denial and opposition. And since this path of denial and opposition was chosen deliberately and knowingly, its cause can be nothing but this stubbornness and hostility: they were angry at Allah as to why He bestowed the blessing of the final religion and the final Messenger upon the Children of Ishmael. Why didn't He make someone from among themselves a Messenger? As if they are the sole monopolists of all Allah's blessings, and they alone have the right to tell Him whom to select for which position and whom not to select.
'Baghyan' here means stubbornness. This stubbornness was the result of their rebellion and their arrogance (Istikbar). Although the words 'ala man yasha'u min 'ibadihi' (upon whom He wills of His servants) are general, the reference here is specifically to the Children of Ishmael. This general style is adopted to manifest the vastness of Allah's authority.
Fabaa'oo bighadabin 'ala ghadabin wa lil-kafireena 'adhabun muheen (So they returned with wrath upon wrath. And for the disbelievers is a humiliating punishment): 'They returned with wrath upon wrath' actually means that from where they were supposed to return with the greatest mercy, due to the consequence of their own evil deeds, they returned with God's wrath. The success and prosperity of both this world and the Hereafter were tied for them to believing in the Final Prophet. And they were expecting and waiting for him; in fact, as mentioned above, they even used to pray for it. But when the time came to benefit from this blessing, their wretchedness caused them to stumble, and as retribution for their opposition, they were deemed deserving of Divine wrath. Furthermore, not just wrath, but wrath upon wrath. They became deserving of one wrath due to breaking the covenant they had made with Allah through Prophet Moses (AS), and they became deserving of the second wrath because when the opportunity came for them to enter God's covenant again, afflicted with stubbornness and jealousy, they refused to benefit from it.
'Adhabun muheen (A humiliating punishment): Refers to a degrading torment. This humiliating punishment will be given to them because the actual motivator of their crimes was 'Istikbar' (arrogance), as has been mentioned above.

'Is it [not] that every time a messenger came to you with what your souls did not desire, you were arrogant...' (Al-Baqarah: 87)

Meaning, when they are invited to believe in the Quran, they arrogantly say that they believe in what was revealed to them. Following this, their statement is clarified to mean that they do not believe in anything revealed after the Torah, even though, according to the prophecies of the Torah itself, the true scripture they are being invited to accept is now the Quran, not the Torah.
Qul falima taqtuloona anbiyaa'allahi min qablu in kuntum mu'mineen (Say, "Then why did you kill the prophets of Allah before, if you are [indeed] believers?"): After clarifying that following the revelation of the Quran, the valid faith is belief in the Quran and not just the Torah, it was made clear that the Jews' claim of believing in the Torah is also completely baseless. If they truly believed in the Torah, how could they have dared to murder the prophets of Allah who came to renew and confirm that very Torah?

This is a further refutation of the Jews' claim of faith. The specific aspect of this refutation is that by reminding them of the calf-worship incident from their early history, they are reprimanded: Today you have exaggerated the tale of your faith and religiosity so much that you are not ready to regard either the Quran or the Final Prophet (PBUH); however, setting aside the state of your faith today, from the very beginning it has been such that right in the presence of Moses (AS), while witnessing his clear miracles, you abandoned your Lord and began worshipping a calf.
Some prophets also reprimanded the Jews for this kind of arrogance, and alluding to this calf-worship incident, even went so far as to say, "O Israel, you are the one who was unfaithful on the very first night." The Quran's words befit its own high status, but the message conveyed is the same as that delivered by previous prophets.
'Wa antum zalimoon' (while you were wrongdoers) carries exactly the meaning of 'Wa antum mushrikoon' (while you were polytheists). In the Quran, Shirk (polytheism) has been expressed with the word 'Zulm' (wrongdoing/injustice) in multiple places. The root meaning of Zulm is the violation of rights. A person violates the rights of God and his own soul by committing Shirk in a way that he does not through any other means. The Holy Quran has clarified this in several places: 'Innash-shirka lazulmun 'azeem' (Indeed, association [with him] is great injustice).

We have already clarified all the important components of this segment while interpreting verse 63 of this Surah. The Jews' response quoted here, 'Qaloo sami'na wa 'asayna' (They said, "We hear and disobey"), is an expression of the actual situation. Their verbal pledge was indeed, "We hear and we will obey." But their action was that they disobeyed what they heard. The Quran has substituted their words with the reality that manifested through their actions. It is as if, from the very beginning, they pledged disobedience rather than acknowledgment and obedience.
Whenever the hypocrites and Jews came to the gatherings of the Holy Prophet (PBUH), instead of 'Sami'na wa ata'na' (We hear and obey), they would say 'Qaloo sami'na wa 'asayna', but they would pronounce it in such a way that the listener would mistake 'asayna' for 'ata'na'. They had learned this tactic from their predecessors. The only difference was that the predecessors said 'ata'na' but meant 'asayna', whereas these people said 'asayna' and meant it, but twisted their tongues to create the illusion for Muslims that they were saying 'ata'na'.

It has already been mentioned in verse 90 above that the Jews were highly infuriated if anyone other than themselves was considered deserving of God's grace and favor. In this world, they considered only themselves entitled to every Divine blessing, and they believed that they alone were entitled to the blessings of the Hereafter. The Quran warned them that if you genuinely believe that all the successes of the Hereafter are exclusively yours, and you do not acknowledge anyone else's share in them, then the logical demand of this should be that you yearn to meet the Lord and wish for death. But your condition is such that, despite being the People of the Book, your love for life surpasses even that of the polytheists of Arabia.
The Quran struck a very sensitive nerve of the Jews here so that they might feel a little shame over their arrogance of being God's beloved and close ones. Although, true to their habit, they could have brazenly replied that they indeed deeply desired death; however, a person's inner state is not hidden from himself. Therefore, this reality was very bitter for them and highly humiliating in their own eyes.

Meaning, despite their claims of closeness to Allah and monopoly over the Hereafter, they will never wish for death. The reason is that the breaches of covenant and betrayals they committed against God and His Shariah—whether visible to others or not—are not hidden from themselves. Because of this, the thought of death makes them tremble. But how long will they run from death? And where will they go by running away from it? Ultimately, one day they must face death and appear before the Lord who is fully aware of all the deeds of these wrongdoers. This reality is clarified in Surah Al-Jumu'ah as follows:

'Say, "Indeed, the death from which you flee - indeed, it will meet you. Then you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, and He will inform you about what you used to do."' (Al-Jumu'ah - 8)

Meaning, on one hand, there are these claims of love and being beloved to God, and on the other hand, their love for life is such that they are the greediest of all people for it. So much so that in this matter, they have even surpassed the polytheists of Arabia, for whom this world is everything and who have absolutely no concept of the Hereafter.
In more than one place, the Quran has compared the Jews with the Arab polytheists to show that in both belief and action, they are even worse than the polytheists. The polytheists, being deprived of a Book and Shariah, were naturally at a very low level intellectually and morally. The Quran clarified that despite highly boastful claims about the Book and Shariah, morally, these Jews put even the polytheists to shame, even though they considered the polytheists utterly despicable.
Wa ma huwa bimuzahzihihi: One translation of this can be what we have done (And he is not going to be removed from the punishment). Another could be that the prolongation of someone's life cannot save them from Allah's punishment. In terms of meaning, there is no difference between the two translations, and grammatically, I consider both correct. However, most interpreters have opted for the second one.
Wallahu baseerum-bima ya'maloon (And Allah is Seeing of what they do): Meaning, even the longest life cannot hide anyone's deeds from God. God is watching all these things they are doing, and since He is watching, how is it possible that He would not recompense them? Here, 'watching' implies what logically follows from this watching (i.e., accountability and recompense). This stylistic device is used in countless places in the Holy Quran.

In this sentence, 'Fa-innahu nazzalahu' stands in the place of the response to the condition (Jawab-e-Shart). In the Arabic language, whenever the response to a condition comes in this manner, it contains a hidden detail which is illuminated by the subsequent sentence. Here, from the context, the meaning of the sentence becomes clear: those who are enemies of Jibril (Gabriel) should be clear on the fact that enmity towards Jibril is, in reality, enmity towards Allah. This is because Jibril did not bring down God's word to the Prophet (PBUH) of his own accord, but brought it down strictly by God's command. Jibril does nothing arbitrarily; rather, whatever he does is according to God's will and under His command. In 'nazzalahu' (brought it down), the pronoun refers to the Quran, whose mention has been continuing from verse 91 above, 'Wa idha qeela lahum aaminoo bima anzalallahu' (And when it is said to them, "Believe in what Allah has revealed"). Therefore, the question of a pronoun preceding its antecedent (Idmar Qabl adh-Dhikr) does not arise here. This is merely a component of the preceding discourse.
It appears that the Jews, in their stubborn opposition to the Quran and the Prophet (PBUH), began to declare Jibril (AS) as their enemy as well. It is possible that when the Jewish scholars and leaders feared that the Quran's call might influence their common people, they concocted this excuse: "According to Muhammad's (PBUH) own statement, the angel Jibril comes to him, and this angel is our long-standing enemy; such and such calamities befell us at his hands. Therefore, we cannot believe in a person who colludes with our enemy angel."
Although it seems very strange that the Jews would reach such a height of foolishness as to consider Angel Jibril (AS) their enemy, when a person gets infected with the madness of stubbornness, jealousy, and sectarianism, nothing remains too far-fetched for him. One sect of the Rawafid (Shia) also holds the belief that the Quran was actually supposed to be revealed to Ali (RA), but Jibril mistakenly took it to Muhammad (PBUH). People of this sect, God forbid, even curse Jibril the Trustworthy for this 'sin'. (1)
The Quran's grip on this foolishness of the Jews is very timely and extremely severe. It means: If, in your anger over why he revealed this to Muhammad (PBUH) and not to one of your men, you have become enemies of Jibril, then think about where your logic leads. This means you consider Allah to be your enemy as well, because Jibril, even according to your own belief, is God's angel. He cannot do anything without God's command. Inevitably, he did this act by God's command too. Then you are not only the enemy of Jibril alone but also the enemy of God, and God too became your enemy.
Musaddiqal-lima bayna yadayhi wa hudan wa bushra lil-mu'mineen (confirming that which preceded it and as guidance and good tidings for the believers): Three more attributes of the Quran are mentioned here. First, it confirms the previous scriptures. Second, it guides towards the path of truth. Third, it brings glad tidings of success and salvation in the Hereafter to those who accept its guidance. This detail is presented here so that it becomes clear that the Jews' opposition is not just opposition to the Quran, but also opposition to their own Book. They are enemies of the guidance that was revealed earlier, and enemies of the guidance that has now been revealed to guide the world.

(1) Mujaddid Alf Sani (RA), while mentioning the beliefs of various Rafidi sects in his treatise 'Radd-e-Rawafid', writes: "And the Ghurabiyyah sect among them says that Muhammad (PBUH) was more similar to Ali (RA) than a crow is to a crow and a fly to a fly, and Allah the Exalted had sent revelation towards Ali. Jibril, due to the perfect resemblance, made a mistake and delivered the revelation to Muhammad... And they curse Jibril."

This is both a detail of whose enmity inevitably follows from the enmity towards Jibril (AS), and a statement of the punishment from Allah for this mischief. In the first degree, it necessitates enmity towards Allah Himself. The reason for this has been explained in the previous verse: when they are angry with Jibril for an action he performed by God's command, it means they are enemies of God Himself. Then, it necessitates enmity towards all angels, all messengers, and both Jibril and Mika'il. This is because there is perfect harmony among God, His angels, and His messengers. Angels and Prophets perform all tasks according to God's will and under His commands, and they belong to the same party (Hizb). For this reason, there can be no differentiation among them. Whoever is the enemy of any one of them is the enemy of all, and whoever belies any one of them has belied all. This brief hint is sufficient here; further details will follow later.
Here, after mentioning the angels in general, the specific mention of Jibril and Mika'il is, firstly, due to their importance, just as the specific is mentioned after the general. The second reason is that the Children of Israel, as is clear from some narrations, considered the angel Mika'il to be their sympathizer, contrary to Jibril. The Quran, by including Mika'il (AS) alongside Jibril (AS) here, clarified that the enemy of Jibril, just as he is the enemy of Allah and all His prophets and messengers, is similarly the enemy of Mika'il. Because the community (Millat) of all of God's angels and messengers is one. Both Jibril and Mika'il belong to this very community. There is no rivalry or friction between them such that one could be enemies with Jibril and maintain friendship with Mika'il.
Fa-innallaha 'aduwwul-lil-kafireen (then indeed, Allah is an enemy to the disbelievers): This segment simultaneously proves two things. First, those who are enemies of Allah, His messengers, and His angels are disbelievers (Kafirs). Second, Allah is also the enemy of such disbelievers. The concise expression of the Quran has wrapped up both these points in such a way that the statement is complete, and no room is left for the addressee to deny or debate.

The address is to the Prophet (PBUH). 'Ayat Bayyinat' (Clear signs) refers to those clear and definitive proofs of your Prophethood and Messengership that were revealed to you. This applies generally, whether they are in the form of the well-reasoned statements of the Quran, or in the form of achievements, signs, testimonies, and miracles that manifested through you. It is stated that these things are so clear in proving your Prophethood that anyone possessing even a trace of rationality cannot deny them; only those who are disobedient and covenant-breakers can deny them.
The root meaning of 'Fisq' is disobedience to God. Disobedience can be minor, and it can be major. In the Holy Quran, this word is used for the greatest of disobediences, and in our view, this is its meaning here as well; the context suggests exactly this. The meaning is that if it were a matter of proofs and testimonies, the truth of the Quran is clear; but for those who have already decided to break God's covenant and pledge, what importance do proofs and testimonies hold?

This style of speech is one of expressing astonishment and sorrow. Mentioning the covenant-breaking of the Jews above, the discourse had reached the point that they adopted this exact attitude of breaking covenants in the matter of the pledge taken from them regarding the Final Book and the Final Messenger. Then, with a tone of astonishment and sorrow, it was said: Will this attitude of theirs persist forever, that whenever they make a covenant with God, when the time comes, they completely shatter it? Only a few people among them are able to remain firm on it. Then, completely unmasking the core reality, it was said: 'Bal aktharuhum la yu'minoon' (Rather, most of them do not believe), meaning their majority consists of people who are fundamentally devoid of faith altogether. They claim to believe in the Torah, but in reality, they believe in absolutely nothing.

The pattern of covenant-breaking by the Jews mentioned in the previous verse is provided with factual testimony in this verse. See, when a Messenger from Allah came to them, perfectly matching the prophecies they already possessed, a group among them threw the Book of Allah behind their backs as if they were never even acquainted with it.
'Rasool' (Messenger) refers to the Prophet (PBUH). Although the word is used in an indefinite form (Nakirah), the subsequent attributes and the context determine the specific reference. Also, it serves to express the greatness of the Messenger.
'Kitab Allah' (The Book of Allah) can refer to both the Torah and the Quran. If it refers to the Torah, the meaning would be that these People of the Book, despite being People of the Book, ignored the Book of Allah in the matter of the Final Prophet (PBUH) as if they did not know it at all. If it refers to the Holy Quran, the meaning would be that, being People of the Book, if anyone was most qualified to recognize a book coming from Allah, it was them. This is because they claimed to be the inheritors and custodians of a heavenly book, and they also had prior news of the revelation of such a book. But woe to stubbornness and jealousy! Despite being People of the Book, they are throwing Allah's book behind their backs as if they do not know it at all.

This explains what they embraced after throwing the Book of Allah behind their backs.
In the Holy Quran, 'Shayateen' (devils) refers in multiple places to the corrupters and evildoers from both the jinn and human groups. In our view, here too, it refers to the evildoers of both groups. 'Ala mulki Sulayman' refers to the era of Prophet Solomon's (AS) reign. According to the general rule of the Arabic language, an annexed noun (Mudaf) is omitted here, i.e., 'Ala ahdi mulki Sulayman' (During the reign of Solomon). The meaning of the verse is that these wrongdoers threw the Divine Book behind their backs and, in its place, adopted the base sciences like magic, sorcery, and astrology, which gained currency during Solomon's (AS) reign through the mutual collaboration of jinn and humans who followed them.
The practice of magic, sorcery, and such base and satanic arts has existed to some extent in every era. However, during the time of Prophet Solomon (AS), it seems that, out of a desire to rival his spiritual sciences, the teaching and learning of magic and sorcery surged among a class of jinn and human devils. These corrupters even organized and codified these arts. In later periods, when the Jews suffered religious and moral decline and their taste for the Book and Shariah died out, naturally their absorption in learning and teaching such absurdities increased tremendously. (1) And, as is the custom, to give these things a color of sanctity, they likely attributed them directly to Prophet Solomon (AS), claiming to the people—to make them devotees—that Prophet Solomon (AS) performed his attributed feats using these very arts.
