Even today, those who have a taste for these base things frequently cite Prophet Solomon (AS) to support their superstitions. Some amulets (Naqsh) are even specifically attributed to his esteemed name. It appears that all such things were transmitted to us through the Jews, and they are part of the evil remnants of that register of misguidance which the evil jinn and humans compiled during Solomon's (AS) reign and which the Jews later promoted.
(1) In 2 Kings 17:16-17, the condition of both Israel and Judah is described as: "They forsook all the commands of the Lord their God and made for themselves two idols cast in the shape of calves, and an Asherah pole. They bowed down to all the starry hosts, and they worshiped Baal. They sacrificed their sons and daughters in the fire. They practiced divination and sought omens..."
Wa ma kafara Sulaymanu wa lakinnash-shayateena kafaroo yu'allimoonan-nasas-sihr (And Solomon did not disbelieve, but the devils disbelieved, teaching people magic): This sentence is by way of correction (Istidrak) or a parenthetical clause. In the middle of the discourse, to exonerate Prophet Solomon (AS) from the accusation leveled by the Jews, it was stated that Solomon's character is completely clean from the pollution of these base arts; he never committed this disbelief. Rather, it was the devils from jinn and humans who adopted these things and then taught these absurdities to people. Regarding the style of expression here, two things should be kept in mind. First, the eloquence of this parenthetical sentence: while reading it, one feels that the speaker finds the attribution of these base arts to Prophet Solomon (AS) so repulsive that in refuting it, he did not even pause long enough to finish the sentence, but interrupted the flow to immediately refute it. Second, the refutation begins with a style that automatically implies that magic being 'Kufr' (disbelief) is such a clear reality that it requires no proof.
Wa ma unzila 'alal-malakayni bibabila Haroota wa Maroot (And that which was revealed to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut): The preceding sentence, as we mentioned, is a correction or a parenthetical clause; therefore, this sentence is inevitably conjoined to 'ma tatloosh-shayateen' (what the devils recited). The meaning is that, firstly, they followed those base arts that gained currency during Solomon's reign through devils. Secondly, they followed that which was revealed to the two angels, 'Harut and Marut', during the Babylonian captivity.
Here the question arises: what was revealed to these angels? Interpreters have generally answered that it was the knowledge of magic. But this answer is problematic from several angles. First, its conjunction, as we said, is to 'ma tatloosh-shayateen', which according to the Quran's own explanation means magic. Now, if this also means magic, its separate mention serves no special purpose. In Arabic, when coordinate elements (Ma'toof and Ma'toof Ilayh) appear like this, according to general rules, there must be a degree of distinctness between them. Without any specific contextual clue, native speakers do not violate this general rule. Here, not only is there no clue that they are the same thing, but the clues point to the contrary. Second, the word 'Unzila' (was revealed/sent down) is used for it, whose clear meaning is understood to be that this knowledge was sent down by Allah. Keeping in mind the grace and utility inherent in this word, its use for something as satanic, impure, and utterly false—in fact, disbelief-inducing—as magic feels heavy on one's sense of propriety. There is no doubt that in the Holy Quran, this word is also used for the creation of cattle, iron, etc., but this alone is not enough to prove its suitability for magic. Iron, cattle, etc., are things of great goodness and blessing for us from a civilizational and economic standpoint, so its use for them is understandable. However, to our knowledge, this word has never been used anywhere in the Quran for something that is disbelief-inducing and satanic like magic. It is also used for sending down Divine punishment on disbelievers, but the punishment that befalls disbelievers is a mercy for the believers, and it purifies God's earth. We do not deny the reality that whatever exists in the world, whether good or evil, exists under God's will. But falsehood receiving respite under God's will is one thing, and a satanic art like magic being 'revealed' to two angels is quite another. Third, this knowledge, as is clear from the Quran's words, was revealed to two angels. And these angels also taught this knowledge to people. It is an accepted fact regarding angels that their character is pure from every pollution of polytheism and disbelief. Allah has designed their temperament such that they are never even touched by this kind of filth. Angels have always been the means in this world for establishing truth and justice and preaching goodness and success from Allah, and these are the things that befit them. Therefore, the revelation of the knowledge of magic upon them and their spreading of it (no matter how cautiously) is far from reason. If angels start doing such things, then what work is left for the devils? Fourth, the words the angels used for their knowledge also imply that their knowledge had different characteristics from the magic of the devils. The knowledge of the devils, as the Holy Quran itself clarified, was outright disbelief; but the angels used the word 'Fitnah' (trial) for their knowledge. The meaning of Fitnah is a test and trial. In the Quran, it generally refers to things that are originally created for the benefit and well-being of humans, but human beings, through misuse or becoming entangled in excessive love for them, turn them into a Fitnah for themselves, causing them to become harmful and even fatal instead of useful. For example, wealth and children are called Fitnah in the Holy Quran. Everyone knows that these two things are not inherently evil but are blessings of Allah. If a person recognizes their proper place, they can be beneficial for him in both this world and the Hereafter. But when a person becomes ensnared in unjustified love for them and neglects God and the Hereafter because of them, these very things become a curse and punishment for him, because in some situations, the love for them drives a person to disbelief. All these aspects contradict taking 'Wa ma unzila 'alal-malakayn' to mean magic. But if not magic, the question is, what is that knowledge whose revelation to angels is appropriate, and whose absorption or misuse can lead to the corruptions described regarding this knowledge here? For example, that absorption in it turns one away from the Book of Allah, its nature is that of a Fitnah whose misuse can lead one into Kufr, and wicked people can use it to ruin relations between husband and wife. In our view, this refers to the knowledge of the spiritual properties and effects of things and words, which became prevalent among the Jewish mystics and spiritual guides, and which they utilized in the form of amulets, charms, and various kinds of 'Amaliyat' (spiritual practices) for different purposes. For instance, to cure certain diseases or pains, to ward off the evil eye and the bad effects of magic, etc., to counter the mischief of tricksters, or to cast spells of love and hatred. This knowledge, in this respect, was completely different from the knowledge of magic and astrology, etc., as there was no mixture of polytheism in it, nor did Satan and jinn have any role in it. But in producing its effects and results, it was as quick-acting as magic. It is possible that this knowledge was given to the Children of Israel during the Babylonian captivity through two angels so that they could use it to counter the magic and sorcery of Babylon and protect the less knowledgeable and simple-minded people of their nation from the awe of the magicians. Our mind goes towards this for two reasons. First, because it is known from the Torah that magic, sorcery, and astrology were rampant in Babylon. (1). Second, it seems in accordance with the Sunnah of Allah that if the awe and power of a false knowledge prevail somewhere, from which corrupt people are benefiting, Allah also grants the believers some knowledge there to counter it, which is permissible and beneficial.
(1): The Prophet Isaiah addresses Babylon saying: "Keep on, then, with your magic spells and with your many sorceries, which you have labored at since childhood. Perhaps you will succeed, perhaps you will cause terror. All the counsel you have received has only worn you out! Let your astrologers come forward, those stargazers who make predictions month by month, let them save you from what is coming upon you." (Isaiah 47:12-13)
Haroota wa Maroot (Harut and Marut): It is clear from the Quran that they were two angels of God, so the absurd story narrated about them in books of Tafseer is, in our view, completely unworthy of attention. They were sent to the world with angelic attributes and remained here with angelic attributes. Their knowledge too, as stated, was a permissible and useful knowledge, but the Jews, due to their moral degradation and corrupted taste, learned it with bad intentions and used it for bad purposes. The result was that this knowledge too became merely an appendage to magic and sorcery for them. They became so lost in its amusements that, firstly, they lost all connection with the Book of Allah; and if any remained, it was merely to the extent of incantations and amulets—that blowing such-and-such verse yields this benefit, and an amulet of such-and-such verse causes this effect.
It is possible that a question may arise in someone's mind here: does this kind of knowledge even exist in the world? In response, our submission is that denying it is denying a completely self-evident fact. Although I myself have never practiced any such knowledge, I have experienced things multiple times that make it impossible for me to deny this part. Our thought is that it is the remnants of this very knowledge that a faction of our Sufis and spiritual guides adopted, and through it, they also benefited people. In fact, events show that in some situations, with its help, they even proved the superiority of Islam and Muslims against yogis and astrologers. But after moral decline, just as among the Jews this knowledge became an appendage to base arts and a means of commercial exploitation, similarly, among us, it became merely a means to run the shop of 'Peeri-Mureedi' (spiritual guide and disciple business). And false elements mixed into it more than the truth, due to which its effects on people were the same as described by the Quran.
Warning from the Angels before Teaching: Wa ma yu'allimani min ahadin hatta yaqoola innama nahnu fitnatun fala takfur (But the two angels do not teach anyone unless they say, "We are a trial, so do not disbelieve [by practicing magic]"): Just as the segment "and Solomon did not disbelieve" came as a parenthetical clause to exonerate Prophet Solomon (AS), similarly this segment comes as a correction to exonerate these angels. The point is that if they disclosed this knowledge of theirs to anyone, they would also definitely warn them: 'Look, this knowledge of ours is a Fitnah (trial), so do not fall into disbelief by using it for bad purposes; rather, use it only for good purposes.'
Meaning of Fitnah: We have hinted at the meaning of Fitnah above. All worldly blessings of Allah—wife and children, wealth and status, power and empire, etc.—act as a double-edged sword. If a human uses them correctly, they are a blessing for him; and if he falls into a trial because of them, they become a punishment for him. Similarly, this knowledge also holds both aspects of harm and benefit within it. It can be used in the service of people to earn reward, and it can also be made a tool for chaos and division, thereby providing the means for misguidance and destruction. Because humans, due to their weaknesses, mostly misuse the powers given by Allah, the angels, like well-wishing teachers, warned anyone connecting with them beforehand that our knowledge is like a double-edged sword; no one should learn it to use for bad purposes, otherwise they could fall into disbelief and polytheism.
Here, the matter of the angels teaching is described in a way that at first glance suggests they taught people in human form. If this is the case, there is no particular difficulty in it. The Quran itself reveals several such incidents where angels appeared among humans in human form and shape. However, the possibility also exists that those obsessed with 'Amaliyat' (spiritual practices) acquired this teaching by establishing some kind of spiritual connection with them through specific types of asceticism and spiritual retreats (Chilla). If this meaning is taken, there is nothing in the words of the Quran that contradicts it.
Fayata'allamoona minhuma ma yufarriqoona bihi baynal-mar'i wa zawjihi (And [yet] they learn from them that by which they cause separation between a man and his wife): The degraded taste of the Jews. Meaning, despite the aforementioned warning of the angels, people specifically learned from them those practices through which they could cause separation between a husband and his wife. This segment sheds light on the moral corruption, low courage, and baseness of the Jews, that their greatest inclination was towards a practice they could use as scissors to cut the bond of love between a husband and wife. However, the foundation of the stability of the entire civilization rests upon the stability of the relationship between husband and wife. If a religious group dedicates its knowledge to uprooting this foundation, it means it has taken upon itself the work of Satan. The knowledge used for this purpose can only benefit the thugs and scoundrels of society, and there can be no more destructive use of the knowledge of creating love and hatred than this. Even though it could have been used correctly to benefit people.
Wa ma hum bidar-reena bihi min ahadin illa bi-idhnillah (But they do not harm anyone through it except by permission of Allah): This segment is also a correction (Istidrak). Meaning, the enthusiasts of these practices think that these things are beneficial and harmful in themselves. However, the reality is not this. Whether satanic practices or spiritual practices, if anyone is harmed or benefited by them, it is only by Allah's permission and under His will. These things are not effective in themselves. This correction highlights the monotheism and sincerity that is the foundation of all Quranic teachings. A monotheist (Muwahhid) learns from this that, while having the Book of Allah, firstly, he should not even desire such things. Secondly, if any of these things come to his knowledge, he should not believe them to be inherently effective (Mu'athir bidh-Dhat). Furthermore, if he fears harm from any such thing, he should turn for help only to the One Allah, and not get trapped in the cycle of spells, charms, practitioners (Aamils), and shamans.
Wa yata'allamoona ma yadurruhum wa la yanfa'uhum (And the people learn what harms them and does not benefit them): This segment reveals the moral and intellectual degradation of the learners. The knowledge they learned originally possessed, as has been shown, both aspects of benefit and harm; but the mindset of the learners was what was mentioned above: to use it to cause separation between a couple, to sow seeds of hatred where there is affection, and to create division where there is union. Due to this corruption of intent, they had completely eliminated its beneficial aspect.
Wa laqad 'alimoo lamanish-tarahu ma lahu fil-akhirati min khalaq (But the Children of Israel certainly knew that whoever purchased the magic would not have in the Hereafter any share): Meaning, the Jews knew very well that those who fall into such trials will have no share in the Hereafter. In the Torah, they were strictly forbidden from these things in very clear words. See Deuteronomy 18:9-12: 'When you enter the land the Lord your God is giving you, do not learn to imitate the detestable ways of the nations there. Let no one be found among you who sacrifices their son or daughter in the fire, who practices divination or sorcery, interprets omens, engages in witchcraft, or casts spells, or who is a medium or spiritist or who consults the dead. Anyone who does these things is detestable to the Lord; because of these same detestable practices the Lord your God will drive out those nations before you.' Despite these clear warnings from Prophet Moses (AS), the Jews adopted all these things, and their taste for them grew so much that Saul (Talut) had to purify the entire nation during his time. Thus, in 1 Samuel 28:3 it says: 'And Saul had expelled the mediums and spiritists from the land.'

Wa law annahum aamanoo wattaqaw lamathoobatum-min 'indillahi khayrun law kanoo ya'lamoon (And if they had believed and feared Allah, then the reward from Allah would have been [far] better, if they only knew): To understand this verse properly, also keep in mind verse 101 which passed above. There it was stated that they threw the Book of God behind their backs and remained trapped in the trials of magic, astrology, amulets, and charms. Now here it is stated that if they had believed in Allah's final Messenger and His final Book, and avoided these trials in which they are entangled, its reward would have been very great. But due to their baseness and lack of courage, they consider running the shop of base arts to be a great thing. They have no idea what the reward and recompense for believing in Allah's Book and the knowledge bestowed by Him is in the sight of God. If only they understood this.
Some Important Points of the Verse Group 97 to 103:
Within this group of verses as well, there are some points that require further clarification so they settle well in the mind.
First, a small deviation opens the doors to major deviations. Often, a misguidance or corrupted belief appears minor on the surface, but it hides within it so many misguidances and corrupted beliefs that it uproots the very foundations of a person's religion and faith. In their fervor to oppose the Quran, the Jews became enemies even of Jibril the Trustworthy, and they considered this a minor thing. When the Quran clarified its implications, it became known that enmity towards Jibril is not merely enmity towards Jibril alone; rather, it is enmity towards Allah Himself, enmity towards all His angels, and enmity towards all His messengers. Then, simultaneously, it clarified another inevitable consequence of this enmity, which is even more severe than the aforementioned consequences. That is, those who are such staunch disbelievers that they are enemies of Allah, the angels, and the Prophets—how can Allah be their friend? Therefore, inevitably, Allah is also the enemy of such disbelievers. Reflect on how far the matter escalated. Second, another point based on the very same principle is that there can be no differentiation of any kind among Allah the Exalted, His angels, and His prophets and messengers. Belying any one of them is belied all, and enmity towards any one of them is enmity towards all. For this reason, the belief of Muslims is stated as: 'la nufarriqu bayna ahadim mir-rusulih' (Al-Baqarah: 285) (We make no distinction between any of His messengers). If you reflect further, you will realize that this principle also underlies the Hadith in which the Prophet (PBUH) reported on behalf of Allah: 'Man 'ada li waliyyan faqad barazani bil-harb' (Whoever shows enmity to a friend of Mine, I have declared war against him). The reason for this is that those who know and believe in Allah truly belong to the same community (Millat) and the same party (Hizb) which includes the prophets, messengers, and angels. Just as enmity towards any of them is an indirect war against Allah, similarly, enmity towards any of the righteous and pious is also an indirect declaration of war against Allah. Third, just as things like magic, sorcery, astrology, séances, omens, and fortune-telling turn a person away from God and His Shariah, similarly, the knowledge of the spiritual properties of objects and words—i.e., amulets, charms, and incantations—is also a trial (Fitnah) for a human and diverts one from the Book and Shariah. To establish a strong and firm connection with the Book of Allah, the correct path is that a person should not only stay away from magic and sorcery but also avoid this second category of things as much as possible. When a person gets entangled in the cycle of spiritual practices (Amaliyat) etc., he inevitably falls into the very Fitnah that Harut and Marut had warned about, and then the manifestation of all those evils becomes inevitable which manifested at the hands of the Jews and because of which they were deprived of the light of the Book of Allah.

The Upcoming Theme: Verses 104 to 121: Going forward, Muslims are warned about some of the mischiefs the Jews were committing with the purpose of depriving the Children of Ishmael generally, and Muslims specifically, of the blessing of believing in the Quran and the Prophet (PBUH). In this context, some of such objections of the Jews are also quoted and answered, which they used to raise to create doubt and suspicion in the hearts of Muslims. Furthermore, instructions are also given which, by acting upon, Muslims could remain steadfast on the path of truth against these trials.
Lexical Research and Explanation of the Verses:
Ya ayyuhal-ladheena aamanoo la taqooloo ra'ina wa qoolun-zurna wasma'oo; wa lil-kafireena 'adhabun aleem.
Meaning of 'Ra'ina': 'Ra'ina' is an imperative form from 'Mura'at' (consideration/regard). In Arabic, if the listener has not properly heard or understood the speaker's words, the word 'Ra'ina' is used to draw the speaker's attention again, meaning, 'Please have regard for us; please repeat,' similar to 'I beg your pardon' in English. In Arabic, the word 'Unzurna' is also used for this very context, which is an imperative form from 'Nazar'. Its meanings include to look, to give respite, to wait, and to pause.
The Mischief of the Jews in the Gatherings of the Prophet (PBUH): It has been mentioned above that warnings are being given about the mischiefs and objections the Jews used to raise against the Prophet (PBUH) and the Quran to vent their inner frustration and, if possible, to deprive Muslims of the supreme blessing of Islam. Looking at the context, it becomes apparent that some Jews used to attend the gatherings of the Prophet (PBUH) purely for hypocritical purposes. As a show of their desire to benefit and their passion for learning, they would repeatedly say the word 'Ra'ina' to impress upon the attendees that they are great seekers and appreciators of knowledge. However, these people used this word solely because, by slightly twisting the tongue, an aspect of insulting the Prophet (PBUH) could be derived from it. If you pronounce 'Ra'ina' by slightly suppressing it downwards, it easily becomes 'Ra'eena', which means 'our shepherd'. This mischief of the Jews is also mentioned elsewhere in the Holy Quran: "Among the Jews are those who distort words from their [proper] usages and say, 'We hear and disobey' and 'Hear, may you not be heard' and 'Ra'ina', twisting their tongues and defaming the religion." (An-Nisa: 46). From this verse, it is clear that this mischief was created by twisting the tongue in the pronunciation of 'Ra'ina'. Similarly, they would pronounce 'Asayna' in such a way that the listener would mistake it for 'Ata'na', and while saying 'Isma'' (hear), they would slightly suppress their tongue and quietly attach 'ghayra musma'' (may you not be heard) to it. Meaning, listen to this unmentionable thing. The purpose of these mischiefs, as the Quran clarified, was to taunt and throw slurs at Islam and the Prophet (PBUH).
A Psychological Reality Regarding Words: Because the Jews used this taunt, as mentioned, to vent their frustration, insult the Prophet (PBUH), and lower him in the eyes of Muslims, Allah Almighty entirely banned this very word from the social vocabulary of the Muslims and commanded them to use another well-known word of the language which was free from any hint of mockery. Regarding words, this psychological reality should be kept in mind that if a spirit of corruption exists within them or has been created through misuse, then safety lies only in staying away from them; otherwise, their poison subconsciously permeates into their speakers and listeners. To protect Muslims from this contagion, Allah prohibited the use of 'Ra'ina'. Then, it had another benefit: the prohibition of 'Ra'ina' and the permission for 'Unzurna' also created a mark of distinction between the sincere believers and the hypocrites. Obviously, after clear prohibition, only those people could dare to use this word in the Prophet's gathering whose hearts were so filled with the fever of jealousy and malice that they were unable to suppress it by any means. In this verse, the word 'Isma'oo' (And listen) is in its perfect and true meaning. That is, listen to the words of the Prophet attentively and understand them so that you do not even face the need to repeatedly draw the Prophet's attention. This also contains a subtle hint that these Jews do not come to listen or to understand, but only to create an opportunity to use 'Ra'ina' and vent their heart's frustration.

Shedding Light on the Inner State of the Antagonists: This verse reflects the inner state of the antagonists of Islam, and the address is to the Muslims: The issue is not merely the use or non-use of a word; rather, both these Jews and polytheists are burning with this anger and jealousy as to how you were deemed worthy of this immense goodness from God. According to them, they were the heirs and progenitors of all goodness and nobility, not you, the destitute and unequipped. But when they see that, despite their opposition, the inheritance of the greatest goodness is transferring to you and you are about to become its bearers, they have started these kinds of petty antics to somewhat diminish the prestige of Islam and the Final Prophet (PBUH) in your eyes if possible, so that just as they are deprived of this blessing, you too remain deprived of it. You must beware of these tricks of theirs and do not become tools to fulfill their desires by falling for their deceptions. Then it was said that Allah has not made the Jews or the chiefs of Quraish monopolists of His grace and mercy; rather, He Himself is the Master and in charge of His grace and mercy. He bestows it upon whomever He wills, according to His discretion and the demands of His wisdom.

Meaning of Naskh (Abrogation): The root meaning of Naskh is to remove and erase. In the Holy Quran, it is stated: 'Fayansakhullahu ma yulqish-shaytanu thumma yuhkimullahu ayatihi' (So Allah abolishes that which Satan throws in; then Allah makes precise His verses) (Al-Hajj: 52). Here it is used to mean removing one law and bringing another law in its place. 'Insa' means to cause to forget.
Refuting the Whisperings of the Jews: The Jews used to plant this doubt in the hearts of Muslims that when the Quran accepts Prophet Moses (AS) as God's Prophet and the Torah as God's Book, then what is the meaning of altering the laws of the Torah? Does God change the laws He Himself made with His own hands? Have His mistakes now become apparent to God after experience, and is He correcting them? By raising such objections, the Jews were trying to make Muslims suspicious of the Quran and the Prophet (PBUH). The Quran has given them this answer: the law of the Torah that is abrogated is replaced with a better law. Similarly, the commands of the Torah that the Jews had forgotten are renewed, and if they are not renewed but overlooked, similar commands are given. Meaning, through this change, Allah the Exalted is, firstly, advancing His servants towards perfection (from good to better); secondly, He is filling the house of religion with new wealth in place of the wealth of religion that was lost. Neither of these two things is such that it can be deemed objectionable.
Alam ta'lam annallaha 'ala kulli shay'in qadeer (Do you not know that Allah is over all things competent?): This address is general. To those who were planting this doubt, and also to those who were being affected by this doubt. They are being told that Allah did not resign from His power and authority after giving the Shariah to the Jews, such that now He will neither give Shariah to anyone else in the world, nor make any amendment or alteration to it, nor renew it, even if they completely destroy it. Rather, He remains the Master of all His powers, and in accordance with His wisdom, He has always used them and will continue to do so.
The Reality of Naskh and its Necessity: The issue of Naskh mentioned in this verse requires further clarification. We will shed light on some of its aspects here and also benefit from the thought of Ustadh Imam Maulana Farahi (RA) in this regard.
It is stated: 'Ma nansakh min ayatin aw nunsiha nati bikhayrim-minha aw mithliha' (We do not abrogate a verse or cause it to be forgotten except that We bring a better one or one similar to it). In light of the context and the structure of the discourse, we consider this verse to relate solely to previous religions. The objection raised by the People of the Book—that when the Quran accepts our books as heavenly, why does it abrogate their teachings?—the Quran has answered this objection of theirs. Now, reflect on the nature of this answer and see if it is rational and satisfactory from every angle. Contemplating the verse clearly brings two aspects of the answer to the fore: (1) First, that this abrogation is from the perspective of ascent and progress towards perfection (from good to better). In other words, it is the fulfillment of the promise Allah made to Prophet Moses and Prophet Jesus that He would send His final Prophet who would perfect Allah's Shariah, make all good things lawful, prohibit all evil things, and free people from many of the restrictions that were upon them at that time. To firmly grasp this reality, keep the following points in mind: (a) Allah's Shariah progressed gradually and reached that point of perfection at which it is seen in the Wise Quran. The thing that necessitated this gradual training is human nature. Allah designed human nature in such a way that it could only reach the stage where it became worthy of God's perfect religion through gradual training. The religion he received before reaching this stage was fundamentally Islam itself, but in its outward shape and form, or in other words, in terms of its Shariah, it was largely molded onto those templates that suited the mental, intellectual, social, and civilizational demands of that era. Through gradual training, when all the potentials of his nature became manifest and his intellect reached maturity, freeing himself from the confines of tangibles, rituals, and national and tribal narrow-mindedness, he began to think and understand; then Allah gave him Islam in that shape and form, and in that attire of Shariah, which exactly matches the demands of his nature. Nothing is less than it, nor more. This progress demanded that many things of previous Shariahs change, and in Islam, they manifest in forms that are absolutely their standard and natural forms. (b) The outward form of many commands of the Torah also testifies that when they were revealed, they were in a raw state; they were waiting for another season and climate to ripen. The advent of Islam provided that awaited season for them, and they reached maturity. For example, wine was forbidden among them only for the officials of the place of worship. Obviously, this was a hint that this thing is contrary to piety and purity, and a day will come when it will be forbidden for everyone. Thus, Islam took the first step regarding its prohibition from this point, making it forbidden during prayer times. Then, gradually, it made it completely forbidden. Narrations, and even hints in the Quran, show that those among the Muslims who were more intelligent and familiar with the spirit of the religion recognized the direction of the wind upon hearing the very first command, and completely repented from wine from that moment. Similarly, reflecting on the permissibility and prohibition of other food and drink items reveals that some things were either forbidden for the Children of Israel based on their specific national taste, or as a punishment for their unjustified questions—for example, the camel or the fat of certain parts of a slaughtered animal. These prohibitions indicated by their very form that they were temporary and conditional, and a day would come when all such restrictions would be lifted because they are contrary to human nature. Thus, the Religion of Nature (Deen-e-Fitrat) issued the general declaration, 'Al-yawma uhilla lakumut-tayyibat' (This day [all] good foods have been made lawful, and you can eat them), abrogating all such restrictions. Many such examples can be presented from the Torah. But the purpose here is not detail, but merely to point towards a reality. (c). Prophet Moses and Prophet Jesus (peace be upon them) had also pointed to this need for completion and progress in highly unambiguous words. For example, Prophet Moses (AS) said: "The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your fellow Israelites. You must listen to him. For this is what you asked of the Lord your God at Horeb on the day of the assembly when you said, 'Let us not hear the voice of the Lord our God nor see this great fire anymore, or we will die.' The Lord said to me: 'What they say is good. I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their fellow Israelites, and I will put my words in his mouth. He will tell them everything I command him. I myself will call to account anyone who does not listen to my words that the prophet speaks in my name.'" (Deuteronomy 18:15-20). In these verses, while on one hand there is an explicit promise of the advent of the Final Prophet (PBUH), there is also a hint that the completion of the religion will be through him. At the location of Horeb, the Children of Israel themselves expressed that they no longer found the strength within themselves to bear the burden of further Shariah. Allah appreciated their admission of weakness and promised that He would raise another prophet like Moses from among their brethren, and through him, He would complete His religion.
In even clearer words, Prophet Jesus (AS) manifested this reality. Observe: "But now I am going to him who sent me. None of you asks me, 'Where are you going?' Rather, you are filled with grief because I have said these things. But very truly I tell you, it is for your good that I am going away. Unless I go away, the Advocate will not come to you; but if I go, I will send him to you. When he comes, he will prove the world to be in the wrong about sin and righteousness and judgment: about sin, because people do not believe in me; about righteousness, because I am going to the Father, where you can see me no longer; and about judgment, because the prince of this world now stands condemned. I have much more to say to you, more than you can now bear. But when he, the Spirit of truth, comes, he will guide you into all the truth. He will not speak on his own; he will speak only what he hears, and he will tell you what is yet to come." (John 16:5-13). In these verses, who else can be the subject of the words 'Advocate' and 'Spirit of truth', or in some other translations 'Comforter' and 'Counselor', other than the Holy Prophet (PBUH)? It is only to the Prophet (PBUH) that the statement "he will guide you into all the truth" can apply, and it is only the Prophet's (PBUH) stature that "he will not speak on his own; he will speak only what he hears." This exact same thing is mentioned in the Holy Quran in these words: "Wama yantiqu 'anil-hawa in huwa illa wahyun yooha" (Nor does he speak from [his own] inclination. It is not but a revelation revealed). Similarly, Prophet Moses (AS) prophesied about the Holy Prophet (PBUH) that the promised Prophet will say whatever the Lord God puts in his mouth, and whatever news he gives, he will prove true in it.
These very prophecies of the Torah and the Gospel have been pointed to in the following verses of Surah Al-A'raf.
[Allah] said, "My punishment - I afflict with it whom I will, but My mercy encompasses all things." So I will decree it [especially] for those who fear Me and give Zakah and those who believe in Our verses - Those who follow the Messenger, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written in what they have of the Torah and the Gospel, who enjoins upon them what is right and forbids them what is wrong and makes lawful for them the good things and prohibits for them the evil and relieves them of their burden and the shackles which were upon them. So they who have believed in him, honored him, supported him and followed the light which was sent down with him - it is those who will be the successful. (Al-A'raf: 156-157)
(2) Naskh for the purpose of the renewal of religion: The second aspect of the answer is that this Naskh is under the requirement of renewing and reviving the religion. The detail of this brevity is that the Shariah given to the Jews and Christians, some parts of it, as explicitly stated in the Holy Quran, they had forgotten. From this forgotten portion, whatever Allah deemed necessary, He renewed it through this perfect religion, so that the wealth from the treasure of religion that was lost due to the negligence and unworthiness of the guardians is secured anew. And if Divine wisdom did not deem a certain part of it necessary, but rather His wisdom required that part to be overlooked, He graciously bestowed other commands of equal rank and status in its place.
The word 'Insa' used here means causing to forget. In the verse under discussion, Allah has attributed this action to Himself. This attribution is similar to how it is stated elsewhere in the Holy Quran: 'Falamma zaghoo azaghallahu quloobahum' (And when they deviated, Allah caused their hearts to deviate). This style of expression highlights the reality that Allah dealt with them according to His law of wisdom, and did so because they deserved this very thing due to their carelessness regarding their Shariah. But since the Divine Shariah is the common heritage of all humanity, just as its temporary commands were completed through the eternal and supreme commands of Islam, similarly, its forgotten and lost commands were renewed in the Quran through similar commands.
(3) Purification of the Shariah: This necessity of Naskh was explained from the perspective of the completion of religion and the renewal of the Shariah, and it is so clear that there was no room for denial by the Jews and Christians. However, the Holy Quran has not sufficed merely with stating these two aspects of Naskh, but has also explained its necessity from a third perspective. This aspect is the purification of religion and Shariah: meaning, purifying the Book of Allah and His Shariah from those innovations and adulterations that innovators and followers of desires mixed into them. This is mentioned in the verse of Surah Al-Hajj which we referenced above. It states: 'Fayansakhullahu ma yulqish-shaytanu thumma yuhkimullahu ayatihi' (So Allah abolishes that which Satan throws in; then Allah makes precise His verses). If you reflect from this perspective, you will realize that Naskh is a very great means for refuting innovations and nullifying falsehood. The innovations and fabricated things that the wicked and corrupters mixed into the heavenly scriptures and divine Shariahs, the Prophets (peace be upon them) cleansed and purified the religion from them and revived and established its original teachings. The majority of the Prophets (peace be upon them) who came before the Holy Prophet (PBUH) consisted of those prophets who did not bring any new Shariah. Rather, the mission of most of them was simply to purify the previously revealed Shariah from innovations and interpolations and return it to its original state. In this Ummah (community), this service has been entrusted to the scholars by Allah and His Messenger: that they continuously purify the religion from innovations and interpolations and keep returning the Ummah to the Book and Sunnah.
The types of additions made in previous Shariahs which Islam abrogated to present their true reality—we mention a few examples of them here so that the necessity and importance of Naskh from this perspective becomes clearly visible.
In the chapter of beliefs and articles of faith, the absurdities added by the Jews and Christians which the Quran corrected include their belief that God is the third of three; or for example, that the Jews are the sons of God and His beloved; or that Allah has taken a pledge from them that they will not believe in any prophet until he brings a sacrifice which fire from heaven consumes; or that Moses (AS) had the disease of leprosy in his hand. The Holy Quran refuted all such things and clarified the actual facts. Similarly, to justify their sinful lives, the Jews included highly obscene narrations regarding most Prophets (peace be upon them) in their scriptures, which were completely damaging to their moral character. The Holy Quran exonerated these Prophets from all such accusations and presented their lives in their true colors. In the chapter of actions, some examples of the innovations they committed have already passed in this Surah, and some will be mentioned later. For example, their attitude regarding the prisoners of their nation, or the approach they adopted in the matter of usury (Riba). The Christians made the pig and strangled animals permissible. Similarly, these people also distorted history and events to present them in the color of their desires. For example, most of the history of Prophet Abraham (AS) and the Kaaba was covered up so that Prophet Abraham's (AS) connection with the House of Allah could not be proven, and in this way, the prophecies regarding the Holy Prophet (PBUH) could be distorted. Many modifications were also made in the narratives regarding Ishmael and Hagar under this very motive. The Holy Quran tore the veil off all these interpolations and exposed the actual facts. Ustadh Imam has discussed these things in detail in his treatise 'Dhabeeh'. Going forward, we too will hint at some useful points at appropriate places.
The Nature of Naskh in Islamic Shariah: From the above detail, the reality is well clarified that the verse of Surah Al-Baqarah under discussion relates entirely to previous religions, and the necessity and wisdom of the Naskh referred to in it is so clear that there is no room for denial by any fair-minded person. As for the question of whether there is Naskh in the Islamic Shariah or not, there are three groups among us regarding this. One group is of those who not only believe in Naskh but give it a very wide scope. The second group outright denies it entirely. The third group believes in it but limits it to only a few commands. The reason the first group has given its scope such a wide expansion is that, according to them, Naskh has a specific meaning. These people accept Naskh even in all those places where a statement restricts a general rule (Takhsees al-'Aam) or generalizes a specific rule, or gives detail to a brief statement (Tafseel al-Mujmal). However, in such places, a more rational approach than assuming Naskh is to attempt to find harmony (Tawfeeq) between the general and specific, and the brief and detailed, and this harmony can be created very easily.
The standpoint of the group that outright denies Naskh is that the commands of the Islamic Shariah are subject to circumstances. The commands that were abrogated were abrogated solely because the circumstances under which they were revealed had changed. Now, if those exact circumstances return, those commands will also be reinstated anew. Because of this, the commands that are apparently abrogated are not actually abrogated, but remain established and alive within their specific circumstances. In support of its viewpoint, this group also presents the argument that the evolution of the Islamic Shariah progressed gradually from leniency to strictness. Because of this, when circumstances demand reverting to leniency, this reversal would be perfectly in accordance with the temperament of the Islamic Shariah.
In our view, there are several errors in this opinion. First, the claim itself is completely baseless that initially the Shariah was light, and later it became strict. Reflecting on the Holy Quran reveals that while in some commands its evolution was from leniency to strictness—for example, the prohibition of wine and the command of fasting, etc.—in some commands it was also from strictness to leniency—for example, regarding night prayer (Salat al-Layl) and the number of combatants. Because of this, making a formula that the Shariah's evolution was from leniency to strictness and drawing any conclusion regarding Naskh is not safe from fallacy. Second, it fails to consider the difference between the era of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and our era. When the Holy Prophet (PBUH) invited the world to the Islamic Shariah, the teachings of Islam were completely novel and alien to the people, the number of his companions was very small, and people were so accustomed to Jahiliyyah customs and habits that it was not easy for them to escape them. In contrast, in this era, the circumstances are very different. Muslims in the world number in the hundreds of millions. Islamic commands and laws are not unfamiliar and alien things to people. Therefore, drawing an analogy between this era and that era and applying the commands of one to the other is not correct in our view in any way. Third, if the justification for returning to the abrogated elements of the Shariah under the pretext of changing circumstances is accepted, it opens a path for escaping the Shariah for mischief-loving dispositions that will become impossible to close. In this era, using this argument, convenience-seeking people will very easily start making such Ijtihad (independent reasoning) regarding fasting, prayer, the prohibition of wine, and the penal code (Hadd) for adultery, etc., that safety in the matter of religion will vanish. Indeed, this has been experienced in the past at the hands of misguided preachers, and it is being experienced today as well. Using this very cover, innovators made many things prohibited by the Shariah lawful for their followers. And subsequently, the sense of prohibition regarding these things also departed from among them.
There is no doubt that in a corrupted environment, good callers to religion have sometimes also shown leniency in certain matters of the Shariah for newcomers and new Muslims. But the reason for this leniency was absolutely not that they adopt the abrogated elements of the Shariah due to changing circumstances. This kind of tolerance is part of wise nurturing. It is adopted with the expectation that gradually the condition of such unpolished people will improve through companionship and training. Experience shows that in most cases, this expectation has been fulfilled, provided that the trainers themselves possessed the attributes of Taqwa (piety) and were not merely running a shop of showing wheat and selling barley (deceit). It is not correct, in our view, to construe this to mean that these people believed in abandoning the definitive/firm (Muhkamat) elements of the Shariah and adopting the abrogated (Mansookhat) elements due to changing circumstances.
Based on these reasons, we consider both the aforementioned approaches regarding Naskh to be weak. Now remains the third approach, meaning the approach of those who believe certain verses of the Quran are abrogated. In our view, this is the correct approach. As for the question of which verses those are, which verses abrogated them, and what is the reason for their abrogation, this is not the place to answer those questions. In this book, God willing, these discussions will come at their respective places. Here, just keep a few principled points in mind. First, if any command of the Quran has been abrogated, it has been abrogated by the Quran itself, and both the abrogating (Nasikh) and abrogated (Mansookh) are present in the Holy Quran. No other thing besides the Quran can abrogate any command of the Quran. Some jurists have considered Hadith to be an abrogator for the Quran as well. But in our view, this approach is not correct. The weakness of this approach is so obvious that there is no need to refute it. Second, this Naskh relates entirely and exclusively to commands and laws; it has no relation whatsoever to beliefs and articles of faith, morals and attributes, or historical events and facts. Beliefs, articles of faith, events, and facts are not things that are one way today and become something else tomorrow. But in commands and laws, if the Lawgiver Himself makes some amendment and correction, it does not harm the purpose of the law; rather, it strengthens the main purpose. Third, the necessity for this Naskh did not arise because, God forbid, there is a deficiency in Allah's knowledge due to which His revealed law had to pass through stages of experiments and trials. Rather, its sole reason is some innate flaws and weaknesses of the servants, due to which they often require gradualism and training to accept a law. And since Allah the Exalted is extremely merciful to His servants, He chose to incorporate this gradualism and training into His law. Reflecting on the abrogating and abrogated commands of the Quran reveals that this gradualism and training necessitated different courses of action under different requirements. For example, in some situations, it required giving a temporary command in a specific matter to suit the demands of the society's initial conditions, and when the society reached its maturity, replacing that temporary command with a final and perfect command. For instance, initially, the command for a will (Wasiyyah) was given to protect the rights of heirs; to curb immorality, a reprimand similar to a community council (Panchayat) was instructed; the brotherhood between the Ansar and Muhajirun was elevated from a moral brotherhood to the status of legal brotherhood. But later, when the society stood on its own feet as an Islamic society, the final and definitive law of inheritance and the specific and absolute penal code for adultery abrogated those temporary laws and took their place. In some situations, it required that, keeping general human nature in view, a law should reach its final limit step by step. For example, since wine was ingrained in the nature of the Arabs, initially it was forbidden only for prayer times. Since fasting was a very difficult thing for a hot country like Arabia, initially room was kept to give a ransom (Fidyah) in case of travel and illness. But later, when dispositions became accustomed to these things, the command for the absolute prohibition of wine, the instruction to complete the number of days of Ramadan, and the abrogation of the permission for Fidyah perfected the Shariah in these chapters as well. After these commands, only a limited and conditional permission under necessity (Idtirar) remained. In some cases, its requirement was also that the Prophet (PBUH) was left free for some time to act upon a command of the previous Shariah, but later this permission was abrogated, and the independent command of the Islamic Shariah was given in its place. For example, in the matter of the Qiblah, the purpose, as clarified in the Quran, was to test the Muslims to see who is firm in their loyalty to God and the Messenger, and who is still a blind worshipper of their past traditions. Obviously, this test is a part of training. Similarly, in some situations, to compensate for the lack of manpower in the society, it required temporarily giving such commands that would enhance quality and create the capacity to bear a heavier burden in the state of numerical minority. For example, initially, the general Muslims were also commanded to strictly observe Tahajjud (night prayer); on the battlefield, one was made the equal of ten disbelievers; under the requirements of communal strengthening and purification, an instruction was given to give charity (Sadaqah) before having any private consultation with the Prophet (PBUH). Later, when the manpower of the Muslims increased and the temporary goal of community purification was achieved, these things were eased and brought to the general level that was already established for them in the Shariah.
We have only pointed to some principled points here. The intention here is not to exhaustively list all the abrogating and abrogated verses and explain their rationales. The detailed discussion under the abrogated verses, as mentioned, will come at their respective places.
From all this detail, the reality is well clarified that God's Shariah has reached its final stage of progress and perfection in the Holy Quran. Now, there is no room for any abrogation after this. In this Shariah, along with all the commands, concessions and allowances for difficult and compelling circumstances have also been explained. Therefore, there remains no justification to revert to abrogated commands on the excuse of changing circumstances. However, the work of abrogating the misguidances created by innovators will continue until the Day of Judgment, and in Islam, this work is entrusted to the scholars and reformers.

A Specific Aspect of the Refutation: Here too, the addressees are the same as in the verse above. However, keeping in mind the mindset hidden behind the aforementioned question, a little detail has been added to the answer. The real reason why the Jews were raising the issue of Naskh and creating doubts among the simple-minded people was that they had figured out that the abrogation of the Torah's commands and their replacement with other commands was not merely the abrogation of some commands of the Torah; rather, it also contained the implicit message of the Jews' dismissal from the position of leadership (Imamate) and the appointment and designation of another Ummah in their place. In reality, it was the grief and anger over this very thing that was eating them away, and to express it, they were using the question of Naskh as a veil. The Quran lifted this veil and gave them this answer: the dominion of the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah alone, and it is in His authority. He snatches from whom He wills, and He bestows upon whom He wills. Now, if you have proven yourself unworthy of the position to which He elevated you, and the demand of His wisdom is that He elevate someone else to this position in your place, then despite your grief and anger, this will definitely happen, and no protector or helper of yours can save you from this decision of God.

Meaning of the word 'Su'al' (Asking/Questioning): The word 'Su'al' contains several meanings. For example, to ask for, to request, to demand, to inquire, to interrogate, to question. In some cases, a question is also in the nature of an objection, which is why objecting is also included in its meaning. In some situations, it is in the nature of investigation; in this case, its preposition comes with 'an'. In some situations, a question is also in the nature of mockery; in this case, its preposition comes with 'ba', for example: 'Sa'ala sa'ilun bi'adhabin waqi'' (A requester asked for a punishment about to occur) (Al-Ma'arij: 1). In the Holy Quran, this word is used in all the aforementioned meanings. Here, the context of the speech is proof that this word is used in the sense of an objectionable question/demand.
A Warning to the Muslims: Here, the addressees are those weak people among the Muslims who, influenced by the aforementioned question planted by the Jews, would present it before the Prophet (PBUH) and thus represent the Jews among the Muslims in raising and spreading this question. The Quran already gave the answer to the question above so that the propaganda of the Jews could be countered, but just as it warned the Jews in the verse above, it warned those representing them among the Muslims here. These questions, in terms of their mindset and nature, are exactly like the questions the Jews used to ask Moses, and this path is not the path of faith and guidance; rather, it is the path of exchanging faith for disbelief. Those who adopt this path, just like the Jews, inevitably stray from the straight path. Since behind this question were actually the Jews, the Quran, by stating that similar questions were asked of Moses before, has very eloquently pointed towards them so that it becomes clear to them that Allah is not unaware of their whisperings and mischiefs.

This is a further warning that all these activities of the Jews are solely for the purpose of turning you away from faith and plunging you back into a state of disbelief. Do not think that all their running around is for your well-being, or that they consider your previous religion to be true and are supporting it, or that they have some misunderstanding about Islam and that's the reason. Rather, it is merely a fit of jealousy that has overcome them due to the instigation of their ego, despite the fact that the truth of Islam has become entirely clear to them. This warning was necessary because some good-hearted or naive Muslims might fall into the misconception that these People of the Book are so deeply concerned about their faith purely out of well-wishing for them or as a religious service. The Quran dispelled this misconception, stating that all of this is purely 'hasadam-min 'indi anfusihim' (out of envy from themselves). Meaning, it is not driven by any religious sentiment, but is merely the handiwork of the emotion of jealousy incited by the ego.
Fa'foo wasfahoo hatta ya'tiyallahu bi-amrih (So forgive and overlook until Allah brings His command): Warning to the Jews. One meaning of 'Afw' is to forgive from the heart, and another meaning is to overlook someone. For example, 'Yubayyinu lakum katheeram-mimma kuntum tukhfoona minal-kitabi wa ya'foo 'an katheer' (He makes clear to you much of what you used to conceal of the Scripture and overlooks much) (Al-Ma'idah: 15). 'Safh' means to turn a blind eye and overlook. There is a couplet by a Hamasi poet: 'Safahna 'an banee dhuhlin wa qulnal-qawmu ikhwan' (We overlooked the mischiefs of Banu Dhuhl and thought that these people are our own brothers). The meaning of the verse is: overlook the mischiefs of these Jews for a few more days, until Allah issues His decree regarding them. This entire verse is a threat and a warning to the Jews, and within the brevity of this 'bi-amrih' (His command) are hidden all those things which later manifested in the form of the command to fight the Jews, their defeat, their execution and exile, and the payment of Jizyah, etc.

Remedy for the Oppositions of the Antagonists: This is the remedy prescribed for the Muslims against the oppositions of the antagonists of Islam. If you wish to overcome these trials, establish prayer and keep giving Zakah. This alone will provide you with that spiritual and moral training which, on one hand, will make you completely immune to the whisperings of the opponents, and on the other hand, will turn you as a community into such a solid, cemented structure (Bunyan Marsoos) that no power will be able to shake you. In the Holy Quran, prayer and Zakah are declared the foundation of the entire religion, the basis of all training and reform, and the source of all power and strength. All other things are declared subordinate to them. In the Makkan Surahs, where the Prophet (PBUH) is advised to have patience and steadfastness against enemies, the emphasis is placed on prayer. In this very Surah Al-Baqarah, after the command to change the Qiblah, when a storm of opposition arose, it was said: 'Ya ayyuhal-ladheena aamanus-ta'eenoo bis-sabri was-salah innallaha ma'as-sabireen'. 'O you who have believed, seek help through patience and prayer. Indeed, Allah is with the patient.' (Al-Baqarah: 153). Similarly, those who, without solid training, were hastening for war and Jihad, were instructed to train themselves through prayer and Zakah: 'Kuffoo aydiyakum wa aqeemus-salata wa atuz-zakah' 'Restrain your hands [from fighting] and establish prayer and give Zakah'. In Surah Al-Hajj, after giving Muslims the command for Jihad, it instructed: 'Fa aqeemus-salata wa atuz-zakata wa'tasimoo billah'. 'So establish prayer and give Zakah and hold fast to Allah' (Al-Hajj: 78). Here too, the command for prayer and Zakah is from this very perspective. Further discussion on this will follow.

'Hūd' is the plural of 'Hā'id', the research of which has passed in verse 62.
The Joint Propaganda of the Jews and Christians to Mislead the Muslims: Just as the objection of Naskh was raised to create doubt and hesitation in the hearts of Muslims, this propaganda was also carried out by both the Jews and the Christians: that if there is any path to attaining salvation, it is for a person to adopt Judaism or Christianity; these two are divine religions, and in their presence, there is neither a need nor room for any new religion. The Jews and Christians were, in fact, mortal enemies of each other; bloodbaths constantly occurred between them due to religious differences. However, it seems that to oppose Islam, both had become very tolerant of each other. Both had jointly formed a united front and, speaking with one voice, propagated that whoever seeks salvation should become a Jew or a Christian. What is this new religion anyway? It is merely a trial. In their opposition to Islam, the Jews had adopted this attitude of tolerance even in the matter of the polytheists; the Christians, after all, were their own brethren. Thus, the Holy Quran mentions their hostility towards the truth in this way: 'Alam tara ilal-ladheena ootoo naseebam-minal-kitabi yu'minoona bil-jibti wat-taghooti wa yaqooloona lil-ladheena kafaroo ha'ula'i ahda minal-ladheena aamanoo sabeela'. 'Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Scripture, who believe in superstition and false objects of worship and say about the disbelievers, "These are better guided than the believers as to the way"?' (An-Nisa: 51). This propaganda must have been strengthened by the fact that the Arabs already held a good opinion of the People of the Book. Furthermore, in their support, they must have also been saying that the Quran itself does not deny that Judaism and Christianity are heavenly religions. For these reasons, the Quran refuted this in detail as well. It stated, 'Tilka amaniyyuhum', these are their wishful thoughts; meaning, these are merely their fabricated statements that they have concocted on their own without any authority or proof. God has not issued a decree in favor of anyone that whoever becomes a Jew or Christian gets Paradise. If they make this claim, they should present some proof from their Book for the truth of this claim. There were many such wishes and desires of theirs that they had nurtured in their hearts as religion and belief without any authority. Although the Quran mentioned only one here, by using the plural word it pointed to all of them. We have already presented the details of these 'Amaniyy' (wishful thoughts) while interpreting verses 78 to 81 of this very Surah.

The True Path of Salvation: Meaning, to attain salvation and be deserving of Paradise, being a Jew or a Christian is not the condition. Rather, it is that a person first becomes a 'Muslim', and secondly becomes a 'Muhsin' (doer of good). 'Islam' means that a person completely submits himself to God. Without making any distinction among His prophets and messengers, he subjects his entire life to His Shariah. The concept of 'Ihsan' is that he implements the commands of the Shariah with complete sincerity, complete honesty, and perfect righteousness. Those who fulfill the right of God's servitude and obedience in this way, for them is their reward with their Lord; for such people, there will be no fear, nor will they grieve. This is the teaching of all the Prophets and all heavenly scriptures, and this is the demand of intellect and nature. This entire theme has already passed in detail in verses 78 to 81 of this Surah. There we have discussed its various aspects with clarification.

The Mutual Warfare and Dispute of the Jews and Christians: Meaning, to oppose Islam, both Jews and Christians gathered on one platform and are very generously declaring each other saved and destined for Paradise. But away from this platform, the state of their mutual excommunication (Takfeer and Tafseeq) and warfare is such that the Jews do not acknowledge any foundation for the Christians, and the Christians do not acknowledge any foundation for the Jews, even though both claim to follow the same Book; the Torah is common to both. This made it clear that the alliance that has formed between them today is neither for the protection of religion, nor is it based on any sincerity and good intention; rather, it is purely the sentiment of enmity towards Islam that has united them. 'Alladheena la ya'lamoon' (Those who do not know) refers to the polytheists of the Children of Ishmael, because they were unlettered people unfamiliar with any Book and Shariah. Regarding them, it was said that they also said something similar to what these people said. Meaning, they too consider everyone except themselves to be upon falsehood. But to oppose Islam, today they too are included in this united front. They (the Jews and Christians) are performing this 'service to religion' while claiming knowledge and action, and these (the polytheists) have joined the fray without any knowledge whatsoever. The words 'Kadhalika' (Likewise) and 'Mithla qawlihim' (similar to what they say) apparently seem to carry the same meaning, but reflecting upon them reveals these realities: one shows the commonality of the motive and sentiment, the other shows the expression. Meaning, in both intention and action, these people are walking in the footsteps of those very Jews and Christians. Finally, as a warning, it was stated that the decision of their dispute will now happen in the court of God in the Hereafter. This also holds consolation for the Prophet (PBUH) that in this dispute, you are only responsible for preaching the truth. You bear no responsibility beyond this.

Destroying Each Other's Places of Worship: This points to the exploits of these claimants of Paradise that they carried out in the course of destroying and ruining each other's places of worship due to mutual spite and enmity. History proves that very bloody wars have taken place between the Jews and Christians in Jerusalem as well to prevent each other from remembrance and worship. Outside as well, wherever and whenever either of them got the opportunity, they tried to destroy the places of worship of the opposing faction. Furthermore, history proves that the Christians tried to prevent people from the pilgrimage (Hajj) to the House of Allah (Kaaba). But when they failed in this attempt, Abraha marched on Mecca and intended to demolish the Kaaba, in retribution for which Allah's punishment came upon him and his armies. The purpose of pointing to these events is to show what the state of mutual prejudices has been among those who have become so united in their opposition to Islam today. And how dark the deeds of these monopolists of guidance and piety are in the matter of the mosques of God. At the same time, to clarify the status and rank of the mosques of Allah, it was stated that the most unjust in the world are those claimants of guidance and piety who prevent those who remember Allah from His mosques, and who seek their destruction. The house that is built for the worship of God is the House of God. It is not befitting for anyone to enter God's house with the audacity to destroy it. The only way to enter the House of Allah is that whoever enters it, enters it fearing and trembling. Regarding those who violate this, it is stated that for them is disgrace in this world and a great punishment in the Hereafter. Under this very principle of the sanctity of the mosques of Allah, Muslims were not permitted to demolish or desecrate the churches and places of worship of the Jews and Christians even in a state of war with them. This point is particularly noteworthy for those Muslims who, purely under factional prejudices, prevent those holding a slightly different school of thought (Maslak) from their mosques, and sometimes even dare to desecrate the mosques of those holding other schools of thought.

Tafseer of Ayah 115: Pointing to the Cause of Dispute: This points to the cause of dispute and disagreement that led to the desecration of places of worship between the Jews and Christians. The Qiblah (direction of prayer) for both Jews and Christians was Jerusalem, but the Christians specifically chose its eastern side for their Qiblah. The reason for this might have been that the section where Mary (Maryam AS) observed seclusion (I'tikaf) was in this direction. The map of Jerusalem from that era also shows that the section designated for women's worship was on that side, and a similar hint emerges from the Quran as well. In Surah Maryam, it is stated: 'Wadh-kur fil-kitabi Maryama idhintabadhat min ahliha makanan sharqiyya' 'And mention, [O Muhammad], in the Book [the story of] Mary, when she withdrew from her family to a place toward the east.' (Maryam: 16). In opposition to this, the Jews must have chosen its western side, and then this division inside Jerusalem extended outside as well. As a result, both parties desecrated each other's places of worship quite ruthlessly. The Holy Quran, while pointing to this cause of difference and dispute here, also pointed to its absurdity: whether east or west, both directions belong to Allah alone. Whichever direction a human turns his face, if he is attentive towards God, his face is towards God Himself. The meaning is that if the Jews and Christians made this thing a cause for head-bashing and the destruction of places of worship and mosques, this is their ignorance and foolishness. No direction or orientation is exclusive to God. By establishing Jerusalem as the Qiblah, whichever direction they faced, they faced towards God Himself. God's power and the vastness of His knowledge encompass everything. 'Har ja kunam sajdah, ba-daan astaan rasad' (Wherever I prostrate, it reaches that very threshold). This discussion will come with more detail further ahead under the verses regarding the change of the Qiblah.

Meaning of 'Walad' (Child/Son): Above, a reference was made to the exploits of those forming a front against Islam, which they carried out in connection with destroying God's mosques. Now, a hint is also made towards their polytheistic beliefs, so that this aspect of these claimants to the monopoly of guidance and salvation also comes to the fore: what level they are at in terms of belief. In this regard, it was stated that these people hold the belief that God has sons and daughters. The Jews called Ezra (Uzair) the son of Allah, the Christians called the Messiah the son of Allah, and the polytheists of Arabia declared angels to be the daughters of God. Refuting all of them, it was said 'Subhanah', God is pure and far exalted above all such attributions. Nothing is a partner or sharer in His essence, His attributes, or His rights in any aspect whatsoever. Rather, all things in the heavens and the earth are His creation and property. No one has the status to be free from the collar of His servitude and obedience; rather, all are subservient to His command.

Research on 'Bada'a': 'Bada'a' means bringing a thing into existence from non-existence and inventing it without any prior material or precedent. From this comes the word 'Bid'ah', which means inventing something in religion for which there is no precedent, example, or source/origin. 'Badee'' is on the pattern of 'Fa'eel' from this root, and it is in the meaning of the active participle (Fa'il - Originator/Creator).
Further Clarification of Allah's Transcendence: This is a further clarification of the theme of God's transcendence in the preceding verse. These sons and daughters that have been assumed for God are based on the illusion that just as others need assistants and partners to manage and administer their affairs, God too needs partners and assistants. However, God is completely independent and free of such partners and assistants. He brought the heavens and the earth into existence solely by His own power and wisdom, and when He decides on a matter, He simply says to it, "Be," and it becomes. What connection do offspring and children have with such an independent, self-sufficient, and absolutely powerful Being!

Here Ishmaelites (the polytheists of Arabia) are meant. The objections and whisperings of the People of the Book were mentioned above. Now, mentioning some demands of the third pillar of this united 'opposition front'—i.e., the polytheists—their answer is given. Their first demand was that Muhammad (PBUH) claims God speaks to him. If so, why did God choose only him from among us to speak to? After all, we are the chiefs and leaders of the Quraish, and in influence and power, we are far higher than Muhammad (PBUH); why doesn't God speak to us? The Quran has answered this demand in certain places. For example, in one place it is stated: 'And it is not for any human being that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a partition...' Then regarding revelation and messengership, it clarified that not every common person is worthy of this position; 'Allah knows best where He places His message.' But here, this specific demand has not been answered. In our view, not answering it points to the fact that this demand is so insolent and foolish that not answering it is its very answer. Reflect on what a devastating blow this silence of the Quran on this occasion must have dealt to the Quraish leaders' arrogance of chieftainship. The second demand was: why doesn't a sign come to us? By 'sign', they meant some sign in the nature of a tangible miracle, seeing which every person would cry out that the one showing this sign is indeed the sent one of God and His Messenger. For example, an angel should accompany this Messenger proclaiming his messengership everywhere; or the dead should come back to life by his command; or mountains should start moving by his gesture; or a desert should turn into a garden at his wish; or if nothing else, at least a sample of the very punishment he threatens them with every day should manifest upon his indication. In response to this demand, the first thing stated was that the kind of sign they are demanding is exactly the kind of sign previous nations demanded from their respective messengers. After the truth became completely clear, just to frustrate the messenger, they too demanded such signs, and these people too, despite understanding the truth, are making this demand purely out of a desire to frustrate him. Then it was said that their hearts have become like the hearts of those people. Meaning, the darkness of hard-heartedness, rebellion, and hostility towards the truth that is overshadowing their hearts as well will inevitably result in a punishment from God coming upon them similar to the punishments that came upon those people. The second thing stated was that as far as the truth of your messengership and your message is concerned, We have clearly explained its proofs from the horizons, from within the self, from the sky, from the earth, from history, from remnants—from every angle—in the Quran. These proofs are so clear that after them, no need remains for any sign or miracle. But these proofs are useful for those who want to believe; those who do not want to believe cannot be convinced by anything in the world. Such people do not believe even after seeing the punishment, until that punishment breaks their backs. The third thing stated was: 'Inna arsalnaka bil-haqqi basheeran wa nadheeran wa la tus'alu 'an as-habil-jaheem' (Indeed, We have sent you, [O Muhammad], with the truth as a bringer of good tidings and a warner, and you will not be asked about the companions of Hellfire). We have sent you with the truth so that you give the glad tidings of salvation and success to those who accept it, and warn those who deny it of the evil end of this denial. After performing this duty of giving glad tidings and warnings, your responsibility ends. It is not your responsibility to show signs and miracles according to their desires in compliance with their demands. You will be questioned about the fulfillment of your duty of messengership; you will certainly not be questioned about why these people destined for Hell went to Hell and why they did not believe.
All these points mentioned above will be described in various styles within the narratives of past nations in the Makkan Surahs, which is why we do not detail them much here.

The True Disease of the Jews and Christians: After making him despair of the attitude of the polytheists, the attitude of the Jews and Christians is clarified to the Prophet (PBUH): that they too are not going to be pleased with you until you become a follower of their religious community (Millat). That is, until you adopt Judaism or Christianity. Because for them, the issue is not merely the clarification of truth and the manifestation of proofs, but rather stagnation upon their own respective ways. They are worshippers of their own desires more than they are worshippers of the truth. And for you, after 'Al-Ilm'—that is, the knowledge of revelation—has come to you from God, there remains no question of following their desires and innovations. Therefore, give them this decisive answer: the true guidance is that which comes from Allah. So now that Allah's guidance has come to me, how can I leave it and follow any other path? Here, the paths adopted by the Jews and Christians have been described with the word 'Ahwa' (desires). The reason is that after guidance has come from God, remaining stubbornly fixed on any other path is, in reality, following one's own desires. In 'Wa la'in-ittaba'ta' (And if you were to follow), although the address is apparently towards the Prophet (PBUH), the warning and reprimand within it are directed towards the Jews and Christians. Many examples of this style of address will be found in the Holy Quran. The word 'Millat' used in this verse originally means 'way/method', but it refers to a person's or group's way of life whose foundation is based on religion and religious traditions.

Mention of the Righteous People of the Book: After expressing disappointment with the general attitude of the People of the Book, mention is made of those among them who truly believed in their Book. Regarding them, it is stated that they will believe in this divine guidance that you are presenting to them. In our view, there are several reasons for taking this to mean the righteous among the People of the Book. First, it is said about them, 'Yatlunahu haqqa tilawatihi' (They recite it as it should be recited).
