In our view, this pronoun is in the objective case expressing a state, and the purpose is to show that their condition from the beginning has been that they truly valued the Book given to them. They have never been like those whom the Quran describes as 'Kamathalil himari yahmilu asfara' (Like a donkey carrying books)—carrying a load of books on their backs but completely unaware of what is inside them. Instead, they have been reciting it continuously with reflection and contemplation, and their recitation was for seeking guidance, not merely to invent arguments to support their fabricated desires. Second, it is foretold about them that they will believe in the guidance sent down to them through the final Messenger. Third, the phrase 'Atainahum al-Kitab' (We gave them the Book) is used here for these People of the Book. Quranic parallels show that this phrasing is generally used for the People of the Book in contexts of praise. Consider a few examples: (1) 'Those to whom We gave the Book recognize him as they recognize their own sons' (Al-Baqarah: 146). (2) 'And those to whom We gave the Book know that it has been sent down from your Lord in truth' (Al-An'am: 114). (3) 'And those to whom We gave the Book rejoice at what has been revealed to you' (Ar-Ra'd: 36). (4) 'Those to whom We gave the Book before it, they believe in it' (Al-Qasas: 52).
The aspect of care and favor evident in the words 'Alladhina Atainahum al-Kitab' (Those to whom We gave the Book) as opposed to 'Alladhina Utu al-Kitab' (Those who were given the Book) cannot be hidden from those familiar with the usage of active and passive voices and the literary nuances of both styles in the Arabic language. In this style, the active voice reveals the reality that Allah Almighty truly gave the Book only to those who valued it; as for those who did not value it, it is as if God never gave them the Book. Due to this difference, the passive phrase 'Utu al-Kitab' is rarely used in contexts of praise. 'Ula'ika yu'minuna bihi' is the predicate of 'Alladhina atainahumu-l-kitaba yatlunahu haqqa tilawatihi'. Meaning, those People of the Book who have been fulfilling the true right of their Book are the ones who will believe in this guidance of Allah that the Prophet (PBUH) is presenting to them. The reason for this is that it is the Sunnah (practice) of Allah to bestow blessings in His spiritual favors only upon those who value them; as for those who do not, let alone being given more, even what they have is taken away. This very promise was made by Allah to Prophet Abraham (PBUH) regarding the final Sharia, that only the good people among your descendants will partake in it, while the wicked will be deprived. Then, Allah made it clear to Prophet Moses (PBUH) that only those who remain steadfast in piety will believe in the final Prophet. Jesus Christ also clarified this reality in various ways. The details of these matters will come in their appropriate places.
The upcoming subject, Verses 122 to 141: The Story of Prophet Abraham (PBUH): From the previous discussions, it has become quite clear that the biggest obstacle for the People of the Book, especially the Jews, in accepting Islam was their arrogance that they were the descendants of Prophet Abraham (PBUH), and therefore, guidance was exclusively theirs, and true religion was exclusively theirs. Outside their circle, they neither believed in salvation for anyone else nor held the concept of any other prophethood or messengership. The only way to attain salvation and guidance in their view was for a person to become a Jew or a Christian. The Quran refuted this presumption of theirs from various angles. Now, to further refute their false beliefs, that part of the life story of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) and his sons is being presented to them which supports the call and messengership of the Prophet (PBUH) and completely demolishes all the claims of the Jews, Christians, and polytheists. Since Prophet Abraham (PBUH) was the supreme ancestor and spiritual leader of both the Israelites and the Ishmaelites, this part of history serves as an equal proof against all. Viewed from this angle, one will feel that the argument with the Israelites (and implicitly the Ishmaelites) that began at the start of this Surah has reached its absolute climax here. The details of the points the Quran has clarified in light of this history will come under the exegesis of the verses, but we point out the main fundamental principles here so that the order and continuity of the discourse remain in sight. These principles are as follows: 1. The position of leadership and Imamate granted to Prophet Abraham (PBUH) by Allah was not given as an inheritance, but it was a gift from Allah. First, Allah thoroughly tested his obedience and loyalty through various trials; when he fully passed this test, he was granted this position. This position is based entirely on qualities; it has absolutely no connection to lineage or family. Therefore, only those among his descendants will be worthy of this position who possess the qualities befitting it. Covenant-breakers and disobedient people cannot be entitled to it. 2. Allah established the House of Allah (Baitullah) as the center for all the descendants of Abraham, commanded it to be made the Qibla (direction of prayer), and entrusted its custodianship to Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael (PBUT). 3. At the time of building this House, Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael prayed to raise a Muslim Ummah from their progeny and to send a Messenger among them from themselves. 4. This Prophet (Muhammad PBUH) is the manifestation of that Abrahamic prayer and the caller to that Abrahamic religion. Therefore, those who claim to follow the religion of Abraham while avoiding his call are making fools of themselves. 5. Prophet Abraham and Prophet Jacob (PBUT) bequeathed this very religion of Islam to their respective offspring, and Jacob's offspring made a covenant with him to live and die upon this very religion. 6. The demand of all these events and facts is that the People of the Book, instead of being entangled in Jewish or Christian prejudice, should follow this religion of Abraham to which Muhammad (PBUH) is calling. They should make no distinction between the Prophets of God but adopt this religion of Islam, which is the common religion of all Prophets and Messengers. Those who want to dye themselves in the color of Allah should adopt the color of Islam, as this is the color of Allah, not Judaism or Christianity. Those who want to adopt a color other than this are trying to form their own faction apart from Allah and His Messengers. 7. The claim that Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the other prophets in their lineage (PBUT) were Jews or Christians is completely baseless. Those making such claims are covering up the truth. Allah is more knowledgeable about the religion of these prophets than these claimants. 8. The final point, stated twice as a concluding refrain with a brief interval in this discourse, is that the forefathers upon whom you rely have lived their lives and taken their deeds with them; neither will you get credit for their achievements, nor will you be questioned about any of their deeds. Keeping these points in mind, now recite the following verses.
Verses 122 and 123: Both of these verses have already appeared above with a minor change in wording, and we have discussed them in full detail there. Please refer to the Tafseer of verses 47 and 48.

The Purpose of Trials: 'Ibtila' means to test and examine. This testing is a Sunnah (practice) of Allah for the moral training of His servants. It is through this that the capabilities Allah has endowed within them emerge and develop, and it separates the genuine from the fake. This examination occurs through mild and harsh, cold and hot, pleasing and painful, encouraging and challenging circumstances, and in no case is its purpose to afflict the servant with pain; rather, as we indicated, it is solely to awaken and nurture their capabilities. A brief indication is sufficient here; detailed discussions on this will come later.
The Meaning of 'Kalimat': 'Kalimat' is the plural of 'Kalimah'. It can mean a single word or a complete statement. Here, 'Kalimat' refers to the commands of Allah that He gave to Prophet Abraham to test his resolve and steadfastness, which he obeyed without hesitation. For example, in obedience to God's command, he called to prayer right inside his nation's idol temple and shattered the idols that had been worshipped for centuries. He was thrown into the fire for the crime of insulting the ancestral religion; he fearlessly jumped into that fire. A tyrannical king tried to turn him away from the true religion; he left him speechless with Abrahamic reasoning. He was commanded to leave his family, property, nation, and homeland to migrate; he left everything and migrated. He was ordered to run a knife across the neck of his beloved and only son in a foreign wilderness; he rolled up his sleeves for this sacrifice without hesitation and laid his thirteen-year-old son face down. Every page of his life is illuminated with such magnificent feats of dedication and self-sacrifice in obeying the divine command. We have only pointed out a few events as examples. The word 'Kalimat' that Allah has used here to express His commands and instructions contains a special point of eloquence. That is, the word 'Kalimah' carries a kind of brevity and ambiguity. Like the word 'Kun' (Be), this word presents a mandatory command to the addressee, but it does not necessarily explain its philosophy, its reward, or its prize. Such commands are the hardest test of loyalty and obedience. Therefore, the servant who passes such a test receives a tremendous reward. For example, Allah commanded Prophet Abraham (PBUH) in a dream as a sign to sacrifice his son, without explaining its wisdom or stating its reward. Had Prophet Abraham (PBUH) wanted, he could have treated the dream merely as a dream, or he could have interpreted it differently. But just as everything in this universe obeys God's word 'Kun' without questioning its philosophy or reward, Prophet Abraham (PBUH) obeyed every word of his Lord without asking for its philosophy or reward. Commanded to jump into the fire—he jumped. Commanded to leave his nation and homeland—he left. Commanded to run a knife over his son's neck—he laid him down. Due to the specific nature of these testing commands, the Quran referred to them with the word 'Kalimat'.
The Trial of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) Sacrificing His Son: Though every one of these tests was extremely arduous, the test of sacrificing his son was such that let alone passing it, even imagining it was a massive trial. But when Prophet Abraham (PBUH) passed this as well, Allah promised him: 'Inni ja'iluka lin-nasi imama' (Indeed, I will make you a leader for the people). This single promise actually contains two promises simultaneously: first, that great nations would emerge from Abraham's lineage; second, that Abraham would be the leader of them all. He became entitled to this great reward because, for the sake of Allah, he not only left his family and nation but also became ready to sacrifice his only son in a desolate wilderness, a son who was the sole center of all his hopes in his old age and loneliness. Although the Jews have heavily altered this specific part of Prophet Abraham's biography in the Torah, this promise still exists with slight verbal variations. See the Book of Genesis, Chapter 22: 'And the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time, And said, By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD, for because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son: That in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand which is upon the sea shore; and thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies; And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice' (15-18). In fulfilling this promise, Allah raised great nations from the lineages of both Isaac and Ishmael, whose supreme ancestor and spiritual leader was unanimously Abraham. Then, the chain of prophethood and messengership continued among them. Magnificent kings were born among them who conquered the gates of their enemies. Then, in one of those branches, the Seal of the Prophets (PBUH) was sent, through whom the entire world was blessed with faith and guidance.
Polytheists are Excluded from this Promise: When Allah made this promise to Prophet Abraham (PBUH), he asked whether this covenant of leadership was solely for him or if his descendants were also included. In response, it was declared: 'La yanalu 'ahdi al-zalimin' (My covenant does not include the wrongdoers). In the Quran, 'wrongdoers' (Zalimin) does not only mean those who oppress others, but primarily refers to those who fall into shirk (polytheism) and disbelief, thereby wronging their own souls. For example: 'Faminhum zalimun linafsihi waminhum muqtasid' (Then among them is he who wrongs himself, and among them is he who is moderate) (Fatir: 32); 'Wamin dhurriyatihima muhsinun wazalimun linafsihi mubin' (And among their descendants is the doer of good and the clearly unjust to himself) (As-Saffat: 113). The meaning is that those among your descendants who remain steadfast on your path and firm on My given Sharia and guidance will be the inheritors of this leadership after you. But those who break the covenant, disobey, and follow the path of Satan will have no share in this leadership. This clarification is made here so that it becomes evident to both the Israelites and the Ishmaelites that the pride they take in being Abraham's descendants—due to which they consider themselves absolved of all responsibilities of faith and action—is completely false. Those who have fallen into shirk and disbelief have no share in Abraham's inheritance. Allah made this reality clear to Prophet Abraham on the very day He exalted him to this position of leadership. We believe that the reference from the Torah we quoted above must have originally contained this clarification as well, but since this went against the desires of the Jews, just as they made many other alterations in the events of this series, finding it contrary to their wishes, they deleted this clarification too. Our respected teacher Imam Farahi (RA) has exposed these alterations in his treatise 'Az-Zabih' (The Sacrificed). Those seeking details must read that treatise.

Bait refers to Baitullah: 'Bait' refers to Baitullah, i.e., the Kaaba. In the Quran, this word in this form is used exclusively for the Kaaba. In the Book of Genesis in the Torah (Chapter 12), it is referred to as 'Bethel'. In Hebrew, 'El' means Allah.
The Meaning of 'Mathaba': 'Mathaba' means a center and place of return, to which everyone turns, with which everyone is connected, which is everyone's focal point and everyone's Qibla.
The Meaning of 'Lin-Nas': Here, 'Lin-Nas' (for the people) refers to the same people mentioned in 'Inni ja'iluka lin-nasi imama' (I will make you a leader for the people). That is, all the descendants of Abraham over whom Prophet Abraham (PBUH) was granted leadership, regardless of whether they are from the lineage of Isaac or Ishmael. Just as it was decided to make Prophet Abraham (PBUH) the leader of all his descendants, Allah also decided that the House built for the worship of God would be the center and Qibla for all the progeny of Abraham. And then, through the descendants of Ishmael, as mentioned later, all the nations of the world would share in the blessings of this House.
Mention of the Kaaba in the Torah: Ustadh Imam Maulana Farahi presents his research on this issue as follows: A study of the Torah reveals that the Jews were commanded from the beginning to face the direction of Makkah for their major sacrifices. The detail of this summary is that for a sacrifice to be valid, it had to be presented before the Lord in the temple. As we mentioned in Chapter 2, Letter 'Ya', the sacrifice they called 'Holy of Holies' had to face south. Similarly, the annual sacrifice, which they considered the greatest, also faced the south. The Jews were either unaware of the original secret of this matter—as we hinted in Chapter 2—or they intentionally did not want to dig into it, preferring, according to their habit, to keep it veiled ('It is not wise for the secret to fall out of the veil'). However, it is proven with absolute certainty that the direction of their Tabernacle was towards the north from the beginning. See Exodus 27:9, the court of the tabernacle should be built facing the south to receive blessings. Also, in Exodus 40:22-24, it says: 'And he put the table in the tent of the congregation, upon the side of the tabernacle northward, without the vail. And he set the bread in order upon it before the LORD... And he put the candlestick in the tent of the congregation, over against the table, on the side of the tabernacle southward.' In our view, the underlying philosophy of this entire arrangement is that whoever comes before the Lord should face south, meaning towards Makkah and the Abrahamic altar. This is further supported by the fact that inside the tent, the holy dwelling was also situated towards the south, and the altar was in front of it towards the door. Therefore, a person presenting the 'Holy of Holies' sacrifice would stand on the northern side of the altar so that he would face the divine dwelling. This meant he would inevitably be facing the Kaaba, near which is Marwah, which holds the honor of being the first altar, and near which is the dwelling of Ishmael. (See the treatise 'Az-Zabih', Chapter 15).
From this detail, it becomes clear that just as the Qibla for our prayers and sacrifices is the Kaaba, from the very beginning, it was decided that the Kaaba would be the Qibla for the worship and sacrifice of all the descendants of Abraham. Therefore, their Tabernacle was built facing this direction, and later, Bait al-Maqdis (Solomon's Temple) was also built facing this direction. But out of sheer prejudice, the Jews tried to veil this reality.
Further clarifying this, Maulana Farahi states: 'Our aforementioned claims are further supported by the fact that Allah made the dwelling of Prophet Ishmael (PBUH) the Qibla for all the descendants of Abraham. The detail of this is that Prophet Abraham (PBUH) settled his other offspring to the east and north of Arabia and made the dwelling of Prophet Ishmael their Qibla. It is proven from the Torah that they were settled in front of all their brethren.' Genesis 25:18 says: 'And they dwelt from Havilah unto Shur, that is before Egypt, as thou goest toward Assyria: and he died in the presence of all his brethren.' And Genesis 16:12 says: 'And he will be a wild man; his hand will be against every man, and every man's hand against him; and he shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren.'
The interpretation we have given of 'dwelling in the presence of all his brethren' is the only correct one possible, because it is known that all of Prophet Abraham's (PBUH) children, except the Ishmaelites, settled in the east and north. Thus, Prophet Ishmael can only be 'in front of' all of them if it is accepted that his settlement was in the direction of their Qibla. In our view, there is no room for hesitation in accepting this, because it is known that Allah made Prophet Abraham the Imam (leader) of all, and after him, Prophet Ishmael inherited this Imamate. The Quran has made certain references to this matter. (Maulana then quoted the verse currently under discussion; see Maulana Farahi's treatise 'Az-Zabih', Chapter 16).
'Wattakhidhu min maqami Ibrahima musalla' (And take from the standing place of Abraham a place of prayer). The term 'Maqam Ibrahim' appears in the verse. What does 'Maqam' mean? Two views are reported from the scholars of Tafseer. One group believes it refers to the specific stone upon which Prophet Abraham reportedly stood to build the Kaaba. The second group believes it refers to the entire Haram area. This group takes the word 'Maqam' to mean a dwelling or settlement rather than a specific standing place. (This group includes great exegesis scholars like Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, and Ata). In our view, this interpretation is correct. The broad and comprehensive aspect of this interpretation, which is particularly important, is that in the context of the discourse, it more clearly highlights the purpose for which this was said here. It is being established here that this very House has been the Qibla for all the descendants of Abraham, because this is the House that Prophet Abraham, in obedience to Allah's command, first built in this settlement where he took residence with Prophet Ishmael after their migration.
The Disputed Issue Between Us and the Jews: This is a major point of contention between us and the Jews. To completely sever Prophet Abraham's connection to the Kaaba and the altar of Marwah, the Jews have made absurd alterations in both the story of the sacrifice and the narrative of his migration. Thus, they attempted to prove that the son Abraham sacrificed was Isaac, not Ishmael; that the place of sacrifice was Mount Moriah (Jerusalem), not Marwah; that the house he built for the worship of God was Bait al-Maqdis, not Baitullah; and that the place he settled after migration was Canaan, not the vicinity of the Kaaba. Since the only source to verify or refute these claims was the Torah, and the Jews, as we mentioned, altered it to suit their desires, uncovering the original facts was a very difficult task. However, our teacher, Maulana Farahi, has completely torn away the veil of all these Jewish alterations using arguments from the Torah itself in his treatise 'Az-Zabih'. He has proven from the Torah's own statements that after leaving his homeland, Prophet Abraham left Isaac's mother in Canaan and stayed with Ishmael and his mother in the wilderness of Beersheba. This was an uninhabited place, so he dug seven wells there and planted trees. It was here that he received the command in a dream to sacrifice his only son, and he took Prophet Ishmael to the hill of Marwah and fulfilled this command. He settled Prophet Ishmael near this hill. Then, returning from there, he went to Beersheba and chose a place to stay that was close to the Kaaba and from where it was easily possible to visit Isaac from time to time.
Maulana has proven all these points with absolutely irrefutable arguments from the Torah. Since quoting all the original excerpts for every question would be lengthy, we have just summarized the discussion in our own words. Those seeking details should study Maulana's aforementioned treatise. Obviously, when Prophet Abraham resided in this area and not in Syria, he should have also been commanded to build a center for prayer in this very area. Thus, in obedience to this command, he built the Baitullah, which is mentioned in the Book of Genesis as Bethel. Baitullah and Bethel have exactly the same meaning; 'El' in Hebrew means Allah. If the Jews interpret this Bethel as Bait al-Maqdis, then setting aside the fact that Prophet Abraham never made that land his residence, the biggest thing refuting the Jews' claim is that Bait al-Maqdis was unanimously built hundreds of years after Prophet Abraham (PBUH) during the era of Prophet Solomon (PBUH). Therefore, due to this antiquity and primacy of the Kaaba, the Quran has also referred to it as the 'Ancient House' (Bait al-Atiq) and the 'First House' (Awwal Bait): 'Indeed, the first House established for mankind was that at Bakkah - blessed and a guidance for the worlds. In it are clear signs, the standing place of Abraham. And whoever enters it shall be safe' (Al Imran: 96-97).
Referring to Baitullah as 'Musalla': Here, by referring to Baitullah with the word 'Musalla' (place of prayer), the intention is to point to the original purpose of building this House: that it will be a center for prayer. When Prophet Abraham (PBUH) settled Prophet Ishmael in its blessed vicinity, his prayer was: 'Rabbana liyuqimu as-salah' (O our Lord, I have settled them here so that they may establish prayer). But during the pre-Islamic era of ignorance (Jahiliyyah), its polytheistic and innovating custodians turned it into a hub of innovations, and their prayer became a pagan ritual of whistling and clapping. If you consider this aspect, you will realize that the word 'Musalla' here hints that both branches of Abraham's lineage wasted the fundamental purpose of their Qibla, and now God has sent this Prophet to renew its original purpose. It should be noted that just as the Jews removed the mention of the Kaaba from their religious literature, they also completely removed Salah (the Islamic concept of prayer). If they have anything, it is sacrifice. The original status of their temple is also not that of a prayer center, but of an altar. We sometimes think that one reason they were deprived of the blessing of prayer is that they broke their connection with their original Qibla, the Kaaba.
(Bakkah means city; this is the word used for Makkah in ancient scriptures. The Jews altered it to the Valley of Baca; we will discuss this alteration under the relevant verse).
'Wa 'ahidna ila Ibrahima...' (And We made a covenant with Abraham...). When 'Ahida' is used with the preposition 'Ila', it means to place a responsibility on someone or to bind them to a condition. For example: 'And We had already taken a promise from Adam before, but he forgot; and We found not in him determination' (Ta-Ha: 115). 'Did I not enjoin upon you, O children of Adam, that you not worship Satan?' (Ya-Sin: 60). Thus, the meaning of 'Wa 'ahidna ila Ibrahima wa Isma'il' is that Allah placed the responsibility of the custodianship of the Kaaba upon them and bound them to the condition that they keep this House pure and clean for those who circumambulate, those who stay in I'tikaf, and those who bow and prostrate.
The Purpose of Purifying Baitullah: The purpose of keeping it clean is obviously to keep it free from all things that contradict the purpose of building this House. Whether it be filth and impurity that disturbs the minds of the worshippers, or the clamor of people engaged in idle play that disrupts their concentration, or idols and statues that turn the House of God into a stronghold of polytheism and idolatry. Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael were tasked with keeping this House free from all these things. History bears witness that they fulfilled this responsibility. But later, when their descendants fell into polytheism, contrary to this condition of custodianship, they placed idols in every corner of it and cruelly and ruthlessly expelled those who wanted to repopulate it with the chants of God's remembrance, the liveliness of Tawaf and I'tikaf, and the prostrations of bowing and falling to the ground. The Quran has pointed out this reality of the early history of the Kaaba here so that the Quraysh understand their responsibilities regarding this House. But when they refused to understand them, Allah finally announced their dismissal from the position of its custodianship: 'It is not for the polytheists to maintain the mosques of Allah while witnessing against themselves with disbelief. For those, their deeds have become worthless, and in the Fire they will abide eternally. The mosques of Allah are only to be maintained by those who believe in Allah and the Last Day and establish prayer and give zakah and do not fear except Allah' (At-Tawbah: 17-18).
The Meaning of 'Tawaf': Here, the command was given to dedicate this House for three things: Tawaf (circumambulation), I'tikaf (seclusion for worship), and Ruku' and Sujud (bowing and prostration). Tawaf means to walk around the Kaaba. The Sunnah of the Prophet (PBUH) has clarified the method that is the original Abrahamic way. Tawaf is essentially a type of prayer, but this prayer can only be performed near the Kaaba; it cannot be performed anywhere else. Because of this uniqueness, it is mentioned first. While staying within the bounds of dignity and respect, the emotions of divine love that emerge in this prayer are specific only to it. The tale of the moth and the flame becomes a reality in Tawaf, provided a person has a spark of living faith.
The Meaning of 'I'tikaf': 'Aakif' comes from 'Ukuf', the essence of which is to detach oneself from other things and hold fast to one specific thing. From this comes I'tikaf, which is the worship of meditation, reflection, and remembrance. When a servant cuts himself off from everything to seclude himself for the remembrance of his Lord, this is I'tikaf. Its correct form was clarified by the Prophet (PBUH) through his Sunnah. Just as Tawaf possesses a special quality for arousing emotions of divine love, I'tikaf possesses a special quality for anchoring the mind and heart on the remembrance of Allah.
The Meaning of 'Ruku' and 'Sujud': 'Rukka'' is the plural of 'Raki'' (one who bows), and 'Sujud' is the plural of 'Sajid' (one who prostrates). The linguistic investigation of Ruku' and Sujud has been discussed under the exegesis of verses 34 to 42. Here, both these words have been used to denote prayer (Salah). Expressing Salah through Ruku' and Sujud highlights two important facts. First, both of these are among the oldest and most important pillars of prayer. Whatever changes may have occurred in the outward form of prayer, just as these two elements are included in our prayers, they were also included in the Abrahamic prayer. Second, prayer does not merely require remembrance and reflection; its specific form and posture are also required, and the true beauty of its form lies in its Ruku' and Sujud.

Two specific issues of the Haram territory: 'Rabbij'al hadha baladan aminan warzuq ahlahu minath-thamarat. My Lord, make this a secure city and provide its people with fruits.' 'Amin' means secure and peaceful. Prophet Abraham made this supplication for the land where he settled Prophet Ishmael (PBUH) and where he built the Haram. This area, as has been clarified, was completely devoid of civilization, culture, population, and fertility. Nomadic tribes used to move back and forth with the changing seasons in search of water and pastures. The means of livelihood were either herding, hunting, or looting. For this reason, two issues were particularly crucial in this region during the time of Prophet Abraham (PBUH): one was security, and the other was food. Abraham's aforementioned prayer was for these two very things. The way Allah Almighty accepted this prayer and the blessings that manifested for Abraham's progeny and the inhabitants of this region is a living, tangible historical reality that even the staunchest opponent cannot deny. Furthermore, the wondrous, faith-inspiring fact is that Allah bestowed both these things upon the people exclusively through Baitullah (the Kaaba). They never needed to seek any other medium or means besides this door. When the Quran refers to this House as 'Mubarak' (a source of goodness and blessing), this is also one aspect of it.
In what ways was Prophet Abraham's (PBUH) prayer fulfilled? Now let us see in what ways this prayer of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) was fulfilled for the inhabitants of this land. The very first thing is that Allah made not only Baitullah but also the entire territory where Baitullah is located a sacred sanctuary. Fighting, attacking someone, or killing anyone within it was completely forbidden. Whoever entered it entered the protection of God. No one had the right to harm them in any way. Outside its borders, there was constant danger, but inside, the Lord of Abraham created absolute peace. It was even forbidden to harm any animal within its boundaries. Allah mentioned this very favor addressing the Quraysh in these words: 'Have they not seen that We made [Makkah] a safe sanctuary, while people are being taken away all around them?' (Al-'Ankabut: 67). Secondly, four months of the year were made sacred for the Hajj and visitation of this House. During these months, fighting, bloodshed, and corruption were strictly forbidden. Even the most savage people would sheathe their swords in reverence for these months, and the most dangerous regions would become entirely peaceful so that people could travel from every nook and corner of the country for Hajj and Umrah, and then return safely to their homes. Thirdly, Allah made this House completely secure and protected from the threats of external enemies. The history of this House bears witness that external enemies rarely ever dared to attack it, and if anyone ever had the audacity to do so, Allah meted out a terrifyingly exemplary punishment through His overriding power. The fate of Abraha's army is a well-known historical fact, and it is also mentioned in the Quran's Surah Al-Fil. Similarly, the blessing of this House also opened the doors of economic prosperity for the inhabitants of this land. We wish to draw attention to some aspects of this as well.
Various aspects of economic prosperity: One aspect is that because it became the center of Hajj, the influx of people to this land increased immensely. As the call of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) spread, people began arriving for Hajj and pilgrimage from every corner accordingly. And consequently, naturally, trade and business flourished. All kinds of goods from the outside started reaching the markets of Makkah, and things that could be exported from there began to go out. Before the construction of this House, the entire livelihood in this region depended, as we mentioned, either on herding and hunting or on raiding. But now, with the avenues of trade opened, there was an abundance of all kinds of commodities, fruits, and other necessities, bringing a highly pleasant change to the people's economy. The second aspect is that due to the custodianship of the Kaaba, the descendants of Abraham attained such a position of dignity and respect that their political and religious dominance was established over all of Arabia. As a result, their trade caravans would travel as far as Syria and Yemen, and no one dared to interfere with them. In fact, history reveals that the tribes living along the highways their caravans passed through, instead of harming them, would provide escorts for their protection and guidance within their respective territories. In Surah Quraysh, the Holy Quran refers to these very trade journeys of the Quraysh and demands of them: 'Let them worship the Lord of this House, Who has fed them, [saving them] from hunger and made them safe, [saving them] from fear.' For it was indeed the blessing of this House that they enjoyed peace in a perilous and barren desert, and the avenues of livelihood were made expansive for them.
Answer to a question: All these aspects of the discussion are quite clear; however, there is one point here that might trouble some minds. It is that the prayer Prophet Abraham (PBUH) made for his offspring's provision here specifically asks for fruits. Praying for provision and bounty for one's children, especially when they are being settled in an arid, waterless desert, is a completely natural thing. But it is difficult to understand why Prophet Abraham (PBUH) placed the condition of fruits for this provision. Good taste suggests that prescribing a specific form of provision does not seem very appropriate for a Prophet. If you reflect on other prayers of the Prophets, or even other prayers of Prophet Abraham himself mentioned in the Quran, you will find that they generally avoid this kind of specification and determination that takes the form of a prescription. In our view, this doubt only arises because people think 'Thamarat' refers only to fruits, whereas the word 'Thamarat' does not mean just fruits; it also includes crops and grains within its meaning. In Arabic, the specific word for fruits is 'Fawakih'. The word 'Thamarat' is more general and broader than that. In another place in the Quran, pointing to the blessings of this very Abrahamic prayer, the words 'thamarat kulli shay' (fruits of all things) are used: 'Have We not established for them a safe sanctuary to which are brought the fruits of all things?' (Al-Qasas: 57).
We mentioned earlier that this land where Prophet Abraham (PBUH) settled Prophet Ishmael was a barren, uninhabited place. The Torah uses the word 'wilderness' for it, and Prophet Abraham himself referred to it in his prayer as an 'uncultivated valley' (Wadi ghayri dhi zar'). We also learn from the Torah that Prophet Ishmael's livelihood depended on herding and hunting, meaning he spent most of his time outdoors. Obviously, when livelihood depends on herding and hunting, the peaceful and settled life required to fulfill the duties of Kaaba's custodianship and the mission Prophet Abraham (PBUH) entrusted to Prophet Ishmael could not materialize. For this reason, Prophet Abraham (PBUH) prayed for them to be granted the peace and stability of a settled (urban) life, escaping the restlessness and anxieties of nomadic life, so they could serve this universal center of monotheism and divine worship with full peace of mind. This prayer of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) is also recorded in Surah Ibrahim. There are some additional words there that clearly reveal the reality we just pointed out. He said: 'Our Lord, I have settled some of my descendants in an uncultivated valley near Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may establish prayer. So make hearts among the people incline toward them and provide for them from the fruits that they might be grateful' (Ibrahim: 37). If you ponder carefully over the words of this prayer, you will see that Prophet Abraham requested two things from his Lord for Prophet Ishmael and his descendants here, and he presented two things as a recommendation in support of this request. The request was: 'Incline the hearts of people toward them and provide them with fruits.' And the recommendation in support of it was: 'This land is completely devoid of agriculture, but I have placed my children here solely so they may serve Your sacred House and establish prayer to call towards Your worship.' Consider that when he gives the reason for requesting the provision of 'Thamarat' that this is an uncultivated land, his intention cannot be just fruits; it must be that they are freed from the anxieties of the nomadic life of herding and hunting and are blessed with the tranquility of a settled life, so they can serve Your House and Your religion to the fullest. The words 'la'allahum yashkurun' (so that they may be grateful) at the end of the verse are also highly meaningful. Meaning, the settled life I seek for them is not because I want them to have an abundance of luxury, but solely so they can remain singularly focused on their mission and fulfill the duty of being grateful to You to the maximum.
Prophet Abraham's (PBUH) Condition: 'Man amana minhum billahi wal-yawmil-akhir...' (Whoever among them believes in Allah and the Last Day). Prophet Abraham (PBUH) attached this condition to his prayer for provision, that only those who believe in Allah and the Hereafter should be entitled to it. Certainly, Prophet Abraham (PBUH) added this precaution here because earlier, regarding the matter of Imamate and Caliphate, he had received a clear answer that this covenant would not apply to those who fall into polytheism and disbelief. Having this in mind, Prophet Abraham (PBUH) deduced from it and voluntarily added this restriction to his prayer, saying I am making this request only for the believers. This shows the station of submission and pleasure (Taslim o Rida) that Prophet Abraham (PBUH) had attained. Even a mere hint of the direction of his Lord's pleasure was enough for him to rush toward it, even if the meaning of that hint became clearer differently later on. Here, Allah Almighty clarified that it is not correct to equate the matters of Imamate/Caliphate with worldly provision. Those who are disobedient to God are certainly not worthy of God's Caliphate, but this does not mean God should also snatch their daily bread due to their disobedience. Allah provides sustenance to both the disobedient and the obedient in this short life. However, it is certain that those who enjoy His provision in this life while being disobedient will be thrown into Hell after death. The Sunnah of Allah regarding this matter will be explained with great clarity in various chapters ahead, which is why we restrict ourselves to a brief indication here.

Prophet Abraham's (PBUH) Prayer at the Time of Building the Kaaba: 'Qawa'id' is the plural of 'Qa'idah', which means foundation and base. The previous verse referred to the command to build this House. Now, it is being recalled what Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael prayed for while raising its foundations, what hopes and aspirations they associated with this House, and what universal blessing they implored their Lord to let flow from it in the future. This part of Prophet Abraham's (PBUH) story has come to our knowledge only through the Quran, because the Jews, as we mentioned earlier, either deleted this specific part from the Torah or altered it to suit their desires. However, this is such a necessary part of his life story that without it, the narrative seems entirely incomplete. The Quran uncovered this altered section and completed it.
Rabbana taqabbal minna innaka antas-sami'ul-'alim: 'O our Lord, accept from us' could mean: 'Grant acceptance to this House we are building for Your worship and the call to Your obedience, and accept this service of ours.' It could also mean: 'We present some supplications while raising the foundations of Your House; please accept these supplications.' We prefer this second meaning. Firstly, because in this case, the sentence serves as a preamble to the entire prayer that follows, rather than being related solely to the Kaaba. Secondly, as far as building the Kaaba is concerned, Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael (PBUT) were doing this work according to Allah's command, which has already been mentioned above. Therefore, its acceptance was already known. In 'Innaka antas-sami'ul-'alim', there is a reference to two attributes of God, relying upon which a servant prays to God. And the element of exclusivity contained in it is an expression of complete submission and complete reliance on the part of those making the prayer.

Explanation of the Prayer: The repeated invocation of 'Rabbana' (Our Lord) throughout the prayer and referring to the divine attributes appropriate for the supplication are among the etiquettes of making dua. This helps the prayer gain acceptance. This prayer is a prime example of the wise use of both these elements. The very first thing the father and son prayed for is to be made 'Muslims' themselves. 'Muslim' means one who is perfectly obedient to God. This brings several realities to light. First, when praying for faith, Islam, the pursuit of piety, and God-consciousness, a person should prioritize themselves; these are not things anyone can dispense with, no matter how high their rank. Second, there is no limit or end to the degrees and ranks of Islam. Even perfect Muslims like Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael, through whom the world became acquainted with the name and spirit of Islam, prayed to be made Muslims. The third reality, which relates specifically to this occasion and unlocks the structure of the discourse, is that on the most historic occasion of their lives, while building the center of their mission, what Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael prayed for themselves was to be made Muslims, not to be made Jews or Christians.
Wa min dhurriyyatina ummatan muslimatan laka: Along with praying to be made Muslims themselves, Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael also prayed for a complete Muslim Ummah to be raised from among their descendants. Since only Prophet Ishmael from Prophet Abraham's (PBUH) descendants shared in this prayer on this occasion, its clear meaning could only be that it related to his progeny. Consequently, Muhammad bin Abdullah (PBUH) appeared in his very lineage, and through his call, the Muslim Ummah for which this prayer was made came into existence. These details were removed from the Torah, but the prophecy concerning the son to be sacrificed is present: 'Through your offspring all nations on earth will be blessed.'
Wa arina manasikana wa tub 'alayna: 'Arina' originally means 'show us'. Allah Almighty guides His servants to His religion and His Sharia through the kind of revelation manifested in the Quran, and sometimes He physically shows or indicates the required task by directly sending an angel in a vision or unveiling (Kashf). This type of guidance is termed 'Ira'at' (showing) in Quranic terminology. For the Prophets who came before Prophet Muhammad (PBUH), Allah's guidance was mostly through Ira'at. Meaning, either a matter was shown to them in a vision, or a divine angel appeared and guided them towards the required task. Many examples of this can be presented from the Torah. Here in the prayer, Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael have requested this very Ira'at.
Linguistic Investigation of 'Manasik': 'Manasik' is the plural of 'Mansak'. The root 'Nusuk' means washing and purifying. 'Nasakath-thawb' means washing a cloth to purify it. From this comes 'Nusuk', which means sacrifice. A devoted servant offers a sacrifice to cleanse himself of the pollutions and impurities of sin to attain closeness to Allah Almighty. Then from this comes 'Mansak', which means both the method of sacrifice and the place of sacrifice. Its plural is 'Manasik', which encompasses all the rites and rituals of Hajj. It is stated: 'Fa idha qadaytum manasikakum fadhkurullaha' (And when you have completed your rites, remember Allah) (Al-Baqarah: 200).
Wa tub 'alayna: The Meaning of 'Tawbah': 'Tawbah' originally means to return and turn towards. When it is used with the preposition 'Ala', as we explained while clarifying verse 37, it signifies that it contains a hidden element of mercy. This hidden element of mercy is explicitly unfolded here by saying 'Innaka antat-tawwabur-rahim' (Indeed, You are the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful). When a servant turns to his Lord with awe and fear, the Merciful Lord turns to the servant with mercy.

'Raise a Messenger among them, from themselves, meaning from our descendants.' Since only Prophet Ishmael (PBUH) was with Prophet Abraham on this occasion, and he was the one being settled in this uncultivated valley, this prayer was necessarily related to his descendants. It cannot have any connection to the descendants of Isaac. The words of the Torah also indicate that the final Prophet was to be sent from the lineage of Ishmael. In the famous prophecy of Prophet Moses in Deuteronomy chapter 18, it is stated: 'I will raise them up a Prophet from among their brethren, like unto thee.' Further on, it says: 'I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their fellow Israelites (brethren).' These words clearly indicate that this refers to the Ishmaelites. If the Israelites were intended, the correct expression would be 'from among them' rather than 'from among their brethren.' Similarly, 'from within yourselves' would have been used instead of 'from among your brethren.' Additionally, the words 'like me' are noteworthy here. This shows that the prophet prophesied was a Messenger possessing a Sharia (Sahib-e-Risalat). In accordance with this, the word 'Rasul' (Messenger) is used in the aforementioned Quranic prayer. We will explain the difference between a Nabi (Prophet) and a Rasul (Messenger) at a suitable place later. Here, the Messenger for whose advent the prayer was made was assigned three purposes. First, the recitation of the verses (Tilawat-e-Ayat); second, the teaching of the Book and Wisdom (Ta'lim-e-Kitab wa Hikmat); third, purification (Tazkiyah).
The Meaning of Reciting the Verses: Lexically, 'Ayah' (verse/sign) means that which can be used as evidence for something. From this perspective, everything in the heavens and the earth is an Ayah. Because each of them is a proof of God's power, wisdom, and His various attributes of creation and administration. Similarly, the miracles manifested by the Prophets (PBUT) are also Ayahs because they too served as proofs for the truthfulness of those who presented them. By the same analogy, this word is used for the individual sentences of the Holy Quran. Because in reality, each of its verses acts as a proof and argument through which one gains knowledge of God's attributes, His commands and laws, and His pleasures. The words 'Yatlu 'alayhim' (He recites to them) express the force and authority with which a Messenger of God comes into this world, armed. It should be clear that a Messenger does not merely come to recite the Quran to people like a melodious reciter, but as an ambassador of God, he informs the people of the commands, decrees, arguments, and proofs of the Creator and Master of the heavens and the earth. Furthermore, using the word 'Ayat' for divine revelation expresses the reality that God's religion is not based on dictation and coercion; rather, it is based entirely on arguments and proofs, and every segment of it contains its own evidence.
The Meaning of Teaching the Book and Wisdom: Now, let us reflect on the words 'Teaching the Book and Wisdom' (Ta'lim-e-Kitab wa Hikmat). It is absolutely clear that teaching (Ta'lim) is completely different from recitation (Tilawat). Reciting the verses simply means the Messenger has informed the people that God has revealed this to him. Teaching means that with immense compassion and attention, the difficulties of the Book are clarified for people of every capability, its summaries are detailed, its implied meanings are unfolded, and its hidden aspects are explained. If questions still arise in people's minds after this explanation, those questions are answered. Moreover, for the mental training of the people, questions are posed to them, and efforts are made to seek their answers so that the ability to reflect and ponder, and the capacity to contemplate the Divine Book, are fully awakened within them. All these things are necessary components of teaching, and anyone who has studied the life of the Prophet (PBUH) knows well that he adopted all these methods of teaching the Book for his Companions. Two things are mentioned here alongside teaching. One is the Book, the other is Wisdom (Hikmat). The Book obviously refers to the Holy Quran. We have already explained the linguistic research of this word under the Tafseer of verse 2 of this Surah. The linguistic research of the word 'Hikmat' explained by Maulana Farahi in his book 'Mufradat al-Quran' is presented here in its essential parts. He writes: 'As for Hikmat, it is an expression of that power and capability by which humans judge matters according to the truth. In praise of Prophet David, it is stated: 'Wa ataynahul-hikmata wa faslal-khitab' (And We gave him wisdom and decisiveness in speech). Here, the word 'Faslal-khitab' (decisiveness in speech/judgment) describes the effect which is the fruit of wisdom. Just as the ability to judge matters is among the fruits of wisdom, purity and refinement of character are also among its fruits. For this reason, the Arabs also use the word Hikmat for the human power and capability that encompasses maturity of intellect and opinion along with nobility of character. Thus, a wise and civilized person is called a Hakam, and a statement that is absolutely clear to both the intellect and the heart is termed Hikmat.' The mention of Hikmat here alongside the Book is proof that teaching Wisdom is something additional to teaching the Book, even if this Wisdom is entirely derived and deduced from the Wise Quran. Therefore, in our view, the argument of those who interpret Hikmat as Hadith carries great weight. It should be kept in mind here that since Hikmat refers both to a wise statement and the ability to make wise statements, teaching Hikmat means not only imparting wise sayings to someone but also creating the attribute and capability of wisdom within people. The third purpose of the Messenger is stated to be purification (Tazkiyah). The word Tazkiyah encompasses two meanings. One is to cleanse and purify; the second is to nurture and develop. In our view, these two things are inseparable from each other. Anything that is cleansed of opposing and obstructing impurities and corruptions will naturally flourish according to its innate capabilities. The Tazkiyah of human souls that the Prophets (PBUT) perform contains both these aspects. They cleanse people's hearts, actions, and morals from wrongful things, and by nurturing their actions and morals, they instill in them the strength to stand firm and show steadfastness against corruptions and opposing/obstructing forces. This is why, compared to teaching the Book, the purification of souls demands far more meticulous care, hardship, and patient discipline. Therefore, it is mentioned in the Holy Quran as the ultimate goal and purpose of the entire religion and Sharia. God willing, we will clarify this reality at a suitable place later. The Meaning of Aziz and Hakim: At the end of the verse, there is a reference to two attributes of God: Aziz and Hakim. Aziz means the Mighty, Possessor of Honor and Power. That is, the Entity Who is ruling over this universe with absolute power, authority, and complete control. Hakim means the One whose every action has wisdom, rationale, and a specific purpose and goal. In the Holy Quran, the reference to these two attributes of Allah generally appears together. This expresses the reality that Allah Almighty encompasses and controls this universe with full power and dominance, but His dominance and authority do not mean that He does whatever He pleases out of sheer force; rather, whatever He does, He does with wisdom and purpose. None of His actions is devoid of wisdom and rationale. The purpose of referring to both these attributes here is to show that the necessary demand of the Might and Wisdom of the One Who is Aziz and Hakim is that He sends His ambassador and messenger to this kingdom He created, to inform His subjects of His commands and laws and to teach them the Sharia and Wisdom.
The Official Duties of the Prophet (PBUH): The deniers of the Sunnah (Munkirin-e-Sunnat) claim that the Prophet's (PBUH) sole primary responsibility as a messenger was to convey the revelation Allah sent down to him to the people. After that, his duty as a messenger ends. Beyond that, neither does he have any responsibility from Allah, nor does any of his words or actions have any independent legal (Shari) significance apart from divine revelation, or in other words, the Quran. In our view, this single verse of the Quran is sufficient to refute this claim of the deniers of the Sunnah. The details of the official duties of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) given in it do not merely mention reciting the Quran to the people; rather, several other things are mentioned alongside it. And it is clear from this verse that these things have also been mentioned in the capacity of his prophetic duties. Take another look at the verse. It says: 'Rabbana wab'ath fihim rasulam minhum yatlu 'alayhim ayatika wa yu'allimuhumul-kitaba wal-hikmata wa yuzakkihim. Innaka antal-'azizul-hakim' (Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them. Indeed, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise).
These are the words of the prayer made by Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael for the advent of the Holy Prophet (PBUH). When the Holy Prophet was sent in accordance with this prayer, Allah Almighty expressed this immense favor upon the Arabs thus: 'Huwalladhi ba'atha fil-ummiyyina rasulam-minhum yatlu 'alayhim ayatihi wa yuzakkihim wa yu'allimuhumul-kitaba wal-hikmata wa in kanu min qablu lafi dalalim-mubin' (It is He who has sent among the unlettered a Messenger from themselves reciting to them His verses and purifying them and teaching them the Book and wisdom - although they were before in clear error). (Al-Jumu'ah: 2).
If you reflect on both these verses, the reality becomes clear that the Holy Prophet (PBUH) was sent with the exact same attributes that Prophet Abraham (PBUH) had prayed for in a prophet, and he practically performed all those tasks among the unlettered people for which Prophet Abraham (PBUH) had prayed. In both these places, as far as the duties of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) are concerned, there is no difference in their description. If there is any difference, it is only that in the first verse, the mention of purification (Tazkiyah) is at the very end, and in the second verse, it is before the teaching of the Book and Wisdom, but after the recitation of the verses. This difference is not of major significance. The reason for Tazkiyah being placed earlier or later has been explained by us in another place: Tazkiyah is the ultimate goal of the entire religion and Sharia and the main purpose of sending the Prophets. And whatever serves as the ultimate goal and purpose of a task, although it comes last in execution, it comes first in intention. Therefore, in the primary scheme, its mention can be both prior and posterior. Consequently, based on this, the mention of Tazkiyah has preceded in one verse and followed in the other. Apart from this difference in sequence, all other things are entirely common in both verses, and they outline the following duties of the Prophet (PBUH): 1. Recitation of the Verses. 2. Teaching of the Book and Wisdom. 3. Purification (Tazkiyah). Among these, as far as the first item—reciting the verses—is concerned, we accept without any debate or dispute that it means conveying the Holy Quran to the people. Both religion and intellect testify that the primary duty of a Messenger of God should be to convey His revelation to His servants. But regarding this recitation, it must be remembered that it did not happen in a way that the entire Book was recited to the people all at once; rather, it was sent down gradually over a vast and long period of 23 years, and with this same gradualness, the Prophet (PBUH) taught it to the people lesson by lesson. This is proof that this Book is not a simple, flat book, but a book of profound sciences, knowledge, and high mysteries and realities. Therefore, it became necessary for it to be taught lesson by lesson so people could access its treasures. The Quran has clarified this reality as follows: 'Wa qur'anan faraqnahu litaqra'ahu 'alan-nasi 'ala mukth...' (And [it is] a Quran which We have separated [by intervals] that you might recite it to the people over a prolonged period) (Al-Isra: 106). This aforementioned characteristic of the Wise Quran necessitated that the Prophet (PBUH) should not merely suffice with reciting it to the people like a reciter, but as a teacher, he should also teach it to the people with full compassion and care. Consequently, on this basis, his second duty was stated as teaching the Book, alongside recitation. This duty of teaching the Book is a part of his prophetic duties, and his being a teacher is an aspect of his messenger-ship. Therefore, whatever he taught and instructed the people in this capacity can neither be excluded from his prophetic duties nor can its status be degraded in comparison to the original Book. Now, reflect on what the demands of this teaching could be. Its very first and basic demand is that the Shari terminologies used in the Quran—such as Zakah, Salah, Hajj, Sawm (fasting), Tawaf, Umrah, Nikah, Talaq, etc.—whose practical forms are not detailed, should be thoroughly clarified by him for the people so they can adopt them in their practical lives and determine the status of their various components in the religion. The second thing is that the necessary aspects of the implications and corollaries of the principles given in the Quran for the correction of thought and action should be clarified, so they can serve as lighthouses for seeking further guidance in these matters. Similarly, another aspect is that the Sharia commands given in the Quran only have the status of principled commands. Under each of these categories, there are countless situations where the determination of commands has been left to the guidance and Ijtihad (independent reasoning) of the teacher. Obviously, for this Ijtihad, the Ummah could only get the best guidance from the examples set by the infallible teacher of this Book through his Ijtihad. The fourth thing is that the Quran also presents a system for collective life, but by providing only the principles that define its four corners, the matter of its details, specifics, and practical framework was left to the responsibility of the teacher. People learned this too from the teachings of the Prophet alone. Besides these, an important point is that the verse under discussion does not only mention teaching the Book but also mentions teaching Wisdom. Teaching Wisdom is much broader than teaching Sharia. As we explained while clarifying this word, it refers to the intellect, insight, vision, and gnosis that guide a human being even in those remote corners of life where there is no other light to guide them. Now consider, are all these things among the demands of teaching or not? And was the Holy Prophet (PBUH) appointed as a divine teacher to teach all these things or not? If the answer to these questions is affirmative—and obviously, the answer can only be in the affirmative—then reflect on how whatever the Holy Prophet (PBUH) said and did in this capacity can be separated from the sphere of his prophetic duties, and how its importance can be diminished? And then ponder over this: what else is there in the Ahadith (prophetic traditions) besides these things that the Holy Prophet (PBUH) taught or practically demonstrated in his capacity as the teacher of the Book and Wisdom? Similarly, now reflect on Tazkiyah (purification). The process of Tazkiyah is obviously much more complex and multidimensional than teaching. We have clarified above that this word includes the meaning of both cleansing and nurturing. It is simultaneously academic and practical, outward and inward, material and physical, as well as intellectual and spiritual; moreover, it is individual as well as social and collective. In short, keep some of its basic demands in mind as well. One necessary demand of it is to cast a microscopic glance over the minds, actions, and morals of the people and cleanse them of those germs that cause spiritual and moral diseases, while simultaneously sowing the seeds of virtues within them that beautify a person's inner and outer self and civilize their habits and traits. Its second demand is that people should be trained in such a way that every virtue takes root within them, and an aversion against every evil settles in their nature. Its third demand is that through this education and training, such an environment should be created that serves as a vast training ground for the purification of souls; whoever grows up in it absorbs the influences of that environment, and whoever enters it takes on its color. From this detail, the reality becomes clear that the notion that the Holy Prophet's (PBUH) official duty as a Messenger was solely to deliver the Quran to the people is highly deceptive. Delivering the Quran was only one part of his official duties. Besides this, it was also his responsibility that, like a teacher, he should teach this Quran to the people, clarify its implications, corollaries, summaries, hints, secrets, and realities for the people, determine the principles and branches of training individuals and society in the light of its wondrous wisdom, and also purify the people according to these principles. All these tasks were included in his prophetic duties. Therefore, whatever he instructed or did under these objectives, the Ummah has considered it just as obligatory to follow as they considered the Quran, and they have managed its preservation and its transmission and narration with the same importance. Regarding any of its parts, the question can certainly be raised whether its attribution to the Holy Prophet (PBUH) is proven with full authenticity or not, but refusing to consider it as Religion and Sharia is synonymous with denying the Holy Quran itself.

When 'Raghiba' takes the preposition 'An', it means to be averse to or disgusted with something. 'Safiha' mostly comes as an intransitive verb but also comes as transitive. For example, 'Safiha nafsahu' means he ruined his own portion/fortune. 'Safiha ra'yahu' means he adopted a foolish opinion. Similarly, 'Safiha nafsahu' here means he involved himself in foolishness. This style of speech comprehensively expresses both astonishment and regret. The reference here is to the Jews. The meaning is that on one hand, they claim to be followers of the religion of Abraham—in fact, in their presumption, they sit as its sole monopolists—yet on the other hand, their condition is such that they are the most averse to the Prophet who has come as the caller to the religion of Abraham and to his call, and they label those who support this call as fools. What greater example of foolishness and loss of intellect could there be than this? 'Wa laqad istafaynahu fid-dunya' (And We had chosen him in this world): This points to the chosen status granted to him by Allah Almighty for the leadership and guidance of the nations of the world, which has already been mentioned in verse 124 above. The meaning is that anyone who turns away from the religion of the one whom Allah chose for the leadership of all is nothing but a fool. The word 'Salihin' (righteous) in the Holy Quran is used for general doers of good as well as for the entire group that encompasses the Prophets, the Truthful, the Martyrs, and the Righteous. In this verse, this word is used in this second meaning.

The Meaning of 'Islam': Islam means to completely surrender oneself to the will of God and His commands. In our view, this refers to the sacrifice of the son. Although all the trials they were put through were meant to test their Islam—as if every trial appeared before them, demanding 'Aslim' (Submit) in the language of its circumstance—the specific command to sacrifice Prophet Ishmael (PBUH), after succeeding in which they were blessed with the honor of being chosen and granted leadership, was a demand for such complete submission and total surrender that if there could be one most comprehensive and truth-revealing word to express it, it could only be the word 'Aslim'. This word has also appeared in other places in the Holy Quran to point to the event of the sacrifice, for example: 'Falamma aslama wa tallahu liljabin' (And when they had both submitted [aslama] and he put him down upon his forehead) (As-Saffat: 103). By pointing to this event with the word Islam here, the Quran has clarified several realities. First, the chosen status that Allah Almighty granted to Prophet Abraham (PBUH) in both this world and the Hereafter, as indicated in verse 124, is the reward for the life-risking sacrifices he made in the path of seeking the Lord's pleasure. He did not achieve this greatness for free, the way the Jews want to achieve it. Second, Prophet Abraham (PBUH) was commanded to show complete submission and total surrender, i.e., Islam, and he demonstrated this very Islam through his words and actions, not Judaism or Christianity as the Jews or Christians presume. Third, the true spirit of Islam is to surrender oneself to one's Lord to the extent that no beloved thing remains dearer to the servant than God.

'Tawsiya' means to teach and instruct, regardless of whether a person gives this instruction at the time of their death or at any other stage in life. The pronoun 'biha' refers to the religion of Islam, which is mentioned in the previous verse with the phrase 'Millat-e-Ibrahim'.
Although the mention of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) is nowhere to be found in the Jewish scriptures, it is obvious that advising and instructing one's children and followers regarding matters of religion has been the general practice (Sunnah) of the Prophets (PBUT). Abundant traditions of such teachings and advice are reported regarding the general patriarchs and tribal chiefs of the Israelites and Ishmaelites. So much so that the Talmud even contains a will of Prophet Jacob that is quite similar to the will mentioned in the Holy Quran. In families and nations, such traditions are established by the conduct of the elders of those families themselves. Therefore, it is highly plausible that Prophet Abraham (PBUH) left this Sunnah for his successors through his own conduct. As for the fact that he bequeathed the religion of Islam to his children, it is so clear that it requires no further proof. From the life story mentioned above, it is evident that the religion he became acquainted with, the religion he called towards, and the reality of the religion he demonstrated through his immense sacrifice, is Islam. So how could he leave this religion and instruct his children in Judaism or Christianity, with which he was never even acquainted? Prophet Jacob is mentioned alongside Prophet Abraham (PBUH) here because of the specific reason that the Israelites were his direct descendants. The meaning is that if there is a tradition, from Prophet Abraham (PBUH) down to three generations, they consistently bequeathed the religion of Islam, not Judaism or Christianity. So, for the claimants of following the Abrahamic religion, is the thing to be followed Islam or Judaism and Christianity? 'Ad-Din' refers to that true religion which has been the religion of Allah from the beginning, i.e., Islam. Thus, it is stated elsewhere: 'Innad-dina 'indallahil-Islam' (Indeed, the religion in the sight of Allah is Islam) (Al Imran: 19). In another place it is declared: 'Afa ghayra dinillahi yabghuna walahu aslama man fis-samawati wal-ardi taw'an wa karhan wa ilayhi yurja'un' (So is it other than the religion of Allah they desire, while to Him have submitted [all] those within the heavens and earth, willingly or by compulsion, and to Him they will be returned?). This very religion is the religion of Allah, and this is the religion He always sent through His Prophets and Messengers. Prophet Abraham and Prophet Jacob enjoined their respective offspring to follow this very religion and to live and die upon it. But later, the Israelites corrupted its appearance by making alterations in it and raised the tribulations of Judaism and Christianity in its place. 'Fala tamutunna illa wa antum muslimun' (Then do not die except while you are Muslims) hides the implication that the trust of this religion is a heavy trust; you must fulfill the rights of this trust from the cradle to the grave. Great trials will be encountered on this path, and you must face these trials with full resolve and courage. Be careful, let Satan not remove you from this station at any stage. You must live for this alone and die for this alone.

The Will of Prophet Jacob (PBUH): This questioning style is adopted to alert the addressee and make the speech more impactful. The meaning is: You claim that your great forefathers were upon Judaism or Christianity; were you present when Jacob's final hour approached, and he called his sons to make his will? What did he make them pledge at that time? Monotheism and Islam, or Judaism and Christianity? After this question, the Quran quotes Prophet Jacob's (PBUH) question to his sons and the unanimous answer of his sons, which is clearly in favor of the religion of monotheism and Islam. Even though the wording of the tradition found in Jewish literature regarding this will is somewhat different from the Quran's wording, it nonetheless supports the Quran's statement, not the aforementioned claim of the Israelites. Because there is no hint of Judaism or Christianity in it. Our respected teacher Maulana Abdul Majid Daryabadi searched Jewish literature and quoted two references in his Tafseer at this point; one relates to the will of Prophet Isaac, the other to the will of Prophet Jacob: 'When Isaac saw that his appointed time had come, he called his two sons to him and said, I charge you by God Almighty, whose attributes are High, Great, Sustaining, Mighty, and who is the Creator of heaven and earth and everything between them, that you fear only Him and worship only Him. Jacob said to his sons, I fear that someone among you harbors an inclination toward idolatry. In response, the twelve sons said, Hear O Israel, O our father, our God is the same eternal God. Just as your heartfelt faith is in One God, similarly, our heartfelt faith is in One God.' (Ginzberg's Legends of the Jews, Vol 1, p. 416). Note this point here as well that the Quran specifically referenced the will made by Prophet Jacob (PBUH) at the time of his death, to point towards several things. First, to the fact that Prophet Jacob took this covenant and pledge from his children in the absolute final moments of his life, leaving no room to suspect that his creed and religion might have changed after this. Second, to the fact that the covenant and pledge an affectionate and kind father, who is also a Prophet of God, takes from his children in the absolute final moments of his life must be the most important event between him and his children, and it is the greatest and most sacred duty of loyal children to uphold this covenant under all circumstances; only unworthy children violate a covenant of this nature. Third, that the final duty in life of a God-fearing father who truly loves his children is to worry more about their Hereafter than their worldly life, and to instruct them to remain steadfast on the true religion and to live and die upon it.
Some Subtle Aspects of the Will and its Answer: By using the word 'Ma' (What) in 'Ma ta'buduna mim ba'di' (What will you worship after me?), Prophet Jacob (PBUH) created maximum vastness in the question so that if there were any hesitation in the minds of the responders regarding the deity, it would be exposed in their answer to this question. However, his sons' answer clarifies that up until that time, there was no confusion in their minds regarding the deity. They affirmed His Oneness in very clear terms and declared Him alone worthy of worship and obedience. It is also worth noting in this verse that the sense of pride and confidence with which Prophet Jacob's children counted Prophet Isaac (PBUH) and Prophet Abraham (PBUH) among their forefathers, they cited Prophet Ishmael with that same pride and confidence. This proves that until the time of Prophet Jacob, the prejudices against Prophet Ishmael (PBUH) and his descendants, which developed later, had not yet arisen among his children.

Summary of the Argument: This verse has appeared twice in this sequence of discourse. Once here, and then again after a few verses at the end of the section (Juz), where this sequence of discourse concludes. The reason for this is that this is the very summary of the argument that is intended to be placed before the addressee. The point to convey is: All your pride and reliance rests on your forefathers; you think that they have fulfilled the responsibilities of your share as well, and now you only have to reap the fruits of their good deeds, with no responsibility remaining upon you. This pride and reliance is based entirely on delusion and imagination. They fulfilled the responsibilities of their share and, having fulfilled those responsibilities, have reached their Lord. They will receive the reward for their good deeds themselves; you will not get any share of it. Your responsibilities are upon you; if you fulfill them, you will get their reward, otherwise, you will suffer the punishment for them. With God, you will not be questioned about the deeds of your forefathers, but rather about your own deeds.

The Original Religion in Islam is the Millah of Abraham: As we explained while clarifying verse 111 above, in their zeal to oppose Islam, both Jews and Christians unitedly claimed that whoever seeks guidance and salvation should adopt either Judaism or Christianity; these two are divine religions, and this third religion being presented under the name of Islam is no religion at all. In response, it is said: 'Qul bal millata Ibrahima hanifa' (Say, 'Rather, [we follow] the religion of Abraham, inclining toward truth'). The word 'Millah' here is in the accusative case (Nasb); therefore, an omitted verb must necessarily be assumed here. Generally, people assume a past tense verb is omitted here. Meaning: Say, we have followed the religion of Abraham. I have assumed an imperative (command) verb is omitted here, and I have kept this in mind in the translation. One reason for this is that here, as is clear from the word 'Qul' (Say), this response is delivered through the tongue of the Prophet (PBUH). An address of invitation was most appropriate from the tongue of the Prophet (PBUH), especially in response to the misleading invitation of the People of the Book. Secondly, the declaration of the Muslims' own faith and Islam is coming in the next verse in the words 'Qulu amanna billahi'. Therefore, it is more appropriate to take this verse in the sense of an invitation. Thirdly, in the Arabic language, when such an accusative form comes, its temperament is more suited to the context of urging the addressee towards something or warning them against something, for which an imperative verb is more appropriate. 'Hanifa' comes from 'Hanaf', the root meaning of which is to incline and lean. 'Hanif' refers to that person who cuts himself off from all directions and devotes himself entirely to God with complete single-mindedness. Here, this word is in the accusative state of condition (Hal) originating from Abraham. Although Abraham is in the genitive case (Jarr), and Arabic grammarians are very hesitant about a Hal occurring from a Majrur (genitive), Maulana Farahi (RA) has proven with very strong arguments in his Tafseer of Surah Al-Fil that this method is recognized in the Arabic language. The Holy Quran has repeatedly used the attribute of Hanif for Prophet Abraham (PBUH). The reason for this is that the Jews, Christians, and polytheists all considered him their spiritual leader, and all three groups claimed that the religion they were on was the very inheritance of Prophet Abraham. The Quran first refuted their claims with various arguments. Then it stated that Abraham was a Hanif; he did not deviate a hair's breadth from the straight path established by God, the religion of Islam. He neither turned towards the narrow footpaths of Judaism and Christianity, nor did he fall into the misguidances of the polytheists. Rather, he remained steadfast on this main highway of Islam which God had opened, and which is the only straight path leading to God.

The Stance of the Muslim Ummah: This is the Muslims' response to the Jews' and Christians' invitation that 'become a Jew or a Christian to be guided'. Say to them that we believe in Allah and in this guidance that has been sent down to us, and in the guidance that was sent down to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the various branches of Jacob's offspring through their prophets, and we also believe in the guidance that Moses, Jesus, and other prophets received from their Lord. We make no distinction between these prophets, and we are submissive to Allah alone.
