This is the stance, or in other words, the 'Kalimah' (declaration), of this Muslim Ummah or 'Ummat-e-Wasat' (the Just Nation) regarding God's Sharia and His prophets and messengers. It means that this Ummah neither rejects any guidance sent down by God nor belies any prophet or messenger; rather, it believes in all without any distinction or exception. Its stance is that these prophets of God imparted teachings to their respective nations, but those nations either adulterated them or forgot parts of them. Now, the Sharia given to this Ummah presents God's original guidance in its final and perfect form.
The Meaning of 'Asbat': In the verse, the word 'Asbat' is the plural of 'Sibt'. Its literal meaning is to grow and spread. Keeping this meaning in mind, it was used for the children of one father and their various branches, and its usage for the various branches of Jacob's lineage is so well-known that it seems it was coined for them. 'La nufarriqu bayna ahadim minhum' (We make no distinction between any of them) means that we do not act like the Jews and Christians, believing in some of them and disbelieving in others. The Quran itself has clarified this meaning in another place: 'Yuriduna ay-yufarriqu baynallahi wa rusulihi wa yaquluna nu'minu bi ba'diw-wa nakfuru bi ba'diw-wa yuriduna ay-yattakhidhu bayna dhalika sabila' (And they wish to discriminate between Allah and His messengers and say, 'We believe in some and disbelieve in others,' and wish to adopt a way in between) (An-Nisa: 150). From this, it is known that accepting some prophets and rejecting others is synonymous with rejecting all of them, and this is not just making a distinction between prophets and prophets, but making a distinction between Allah and His Messenger.

The Path of Salvation for Jews and Christians: Meaning, if they also accept this comprehensive declaration, and just as you have believed in all the prophets and all the guidances, if they too believe similarly, then undoubtedly they will be guided. The path to being guided is not to become a Jew or a Christian, as the Jews and Christians claim, but the path is the one you have adopted. That is, to believe in all the prophets and all the messengers without any distinction or prejudice. If they refuse this, its clear meaning is that these people are bent on opposing you, and abandoning the path of unity and agreement, they want to establish their own separate faction against Allah and His messengers. If this is the case, then leave them to walk on their chosen path; Allah is sufficient from your side to deal with them. Finally, referring to His attributes of 'Sami'' (All-Hearing) and 'Alim' (All-Knowing) aims to assure the Prophet (PBUH) not to be intimidated at all by whatever conspiracies and machinations they plot in opposing you; the God Who has stood up to fight them on your behalf hears everything and knows everything.

Invitation to the Jews and Christians: Addressing the Jews and Christians, the invitation is given that if you want to dye yourselves in the color of Allah, abandon Judaism and Christianity and adopt this color of Allah. This comprehensive declaration mentioned above encompasses all of Allah's guidances and all His prophets and messengers. This is the declaration that imbues life with the true color of God. So, if you want to dye your life in God's color, dye it in this color; what color could be better than this color? This also contains an implicit critique of the baptism of Jews and Christians, and the word 'Sibghat' (color) being in the accusative case (Nasb) without any visible verb is, in our view, proof that a verb containing the meaning of urging and motivating must be assumed here.

Disavowal from the Jews and Christians: Meaning, if they do not accept this comprehensive declaration—which entails belief in all the prophets and messengers and all the guidances sent down by Allah, and the pledge of worship and obedience to Allah alone—but instead obstinately insist that they will accept some of God's prophets and reject others, accept some of His guidances and reject others, whereas all these prophets and messengers are sent by God and all these guidances are revealed by Him, then this means they are arguing with you about God Himself, as if their God is someone else and your God is someone else, even though your Lord and their Lord is One. If they have indeed escalated the matter to the extent of making their God separate, then do not expect any good from them. Instead, completely end this argument and discussion and clearly say that our deeds are with us and your deeds are with you. Meaning, we now consider engaging in any debate or polemics with you completely futile. When you are not even unified regarding God, instead of arguing with you, we consider it sufficient to clarify that we are purely and exclusively devoted to our Lord alone.

This demands the Jews and Christians to repeat their claim regarding Prophet Abraham and his descendants once again, as a way of exhausting the proof against them (Itmam al-Hujjah). Meaning, do you really make the grave assertion that Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and their descendants were Jews or Christians? Then, in a tone of reprimand, it is asked: Do you know more about the religion and beliefs of these people, or does Allah? Then, with a tone of sorrow and regret, it is stated that who could be more unjust than those who conceal a testimony from Allah? Meaning, the Torah is present; the details of these people's religion and beliefs are present in it; from it, one can know that in their times, there was not even a trace or sign of Judaism and Christianity. You coined these names centuries after them. God always sent down the same religion to His prophets and messengers, the name of which is Islam. After this, in a very stern, threatening tone, it is declared that God is not unaware of the mischief you are committing against the religion of Allah; its consequence will come before you.
This exact same verse has already passed above. It appears here in the same context as it appeared above. We have already explained it there. Please refer to the Tafseer of verse 134.

Summary of Verses 142 to 162:
The detailed reasons and causes for the dismissal of the Jews from the position of Imamate (leadership) conclude with this group of verses. Now, having dismissed them, the establishment of a new Ummah is essentially being announced. This Ummah is the 'Ummat-e-Wasat' (the Just/Middle Nation), meaning it is established on that straight path which is the original divine highway of the true religion. Its creed is the 'Millah' (religion) of Abraham, and its Qibla is the Abrahamic Qibla, the Sacred House of Allah (Baitullah Al-Haram). Its official duty is that just as the Prophet has borne witness to Allah's original religion before it, it shall bear witness to Allah's religion before the creation of God. Until the revelation of these verses, the Prophet (PBUH) and the Muslims used to face Bait al-Maqdis (Jerusalem) in their prayers. Now, in relation to the Abrahamic religion, it became necessary for the Qibla of this Ummah to be Al-Masjid Al-Haram. For this reason, the command to change the Qibla was given. Then, the details of the reaction to this event by the Jews and certain groups of Muslims are explained. Along with this, the wisdom behind changing the Qibla and essential instructions regarding the Qibla are provided, which were necessary to keep the Muslims steadfast on the straight path regarding the Qibla, and the neglect of which caused the Jews and Christians to deviate from the original Qibla. Then, as an independent Ummah, a covenant was taken from the Muslims that after the change of Qibla, you are now distinguished as an independent Ummah apart from the Jews and Christians. Just as your Messenger is a distinct Messenger who is the manifestation of all the attributes Prophet Abraham (PBUH) prayed for, similarly, your Qibla is the Abrahamic Qibla. Now, do not fear these Jews in the slightest. Fear only Allah so that you may be blessed with the bounty of Allah's perfect religion and the paths of divine Sharia may open for you. You remember Me, and I will remember you. Continue to be grateful to Me, and do not be ungrateful. After this, an indication is given toward the expected dangers that could arise from opponents and enemies after emerging as an independent Ummah, and guidance is provided on the preparations and the spiritual/moral armor the Muslims need to arm themselves with to counter these dangers. Finally, in relation to the Kaaba, the reality is clarified that just like the Kaaba, Safa and Marwah are also included among the symbols of Allah, because Marwah is the original site of sacrifice. But the Jews tried to veil these landmarks through alteration in order to completely sever Prophet Abraham's (PBUH) connection to this House. Because of this mischief, the Jews deserve the curse of Allah and the curse of all those who curse. After this introduction, now look at the translation and exegesis of the verses.
The Reason for Calling the Jews Fools: 'Sufaha' is the plural of 'Safih', which means ignorant and foolish. Here, it refers to the Jews. The reason for calling the Jews fools is the same one we pointed out in 'Wa may-yarghabu 'am-millati Ibrahima illa man safiha nafsahu' (And who would be averse to the religion of Abraham except one who makes a fool of himself) (Al-Baqarah: 130). On the one hand, the Jews claimed to be followers of the religion of Abraham, but on the other hand, they stood up as fierce enemies of the Prophet (PBUH) and his teaching and call, even though he came as the caller to the original Abrahamic religion. Obviously, only ignorant and foolish people could commit such a contradictory act. For this reason, the Quran used the word 'Sufaha' for them.
Prior Indication of the Expected Reaction to the Change of Qibla: This is a prelude to the command to change the Qibla, which is mentioned two verses later. This prelude indicates the reaction that this command will evoke from the Jews and the hypocrites. The reason for stating its reaction before the actual command is, first, that the command to change the Qibla was no ordinary command; it was a command that caused significant stir among both the opponents and the supporters of Islam. Therefore, it was necessary to prepare minds for the expected reaction before the command was given. The second reason is that the history of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) and his descendants narrated just prior to this has made it absolutely clear to every reader that the religion of Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and their descendants was Islam. The center built by Prophet Abraham (PBUH) in Makkah for the worship of Allah was the center and Qibla for all his descendants. Even when Bait al-Maqdis was built, it was constructed in such a way that the sacrifices of the Israelites faced the Kaaba. All these points unambiguously indicated that the Prophet's (PBUH) praying towards Bait al-Maqdis was a purely temporary matter, and the time had now come for him to be commanded to pray towards the Kaaba instead of Bait al-Maqdis. Thus, this prelude was for an event whose occurrence was awaited by both the Jews/Christians and the Muslims. 'Ma wallahum 'an qiblatihimul-lati kanu 'alayha' (What has turned them away from their Qiblah, which they used to face?):
The Jews' Objection to the Change of Qibla: This is the statement of the People of the Book's reaction to the command of changing the Qibla. The objections these people have been raising against the Muslims up to now have already been mentioned. Now, when the Qibla is changed to the Kaaba instead of Bait al-Maqdis, they will also raise a commotion over why the Muslims abandoned Bait al-Maqdis—the Qibla of all the prophets, towards which they had been praying until now—to build their own separate little mosque. 'Qul lillahil-mashriqu wal-maghrib. Yahdi may-yasha'u ila siratim-mustaqim' (Say, 'To Allah belongs the east and the west. He guides whom He wills to a straight path'). This is the answer to the aforementioned objection of the Jews and Christians: What do you have to do with the Qibla now? You have abandoned the original Qibla and gotten stuck in the cycle of East and West. The Christians have made the East their Qibla, and the Jews the West, even though no direction holds any intrinsic specific relevance to Allah. Allah Almighty is in every direction; East and West, North and South are all under His dominion. If there is any specificity, it can only belong to a House that He specifically designates and makes the Qibla. The House holding this specificity is the House built by Abraham and Ishmael, the Baitullah of Makkah. It was established as the Qibla for all of Abraham's descendants, and Bait al-Maqdis itself was built treating it as the Qibla. Traces and signs of this reality were present in the Torah. But due to prejudice, you erased these signs. However, despite your hostile efforts, Allah Almighty guided whom He willed to the straight path through His final Prophet, and now they have emerged from the twists and turns you created and have set out on the straight path. (To properly understand what we have discussed here, please review what we wrote under verses 115 and 125).

'Kadhalika' refers back to the aforementioned matter. Meaning, just as We brought you out of the twists and turns created by the Jews and Christians and the cycle of East and West regarding the matter of the Qibla and guided you to the straight path, similarly, We saved you from the narrow footpaths of Judaism and Christianity and made you an Ummah established on the main highway of religion, so that the Messenger may bear witness to Allah's religion over you, and you may bear witness to Allah's religion over the people of the world.
'Ummat-e-Wasat': The word 'Wasat', like the word 'Walad', is used for masculine, feminine, singular, and plural alike. It means something that is exactly in the middle between two extremes. From here, it developed the meaning of being 'better', because a thing that is between two extremes will be at the point of moderation and balance, and this is a natural proof of its superiority. The reason the Muslim Ummah is called 'Ummat-e-Wasat' is that this Ummah is established exactly on that central highway of religion which Allah Almighty opened for the guidance of creation through His prophets and messengers, and which has been the original highway of guidance from the beginning. The Jews and Christians deviated from this highway by making distinctions among Allah's prophets, and they carved out the side-paths of Judaism and Christianity. Similarly, they deviated from the original Qibla and fell into disputes over East and West. But this Ummah, instead of wandering in these crooked paths, remains established on the original path of religion. Its declaration is one of unity rather than division, a reference to which has already passed above in these words: 'Qulu amanna billahi wa ma unzila ilayna...' (Say, 'We have believed in Allah and what has been revealed to us and what has been revealed to Abraham and Ishmael and Isaac and Jacob and the Descendants and what was given to Moses and Jesus and what was given to the prophets from their Lord. We make no distinction between any of them, and we are Muslims [in submission] to Him') (Al-Baqarah: 136). Similarly, regarding the Qibla, instead of falling into the dispute of East and West, this Ummah followed that Abrahamic Qibla which had consistently been the Qibla of all prophets and messengers since the blessed era of Prophet Abraham (PBUH). Accordingly, the construction of Bait al-Maqdis, as mentioned above, was also done by making it the Qibla. But out of prejudice, the Jews tried to hide this fact. This very characteristic of the Muslim Ummah regarding religion is why it is called 'Khayr Ummah' (the best Ummah) elsewhere in the Quran. As mentioned above, whatever is exactly at the point of balance and moderation will necessarily also be the best. Since this Ummah is 'Ummat-e-Wasat', it is therefore also 'Khayr Ummah'.
Some people think that the religion of the Jews is very harsh and the religion of the Christians is very lenient, and Islam is a moderate religion between these two, which is why the Ummah bearing this moderate religion is called 'Ummat-e-Wasat'. However, in our view, this idea is incorrect. As far as the core religion is concerned, the religion of both Jews and Christians is the same. Prophet Jesus (PBUH) made the observance of the Torah as mandatory for his Ummah as it was for the Jews. If he gave any teaching separate from it, it does not hold the status of an independent teaching apart from the Torah, but rather its status is merely that of the wisdom of the religion and the spirit of the religion. Due to their worldliness, the Jews had drifted away from the original realities of the religion and had merely become slaves to rituals and restrictions and worshippers of words and phrases. Prophet Jesus (PBUH) acquainted them with the wisdom of the religion. The Gospel is not something separate from the Torah; rather, it draws wise attention to the mysteries and realities of the Torah itself.
'Litakunu shuhada'a 'alan-nasi wa yakunar-rasulu 'alaykum shahida' (That you will be witnesses over the people and the Messenger will be a witness over you). This explains the official duty of the 'Ummat-e-Wasat' and the necessity of its establishment. From the details above, it has become abundantly clear that the people Allah Almighty had appointed to the position of guidance broke God's covenant, altered His Sharia, lost His straight path, deviated from the Qibla He appointed, and concealed the testimonies of which they were made custodians. Under such circumstances, if humanity had any greatest need, it could only be that Allah Almighty raise an Ummah that is established on God's straight path, that becomes the bearer of the original religion through Allah's Messenger, and then bears witness to this religion before the people until the end of the world. 'The Messenger is a witness over you and you are witnesses over the people' clearly implies that the duty of 'Shahadat 'alan-nas' (bearing witness over mankind) that was upon the Holy Prophet (PBUH) in his capacity as a Messenger was transferred to his Ummah after him. And now it is the responsibility of this Ummah to bear witness to Allah's religion before the people in every era, every country, and every language. If it falls short in this duty, it will equally share with others in facing the consequences of the misguidance of this world. Our interpreters have generally considered this testimony to be related to the Hereafter—that this Ummah will testify in the Hereafter in support of the Prophets against the misguided, confirming that Allah's religion reached these misguided people, yet they still adopted the path of misguidance. But in our view, there is no proof for this specification and limitation. There is no doubt that this Ummah will also achieve this status of being 'Witnesses of Allah' in the Hereafter, but it will achieve this status in the Hereafter precisely because Allah Almighty has exalted it to this position in this world. The Ummah that is the witness of the true religion in this world will obviously be in the position in the Hereafter to testify whether the religion of Allah was accurately conveyed to the people or not.
'Wa ma ja'alnal-qiblatal-lati kunta 'alayha illa lina'lama may-yattabi'ur-rasula mim-may-yanqalibu 'ala 'aqibayh': The word 'Ja'ala' is used in broad meanings. One of its meanings is to make something permissible and lawful, for example: 'Allah has not appointed [such innovations as] bahirah or sa'ibah or wasilah...' (Al-Ma'idah: 103) (Meaning God did not make them lawful). 'Alima Ya'lamu' means not only to know and ascertain but also to distinguish, sort out, and make manifest. For example: 'And We will surely test you until We make evident those who strive among you [for the cause of Allah] and the patient' (Muhammad: 31). 'Or do you think that you will enter Paradise while Allah has not yet made evident those of you who fight in His cause...' (Al Imran: 142). The meaning is that the permission given to you to pray facing Bait al-Maqdis was not because this is your permanent Qibla; rather, this permission was a temporary and provisional one, and the purpose of this permission was so that the subsequent change of this Qibla would become a touchstone of trial for you. Through it, it would be made evident how many people among you truly follow the Messenger, and how many are worshippers of their past traditions rather than the Messenger, and turn back on their heels away from the religion itself. It should be kept in mind here that when the Holy Prophet (PBUH) was commanded to pray, he made Bait al-Maqdis the Qibla. In the beginning, the Prophet's method was that in matters where he had no clear guidance from divine revelation, he would follow the practice of the previous Prophets. Therefore, he did the same in the matter of the Qibla. As long as he remained in Makkah, traditions indicate that he would stand for prayers in such a way that both Baitullah (the Kaaba) and Bait al-Maqdis were in front of him. But when he migrated to Madinah, due to the change in direction, it was no longer possible to face Baitullah. Naturally, this disconnection from the Kaaba weighed heavily on his blessed heart, and he began waiting for divine revelation regarding this matter. However, divine wisdom dictated that he and the Muslims should pray towards Bait al-Maqdis for some more time. Thus, even after the migration, he continued to pray towards Bait al-Maqdis for about 16 or 17 months, until the command to change the Qibla was revealed approximately two months before the Battle of Badr. Allah Almighty explained the wisdom behind keeping them on the Qibla of Bait al-Maqdis for that long and then shifting it to the Kaaba: that in this way, He put the Muslims through a test to distinguish the genuine from the fake, so that the specific elements from the People of the Book who had joined the Muslims after coming to Madinah would pass through this test and either become entirely dedicated to Islam or be sifted out and separated from them. 'Wa in kanat lakabiratan illa 'alal-ladhina hadallah. Wa ma kanallahu liyudi'a imanakum.' Meaning, this change of Qibla is indeed a severe test, because in such matters relating to religion, and that too a fundamental aspect of religion, a person naturally tends to become emotional and tradition-bound; even a minor change in them is very hard to bear. But the core thing that holds weight in religion is complete obedience and sincerity to God and the Messenger. For this reason, Allah Almighty and His Prophets periodically strike at these prejudices that become veils over sincerity. The history of Prophets and religions bears witness that upon the arrival of every Prophet, nations had to pass through such trials. This trial is a Sunnah of Allah; driven by this Sunnah, many changes occurred in the rituals and outward forms of religion during the time of every Prophet so that the genuine could be distinguished from the fake. The falseness of those entangled in their national and group prejudices is exposed by these trials; instead of accepting the guidance of God and the Messenger, they stubbornly cling to their traditions. But those who possess the spirit of sincerity are blessed to accept Allah's guidance through the grace of their sincerity. Consequently, the reaction to this change of Qibla was exactly like this. The people who, wrapped in their past prejudices, had infiltrated the ranks of Islam merely due to some temporary expediency, turned back after this change. Conversely, for those who entered Islam purely with the spirit of worshipping Allah and obeying His Messenger, this change opened immensely vast doors of Allah Almighty's guidance and mercy.
Answer to an Important Question: The statement 'And Allah would never make your faith [prayers] to be lost. Indeed, Allah is, to the people, Kind and Merciful' provides the answer to a very important question that naturally arises here. That is, when the change of Qibla is, according to the Quran's own statement, a severe test, why did Allah Almighty choose to put people through such a severe test, the result of which could be that many people lose their very faith by failing it? The Quran removed this doubt by explaining that Allah Almighty does not put His servants through such tests so that they lose their faith; rather, these tests are manifestations of Allah Almighty's kindness and mercy. It is through these very tests that the capabilities of the servants develop. It is through them that the powers and potentials whose treasures nature has deposited within them come into action. It is through them that their genuine and fake, their sincere and hypocrite, and their truthful and liar are distinguished. If this test did not exist, there would remain no difference between good and bad, raw and mature, pearl and pebble. One would have to accept every claimant as truthful in their claim and verify the statements of every liar, to the extent that even in the Hereafter, there would be no proof or argument left to reward or punish anyone. If you reflect further, you will realize that all the beauty and grace of this universe's machinery, and all its wisdom and blessing, are hidden within this very Sunnah of trial and testing by Allah Almighty. If this were not there, this entire machinery would become completely unwise, purposeless, and merely a playful sport. 
This linguistic point should also be kept in mind that here Allah Almighty has referred to His names 'Ra'uf' (Kind) and 'Rahim' (Merciful). 'Ra'uf' comes from 'Ra'fah', in which the warding off of evil is dominant; and 'Rahim' comes from 'Rahmah', in which the establishment of good is prominent. If you ponder, you will see that both these aspects are intended in this Sunnah of Allah's trial and testing to which this verse points: meaning, purifying the servants from flaws and weaknesses and adorning them with virtues and merits. We limit ourselves to these indications here; this Sunnah of Allah will be discussed from various angles in different places ahead. Generally, commentators have taken this verse in the context that after the change of Qibla, a question arose among the people regarding those who had already passed away during the period of the previous Qibla—what would happen to them? Would their prayers be accepted or not? This is given as the answer to that question. But in our view, there was neither any reason for this question to arise nor any need to answer it. The true reality is the one we have pointed out.

Qad nara taqalluba wajhika fis-sama'. Falanuwalliyannaka qiblatan tardaha.
A Specific Style in the Arabic Language: Here, a specific style of the Arabic language should be well understood: the forms of deficient verbs (Af'al Naqisah) are usually omitted before the imperfect verb (Mudarib). For example, in 'Kana yaf'alu', simply saying 'yaf'alu' is considered sufficient. Abundant examples of this are found in both Arabic literature and the Holy Quran. We present a few examples from the Quran. In Surah Hud, it is stated: 'Fala taku fi miryatim-mimma ya'budu ha'ula'. Ma ya'buduna illa kama ya'budu aba'uhum min qabl' (So do not be in doubt as to what these [polytheists] are worshipping. They worship not except as their fathers worshipped before) (Hud: 109). Look at this verse: instead of 'Kama kana ya'budu', it just says 'Kama ya'budu'. 'Kana' was omitted. Similarly, in Surah Az-Zukhruf: 'Wa kam arsalna min nabiyyin fil-awwalin. Wa ma ya'tihim min nabiyyin illa kanu bihi yastahzi'un' (And how many a prophet We sent among the former peoples, But there would not come to them a prophet except that they used to mock him) (Az-Zukhruf: 6-7). If you reflect here, 'Wa ma ya'tihim' is actually 'Wa ma kana ya'tihim'. But according to Arabic style, 'Kana' was omitted. In Surah Al-An'am: 'Wa kadhalika nuri Ibrahima malakutas-samawati wal-ard' (And thus did We show Abraham the realm of the heavens and the earth) (Al-An'am: 75). If you reflect, you will find that 'Nuri Ibrahima' is actually 'Kunna wa kadhalika nuri Ibrahima', but according to the common style, 'Kunna' before the Mudarib was omitted. In accordance with this style, in the verse under discussion, 'Qad nara' is actually 'Qad kunna nara'. I have unfolded this omission in the translation because this style of omission does not exist in Urdu. The meaning is: We had been seeing the turning of your face towards the heavens, as you were eagerly waiting for the change of Qibla, so We have decided that We will surely turn you to a Qibla with which you will be pleased. It has been mentioned above that as long as the Holy Prophet (PBUH) remained in Makkah, it was possible to combine both Qiblas. But after migrating to Madinah, this was no longer possible. Because of this, this disconnection from the Abrahamic Qibla began to weigh heavily on him. Especially when it became clear to him through divine revelation that he had been sent upon the religion of Abraham, that Prophet Abraham (PBUH) had prayed for him, and that the Qibla of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) is actually the common Qibla for all of Abraham's descendants, he was constantly waiting for the change of Qibla. And as is the rule, if one is waiting for someone with zeal and longing, their gaze repeatedly goes towards the door. Similarly, his gaze would repeatedly rise upwards to the heaven, because the angel Gabriel would appear from that direction. The word 'Falanuwalliyannaka' expresses the decision Allah Almighty made regarding the change of Qibla. I have uncovered the hidden meaning of the word in the translation, but there was a need to reference some parallels for this. I recall that Maulana (RA) pointed to some of its parallels, but unfortunately, at the time of writing, I do not have the necessary reference books at hand. Perhaps I can make up for this shortcoming at the time of printing the book.
Fawalli wajhaka shatral-masjidil-haram. Wa haythu ma kuntum fawallu wujuhakum shatrahu: 'Shatr' means direction, side, and way. Al-Masjid Al-Haram refers to the sacred mosque that embraces Baitullah from all its sides like a halo. The true Qibla is actually Baitullah itself; thus, inside Al-Masjid Al-Haram, people pray facing Baitullah from all four sides. But for those outside, this mosque also falls under the ruling of the Qibla. In this way, a little vastness and ease have been created for the Ummah regarding the Qibla. Just as the actual place of sacrifice is Marwah, but for the ease of the Ummah, it has been expanded to Mina. In our view, this is the very verse that abrogated the permission given to the Prophet (PBUH) in the beginning to pray facing Bait al-Maqdis and commanded him instead to face Al-Masjid Al-Haram. Along with this command, saying 'And wherever you [believers] may be, turn your faces toward it' is an instruction given to save Muslims from the error that the Jews and Christians fell into. While explaining verse 115 above, we mentioned that the Jews and Christians made Bait al-Maqdis their Qibla while inside it, but once outside it, their Qibla became either the East or the West. To save this Ummah from this kind of misguidance, Allah Almighty instructed that wherever you are, inside Al-Masjid Al-Haram or outside, during prayer times, your face must be toward this specific Qibla alone. Attention should also be paid to the shift in address that is very prominent in this verse. First, the address is in the singular form: 'Fawalli wajhaka' (turn your face). Then it is stated in the plural form: 'Fawallu wujuhakum' (turn your faces). In our view, the reason for this shift is that the first address is to the Holy Prophet (PBUH) in his capacity as the representative of the Ummah. The second address clarified the implicit aspect of the first address: that although it is apparently in the singular form, it is not just for the Holy Prophet (PBUH) alone, but the entire Ummah is included in it. Furthermore, one reason for the first address being in the singular form is that, as indicated above, he was exceedingly anxious for the change of Qibla. This required that he be specifically addressed first and given the glad tidings of this change. Wa innal-ladhina utul-kitaba laya'lamuna annahul-haqqu mir-rabbihim. Wa mallahu bighafilin 'amma ya'malun: 'Annahu' (that it) here refers to Al-Masjid Al-Haram being the Qibla for this Ummah. That this was the truth and from God was absolutely clear to the People of the Book, because from the details the Quran has presented above, the following points have clearly emerged. First, the Jews knew that Baitullah was built by Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael, and this very Baitullah has been the original Qibla for all the descendants of Abraham. Second, the final Prophet would be born among the descendants of Ishmael, and through him, Allah Almighty would raise a Muslim Ummah. Third, the center and Qibla of these descendants of Ishmael had been this very Baitullah from the beginning. Hints and indications of all these things were present in the Torah, and the advent of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and the events of his life were confirming these hints and indications at every step. But the Jews, due to their envy and enmity towards the Ishmaelites and the Muslims, knowingly concealed all these things. Regarding this very concealment of the truth on their part, it is said in a threatening manner that 'Allah is not unaware of what they do', meaning they will definitely receive the appropriate punishment for this concealment of truth.

A Digression (Iltifat): This verse acts as a digression (Iltifat) to console the Holy Prophet (PBUH) that the attitude of the People of the Book regarding the Qibla is not based on any doubt or suspicion but, as clarified above, is purely based on deliberate stubbornness, enmity, and envy. Because of this, even if you were to show them all the miracles of the world, they are still not going to follow your Qibla. If anything could satisfy them, it is not arguments and miracles, but rather that you yourself become a follower of their Qibla. However, after the truth has been thoroughly clarified, there remains no possibility for you to follow their Qibla. Then it is also clarified that this stubborn and obstinate attitude is not specifically directed towards you alone; rather, these Jews and Christians cannot even follow each other's Qibla. Now that they have fallen into this dispute of East and West, this dispute is not going to end. And when those claiming to follow a single Qibla cannot unite among themselves, how on earth could they follow your Qibla? Finally, it is stated that if you were to follow their desires after the knowledge of revelation has come to you, then you too would be among the wrongdoers. This is a kind of threat which, although apparently addressed to the Holy Prophet (PBUH), is actually directed towards the Jews and Christians. Here, 'Al-Ilm' (knowledge) refers to the true knowledge attained through revelation, and 'Ahwa'' (desires) refers to the innovations of the People of the Book. We have clarified the meaning of both these words under verse 120.

'Alladhina ataynahumul-kitaba' here refers to the group of righteous People of the Book who, to the extent of their knowledge, remained steadfast on their religion and earnestly desired from the heart the manifestation of all the prophecies related to the final advent that were present in their scriptures. We have explained the reasons and arguments for taking this to mean the righteous People of the Book in full detail under verse 121. The pronoun in 'Ya'rifunahu' refers to the Holy Quran and its statement given above regarding the final advent and its Qibla. This verse has also appeared with exactly the same words in Surah Al-An'am: 'Alladhina ataynahumul-kitaba ya'rifunahu kama ya'rifuna abna'ahum' (Those to whom We have given the Book recognize it as they recognize their own sons) (Al-An'am: 20). The Eloquence of the Simile: The simile of recognizing it like sons contains the hidden meaning that just as a separated father remains anxious and restless for his long-lost son, and after a long period of separation when he comes, the scent of his garment from afar brings him the glad tidings of joy; similarly, these righteous People of the Book are well acquainted with every referent of all the prophecies related to the final advent, and whichever referent manifests before them, they welcome it like the lost Joseph. The passion of anticipation and longing that existed within the good People of the Book for the promised and awaited truth has been expressed by the Holy Quran in another place like this: 'And when they hear what has been revealed to the Messenger, you see their eyes overflowing with tears because of what they have recognized of the truth. They say, "Our Lord, we have believed, so register us among the witnesses."' (Al-Ma'idah: 83).

The Eloquence of Omitting the Subject (Mubtada'): In our view, 'Al-Haqq' is the predicate (Khabar), and its subject (Mubtada') is omitted. If the subject were made explicit, the complete sentence would be: 'Hadha huwal-Haqq' (This is the truth). 'Mir-rabbika' is connected to the predicate. In Arabic, the subject is generally omitted in situations where the addressee's entire attention needs to be focused on the predicate. 'Al-Haqq' appeared in the position of the predicate in verse 144 as well, and it appears in the same capacity in verse 149. The address 'Fala takunanna minal-mumtarin' (So never be among the doubters) is ostensibly directed towards the Holy Prophet (PBUH), but the direction of the warning and reprimand is towards the opponents. See verse 145. (To fully understand the various aspects of the address, it is advisable to carefully read the chapter on 'Address' in Maulana Farahi's Muqaddamah Tafseer. The Tafseer of Surah 'Abasa will also be useful for this purpose).

The Meaning of the Word 'Kull': Although the word 'Kull' (each/all) is technically indefinite (Nakarah), it generally refers to the specific group or individuals that have already been mentioned earlier in the discourse. For example: 'Wahabna lahu Ishaqa wa Ya'quba wa kullan ja'alna nabiyya' (We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and each We made a prophet) (Maryam: 49); 'Wa Isma'ila wa Idrisa wa dhal-kifl kullum-minas-sabirin' (And Ishmael and Idrees and Dhul-Kifl - all were of the patient) (Al-Anbiya: 85). Similarly, 'Li-kullin' here refers to the same groups of Jews and Christians whose mention has been continuing from above. Regarding them, it is said that each of them has designated a direction for their respective Qiblas. Some made it the East, some the West. They will only make this very direction they have designated their Qibla; no matter how much force you apply, these stones are not going to budge from their place in any way. Therefore, do not waste your time going after them, but move forward on the straight path shown by God and strive to outdo one another in virtues and good deeds. This is essentially saying the same thing that was said in the earlier verse: 'Wa la'in ataytal-ladhina utul-kitaba bikulli ayatim-ma tabi'u qiblatak...' (And if you brought to those who were given the Scripture every sign, they would not follow your Qiblah...)—but expressed here in a different style. The purpose here is absolutely not to express any tolerance regarding the matter of the Qibla, but rather to express disgust at the attitude of the Jews and Christians. The Muslims are being advised to leave these stagnant and stubborn people to their state, and you should try to advance on the path of attaining felicity. 'Fastabiqu' comes from the verbal noun 'Istibaq', which means to strive to outstrip and surpass one another in a race. For example, 'Inna dhahabna nastabiqu' (Indeed, we went racing) (Yusuf: 17). Just as in racing competitions a mark is set and competitors try to outrun each other, similarly, there is a mark or goal set by God for the race in the struggle of servitude, turning to Him, success, and felicity. This mark, as detailed above, was lost by the previous nations; hence, their racing also went completely into other valleys. Allah Almighty highlighted this mark of truth again for the 'Ummat-e-Wasat' and invited them saying that if others are not ready to enter this field, leave them to their state, and you should heat up this field with your own activities. The concept that the Qibla holds the status of a mark and flag for the pursuit of success and felicity is not merely a metaphor but a reality. To properly embed this reality in the mind, try to refresh in your memory the great history engraved on every single stone of the House designated as the Qibla. This House is the one whose construction was carried out by the sacred hands of Abraham, the Friend of Allah, and Ishmael, the Sacrifice of Allah. This House is the one that serves as the very first center for the worship of the One God in this idol-temple of a world. Next to this very House is the hill of Marwah, at whose foothills the eye of the sky witnessed an old father running a knife over the neck of his beloved and only son for the pleasure of God, demonstrating the reality of Islam. This is the very House whose surrounding barren plains were chosen by Providence for the nurturing of this Muslim Ummah, through which God's mercy was to be distributed to all the nations of the world. This is the very House that has consistently been the Qibla for all the holy ones since the time of Prophet Abraham, and in which so many humans have attained the blessing of Tawaf, I'tikaf, Ruku', and Sujud that just as counting the grains of sand on earth and the stars in the sky is impossible, counting those holy souls is also impossible. Near it is the plain whose every grain of sand is a witness to prostrations of repentance and seeking forgiveness, and a trustee of the tears of those weeping out of the fear of God. In one corner of this House is that sacred stone which has been likened to the right hand of God, and by touching or kissing which, millions and billions of prophets, the truthful, the pious, and the righteous have cemented their covenant of servitude and loyalty to their Lord. Near it are the Jamarat (pillars), which are the monuments of the humiliation and trampling of the enemies of this House, and by throwing stones at which, believers have continually renewed the spirit of Jihad against the enemies of the religion within themselves. And then, above all, it was in the shade of this very House that God's final Prophet, Muhammad (PBUH), was raised, whose brought light and bestowed radiance illuminated the entire world. Assigning a House with such grand traditions as the Qibla certainly means that by setting it as a mark, one should strive to attain those spiritual treasures that were entrusted to this House from Sayyidina Abraham down to the Holy Prophet (PBUH); or in other words, consider it a powerhouse from which the entire Ummah derives life, heat, light, and power. People to whom this aspect of the Kaaba's greatness and importance is not clear are often bewildered as to why a building made of bricks and stones has been given such importance in the religion. But from the above details, it has become abundantly clear that the real importance is not of the brick and stone building, but of those magnificent traditions attached to this House, which are the sole source of the spiritual and faith-based life of this world. Because of these traditions, this House holds the same importance in the social system of the Millah (nation) as the heart holds in the system of a body. Just as the body has no existence without the heart, the Millah has no concept without the Qibla. We limit ourselves here to these brief indications regarding the Qibla. God willing, we will cast a glance at some other aspects of its importance at suitable places later. 'Ayna ma takunu ya'ti bikumullahu jami'a. Innallaha 'ala kulli shay'in qadir' can have two meanings. One is that by making this Qibla your Qibla, wherever you strive in the path of virtue and goodness, it will not go to waste; God will gather you from everywhere and will reward you for every small or great good deed of yours. This is like a mark for racing toward goodness (Istibaq ilal-Khayrat). Proximity and distance to it are based on the connection of the heart. Every person can establish a connection with it from anywhere, and Allah Almighty can gather those connected to it from everywhere. The second meaning is that let whoever wants to face whichever direction do so; instead of getting entangled in these arguments, you should advance on the paths of virtue and goodness. A day will come when Allah will gather all of you and decide who walked the path of truth and who adopted the attitude of stubbornness and obstinacy.

Instruction to observe the Qibla during travel: In verse 143 above, within the main command of changing the Qibla, it was made clear that wherever a person may be, they should face the Qibla. However, there was no specific clarification there regarding the state of travel—whether the observance of this command is obligatory then too, or if there is some relaxation in it. Finding and investigating a specific Qibla during a journey is a difficult task. For this reason, the thought often arises that there shouldn't be any restriction during it. But the importance of the Qibla explained above clarifies that a person's connection with this spiritual powerhouse should not be severed under any circumstance. If freedom were given during the state of travel, it would provide ample room for the same kind of misguidance regarding the Qibla that the Jews and Christians fell into. Therefore, this Ummah was emphatically instructed in clear terms that just like in the state of residence, maintaining the Qibla during travel is also necessary, so that the Ummah does not fall into negligence regarding its core objective under any circumstance. Along with this emphasis, a warning was also issued: 'This is indeed the true Qibla appointed by God, so remember that Allah is not unaware of your deeds.' This warning cuts the root of any deliberate carelessness and any hypocritical excuse-making regarding the Qibla under the pretext of travel. It begins with the address in the singular form and ends with the plural form. This points to the reality that the initial address is to the Holy Prophet (PBUH) in his capacity as the representative of the Ummah; it implies the entire Ummah. It must be remembered here that since determining the exact Qibla during a journey sometimes becomes extremely difficult, what the Sharia requires is only the search for the Qibla to the extent of the available means of investigation. Islam has not placed any burden on the Ummah beyond its capacity in any matter. One should simply pray according to the dominant assumption achieved through the available means of investigation. This restriction does not in any way negate the concessions the Sharia has given the Ummah in cases of necessity, the explicit details of which are present in the books of Hadith and Fiqh (jurisprudence).

The Wisdom Behind Repeating the Command: The commands regarding both travel and residence have been stated above. Because of this, the immediate repetition of these exact same commands with the exact same words ostensibly carries a heavy sense of redundancy. In the Quran, which is a miracle of brevity and eloquence, this causes a slight uneasiness to the mind. But this uneasiness only arises due to a lack of deep reflection. The purpose of repeating these commands here is absolutely not to state the commands again, but rather to state the three magnificent wisdoms and benefits behind these commands intended for this Ummah, which are mentioned next. These wisdoms collectively relate to both commands, as will be clarified later, and even a slight ignorance or negligence regarding them can plunge this Ummah into such errors that no possibility of correction would remain. Therefore, before stating these wisdoms, the Quran refocused minds as a preamble on these commands: the command being given with such force, emphasis, and warning to face Baitullah everywhere and in every condition—inside and outside, in travel and residence—is not a cursory and superficial command. Rather, it is a command based on immensely profound benefits and wisdoms. If you show the slightest negligence in strictly adhering to it, and due to this negligence, even one step is taken incorrectly, your entire journey will head in the wrong direction. Therefore, observe them fully and embed their wisdoms well in your minds. After this preamble, these wisdoms are now being stated in these words: 'Li'alla yakuna linnasi 'alaykum hujjatun illa-lladhina zalamu minhum fala takhshawhum wakhshawni wa li-utima ni'mati 'alaykum wa la'allakum tahtadun.' If you reflect, it becomes clear that three wisdoms for these commands have been stated here. First, cutting off the argument (Qat'-e-Hujjat); second, completion of the favor (Itmam-e-Ni'mat); third, finding the right path (Rah-yabi). Now we will briefly explain all three so that the benefit of repeating the aforementioned commands and the structure of the discourse become completely clear.
Cutting off the argument means leaving no room for the People of the Book, especially the Jews, to constantly object to you and spread suspicions against you. Here, the context of the speech proves that 'Lin-Nas' (for the people) refers to the People of the Book. Due to sharing the same Qibla, the People of the Book, especially the Jews, would constantly raise the objection against the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and the Muslims that since they pray facing our very Qibla, why do they adopt a different path from ours in their methods of prayer and worship? They would, God forbid, label the differences in other matters after sharing a fundamental thing as fabricated inventions of the Prophet (PBUH). Their propaganda influenced simple-minded people, and it created major hurdles in clarifying the reality that the Prophet's (PBUH) advent was not upon Judaism or Christianity, but upon the religion of Abraham. The time had come to completely block this propaganda. For this purpose, the defensive restrictions mentioned above became necessary. Suppose these precautions were not taken, and the Muslims were left free to pray in whatever direction they wanted when outside Al-Masjid Al-Haram or while traveling. Setting aside the fact that Muslims would have fallen into the same kind of misguidance regarding the Qibla as the People of the Book, merely due to the apparent sharing in certain conditions, the Jews would have found some way or another to wag their tongues and plant doubts against the Muslims. The aforementioned restrictions closed all these loopholes. Even though mischievous people were not going to stop even after this cutting off of the argument—as no precaution in the world can shut the mouths of all kinds of people—the Quran provided the remedy for such people: 'Fala takhshawhum wakhshawni' (So do not fear them, but fear Me).
Completion of the favor refers to the bounty of the perfection of religion, the glad tidings of which Prophet Moses and Prophet Jesus (PBUT) had given regarding this Ummah, and the promise of which Allah Almighty had made to Prophet Abraham (PBUH) when he succeeded in the trial of sacrificing Prophet Ishmael. At that time, he was promised that a great Ummah would emerge from the lineage of this son, through which all the nations of the world would receive the blessing of religion. Consequently, from his very lineage, Allah Almighty sent the final Guide of the world, Muhammad (PBUH), whose Qibla was designated as that Baitullah which was established as the fountainhead of goodness and blessing and the center for the perfection of religion for the entire world.
Finding the right path means finding the 'Sirat al-Mustaqim' (Straight Path) which is the direct and natural path leading to God, about which it is said: 'Say, Indeed, my Lord has guided me to a straight path - a correct religion - the way of Abraham, inclining toward truth' (Al-An'am: 161). The lighthouse guiding towards this religion of Abraham is, as we mentioned above, this very Qibla. Therefore, it is necessary that this sign of guidance remains constantly before the eyes of this Ummah.

In the word 'Kama', the letter 'Kaf' is a particle of similitude. This raises the question: what is being compared here? The answer is that 'Kama' is used in almost the same context as we use the word "just as" or "accordingly." The meaning is that We have carried out this change of Qibla for the completion of favor and guidance toward the religion of Abraham, just as We sent a Messenger among you for these very purposes in accordance with the Abrahamic prayer. We have already discussed this verse in detail under Verse 129; there is no need to repeat it here. Finally, the statement 'wa yu'allimukum ma lam takunu ta'lamun' (and teaches you that which you did not know) is an expression of special grace and favor upon the Ishmaelites, meaning: you were an unlettered people unacquainted with religion and Sharia, and God sent this Prophet for your education and guidance, so you should be the ones to value him the most.

A Great Covenant between Allah Almighty and the Muslim Ummah: After the command to change the Qibla, this Ummah emerged as a completely distinct nation. The Jews were dismissed from the position of leadership, and the responsibility of bearing witness over mankind was entrusted to this Ummah until the Day of Judgment. On this important occasion, they are reminded: "Remember Me, and I will remember you; be grateful to Me, and do not be ungrateful." This reminder takes the form of a great covenant between Allah Almighty and this Ummah. Remembering God means remembering and fulfilling all the responsibilities and duties being entrusted to this Ummah. In response to the fulfillment of these responsibilities and duties, there is a promise from Allah Almighty: "I will remember you," meaning I will fulfill the promises of success, help, victory, and honor that I have made to this Ummah in both this world and the Hereafter. "Be grateful to Me" means to properly fulfill the rights of all the blessings received from Allah Almighty and those to be received in the future. The greatest of these blessings was the Sharia itself, which was now being transferred to this Ummah in its perfect form. The concluding words, 'wa la takfurun' (and do not be ungrateful to Me), contain a warning that if you are ungrateful, just as the Jews met their fateful end due to their ingratitude, you too will not escape the grasp of God's law. A similar reminder was also given to the Israelites, but they paid no heed to it. The Holy Quran references it as follows: 'Remember My favor which I have bestowed upon you and fulfill My covenant, I will fulfill your covenant, and fear Me' (Al-Baqarah: 40).

Difficulties of the Position of Leadership and Their Remedy: Now, in this verse and the four following verses, measures are given to counter the dangers and difficulties that will or might arise after assuming this position of leadership. The enmity the Jews held against the Muslims has already been well-clarified above, but due to the shared Qibla, the Jews still saw a glimmer of agreement amidst all the differences and disputes. However, after the change of the Qibla, they saw with their own eyes that the Muslims had now emerged completely distinct from them with all their specific characteristics, as the inheritors of the religion of Abraham. This naturally doubled their rage and anger against the Muslims. Similarly, the Quraysh—who had expelled the Muslims from Makkah, harboring the vain hope that this call would naturally die out in an alien environment—now realized that, contrary to their expectations, the Muslims were becoming a power in Madinah. Their claim was that they were the true inheritors of the religion of Abraham and the rightful custodians of the Kaaba. Accordingly, they had now made this House their Qibla, which could also imply that they might now attempt to take control of it. This realization alerted them as well, and they began devising plans to curb this threat. Consequently, just two months after the change of the Qibla, they created the causes for the war known in Islamic history as the Battle of Badr. Our research regarding this war, as we will present in the Tafseer of Surah Al-Anfal, is that it occurred due to a mutual conspiracy between the Jews of Madinah and the Quraysh of Makkah. Its purpose was to crush the rising power of the Muslims—who were now emerging as an independent Ummah claiming the Abrahamic religion and the Abrahamic Qibla—before it could fully consolidate. Although these circumstances were still behind the scenes, they were not hidden from the Knower of the Unseen (God), who is aware of both the hidden and the manifest. Therefore, His mercy and wisdom dictated that He should warn the Muslims of the impending dangers and guide them to what could sustain their resolve and courage against these threats. The first point in this series, mentioned in the verse under discussion, is to seek help through patience (Sabr) and prayer (Salah) during the upcoming difficulties. We have already discussed the linguistic research of patience and prayer, their mutual relationship, and their greatness and importance in the struggle to establish the religion under the Tafseer of verse 45 of this Surah. Furthermore, some of their important aspects have been highlighted in Chapter 32; thus, no further detail is needed here. However, there are some points specifically related to this context that must be pointed out.
First, the prayer whose support is recommended during difficulties and afflictions does not only mean the five obligatory daily prayers, but also includes Tahajjud and voluntary prayers. Because it is these prayers that infuse the believer with the spirit and life to overcome the difficulties encountered in the path of truth. It is with their help that a strong connection with Allah is developed, which does not break even in the severest of trials. And it is through them that the station of closeness is attained, which guarantees the companionship of God promised to the patient ones (Sabirin) in this verse. The full clarification of this reality will come in the Makkan Surahs, so we limit ourselves to just a hint here.
Second, among all acts of worship, prayer is the greatest manifestation of remembrance (Zikr) and gratitude (Shukr). The Holy Quran has clarified in various ways that the primary purpose of prayer is the remembrance of Allah Almighty and expressing gratitude to Him. If you reflect from this angle, you will realize that the covenant taken from this Ummah above—'Fadhkuruni adhkurkum washkuru li' (Remember Me, I will remember you, and be grateful to Me)—finds its best means of fulfillment in prayer.
Third, prayer is required to attain resolve and steadfastness in the path of calling to religion and establishing the truth. Therefore, the true blessing of this prayer is manifest when a person observes it while struggling against falsehood in the path of truth. For someone who has no intention of standing against falsehood, this weapon remains somewhat useless. 
Fourth, after mentioning seeking help through patience and prayer, it is stated that "Allah is with the steadfast." It does not say Allah is with those who pray and those who are patient. According to the teacher-imam (RA), the reason is that Allah's companionship in prayer is so obvious that it did not need to be stated; the point to clarify was that Allah becomes with those who remain steadfast in the path of truth and use prayer as a means to achieve that steadfastness. 
Fifth, Allah's companionship promised here for the steadfast is no ordinary thing; rather, the context of the speech testifies that a world of glad tidings is hidden within these few words. Since Allah Almighty is the true King of the entire universe and the Master of all command and authority, when He is behind someone, how can any power on earth, no matter how great, defeat them?

The Correct Concept of Life and Death: This points to the second thing required to remain firm on the path of resolve and steadfastness: the presence of the correct Islamic concept regarding life and death. For those who do not believe in the Hereafter, life is merely the life of this world; once a person dies or is killed, they are finished. But for a believer, this life is temporary and fleeting. The real life, which is eternal, begins for them at the moment this life ends. This life is attained in the realm of Barzakh (the interval between death and resurrection) and then in the Hereafter. As far as life after death is concerned, it is attained by both believers and disbelievers, but since the life of disbelievers is one of distress and punishment, it is not worth mentioning [as 'life' in a positive sense]. However, believers are joyful and successful in the life of Barzakh according to their respective ranks and degrees. Especially those among them who attain the status of martyrdom in the path of truth; the successes of their Barzakh life cannot even be imagined in this physical life. The rewards for the irrigation and life they provide to the field of truth in this world with their sacred blood of martyrdom begin to reach them from the realm of Barzakh itself. Thus, it is stated in another place: 'And never think of those who have been killed in the cause of Allah as dead. Rather, they are alive with their Lord, receiving provision' (Al Imran: 169).

A Brief Indication of Upcoming Difficulties: This is a brief indication of the trials that will be encountered in the upcoming stages. Although these trials will occur due to the mischief and conspiracies of enemies, they fall under the Sunnah of Allah, which He established from eternity to distinguish between the people of truth and the people of falsehood. For this reason, He attributed them to Himself, saying "We will test you," and stated it with emphasis. This is because for the people of truth, this trial and testing is inevitable in the divine law. Only by passing through such tests do the capabilities of servants flourish and the genuine are distinguished from the fake. Without this test, no group is deemed worthy of Allah Almighty's rewards in the Hereafter.
In this series, fear is mentioned first. Fear refers to the apprehension of enemy attacks and onslaughts. It was hinted above that as soon as they emerged as an independent Ummah, the Quraysh began creating excuses to attack the Muslims, and the Jews began their machinations. Gradually, an endless series of attacks began from their side, until foreign nations also began taking interest. This series only ended when the Muslims, through their resolve and steadfastness, thoroughly broke the power of all their rivals. This fear is mentioned with the qualifier "bi-shay'in" (with something), meaning "to some extent," which aims to encourage the Muslims: this condition will arise, but it will not exceed the amount necessary to test your resolve and steadfastness. Therefore, instead of being heartbroken or discouraged, one should face it firmly.
'Ju'' (hunger) refers to the economic difficulties that could arise due to the joint opposition of the Quraysh and the Jews, the state of fear and danger, and food blockades from their side. At that time, the Jews and Quraysh practically controlled all the trade and other economic resources of the country. Therefore, inviting a conflict with them was synonymous with picking a fight with crocodiles while living in the river. But the companionship of truth demanded that the Muslims take this risk as well. Consequently, they took this risk, and history bears witness that although this danger manifested—in some cases in a very terrifying form—compared to resolve and faith, it proved to be as insignificant as a straw (bi-shay'in).
After this, an indication is made toward the trial of loss of wealth and lives ('amwal wa anfus'), because in war and Jihad, these two things are the primary means used, resulting in the greatest sacrifice being made of them. Furthermore, due to the lack of peace and tranquility, they are also deprived of the care necessary for their growth.
The mention of 'thamarat' (fruits/crops), although it follows the mention of wealth and seemingly appears redundant as it is included in wealth, respects the context of the speech. The wealth of the Arabs consisted either of camels, sheep, and goats—for which the word 'amwal' was used—or fruits, especially dates. Due to this specific condition of the country, 'thamarat' was mentioned alongside 'amwal.'
Finally, glad tidings are given to those who remain firm on the truth despite all these trials and do not allow any weakness in their resolve and faith. From clarifications in other parts of the Quran, it is known that this glad tiding encompasses success in both this world and the Hereafter. For example, it is stated: 'O you who have believed, shall I guide you to a transaction that will save you from a painful punishment? [It is that] you believe in Allah and His Messenger and strive in the cause of Allah with your wealth and your lives. That is best for you, if you should know. He will forgive for you your sins and admit you to gardens beneath which rivers flow and pleasant dwellings in gardens of perpetual residence. That is the great attainment. And [you will obtain] another [favor] that you love - victory from Allah and an imminent conquest; and give good tidings to the believers' (As-Saff: 10-13).

The Shield of the Patient: This describes the quality of the patient ones (Sabirin)—that they do not face trials with dejection or discouragement, but with a cheerful countenance, resolve, and stability. Their statement quoted here is, in reality, an expression of the creed upon whose rock the building of patience and steadfastness is established. One part of this creed is for a person to believe that in this world, they belong to Allah and are for Allah. The second part is that after death, they must return to Allah. A person who holds firm faith in these two realities cannot be moved from the path of truth by even the greatest calamity. When we have come into this world sent by God, our living and dying are for Him, and He is the one to whom we must go after death, then for His sake, we can turn away from everything, but what power could there be that could turn our face away from Him? This declaration is the shield and armor of the patient. With it, they block every blow of calamity. The entrustment and surrender to Allah within it is the surrender of self-sacrifice and life-risking devotion, meaning that when the time comes, the believer, chanting this slogan, fights even rivers and mountains for their Lord. They uproot everyone else's feet, but nothing can uproot theirs.

The Meaning of 'Salawat': 'Salawat' is the plural of 'Salah', which originally means "turning toward something" in linguistics. Based on this meaning, the word was used for prayer, as the servant turns toward their Lord in prayer. Similarly, this word also applies to the attention and favor that Allah Almighty bestows upon His servants. In this case, its meaning becomes divine grace and favors. The spirit of the word remains the same, but due to the change in relationship, it acquires the meaning of humble supplication in one case and grace and favor in the other. For this reason, we have translated it as "favors" ( عنایتیں). Examples of such changes in the meaning of words due to a change in relationship are abundant in the Arabic language. The glad tidings of favor, mercy, and guidance for these patient ones relate to both religion and the world, and this world and the Hereafter, as mentioned above. Through patience and steadfastness, the believers become worthy of their Lord's bounties and favors, and through these very bounties and favors, they attain guidance to the straight path which guarantees success in both this world and the Hereafter.

Return to the Original Sequence of Discourse: This verse relates to the original subject of discussion, namely the Qibla. The preceding subject matter, as clarified, appeared incidentally as a warning that this change of Qibla is no ordinary change but a precursor to many trials for the Muslims, for which patience and prayer serve as the means of fulfillment. After this incidental subject, the original sequence of discussion is resumed, mentioning that Safa and Marwah are among the symbols (Sha'air) of Allah and providing the commands and instructions related to them. This is because just as the Jews tried to conceal the matter of Baitullah being the Abrahamic Qibla (as discussed in detail above), they also tried to conceal Marwah, which is the original site of sacrifice for Prophet Abraham, the details of which follow in the next verse.
Safa and Marwah are the two hills near Baitullah between which the 'Sa'i' (ritual walking) is performed during Hajj and Umrah. Maulana Farahi, in his book 'Ar-Ra'y as-Sahih fi man huwa-dh-Dhabih', has proven in full detail that the original site of sacrifice, where Prophet Abraham sacrificed Prophet Ishmael, is this very Marwah mentioned in the Torah. However, the Jews, in order to sever Prophet Abraham's connection to Baitullah, altered this word into something else.
It should be clear that the original site of sacrifice is this very Marwah, but in view of the expansion of the Ummah, it was extended to Mina. For details on this series, please refer to the late teacher's treatise 'Az-Zabih'.
The Purpose of 'Sha'air' (Symbols): 'Sha'air' is the plural of 'Sha'irah', which means something that creates awareness of a reality—a manifestation and a sign. In religious terminology, it refers to those manifestations of the Sharia appointed by Allah and His Messenger as a sign and symbol to create awareness of a spiritual reality. In these manifestations, the primary objective is the realities hidden within them, but because they are appointed by Allah and the Messenger, these manifestations also acquire a status of sanctity in relation to those realities. For example, sacrifice is a manifestation of the reality of Islam. The reality of Islam is for the servant to surrender themselves entirely to their Lord and not withhold even their most beloved thing from Him. The practical demonstration of this reality, as performed by Prophet Abraham through the sacrifice of his son, is an unparalleled event in human history. For this reason, Allah Almighty appointed the sacrifice of animals as a 'Sha'irah' (symbol) in its memory so that the original reality of Islam remains constantly fresh within people. Similarly, the Black Stone (Hajar al-Aswad) is a 'Sha'irah'. This stone has been a sign of the tradition since the era of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) that by kissing it or touching it, the servant renews their covenant of servitude and their pledge of obedience to their Lord. Thus, in some Hadiths, it is described as 'Yamin Allah' (the Hand of God), which points to the fact that when the servant touches it, it is as if they are placing their hand in God's hand to renew their pledge of allegiance. And when they kiss it, it is an expression of their covenant of love and loyalty to God. Similarly, the Jamarat (pillars for stoning) are also among the symbols of Allah. These signs were established so that pilgrims, by throwing pebbles at them, express their resolve that they curse the enemies of Baitullah and the enemies of Islam—whether they belong to the progeny of Iblis or any group of humans—and are always ready for Jihad against them. By the same analogy, Baitullah is also a symbol—in fact, the greatest symbol—which is the Qibla of the entire Ummah and the center of monotheism and prayer. By circumambulating it and making it the Qibla of our prayers and all our mosques, we express the reality that we are the servants of the One God for whose worship this House was built, we turn toward Him, we are His worshippers, and we are devoted like moths to His lamp of monotheism. Similarly, Safa and Marwah are also among the symbols of Allah Almighty. The reason generally given for them being symbols is that Hagar ran between these two hills in search of water for Prophet Ishmael. However, the teacher-imam's inclination is toward the fact that the original site of sacrifice is Marwah. It was here that Prophet Abraham showed obedient and servant-like activity in fulfilling his Lord's command; therefore, these two hills were declared among the symbols, and the memory of their 'Sa'i' was preserved forever.
Some Fundamental Points Regarding Symbols: A few fundamental points regarding these symbols should be remembered. First, these symbols are appointed by Allah and His Messenger. No one else has the right to declare something as a symbol of religion on their own or to remove something that is included in the symbols from the list of symbols. Such arbitrary interventions in religion open the paths to Shirk (polytheism) and Bid'ah (innovation). History bears witness that nations that appointed their own symbols opened the paths to Shirk and idolatry. Second, just as the symbols are appointed by Allah, the limits of venerating these symbols in Islam are also appointed by God and the Messenger. The form of veneration for a symbol established in the Sharia is the only way to express the reality hidden within that symbol; any deviation from it not only deprives a person of the reality of that symbol but can also open the doors to Shirk and Bid'ah. Suppose the Black Stone is a symbol. For its veneration, the forms of kissing it during Tawaf, or touching it and kissing the hand, or pointing toward it have been appointed by the bringer of the religion. If someone does not suffice with these forms of veneration but, in a zeal for venerating the symbols of Allah, begins to kneel before this stone, or present offerings to it, or shower flowers upon it, or perform any other such act, then through these things, they will not only become distant from the reality hidden within the symbol but will also fall into Shirk and Bid'ah. Third, the primary focus in these symbols should be the realities hidden within them. These symbols serve as the mold for expressing those realities. Therefore, the most necessary task for the life of the nation is to keep these realities alive and fresh in people's hearts and minds. If this care cools down, the original spirit of the religion leaves, only the mold remains, and then gradually people's primary attention focuses only on the molds, resulting in the religion becoming merely a collection of rituals.
In the verse under discussion, stating that "Safa and Marwah are among the symbols of Allah" aims, on one hand, to cleanse these two symbols from the dust of the era of ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and highlight them anew for the Ummah carrying the Abrahamic inheritance. The detail of this summary is that the pagan Arabs, as known from traditions, had placed two idols on these two hills and began performing 'Sa'i' and Tawaf for these idols, causing the status of these symbols as Abrahamic symbols to become doubtful and making them manifestations of open Shirk and idolatry. Just as the Quran in the preceding verses presented Baitullah in its original Abrahamic beauty by cleansing it of all polytheistic pollutions, it here states the original history of Safa and Marwah: that they have been among the symbols of Allah since the time of Prophet Abraham, and the Sunnah of their 'Sa'i' and Tawaf is a memorial of Prophet Abraham's (PBUH) 'Sa'i' and Tawaf. But just as the polytheists brought hundreds of idols into the center of monotheism, Baitullah, they also tainted these symbols with idolatry. Now it is your responsibility to remove this heap of filth, highlight these symbols anew, and dedicate their 'Sa'i' and Tawaf solely to Allah. On the other hand, the Jews had placed a veil of alteration and concealment over these symbols, as mentioned in the next verse; the Quran lifted that veil as well. We hinted above that the Torah explicitly mentioned that Prophet Abraham sacrificed his only son near Marwah, but the Jews, solely with the intention of proving this site was in Bait al-Maqdis instead of Makkah, completely distorted the correct pronunciation of this word. In this way, the prophecies in the Torah regarding the advent of the final Prophet could be transferred from the lineage of Prophet Ishmael to the lineage of Prophet Isaac. By referencing Marwah here, the Quran pointed its finger at the landmark that had been attempted to be hidden out of sheer envy and mischief.
The command given for the 'Tawaf' of these two hills—its correct form and the determination of its limits—comes from the Sunnah of the Prophet (PBUH), like other rites of Hajj. Although the Quran uses the word 'Tawaf', it refers to the 'Sa'i' performed between them. The reason for describing this 'Sa'i' with the word 'Tawaf' is that its form is similar to the Tawaf performed around the Kaaba. This 'Sa'i' is made conditional upon Hajj and Umrah, which implies that it is a part of the collective rites of Hajj and Umrah and has no independent status separate from them. This completely negates the polytheistic rituals that the pagans had added to these symbols. The command for this Tawaf is stated in words that require some clarification. It says: 'fa-man hajjal-bayta awi-'tamara fala junaha 'alayhi ay-yattawwafa bihima' (So whoever performs Hajj to the House or performs Umrah - there is no blame upon him for walking between them). From this style of expression, it seemingly appears that there is only permission for this 'Sa'i' in the Sharia; if someone does not do it or cannot do it, there is no particular harm. But in our view, this idea is incorrect. If that were the intention, the style of the discourse, as Lady Aisha Siddiqa (RA) stated, would have been 'an la yattawwafa bihima' instead of 'ay-yattawwafa bihima'. Secondly, after stating that Safa and Marwah are both among the symbols of Allah, it would be somewhat inappropriate and disjointed to say that performing Tawaf of them or not is equal. After the first statement, the only thing of equal weight and harmony would be to make their Tawaf mandatory. As for the question of what degree of mandatory it is—whether its status is Fard (obligatory), Wajib (necessary), or Mustahabb (recommended)—there can be disagreement, but there is no room to think that after such a magnificent preamble, the actual point would be of such a weak degree. For this reason, our view is that this is a command for 'Sa'i', and this command is at the level of 'Wujub' (necessity). But this certainly raises the question: if this is the aim, what does 'fala junaha' (no blame/sin) mean here? The answer is that this removal of blame does not relate to the command of 'Sa'i' itself, but to the repulsion felt at the time the command was revealed due to the presence of idols at the site of 'Sa'i'. The meaning is: although this repulsion currently exists at Safa and Marwah, since they are among the symbols of Hajj appointed by Allah, perform 'Sa'i' between them during Hajj and Umrah; your action will be according to your intention.
