In 'wa man tatawwa'a khayran fa-innallaha shakirun 'alim', 'tatawwu'' refers to a person performing an additional voluntary good deed to seek God's pleasure and closeness after having fulfilled an obligatory duty. Here, this 'tatawwu'' does not relate only to the command of 'Sa'i'. It has been clarified above that 'Sa'i' is not an independent act of worship but an appendix to Hajj and Umrah; therefore, this 'tatawwu'' can also relate to Hajj and Umrah. The meaning is: first, there is the Hajj and Umrah performed as an obligatory duty; second, Hajj and Umrah can also be performed as voluntary acts. For those who do so, Allah will accept their good deed, and it will remain in His knowledge. One day He will give the full reward for it. The word 'Shukr' (gratitude/appreciation), like the words 'Salah' or 'Tawbah', is among those words whose meaning changes with a change in relationship. When attributed to the servant, it means gratitude. But when attributed to God, it means acceptance.

The Jews' Concealment of Truth: This refers to the Jews. Although 'bayyinat' (clear proofs) and 'huda' (guidance) in the verse also refer to the general teachings that the Jews tried to hide, the context of the speech here proves that it specifically refers to those signs which Allah Almighty clarified in the Torah so that the Jews could obtain guidance regarding the final Prophet with their help. But instead of benefiting from these signs, the Jews tried to hide them. We have presented some examples of this in the previous pages of this book. Here, we will reference the eighth chapter of the teacher-imam's great work 'Ar-Ra'y as-Sahih fi man huwa-dh-Dhabih', in which he discusses the Jews' alterations regarding Marwah and shows in great detail the tactics they used to make the site of Prophet Abraham's sacrifice disappear from the Torah and how ruthlessly they distorted the word 'Marwah' so that confusion could be created in people's minds regarding the prophecies of the final Prophet. Concealing a great reality when it has been well-clarified in their own Book, and after a covenant has been taken from them to clarify it to the creation—as referenced in Al Imran 187: 'And [remember] when Allah took a covenant from those who were given the Scripture, [saying], "You must make it clear to the people..."'—was such a crime by the Jews that they deserved God's curse, and the trust of the Divine Book entrusted to them was snatched away and given to others. Regarding this curse, it is said: 'yal'anuhumullahu wa yal'anuhumul-la'inun' (Allah curses them and those who curse curse them). This will be clarified further. It should be remembered here that just as 'Istifa' (being chosen for the leadership of the world) is Allah Almighty's greatest blessing, this curse is the greatest punishment from Allah Almighty. The nation given this punishment is deprived of the success of guidance and the position of leadership in this world and is plunged into humiliation and disgrace, and for it in the Hereafter is eternal punishment. The reason is that through their concealment of truth, they do not merely provide the means for their own misguidance but, by making the landmarks of guidance disappear, they also plunge countless other people into misguidance and destruction.

Condition for Repentance: This mentions those who will remain protected from that curse. These are the people who repent for the crime of concealing the truth. Along with this repentance, the condition of 'aslahu' (they reform) is attached, which clarifies the reality that repentance is not valid until a person reforms the error they were committing. A further condition 'bayyanu' (they make manifest) was added. This is appropriate to the occasion and explains the aforementioned 'aslahu'. Meaning: they should reveal the realities and clear proofs of the Torah regarding the final Prophet which they had hidden. This shows that until that time, at least the elite and scholars of the Jews were not unaware of the alterations made or being made under the conspiracy of concealing the truth. Proof of this is also provided by the fact that those among the Jewish scholars who were blessed with the bounty of Islam did indeed lift the veils from many such realities. In 'atubu 'alayhim', the preposition 'ala' with 'tawbah' points to the fact that a hidden meaning of mercy is contained within it. Meaning: I accept the repentance of such people and have mercy on them. This hidden reality of the word is clarified by saying 'wa ana-t-tawwabur-rahim' (and I am the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful).

"Those who disbelieved and died while they were disbelievers" refers to those who remained obstinate and left the world deprived of repentance, reform, and the expression/declaration of truth. This shows that concealing the truth is a crime that will completely nullify their other acts of religiosity and all their claims of faith and love for God. In retribution for this, they will deserve the curse of Allah, His angels, and all of mankind. Mentioning the curse of the angels and all people alongside Allah's curse clarifies the summary present in the previous verse's words 'wa yal'anuhumul-la'inun'. The qualifier 'ajma'in' (all together) with 'an-nas' (mankind) clarifies the reality that on the Day of Judgment, when the true reality is unveiled, not only will the righteous curse them, but even the sinners who were misled by following them will curse them.
Meaning, neither will there be any reduction in the punishment they are cast into, nor will there be any interval or break in its continuity that would give them even a moment to catch their breath.

Summary of Verses 163 to 176:
The Second Chapter of the Surah's Contents: The first chapter of this Surah ended at Verse 162. In that chapter, the Jews were dismissed from the position of leadership, and a new Ummah emerged with all its characteristics. Now, from Verse 163, the second chapter of the Surah's contents begins, in which the Divine Sharia is being renewed for this new Ummah. In this chapter, the Ummah is given those commands in a suitable order that the circumstances of the Surah's era of revelation demanded, and simultaneously, under each command, the innovations (Bid'ah) that the Jews or polytheists had mixed into the Divine Sharia are refuted. This chapter begins with the statement of Tawheed (Monotheism) because the foundation of the entire religion rests upon it. After mentioning the claim of Tawheed, its proof is stated. This proof of Tawheed is the same one we referred to in Chapter 23 of this book as the 'Argument from Harmony' (Dalil-e-Tawafuq). Here, this argument appears with some new aspects that will be clarified under the exegesis of the verses. Then, Shirk (polytheism) is refuted; in this context, declaring something prohibited (Haram) or permitted (Halal) without God's command is also condemned, because in its essence, this act also falls under Shirk. Then, a brief indication is made toward the things that Allah has actually made Haram, so it becomes clear that the things the polytheists or People of the Book prohibited or permitted on their own—merely under their polytheistic superstitions or for their desires—have no connection to the Divine Sharia. Following this, in a few verses, the polytheists are reprimanded for their blind and deaf following of their ancestors, and the People of the Book for their concealment of the truth—that if they had used their reason and not preferred misguidance over guidance merely in pursuit of their base desires, they would not have opposed Tawheed and supported Shirk. But through their wretched deeds, they chose this very path of eternal destruction for themselves. In this light, now observe the translation and exegesis of the following verses.
'Ilah' means the one worshipped. By adding the definite article 'Al' to it, the word 'Allah' is used as the proper name for Allah Almighty. The linguistic research of 'Rahman' and 'Rahim' and the explanation of the difference between the two has already passed in the Tafseer of Surah Al-Fatihah.
This Tawheed is the very first and greatest thing transferred to this Muslim Ummah as the inheritance of the religion of Abraham. It is mentioned here from both positive and negative aspects so that no room remains for any loophole. If a loophole is created here, it is a loophole in the foundation, through which Satan finds a way to interfere in the entire religion. 
"Aspects of mentioning Rahman and Rahim": Referencing 'Rahman' and 'Rahim' from the Beautiful Names alongside this statement of Tawheed holds two different aspects. One aspect is that among the causes and motives of Shirk is a false concept of God being too exalted and high for any kind of occupation, action, or relationship. This concept is intended as one of transcendence (Tanzihi), but in some forms, it increases God's 'unconnectedness' (be-hamagi) to such an extent that His relationship with creation is completely severed, and attaining access to Him or Him having any connection with the affairs of creation is deemed contrary to His status of Divinity. This concept of God's unconnectedness naturally creates a despair in hearts toward Him, and this despair eventually gives birth to the 'means and intermediaries' (wasail o wasait) that humans use as a source of consolation and tranquility when they feel they cannot reach God. To remove this misconception on the path of knowing God, the Quran has frequently mentioned, alongside the statement of God's Oneness, His unconnectedness, and His superiority, such attributes that clarify His relationship with creation, so that instead of seeking the support of means and intermediaries, humans try to grasp God's own mantle of mercy and hide within it. Thus, from this angle, in Surah Al-Ikhlas—which is the greatest Surah of Tawheed—if on one hand 'Huwallahu Ahad' (He is Allah, the One) was said to clarify God's unconnectedness, then simultaneously 'Allahus-Samad' (Allah, the Eternal Refuge) was also said to clarify His 'connectedness' (ba-hamagi), so it becomes clear that despite being independent of all and superior to all, God is also the refuge for all, the place of return for all, and the rock of support for all. On this same principle, in the verse under discussion, after stating God's Oneness from both positive and negative aspects, it was also clarified that this God is Rahman and Rahim. We explained these two words in Surah Al-Fatihah: the first denotes the surge of God's mercy, and the second denotes the continuity and permanence of His mercy. The objective is: God is indeed One and Unique, independent of all, and superior to all, but He is also Rahman and Rahim. He granted you existence out of His surge of mercy, He is nurturing and raising you in the lap of His mercy, and for this very mercy of His, He has appointed a Day of Judgment and Reward for you; so live for Him alone and die for Him alone, and associate all your hopes and dreams with Him alone. 
The second aspect is that polytheistic and idol-worshipping nations have always considered wrath and anger as necessary corollaries of Divinity. They compared the King of the universe to worldly kings. They saw that when worldly kings, being kings of only a single region, possess such majesty and power that no one except their close associates and courtiers dares to speak a word before them, and they can execute whomever they want whenever they want, then who can estimate the majesty, power, wrath, and anger of the King of the entire world? Thus, they conceived of God as an extremely terrifying and horrific being, and then invented some close associates and courtiers from their own minds and began worshipping them so that these might protect them from the calamities of this horrific God. Although nations possessing heavenly religions were not unacquainted with the correct concept of God, the passage of time and the influence of polytheistic nations tainted their beliefs as well, and among them too, the color of God's attributes of majesty (Jalali) dominated His attributes of beauty (Jamali). Thus, a study of the Torah clearly reveals that the Jews had also increased the narrative of God's wrath and majesty so much that the concept of God being Rahman and Rahim was completely suppressed in comparison. The effect was that they too, like polytheistic nations, became dependent on close associates and intercessors to obtain God's mercy, and for this purpose, they made their elders—whose traditions of sanctity and closeness existed among them—the intermediaries. Gradually, they included the family of Israel among God's favorites and beloveds, while non-Israelites were left for God's wrath and anger. Since in the verse under discussion, this trust of Tawheed is being taken back from the Israelites and handed over to the Muslim Ummah, it became necessary to lift that veil from God's attributes of Rahmaniyyah and Rahimiyyah which the Jews had placed over them in imitation of the polytheists, so that this Ummah arrives at that point of moderation (I'tidal) regarding Divine attributes which is the characteristic of its temperament as the 'Ummat-e-Wasat', and thus the tribulations of Shirk are blocked. We suffice here with mentioning only these two aspects. Some other aspects are also worthy of attention, but more suitable opportunities for mentioning them will come later in this book.

A Comprehensive Reference to the Signs of the Heavens and the Earth: The 'creation' (khalq) of the heavens and the earth refers to their very genesis which clarifies the Creator's great power; it also refers to their structure which testifies to His unparalleled craftsmanship and wonder-inducing wisdom; it refers to their benefit and blessing which proves the Creator's Rahmaniyyah, Rahimiyyah, and Lordship (Rububiyyah); it refers to that purposiveness which testifies that this machinery filled with so much wisdom is not vain or aimless but has a great purpose behind it for whose manifestation a Day is appointed; and it refers to their harmony and compatibility which proves that both the heavens and the earth emerged from the will of a single Creator and are operating under His scheme and His command, with no room for anyone else's will or intervention. These various aspects are clarified in the Holy Quran through diverse styles, the details of which will follow.
'Ikhtilaf al-layl wan-nahar' (The alternation of night and day) refers, first, to the coming and going of night and day one after another with a complete system, total punctuality, and perfect continuity, as stated: 'And He it is who has made the night and the day in succession for whoever desires to remember or desires gratitude' (Al-Furqan: 62). Second, it refers to their difference in temperament, nature, shape, form, and their outward and inward effects and results; yet despite this difference and contrast, both are busy day and night in the collective service and welfare of this universe. 
'Fulk' means ship. This word in this form is used for singular, plural, masculine, and feminine alike. The feminine is used in this very verse. For the masculine, the construction 'fil-fulkil-mashhun' exists in the Holy Quran. 
'Bima yanfa'un-nas' (with what benefits people) refers to the goods of trade and livelihood for which these ships serve as a means of transport, and through which immensely vast paths for the expansion and progress of society and civilization have opened. 
The 'death' of the earth and its 'life' refers to its becoming lush and green again with vegetation and plants after becoming dry and barren. 
'Dabbah' is commonly used for animals that walk on the earth, specifically for those used for riding or carrying loads, but it is also used in the sense we use the word 'living creature.' According to its first meaning, birds are excluded from its definition; only animals that walk or crawl on the earth are intended. Thus, the Holy Quran has kept birds separate from the definition of this word in some places, for example: 'And there is no creature [dabbah] on the earth nor a bird that flies with its wings...' (Al-An'am: 38). But when it is used in its second, broader sense, all living creatures fall under it, whether they are beasts or birds; in fact, in this case, it even encompasses the human race. Consider a few examples from the Holy Quran for this meaning: 'And if Allah were to punish the people for what they have earned, He would not leave upon the back of the earth any creature [dabbah]' (Fatir: 45). In this verse, the word 'dabbah' is used for all living creatures, whether they are beasts, birds, or humans. 'And how many a creature [dabbah] carries not its [own] provision. Allah provides for it and for you' (Al-'Ankabut: 60). In this verse, the word 'dabbah' encompasses all beasts and birds. 'And there is no creature [dabbah] on earth but that upon Allah is its provision' (Hud: 6). In this verse too, this word is used in its broad sense. 
In our view, in the verse under discussion, this word is also used in the sense of 'living creature.' Accordingly, we have translated it based on this meaning.
"Tasrif ar-riyah" refers to the circulation of the winds. Various aspects of this circulation are described in the Quran itself. Sometimes they carry clouds heavy with water on their shoulders and turn the earth into a pool; sometimes they blow these same clouds away so that not a trace of them is seen anywhere. For one nation they appear as a punishment, for another as a mercy. It was through their circulation that Pharaoh and his people were drowned in the sea, and it was through their intervention that Moses (PBUH) and his people were guided across that same sea. Then sometimes they become moist to nurture the crops, making them grow and flourish; sometimes they become hot and dry to ripen and prepare them; sometimes they become autumn to wither the leaves and desolate the garden; sometimes they become spring to load every twig and branch with flowers and buds. Their guises are different, and in every guise there is a new grace and a new glory, and whatever glory there is, it is a great sign of the wisdom and power of their User (God) and His mercy and Lordship. 
The Meaning of "Taskhir": 'Taskhir' means to make someone submissive and obedient and put them into someone's service without any wage or compensation. The clouds being 'subjugated' (musakh-khar) between the heavens and the earth means that they stand perfectly subdued and compelled under God's order and command every moment and every second, ready to obey His command whenever, for wherever, and in whatever form they are ordered. These are subjugated in God's hand, and He uses them in whatever form of mercy or punishment He wills according to the demands of His Lordship and His wisdom. In the Quran, when the subjugation of clouds and winds is mentioned in relation to humans, it does not mean that the clouds, winds, sun, or moon are subjugated in the human hand or that humans can subjugate them; rather, it only means that the Lord of the worlds has subjugated these things and put them into the service and benefit of humans, and despite being in service day and night, they do not seek any wage or reward from humans. For this reason, wherever this subject is described, 'sakh-khara lakum' (He subjugated for you) appears, meaning God has put them to your benefit; it does not mean He has made them subject to your command. They are subject to the command of God alone. The most a human can do is to discover some of the physical laws under which God has kept these things through the power of their science and benefit from them. But the actual control of all these laws remains in God's hand. Humans can never gain control over that source. 
Referencing all the aforementioned things, it is stated that within them are 'Ayat' (signs) for people who use their reason. The meaning of 'Ayah', as we have clarified elsewhere, also includes 'sign' and 'landmark.' Something that is a sign and landmark of something else serves as its proof; therefore, the question arises: for what and in what nature are the aforementioned things proofs? This question arises because it is stated here that there are proofs within these things, but it is not clarified what these proofs are for and what the form of their being a proof is. By pointing to the signs, the actual question is left to our reason and insight, so that those who use their reason and understanding will understand these proofs themselves. The Holy Quran has adopted this method in many places, and its purpose is the training of our reason and thought so that we become capable of understanding the proofs spread throughout the horizons and the souls and reaching correct conclusions from them. 
The Method of Reflecting on the Quran's Brief Indications: In such instances, the method of reflecting on the Quran is to first try to give the color of detail to the brief indications in the light of the Quran itself, so that it becomes clear how a proof is established for the claim from the presented things. For example, from the creation of the heavens and the earth, as we hinted above, sometimes the argument is for God's power and wisdom, sometimes for His Lordship and Rahmaniyyah, sometimes for their being purposeful and goal-oriented, and sometimes from the aspect of their harmony (Tawafuq) for the Oneness (Tawheed) of their Creator and Master. Similarly, the alternation of night and day is sometimes presented as a testimony to the struggle between truth and falsehood and the dominance of truth; sometimes it is used as an analogy for life after death; and sometimes, despite their contrast, the compatibility and harmony found within them for a high and superior purpose is presented as proof of the reality that the Creator of light and darkness, radiance and gloom, is one and the same—He brought these opposites into existence by His power and He creates harmony within these opposites by His wisdom and uses them for the collective service of this universe. The mention of the ship and the sea also occurs in the Quran from various angles. In some places, the ebb and flow of human life is highlighted through it—that humans are creatures who become arrogant in a moment and despair in a moment; if the ship of life sails smoothly, they consider it a miracle of their own strategy and wisdom, and if this ship is surrounded by the turbulence of accidents, they start calling out "God, God." Then, from here, the internal (anfusi) proof of Tawheed is presented: that the true Deity, whose testimony exists in the depths of the heart, is Allah alone, whose support humans seek when their reliance on all other supports is gone. In some places, by pointing to the difference and contrast in temperament between the sea and the ship, the reality is highlighted: how the law of power and wisdom of a superior Being creates harmony between the sea and the ship so that humans, riding on the shoulders of the waves, cast the nets of conquest and subjugation from one continent to another and plant the flags of civilization and culture. The mention of rain from the sky also occurs from diverse angles. It is certainly a testimony of Lordship and mercy. Its testimony for life after death is also very clear and is hinted at here too. It is also used to argue for Tawheed: when the rain descending from the sky grants life and growth to the earth, how can you believe that the gods of the earth are separate and the gods of the sky are separate? If the godhoods of everything were divided like this, how could such amazing harmony be created in the machinery of the universe that water falls from the sky and the earth, by virtue of that water, vomits out all its treasures, and then humans and beasts all benefit from them? In some places, the rain and the difference in its effects are presented as an analogy to highlight the difference that manifests within humans possessing different capabilities regarding the acceptance of heavenly guidance. The same rain makes greenery and vegetation spread in some places, grows thorns and bushes in others, and leaves a clear barren plain in others; similarly, it is stated that the rain of heavenly guidance that descends does not benefit all temperaments equally—some blossom like a garden and meadow as soon as its droplets fall, some remain dead like saline lands, and some grow only the thorny and poisonous bushes of misguidance and enmity. 
The circulation of the winds and clouds is also presented from various angles. Testimony of God's mercy and Lordship is clearly found in them; specifically, what is described in the Quran through various styles is the manifestation of God's mercy and His punishment behind the interventions of the wind, which ultimately provides proof for a Day of Reward and Punishment. Furthermore, the Quran has drawn attention to the aspect that if separate wills are operating over each of the heavens, earth, clouds, and winds, then who creates that link and connection between these different elements, without which the existence and survival of this world is impossible? Here, our purpose is not the detail of the arguments hidden within the above summaries. Understanding or describing these arguments accurately will be more appropriate in the instances where the Quran has clarified them. Our purpose with these indications here is only to show that when the Holy Quran says there are 'Ayat' (proofs and signs) within these things, it is not just a statement but a reality, and this reality is described in the Quran in various forms of summary and detail; therefore, these summaries should be viewed in the mirror of the details.
A Special Look at the Contents of this Verse: This was a general look at this verse. Now we will cast a special look at it, keeping in view the claim which is the subject under discussion here. It has been mentioned above that the primary thing under discussion here is Tawheed, and this verse has appeared as a proof for that Tawheed; therefore, all the aforementioned facts and nuances of this verse are incidental. From the perspective of the structure of the discourse, the main thing to clarify is: what is the proof of Tawheed in this? Accordingly, we will now try to present it briefly. If you cast a look of reflection on this verse, the reality will become clear that from beginning to end, it references the corresponding, or rather contrasting, components and elements of this universe, and simultaneously points to their amazing unity and harmony (Tawafuq) and their unparalleled intermingling and compatibility found within them for the collective service of this universe. The earth with the heaven, the day with the night, the river with the ship. Outwardly, they hold the relationship of opposites (zhiddayn) to each other, but look with a deeper eye and it will be known that if on one hand they hold the relationship of opposites, on the other hand, from the viewpoint of the habitation of this universe, they also hold a connection and attachment like pairs (zawjayn). If this heaven and its shining sun and moon were not there, all the splendors and springs of our earth would end—in fact, its very existence would vanish. Similarly, if this earth were not there, who can say whose home among the countless stars and planets of this infinite space would be desolated? By the same analogy, our life and the life of all living creatures of this world like us, just as it is dependent on the heat, warmth, light, and exhilaration of the day, is also dependent on the coolness, delicacy, peace-giving, and sleep-inducing nature of the night. These two together have inhabited this home. Similarly, look at the sea: how breathtaking and infinite is its expanse, and how terrifying and horrific are its waves; but look, despite this rebellion and turbulence, how it has carved out perfectly smooth, clear roads right on its chest for our boats and our ships, on which our ships are running day and night, connecting the borders of East and West in trade, livelihood, culture, society, and sciences and arts. Further on is the mention of rain from the sky and the earth becoming a garden and meadow and inhabited anew because of this rain. Consider where the earth is and where the heaven is. But despite this distance, what a deep link and connection exists between the two. The earth hides within itself the treasures of growth and life, but all these treasures remain buried until rain descends from the sky and brings them out. A similar relationship exists between the clouds and the winds. The ships of the clouds stand with their sails open and loaded, but they cannot move an inch from their place until the winds push them, move them from their spot, and advance them in their designated directions; it is these winds that drive them around in the East, West, North, and South, making them disappear when they want and making them appear on the horizon when they want. 
Now the question is: what does the eye of reflection and contemplation decide about this world? Is it a battlefield of opposites and contradictions in which a struggle of different wills and powers is taking place, or is a single Wise and Designing Will the ruler and sovereign over all of them, using all these different elements under His wisdom for a specific system and a collective purpose? Obviously, from the observation of this universe, only this second point is proven. Then, reflecting further, another point emerges from here: that this world did not come into existence by itself, nor did the evolution within it happen by itself. If that were the case, where would that compatibility for a superior purpose come from in its different elements, which is present in every corner of this universe? If you reflect, this single reality is on one hand blocking all possibilities of Shirk, and on the other hand, it is cutting the root of all the whispers of Darwinism.

Meaning, despite this clear proof of Tawheed described above, there live in this world people who appoint partners and peers to God and love these partners and peers as God should be loved. This style of speech is one of expressing astonishment. Meaning, there was no room for this lack of intellect, but what is the remedy for those who do not use their reason at all? For them, all the proofs spread throughout the heavens and the earth are useless.
Allah is the True Deserver of Love: Regarding them, it is stated that these people love their presumed partners and peers as the right of loving God is due, even though the true deserver of love is Allah alone. He is the one who created everything, He is the one in whose hand is the entire administration, and the Lordship and mercy spread in every corner of this universe are testifying that He is Rahman and Rahim; so how can anyone else be the deserver of love equal to Him? Furthermore, even if someone else emerges as a deserver of love due to some relationship or connection, their love can nonetheless only be under the love of God, not equal to it or, God forbid, greater than it. From this, while the point emerged that true love is among the rights of God—and associating anyone else in it is Shirk—the point also emerged that love for others is not absolutely negated. Others can also be loved, such as a wife, children, nation, tribe, country, and homeland, or a great elder, teacher, sheikh, or pir; but for this love, it is necessary that it be subordinate to the love of God. Meaning, wherever and whenever a conflict arises between the demands of this love and the love of God, the person should prioritize the demand of God's love and ignore the other loves. In this case, they will undoubtedly be fulfilling the right of Tawheed. Accordingly, this very quality of the true believers has been stated: 'wal-ladhina amanu ashaddu hubbal-lillah' (those who truly believe are stronger in their love for Allah). Meaning, when the contradictory demands of love for Allah and love for other than Allah arise before them, they always lean toward the side of Divine love. This is the reality of pure Tawheed and this is the spirit of faith. From this, it is known that as far as the existence of love is concerned, it can exist for others alongside Allah; this is not contrary to faith and Tawheed. The only condition is that the love for others must be subordinate to the love of Allah, not equal to it or greater than it. 
'Wa law yaral-ladhina zalamu...' (And if those who have wronged could see...): Here, according to the general rule of the Arabic language, the answer (jawab) to 'law' (if) is omitted, and 'annal-quwwata lillahi jami'a' and the words following it clarify this omitted answer. The meaning is: if these people who are wronging their own souls—who have appointed peers and partners to God and are loving them like God—could see the time when they will face the Divine punishment, then this reality would become thoroughly clear to them: that God has no peer or partner who is a deserver of love equal to Him, but He alone is the Master of all power and authority, and He is going to give an extremely severe punishment to those who associate partners with Him, from which no one will be able to save them. 
Examples of this style are abundant in the Holy Quran. For the sake of brevity, we present only one example. It is stated: 'Law ya'lamul-ladhina kafaru hina la yakuffuna 'an wujuhihimun-nara wa la 'an zhuhurihim wa la hum yunsarun' (If those who disbelieved but knew [the time] when they will not be able to ward off the Fire from their faces or from their backs and they will not be aided...) (Al-Anbiya: 39). In this verse too, the answer to 'law' is omitted. Meaning: if they could know today that punishment which they will know in the Hereafter, they would not express the rebellion they are expressing; but they will know it at a time when knowing will yield no benefit but will only cause regret and sorrow. A parallel also exists in Surah Saba.

This is an appositive (badal) for 'idh yarawnal-'adhab' (when they see the punishment) and further clarifies that very punishment: that today, those whom they appoint as partners and peers to God and whom they are loving as God should be loved—when the punishment of the Hereafter manifests, these [leaders] will clearly declare their disavowal of their devotees and followers and will be of no use in saving them from the punishment that will be standing over their heads. 
The Meaning of 'Asbab': 'Asbab' is the plural of 'sabab', which originally means a rope. From there, the meaning of connection, link, and means/resources developed within it, and then expanding further, it began to be used for the attachments and surroundings of a thing; thus, the construction 'asbab as-sama'' (the ways/ropes of the heaven) is used in the Quran. The pronoun in 'bihim' (with them) refers back to 'alladhina-ttaba'u' (those who followed). The meaning is: the people whom these polytheists have established relationships of love and devotion with, thinking them to be partners and intercessors—one day all the threads of these relationships of theirs will be scattered, and they will send curses upon each other.

The Leaders and the Followers: After the leaders (matbu'in), the reaction of the followers (tabi'in) is now described: when the followers see that those whom they gave the status of divinity and deemed worthy of all their love and humble devotion throughout their lives are expressing such disgust and disavowal in this most difficult time, they too will say in a tone of extreme regret: "If only we were granted a return to the world once more, so that we too could express disavowal of them just as they have expressed disavowal of us." But this regret of theirs will remain only a regret; they will not be granted an exit from the punishment they have fallen into. This subject is described in various places in the Holy Quran through different styles. We present some examples here for its clarification. It is stated: 'And [Abraham] said, "You have only taken, other than Allah, idols as [a bond of] affection among you in worldly life. Then on the Day of Resurrection you will deny one another and curse one another..."' (Al-'Ankabut: 25). 'And they will say, "Our Lord, indeed we obeyed our masters and our dignitaries, and they led us astray from the [right] way. Our Lord, give them double the punishment and curse them with a great curse."' (Al-Ahzab: 67-68). 'Close friends, that Day, will be enemies to each other, except for the righteous' (Az-Zukhruf: 67). Here, the statement that "Allah will show them their deeds as regrets for them" refers, in our view, to those loyalties and sacrifices that these polytheists performed for their false deities or for their misleading masters and leaders.

This address is to the Arabs, whose Shirk was hinted at in the preceding verses. First, their innovations regarding Tawheed are addressed. Then, further on, the innovations of the People of the Book are refuted. Addressing the Arabs, it is said: "Eat of what is lawful and good on the earth and do not follow the footsteps of Satan." Following the footsteps of Satan means that the things you have declared Halal (permitted) and Haram (prohibited) on your own, merely under your polytheistic superstitions, have no Shari authority; rather, this path was suggested to you by Satan, and in following him, you prohibited things that God made lawful, and thus committed Shirk by interfering in God's right of prohibition and permission.
Prohibition and Permission without Divine Command is Shirk: Since prohibition and permission without God's command is Shirk, the subjects of Shirk and prohibition/permission are mentioned together in various places in the Quran. For example, in Surah An-Nahl: 'And those who associate others with Allah say, "If Allah had willed, we would not have worshipped anything other than Him, neither we nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything other than through Him."' (An-Nahl: 35). Similarly, in Surah Al-An'am verse 148: 'Those who associated with Allah will say, "If Allah had willed, we would not have associated [anything] and neither would our fathers, nor would we have prohibited anything."' (Al-An'am: 148). From this, it is known that Shirk and prohibition/permission are two related subjects. In this connection, in the verse under discussion, within the refutation of Shirk, it was stated: eat all lawful and pure things and do not prohibit things God made lawful under polytheistic superstitions in following Satan. As for what things the polytheists of Arabia had prohibited or permitted under their polytheistic superstitions in following Satan, the Quran has made hints in various places. We present some examples. It is stated: 'And they assign to Allah from that which He created of crops and livestock a share and say, "This is for Allah," by their claim, "and this is for our partners." But what is for their partners does not reach Allah, while what is for Allah reaches their partners. Evil is that which they judge. And likewise, to many of the polytheists their partners have made [to seem] pleasing the killing of their children in order to ruin them and to confound for them their religion. And if Allah had willed, they would not have done so. So leave them and that which they invent. And they say, "These animals and crops are forbidden; no one may eat from them except whom we will," by their claim. And there are animals whose backs are forbidden [to be used] and animals upon which the name of Allah is not mentioned [at slaughter] - [all] as an invention against Him. He will recompense them for what they used to invent. And they say, "What is in the bellies of these animals is exclusively for our males and forbidden to our wives. But if it is [born] dead, then all of them have a share in it." He will recompense them for their description. Indeed, He is Wise and Knowing. Those will have lost who killed their children in foolishness without knowledge and prohibited what Allah had provided for them, inventing [lies] about Allah. They have gone astray and were not [rightly] guided' (Al-An'am: 136-140). 
Similarly, the polytheists had given a status of sanctity to certain types of livestock under their polytheistic superstitions or in relation to their idols, regarding any kind of use of them as unlawful. The Quran refuted this in one place: 'Allah has not appointed [such innovations as] bahirah or sa'ibah or wasilah or ham. But those who disbelieve invent falsehood about Allah, and most of them do not use reason' (Al-Ma'idah: 103). In another place, their polytheistic prohibition and permission was criticized in these words: 'And He it is who causes gardens to grow, [both] trellised and untrellised, and palm trees and crops of different [kinds of] food and olives and pomegranates, similar and dissimilar. Eat of [each of] its fruit when it yields and give its due [zakah] on the day of its harvest. And be not excessive. Indeed, He does not like those who commit excess. And of the grazing livestock are carriers [of burdens] and those [too] small. Eat of what Allah has provided for you and do not follow the footsteps of Satan. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy. [They are] eight mates - of the sheep, two and of the goats, two. Say, "Is it the two males He has forbidden or the two females or that which the wombs of the two females contain? Inform me with knowledge, if you should be truthful." And of the camels, two and of the cattle, two. Say, "Is it the two males He has forbidden or the two females or that which the wombs of the two females contain? Or were you witnesses when Allah enjoined this upon you? Then who is more unjust than one who invents a lie about Allah to mislead the people without knowledge? Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people." Say, "I do not find within that which was revealed to me [anything] forbidden to one who would eat it unless it be a dead animal or blood spilled out or the flesh of swine - for indeed, it is impure - or it be [that slaughtered in] disobedience, dedicated to other than Allah. But whoever is forced [by necessity], neither desiring [it] nor transgressing [its limit], then indeed, your Lord is Forgiving and Merciful."' (Al-An'am: 141-145). 
From the above detail, it is known that in the verse under discussion, following the footsteps of Satan refers to prohibiting things created by Allah Almighty under these very polytheistic superstitions. Here, the reality should also be kept in mind that Satan and his progeny have a great interest in this specific issue. He has found this path very successful and easy for turning people away from the path of Tawheed. For this reason, he included it in his program from the beginning and announced it with full audacity and clarity. Reflect on the following verse of the Quran: 'And [Satan] said, "I will surely take from Your servants a specific portion. And I will mislead them, and I will arouse in them [sinful] desires, and I will command them so they will slit the ears of cattle, and I will command them so they will change the creation of Allah." And whoever takes Satan as an ally instead of Allah has certainly sustained a clear loss' (An-Nisa: 118-119). 
In the verse, the attribute 'Tayyib' (pure/good) alongside 'Halal' (lawful) shows that the things permitted in Islam are necessarily pure as well. Thus, just as there is a Shari and legal standard for distinguishing between permission and non-permission for everything, there is also a rational and natural standard. Things that are not tainted with outward filth and rational/moral corruptions are all Halal; conversely, things within which some outward or inward filth exists have been declared unlawful. The attribute 'aduwwun mubin' (clear enemy) for Satan shows the reality that his enmity with the children of Adam is not a hidden matter; rather, he has been the enemy of Adam and his descendants since the first day and has openly announced this enmity until the Day of Judgment. We quoted a verse from Surah Al-An'am above, which clarifies that he has expressed this enmity of his with full audacity even before Allah Almighty. Consider another verse of the same subject: '[Iblis] said, "Should I prostrate to one You created from clay?" [Iblis] said, "Have You seen this one whom You have honored above me? If You should delay me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his descendants, except for a few." [Allah] said, "Go, for whoever of them follows you, then indeed Hell will be your recompense - an ample recompense. And incite [to senselessness] whoever you can among them with your voice and assault them with your horses and foot soldiers and become a partner in their wealth and their children and promise them." But Satan does not promise them except delusion' (Al-Isra: 61-64). In another place, consider the words of Satan's ultimatum: '[Iblis] said, "Because You have put me in error, I will surely sit in wait for them on Your straight path. Then I will come to them from before them and from behind them and on their right and on their left, and You will not find most of them grateful [to You]."' (Al-A'raf: 16-17). An enemy who has given a declaration of war in such open words leaves no room for doubt about being an 'Open Enemy.' For this reason, the Quran described him as 'aduwwun mubin', and the purpose is to warn the children of Adam that being deceived by a hidden enemy is not far-fetched, but being deceived by an open enemy—to the extent of considering him a friend and ally and acting upon his advice—is a foolishness greater than which no other can exist.

The Meaning of "Amr": Just as 'Amr' means to command something, it also means to suggest something or give advice for it. For example: "Amartuhum amri bi-mun'arij al-liwa..." (I informed them of my advice at Mun'arij al-Liwa, but my statement did not enter their understanding until the next morning) or "Ata'tal-amrika bi-sarmi habli" (You finally listened to the advice of those who were suggesting you sever ties with me). 
"Su'" (Evil) is used in broad meanings. It can refer to physical and material harm or injury, for example: 'fanqalabu bi-ni'matim-minallahi wa fadhlil-lam yamsashum su'' (So they returned with favor from Allah and bounty, no harm having touched them) (Al Imran: 173). It can also refer to disease, for example: 'wa adkhil yadaka fi jaybika takhruj baydha'a min ghayri su'' (And put your hand into [the opening of] your garment; it will come out white without disease) (An-Naml: 12). Similarly, it is used for badness and sin, whether the badness is small or great, for example: 'innam-at-tawbatu 'alallahi lilladhina ya'malunas-su'a bi-jahalatin thumma yatubuna min qarib' (The repentance accepted by Allah is only for those who do wrong in ignorance [or at a moment of passion] and then repent soon after) (An-Nisa: 17). 
The Meaning of "Fahsha'": The word 'Fahsha'' is used for open immorality and indecency. In the Quran, it hints at evils like adultery, sodomy, and performing Tawaf naked. When 'Su'' and 'Fahsha'' are gathered together, they not only encompass all small and great evils but also include all kinds of financial, physical, and rational losses and calamities. 
'Qawl 'alallah' (speaking about Allah) and 'Iftira' 'alallah' (inventing against Allah) have the same meaning: attributing a false and fabricated statement to God. For example, saying that God has appointed so-and-so as His partner and peer, or claiming without any authority that God has prohibited such-and-such types of things. 
Satan's 'commanding' (amr) here means his whispering into hearts for these things and making them seem appealing to the eyes. Satan's definition includes all his progeny, whether from the Jinn or from humans. This same subject is described in another place like this: 'And do not eat of that upon which the name of Allah has not been mentioned, for indeed, it is grave disobedience. And indeed, the devils do inspire their allies [among men] to dispute with you. And if you were to obey them, indeed, you would be polytheists' (Al-An'am: 121). 
Here, one point is worthy of attention: there exists such a clear and perceptible rational and natural distinction between the commands of the Rahman (Merciful) and the commands of Satan that no person of sound nature and good taste can face a confusion between them. It has passed in the previous verse that the things Allah Almighty made lawful for eating and drinking are pure, pleasant, moderate, health-giving, and soul-nurturing in their effects, their outward, and their inward; in contrast, the things Satan invites one to adopt are all harmful to the soul, reason, body, and morals, and open the paths to indecency and immorality. After this clear difference, those who choose to follow Satan are only inviting their own wretchedness.

Respect for the Heritage of the Past with Criticism: Meaning, regarding all these polytheistic rituals, their reliance is not on any proof or authority but merely on the imitation (taqlid) of those before them and following their unauthorized traditions. When they are invited to follow the Book being revealed to you to inform them of God's original Sharia instead of these unauthorized things, they respond with great arrogance: "We will remain firm on the way of our forefathers." Upon this, the Quran raised the question: can their stagnation and insistence on following the way of their forefathers be considered rational even in a case where it is clear that their forefathers relied neither on the guidance of reason nor on the teaching of God in these matters, but either mindlessly beat the track of those before them or invented innovations in following their desires and Satan? 
From the style of the Quran's question, the point emerges that the mere fact that a thing has been coming down from forefathers is not sufficient to prove its correctness and truth; rather, it is also necessary to place it on the touchstone of investigation and criticism to see whether the matter, if related to pure reason and opinion, stands up on the scale of reason, and if related to religion, has a strong and reliable authority. In other words, understand that the Quran, on one hand, invites one to keep eyes open for investigation and criticism instead of relying on mere imitation; on the other hand, it also instructs to view the heritage of the past with an eye of respect and does not allow abandoning it without investigation and criticism.

The Meaning of "Na'aqa Yan'iqu": 'Na'aqa yan'iqu' means to shout and call out. 'Na'a-al-mu'adhin' means the caller gave the Adhan. 'Na'aqa-r-ra'i bi-ghanamihi' means the shepherd shouted at or called his flock. 
Analogy of a Situation to a Situation: This is a parable (tamthil) in which one situation is compared to another situation. In such parables, as we wrote while explaining the parables of verses 16-17, it is not necessary for all components of the 'represented' (mumath-thal) and the 'representation' (mumath-thal bihi) to correspond to each other; only the correspondence of the 'state of the event' to the 'state of the event' is necessary. Furthermore, it is not necessary to see the reflection of the thing being compared in the mirror of the thing it is compared to. Those who, instead of using reason and insight, have become blind and deaf and insist on merely imitating their forefathers, are likened to a flock of sheep and goats which is completely devoid of reason and perception and entirely lacking the capability to think and understand. The voice of the shepherd certainly strikes its ears, but beyond that, it has no knowledge of what the shepherd is calling for or what he is saying. After this parable, it is stated that "they are deaf and blind," the purpose of which is to clarify that this parable is an analogy for their deprivation of all rational and spiritual requirements. Some nuances of this style have also passed under verse 18.

Removal of the Muslims' Hesitation: Addressing the Muslims, it was said: if these polytheists want to insist on their polytheistic innovations, leave them to their state, and you, lifting these improper restrictions, eat all the pure things Allah has granted you. Then it was said: if you are worshippers of Allah alone, then be grateful to Him. Prohibiting the provision He granted and the livestock He created in relation to someone else is contrary to both the worship of God and gratitude to Him. The need to say this specifically addressing the Muslims was because the matter of eating and drinking—especially when it involves eating things that have held a status of prohibition based on religious sanctity since ancient times—was a sensitive matter. In such matters, a person becomes somewhat suspicious and superstitious. Eating something contrary to tradition not only creates a kind of hesitation in the temperament, but some people even consider it against piety and religiosity. In the beginning, this condition was also faced by some Muslims; therefore, the Quran warned them that this thing is contrary to gratitude to God and His worship. From some places in Surah Al-An'am, it is known that when the Quran made lawful the things prohibited by the polytheists—stating that you may eat them with pleasure if slaughtered in the name of Allah—the polytheists started propaganda that the Muslims had even made lawful things that had been prohibited since the times of their forefathers. Since temperaments become very sensitive in such matters, as mentioned above, this propaganda affected some Muslims. The following verses of Surah Al-An'am contain the refutation of this propaganda: 'So eat of that upon which the name of Allah has been mentioned, if you are believers in His verses. And why should you not eat of that upon which the name of Allah has been mentioned while He has explained in detail to you what He has forbidden you, excepting that to which you are compelled. And indeed, many lead [others] astray through their [own] inclinations without knowledge. Indeed, your Lord - He is most knowing of the transgressors. And leave what is apparent of sin and what is concealed thereof. Indeed, those who earn [blame for] sin will be recompensed for what they used to commit. And do not eat of that upon which the name of Allah has not been mentioned, for indeed, it is grave disobedience. And indeed, the devils do inspire their allies [among men] to dispute with you. And if you were to obey them, indeed, you would be polytheists.' In our view, the verse under discussion appeared in exactly this same context and to clarify this same reality to the Muslims.

Prohibited and Permitted in the Religion of Abraham: This refers to the things that were originally prohibited in the religion of Abraham, and the purpose is absolutely not to present a full detail of Halal and Haram, but only to refute the polytheists—that their declaring some livestock as prohibited under their polytheistic superstitions is a completely unauthorized statement; these things were not prohibited in the religion of Abraham. In exactly this context, this same point is stated in Surah Al-An'am like this: 'Say, "I do not find within that which was revealed to me [anything] forbidden to one who would eat it unless it be a dead animal or blood spilled out or the flesh of swine - for indeed, it is impure - or it be [that slaughtered in] disobedience, dedicated to other than Allah."' If you reflect on the words 'Say, I do not find within that which was revealed to me' while keeping their context in view, it will be clearly known that a clarification is being made on behalf of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) to the polytheists: that your attributing the prohibition of certain livestock to the religion of Abraham is a completely unauthorized statement; the revelation I have received regarding the code of permission and prohibition of the religion of Abraham is that except for such-and-such things, nothing among livestock has been made prohibited. 
Some people want to take the verse under discussion completely out of its context and derive the conclusion that only these things mentioned in this verse are Haram in Islam. That nothing besides them is Haram. But this idea is clearly wrong. To refute such people, setting aside other points, this single fact is sufficient: the word 'maytah' (dead animal) that appeared in the verse under discussion is explained in Surah Al-Ma'idah verse 3 by listing five things. Then the prohibition of several other things is also described, to which there is no hint in the verse under discussion. 
Outward Filth and Inward Filth: Among these described things, the prohibition of carrion, blood, and the flesh of swine is due to their outward filth, because in Islam only pure things, as hinted above, are made lawful; things that feel filthy and impure just by looking at them have been prohibited in this religion of nature. As for the prohibition of what is slaughtered for other than Allah, the reason for its prohibition is its inward filth. This reality is perfectly recognized and clear in Islam: that Shirk is the greatest rational and inward impurity; therefore, if its contagion touches even a pure thing from any aspect, it too becomes impure. The Quran itself has hinted at both these types of impurities; thus, in Al-An'am 145, after mentioning carrion, blood, and swine flesh, it said 'fa-innahu rijsun' (for indeed, it is impure) and then used the words 'aw fisqan uhilla lighayrillahi bihi' (or it be disobedience, dedicated to other than Allah), aiming to clarify that its impurity is not outward but rational and creedal. Then, in Surah Al-An'am, within the description of these issues, this very great reality of religion was also clarified: that Islam's demand from every follower is not only to abandon outward sin but also to abandon inward sin; therefore, alongside things tainted with outward filth, it is also necessary to abandon things contaminated with inward and spiritual filth. 'Wa dharu zhahiral-ithmi wa batinahu' (And leave what is apparent of sin and what is concealed thereof). Under this same code, the Prophet (PBUH) also prohibited certain things.
"Idhturra" (is forced by necessity) is from the root 'dharra yadhurru' in the 'Ifti'al' pattern. According to the rules of the Arabic language, the 'ta' of Ifti'al changed to 'tha' due to the correspondence with 'dhad'. 'Dharrahu ila kadha' means 'alja'ahu ilayhi' (he forced/pushed him toward such-and-step). 'Idhturra ilayhi' means he was made needy and forced toward it. 'Baghin yabghi' here means desiring and seeking. 'Ghayra baghin wa la 'adin' are in the state of condition (Hal). In some places, the qualifier 'makhmashah' (severe hunger) is also attached to 'idhtirar'. The meaning is: if someone is forced by hunger, they can use even prohibited things to save their life, but this necessity must be real. Neither should any desire for the prohibited have a part in it, nor should the person exceed the limit that is indispensable for saving life. With these precautions, if someone benefits from a prohibited thing in a real necessity, it is stated that there is no sin upon them. Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.
Concession (Rukhsat) and Resolve ('Azimat): From the words of the Quran, it is apparent that this is a concession (Rukhsat) for that state of necessity which arises from food not being available. And if an analogy is made to this, it can be said that a person who falls into a state of coercion (ikrah) can also benefit from this permission to save their life. However, some jurists have gone beyond this limit and given it the status of resolve ('Azimat). Thus, according to the Hanafis, a person would be guilty of suicide if they put their life in danger instead of benefiting from the prohibited in such circumstances. In our view, with this brevity, this statement is not correct. A concession is, after all, a concession. How can any concession be given the status of resolve in an absolute sense? If a person, despite necessity, does not benefit from the prohibited and their death occurs, how can it be said that their death was a death of 'Haram'? There is no room for doubt that the concessions Allah Almighty has placed in His religion are all manifestations of His kindness and mercy. He is most aware of our weaknesses and our compulsions. For this reason, He has not placed any burden upon us that is beyond our strength. He commanded Wudu (ablution) but simultaneously gave permission that if it is a state of travel, water cannot be obtained, or there is apprehension of harm in performing Wudu due to illness, a person can perform Tayammum. He commanded prayer but simultaneously granted the concession that in a state of travel, a person can perform Qasr (shortening). Similarly, He commanded fasting but also gave permission that if travel occurs during the month of fasting or a person falls ill, they should complete their fasts on other days. Such concessions are mentioned with all those commands of religion in whose fulfillment a difficulty might arise at some stage that is beyond common endurance. The correct attitude regarding them is for a person to benefit from them when necessity arises and not unnecessarily put their life into hardship in a zeal for resolve. If someone insists on Wudu instead of Tayammum despite the apprehension of harm, or considers completing the prayer during travel as the demand of piety despite the troubles, or deems completing the fasts even in a state of travel despite the hardship as resolve, then in our view, such a person has failed to understand the original temperament of Islam. This is extremism in the matter of religion, and a person who adopts the path of extremism in religion and considers concessions as contrary to resolve is actually 'wrestling' with the religion, and such a person, as mentioned in the Hadith, is defeated by the religion. Thus, the Prophet (PBUH) warned a gentleman who was putting himself in severe hardship due to fasting while traveling. But if a person has all kinds of facilities during travel, and can perform full prayers or keep fasts without any specific trouble, then where does the question of any sin being mandatory arise? Similarly, if a person faces a state of necessity and no other strategy remains for saving life except to benefit from something prohibited, then in general circumstances, the temperament of Islam demands that they benefit from that prohibited thing to the extent of saving life. They should not consider this contrary to piety nor contrary to resolve. But there can also be some forms where the only thing befitting a dignified Muslim is to give their life but not tolerate touching the prohibited. For example, if in some place, due to the authority of the wicked and immoral, the distinction between Halal and Haram has vanished and a person is forced to eat something prohibited, then the demand of their faith is to adopt the path of resolve and sacrifice their life to revive the faith of others. By playing this gamble, they will not be a sinner but will, God willing, attain the station of martyrdom in reward for their pride of faith and respect for the rights of the Divine Sharia. At the very least, for the reforming scholars (ulama-e-muslihin), this is the better path in such circumstances. The hardships the Companions (RA) endured for the sake of the word of Tawheed in the early life of Makkah—from whom are they hidden? How many Companions drank the cup of martyrdom at the hands of the enemies of Tawheed, and the lives of all those gentlemen remained in danger, but regarding not a single one of those Companions has it come to our knowledge that they uttered a word of disbelief for the sake of saving their life, even though explicit permission existed in the Quran that in the case of coercion, a person can say a word of disbelief to save their life. From this detail, it became clear that neither the tendency to belittle the concessions of religion is correct, nor is the tendency to declare concessions themselves as resolve correct; rather, the correct path is that just as benefiting from concessions in general circumstances is according to the temperament of the Sharia, similarly, acting upon the demands of resolve in special circumstances is also a demand of the religion.

Some Prohibitions of the People of the Book: This refers to the People of the Book. Just as the polytheists had prohibited certain things under their polytheistic superstitions and considered their permission by Islam as contrary to piety and purity, the People of the Book had also declared Haram as Halal and Halal as Haram on their own. Now, when Islam was returning people to that code regarding Halal and Haram which was based on the foundation of the religion of Abraham and the guidance of Divine revelation instead of superstitions and innovations, these people, instead of supporting it, would hide the statements of the Divine Book. The Quran has hinted at more than one such thing. For example, the Jews used to claim regarding the camel that it has been Haram since the time of Prophet Abraham, even though no proof of this existed in the Torah; thus, the Quran demanded of them: 'Say, "Bring the Torah and recite it, if you should be truthful."' 'Then whoever invents a lie about Allah after that - those are the wrongdoers.' Similarly, some things had become Haram for the Jews due to their rebellion and hair-splitting, or because of their disease of 'question upon question'; but regarding such prohibitions, they had been given the awareness that when the final Prophet is sent, he will make all pure things lawful for you and all the restrictions and bonds currently imposed on you will be removed. But the Jews adopted the same path of concealing the truth and ingratitude in this matter as was their path from the beginning. Instead of declaring the permission of these things as a favor of the final Prophet (PBUH), they declared it contrary to religiosity and piety and used it as a pretext to oppose the Quran, Islam, and the Holy Prophet (PBUH) intensely. 
In this chapter, some crimes of the Christians are also very severe. Although their crime is more of the nature of 'permission' (tahlil) than 'prohibition' (tahrim). Paul, who is the founder of modern Christianity, presented the philosophy that the commands of Moses (PBUH) are not mandatory for non-Israelites. In this way, he openly made wine lawful for Christians and also made swine and strangled animals permissible for them. Regarding these People of the Book, the Quran stated that these people who are concealing the truth and selling the religion for the sake of building their world—this bargain will cost them dearly. The 'world' that comes in exchange for selling the religion is fire that they are filling into their bellies, and now on the Day of Judgment, God will neither speak to them nor purify them. Now there is nothing for them except a painful punishment. 'Not speaking' obviously means not speaking words of kindness and favor. Thus, the negation of the action aims at the negation of the 'real' (beneficial) action. In another place in this series, the words 'wa la yanzhuru ilayhim' (nor will He look at them) also appear; this too refers to the negation of the look of favor. Here, this point should be kept in mind: when God gives a nation a Book and Sharia and sends His Prophet among them to deliver His message, then through the medium of the Prophet, God Almighty essentially blesses that nation with His honor of speech (takallum). Then, specifically the Children of Israel even attained the honor that Allah Almighty blessed their Prophet with the honor of His direct address. The demand of this great exaltation was that the Jews should have valued God's Sharia and His word with heart and soul and spread its mention in every corner; but when they, instead of considering it an honor, tried to hide it like a defect, what face do they have left for God to bless them again with His honor of speech on the Day of Judgment? "And He will not purify them"—meaning Allah Almighty had blessed them with the bounty of Prophethood and the Book precisely to make them pure, but when they preferred to remain smeared in misguidance and pollutions despite this bounty, God will not purify them in the Hereafter. 'Not purifying in the Hereafter' could also mean that the Hereafter is not the place for purification and cleansing but the place for reward and punishment; therefore, the question of anyone's purification does not arise there. It could also mean that because their faith has been snatched away due to these evil deeds of theirs, they will not even get the opportunity to go to Paradise after suffering the punishment for their deeds in Hell and becoming pure; rather, there will be eternal punishment for them and they will remain in it forever. A tradition from Sahih Muslim shows that under this very verse, the Prophet (PBUH) also included the aged adulterer, the lying king, and the arrogant beggar; this Hadith is not an exegesis of this verse but an extension of the verse's ruling due to the sharing of the underlying cause ('illat).

The style of 'fa-ma asbarahum' (how patient they are), like 'ma ahsanahu', is for expressing astonishment. The meaning is: these people who are so audaciously preferring misguidance over guidance and punishment over forgiveness—their boldness and daring regarding Hell is amazing.

This describes the cause of the displeasure and wrath mentioned in the preceding verses. Meaning: these people will deserve this rebuke from Allah Almighty because God sent down a Book to bring them to the right path, which settles all disputes and all differences; but even after that, they chose misguidance instead of guidance, so they deserve to be deprived of God's look of favor forever and fall into that punishment from which they may never emerge. In this, the word 'bil-haqq' (with truth) means that God sent down this Book with a 'decisive word' (qawl-e-faisal). Meaning: the various differences that the People of the Book had created in God's religion by concealing the truth and making alterations—due to which it had become extremely difficult to know what is Haram, what is Halal, what is truth, and what is falsehood—Allah completely removed that difference and dispute through the Quran. Now the path of truth has opened again for every seeker of truth, and God's Sharia has come before the people in its correct and perfect form. After this, those who remain stuck in these same disputes only invite their own wretchedness and misfortune. 'Shiqaq' means opposition and enmity. Thus, it is stated: 
(For the linguistic research of the various meanings of the word 'Haqq', see under verse 71. Here it means 'decisive word'). 
'And O my people, let not my opposition [shiqaqi] lead you to a fate similar to that of the people of Noah...' (Hud: 89). When the attribute 'ba'id' (far/distant) is attached to 'shiqaq', it means that a person has gone so far and advanced so much in opposition and enmity to a person or thing that they no longer have any awareness of their own benefit or loss, and then no possibility remains for them to return from such a distance and make amends for the past. Regarding these People of the Book, it was stated that after the Torah, this difference they have now made regarding the Quran is merely a miracle of their mutual obstinacy, and they have now gone so far on this path that no possibility remains for their return.

The Subject of Verse 177: Religion is Not Merely the Name of a Few Rituals and Outward Forms: In the preceding group of verses, as was clarified, there was the statement of Tawheed; now, in the aforementioned verses, its requirements and fruits—namely faith, spending (infaq), establishing prayer, giving zakat, fulfilling covenants, and steadfastness on the truth in all kinds of circumstances—are being described. The preamble to this subject is that the right of loyalty to God will not be fulfilled merely by turning the face toward the East or the West as the Jews and Christians have understood; consequently, a battle of dispute and fighting has been heated among them for a long time on this issue. As if in their view, this is the original religion. Rather, for this, such-and-such things are needed. The purpose of this preamble is to give Muslims the awareness that religion is not merely the name of a few rituals and outward forms, but it is a collection of actions and morals having a very deep connection with life; therefore, they should not remain merely slaves to rituals like the previous nations but should adopt the original realities of the religion, which are these. Only by adopting these will they be able to fulfill the right of their loyalty to God. Without them, claims of love and loyalty are completely baseless. In this light, observe the translation and exegesis of the verse.
The original meaning of 'Birr' in the Arabic lexicon is to fulfill someone's right, whether it be God's right, the parents' right, or the right of God's servants. Besides these fundamental rights, the fulfillment of rights arising from treaties, promises, oaths of allegiance, contracts, and vows is also included in its meaning. Due to this vastness of the word, all those good deeds that can fall under justice ('adl) or excellence (ihsan) gather under it. 'Barr' and 'Baarr' are the adjective forms from it. 'Barrun bi-walidayhi' refers to that fortunate son who is obedient to his parents and fully fulfills their rights. 'Barra bil-qasam' means he fulfilled his oath. The attribute 'Barr' is also used for Allah Almighty because He is the one who is going to fulfill one by one, both in this world and the Hereafter, the rights He has taken upon Himself for His servants or the promises He has made to them. From this detail, it became clear that whether they are rights and obligations or virtues and good deeds, all are included in its meaning. Due to this vastness of the word, we could not find any such word in Urdu for translation that could convey its entire meaning. The word we have chosen [Righteousness/Piety/Loyalty] conveys the original spirit of the word to an extent. 
(This research of the word 'Birr' is mostly derived from Maulana Farahi's 'Mufradat al-Quran').
Here the original statement is about faith, spending, prayer, zakat, etc., but as we mentioned in the preamble to this chapter, alongside these commands and laws, there is also a refutation of the innovations that the People of the Book or polytheists had introduced into the Divine Sharia under the requirements of renewing the religion, and due to which the entire Sharia had either become distorted or had become merely a collection of a few outward forms and rituals. Here, under the requirement of renewing the religion, the preamble to the statement of the original commands was raised such that the right of God's worship and obedience is not fulfilled merely by turning the face toward the East and the West; rather, the original thing is those actions and morals taught by the Sharia. Obviously, this is a critique of the Jews and Christians, among whom the original teachings of the Torah and Gospel had been placed on the shelf of forgetfulness. But regarding the matter of the Qibla, the dispute over East and West, as we described under verses 115 and 142, had arisen among them as if the entire religion depends only on this thing.
A Warning for the Ummah: This preamble, as we hinted, is a warning for this Ummah: do not abandon the original religion by getting entangled in such secondary matters, otherwise, like the Jews and Christians, you too will become those who strain the gnat and swallow the camel, and just as their claim of God-worship proved meaningless, your God-worship will also become meaningless. Exactly this same meaning of warning is also stated further on in the series of describing Hajj: 'And it is not righteousness [birr] that you enter houses from the back, but righteousness is [in] him who fears Allah...' (Al-Baqarah: 189). The purpose of all these warnings, as mentioned, was to save this Ummah from the innovations and outward-worship of the Jews, Christians, and polytheists and turn them toward the original realities of the religion; but alas, this Ummah too ended up wandering in those same valleys in which the previous nations were destroyed. 
In 'wa lakinnal-birra man amana billahi', a 'mudhaf' (possessive noun) is omitted according to the general rule of the Arabic language; thus, the full phrase would be 'wa lakinnal-birra birru man amana billahi' (but righteousness is the righteousness of the one who believes in Allah). An example of the omission of a 'mudhaf' exists in this very verse under discussion. It is stated 'wa fir-riqab', which is obviously 'wa fi fakki-r-riqab' (and in the freeing of necks).
Context proves that 'faith' (iman) here refers to true faith. This is because true faith is the thing through which a person can fulfill the right of loyalty to God. True faith in Allah is for a person to surrender themselves entirely to their Lord without any trace of Shirk. True faith in the Hereafter is for a person to accept being raised after death, to consider themselves accountable before God for every word and deed, and not to fall into the delusion of false intercessions. Faith in the angels means to accept their existence, to know them as innocent and holy-natured, to believe them to be the bringers of God's guidance, trustworthy and reliable, and to consider them the means for the execution of the decrees of predestination. Faith in the Book means to believe it to be the manual of guidance sent down by Allah, to consider it the touchstone of truth and falsehood, and to have full reliance on its guidance in every aspect of life. Faith in the Prophets means to believe them to be the appointed and mandatory-to-obey guides from God, to consider their knowledge infallible, to designate their action as the model for life, and to deem their obedience, following, and love as necessary.
Faith in the Angels: One point here might trouble some minds: why has faith in the angels been included among the components of faith? Especially since their experience has only been had by the Prophets, and no specific intellectual or practical benefit of believing in them is clear to an ordinary person. The answer is that just as the right of faith in Allah is not fulfilled without believing in the Hereafter, the Book, and the Prophets—as believing in these things makes faith in Allah a perceptible, effective, and active reality of our lives—similarly, faith in the angels is an inseparable part of faith in the Book and faith in the Messengers. Without believing in the angels, the intermediary between God and His Prophets remains unclear and undetermined; and by remaining unclear, not only does an extremely important link in the chain of knowledge and guidance remain missing, but the human intellect also finds many paths to misguidance regarding heavenly guidance. The world has always accepted that God exists, and it has always felt that since He exists, He should also inform His servants of His pleasures; but when He never comes before us unveiled and face-to-face, the question arises: what is that means and intermediary through which He informs the creation of His commands and instructions? If He has chosen His specific servants for this purpose, who are called Prophets and Messengers, then exactly the same question arises about them: what means does He adopt to inform these Prophets and Messengers of His knowledge and guidance? Does He speak to them Himself face-to-face or does He adopt some other means?
