The answer to this question is that the intermediary of knowledge between Allah Almighty and His Prophets is revelation (Wahi), which He sends through His angels, specifically through His close angel Gabriel. These angels are God's most pure and superior creation. They possess the capability to receive revelation directly from God. They are constantly engaged in the praise and glorification of their Lord. They fulfill God's commands without hesitation, and since they do so under God's command and authority and under His supervision, no other creature can obstruct any of their work, nor can they themselves ever be guilty of forgetfulness or any error in it. Among their group is a close being, Gabriel (PBUH), who is the most high-ranking and close to God. In the Quran, his attributes of 'possessor of power', 'obeyed', and 'trustworthy' have also appeared, which clarifies that he is full of all capabilities and powers for the responsibility entrusted to him; other powers or evil spirits cannot influence or overcome him. In his sphere of work, everyone obeys his commands without hesitation; it is impossible for anyone to defy his command. The trust of Divine revelation entrusted to him to deliver to the Prophets and Messengers is delivered by him without any addition or subtraction; it is impossible for even a minor difference to occur in it. Due to this deep connection of the angels with revelation and messengership, believing in them also became necessary for believing in the Prophets and the Books. They perform the duty of messengership between God and His Prophets and Messengers, and in this regard, they are indispensable because they are the only creation capable of maintaining a simultaneous link with both the realm of Divinity (Lahut) and the realm of Humanity (Nasut); due to their luminosity, they can bear the lights and manifestations of God, and from the aspect of their createdness, they can also establish a connection with humans. No other creature besides them holds this degree and station of access to God; therefore, it became necessary that alongside believing in the Prophets and Messengers, one must also believe in those messengers who are the intermediary of messengership between God and His Messengers. This reality should also be kept in view: the human intellect has always been in pursuit of spirits belonging to the realm of Divinity, and it has felt this need with such intensity that in this search, if it could not find anything correct, it grasped the mantle of whatever wrong thing came into its hand. The soothsayers and sorcerers of Arabia considered Jinns, devils, and the 'unseen caller' (Hatif-e-Ghaybi) as the means of connection to the realm of Divinity; the astrologers and astronomers of India sought the secrets of the unseen within the movements of the stars; the priests of the temples of China sought connection with the world of the unseen through the spirits of their forefathers. The Quran negated all these false means and intermediaries and declared the knowledge obtained through them as a collection of 'wet and dry' (useless and useful mixed), and simultaneously clarified the reality that the only reliable source of Divine knowledge is the angels who come to the Prophets and deliver exactly what God gives them without any addition or subtraction. From this detail, it became clear that faith in the angels, faith in the Book, and faith in the Prophets are all links connected to each other, and just as faith in the Book and faith in the Prophets are very perceptible realities of our lives, faith in the angels is also an extremely important intellectual and practical reality of our lives.
The Referent of the Pronoun in 'ala hubbihi': In 'wa ata-l-mala 'ala hubbihi', the genitive pronoun could technically refer to God, meaning a person spends their wealth in the path of the love of God. However, in our view, for various reasons, the statement of those who designate the referent as 'wealth' (mal) is preferable. Meaning: a person spends wealth in the path of God despite the love for wealth. 
Love for wealth can have various aspects. One is that the wealth is inherently valuable and pleasing to the heart; second is that the person themselves is in such need of it that sacrificing for another is weighing heavily on the soul; third is that the era is one of famine and high prices, in which even a generous person becomes cautious and frugal. The word 'ala hubbihi' encompasses all three situations. In our view, there are several reasons for preferring this meaning. 
One reason is that Quranic parallels support this meaning; it is clear that here it is being described what kind of spending (infaq) a person should do to attain the high station of 'Birr'—i.e., obedience and loyalty to Allah Almighty. Wherever this subject is described in other places, it is explicitly stated that this station is attained by spending the wealth that is beloved. For example: 'Never will you attain the good [reward] until you spend [in the way of Allah] from that which you love' (Al Imran: 92). Similarly, in another place, the true believers are praised: 'and give [them] preference over themselves, even though they are in privation' (Al-Hashr: 9). Second, the statements of the Prophet (PBUH) also support this. He was asked which charity is the most superior. He replied: that which a person of little means spends from their hard-earned income on a relative who harbors enmity against them. Third, among the Arabs too, the most praiseworthy generosity was considered to be that of those who were generous in times of famine and high prices, when wealth becomes a very beloved thing even in the eyes of the wealthy. Arab poets have unanimously praised this quality. In other nations too, this quality is praiseworthy without dispute. Fourth, such spending is most likely to be in the love of God, because without this strong motive, it is very difficult for the soul to be ready for this kind of sacrifice. From this angle, this meaning automatically encompasses the first meaning. 
The Recipients of Spending (Infaq): Among the recipients of spending, relatives (qarabat-mand) are placed first. From this, it is known that a person's kith and kin, if they are needy, are the most deserving of their assistance. So much so that even if they are hiding enmity in their hearts, the most superior spending, as clarified by the above Hadith, is that which is done for them. Immediately after relatives, the mention of orphans (yatama) clarifies their rank and status in Islamic society: after one's own relatives, a person's first look should fall on those children who have been deprived of paternal shade and whose entire responsibility of maintenance and training has been transferred to the society. 'Ibn as-sabil' refers to the traveler. A traveler is deserving of assistance merely by virtue of their state of traveling. Regardless of whether they are a person of means or not; if being a person of no means were a condition for being deserving of assistance, there would be no need to mention them separately after the 'miskin' (the needy). 'Sa'ilin' refers to those people who happen to ask for assistance. Their independent mention after the 'masakin' (the poor) implies that regarding a person who asks, there is no need for much investigation as to whether they are truly in need or not. If they are asking without need, their accountability is upon themselves before Allah. Our right is only that if we can help, we help such a person, and if we are unable, then as instructed in the Quran and Hadith, we present our excuse to them in a polite manner.
In 'wa fir-riqab', 'riqab' is the plural of 'raqabah', which means neck. We mentioned above that a 'mudhaf' is omitted here, i.e., 'fi fakki-r-riqab' (in the freeing of necks). By 'necks' here, the necks of slaves are intended. Freeing them from the yoke of slavery and bringing them to the level of free humans is a very great service to humanity; therefore, Islam included them among its categories of goodness. This shows that the matter of slavery was not a part of Islam's own system; under the international law of war of the time, Islam had merely tolerated it temporarily because at that time, no other solution for the problem of prisoners of war existed at the international level. But along with tolerating it, Islam encouraged the freedom of slaves in various ways within its environment. One of these is that buying them and setting them free, or paying the amount for their 'mukatabat' (contract of freedom), was made a rewarding act. Now, in this era, although slavery has legally ended—and this has happened exactly according to the intent of Islam—practically even today, countless humans are trapped in such bonds or locked in jails due to their economic compulsions and specifically the curse of interest-based debts that they can certainly be considered similar to slaves, if not slaves. Freeing such people and releasing their mortgaged houses and fields is also, God willing, a good deed of the level of 'fakku raqabah'.
'Aqama-s-salata wa ata-z-zakata' (established prayer and gave zakat) has been discussed in detail at the beginning of this book. Here, after the mention of faith and spending, the mention of prayer and zakat occurs as the legal and practical manifestations of both. The practical manifestation of the great reality of faith is prayer, and the legal manifestation of the vast reality of spending is zakat. The purpose of mentioning both is that the minimum required to testify to faith and spending is the observance of prayer and zakat. If these two things disappear, it means neither faith remained nor spending, whereas these are the very things through which the servant establishes their relationship with the Creator and the creation on a healthy foundation. By mentioning zakat separately here, the reality also became clear that the 'spending' (infaq) mentioned above is a separate thing from this legal demand. The station of 'Birr' and piety is attained not just by paying zakat, but by spending generously both secretly and openly.
In 'wal-mufuna bi-'ahdihim' (and those who fulfill their covenant), the style of speech suddenly changed. Above, the mention of faith, spending, prayer, and zakat came in the form of verbs. 'Al-mufuna' is conjoined to them, but it is in the form of a noun of the doer (ism fa'il) and an attribute. Then further on comes 'was-sabirina fil-ba'sa'' (and the patient in poverty), which is in the form of an attribute, but despite being conjoined to 'mufuna', it has changed from 'sabiruna' to 'sabirina', i.e., into the accusative case (nasb). This shift in style is not merely for variety but also has some semantic benefits, some of which we will point out. Students of the Arabic language are aware that in Arabic, verb forms only show the occurrence of an action, but attribute forms show a persistent quality, a trait, and a character. In fact, a spirit of resolve and determination is also hidden within them. Similarly, it is not hidden from the people of knowledge that if an attribute is mentioned in the accusative case without any apparent cause in a sequence of discourse, it means the speaker wants to emphasize it specifically. Our grammarians describe this with the terminology 'ala sabil al-madh' (by way of praise) or 'ala sabil al-ikhtishash' (by way of specification). For example, here, after 'mufuna', 'as-sabirina' suddenly appeared in a completely different style, which adds the meaning as if the speaker wants to say: "I specifically want to mention the patient ones." After this clarification of style, the question will arise in the mind: the beliefs and acts of worship above were mentioned with simple verb forms, then what was the specificity of fulfilling covenants and patience that their mention was made by changing the style with this care, specification, emphasis, and warning? In response, keep a few things in view.
The Importance of Character and Conduct in Religion: First, both these things relate to character and conduct. The matter of character and conduct requires great resolve, determination, discipline, and training. As far as outward beliefs and acts of worship are concerned, many people emerge to maintain them even after the decline and decay of religion; but character, which is the marrow and spirit of religion, is not found even among the great. Among religious people, this weakness has been very prominent: they have fought great battles over the outward forms of beliefs and acts of worship, but they have paid very little attention to the construction of character. Since the guidance of this final Ummah is being directed here toward the station of 'Birr' and obedience, the aspect of character was specifically emphasized—that this station cannot be attained without high character, in which fulfilling covenants and patience are of primary importance. Second, if you reflect, you will realize that the original purpose of all beliefs and acts of worship is not merely to accept a few things or perform a few rituals. Their original purpose is that the light created within a human by believing in Allah and the Messenger should illuminate our hearts, and the strong individual and collective character created by prayer and fasting should become the characteristic of our individual and collective life. If this is not there, then consider all beliefs and acts of worship as completely lifeless and spiritless. This is the nuance: the Quran has everywhere drawn attention to the practical effects alongside beliefs and acts of worship so that negligence regarding them does not occur. Third, the primary field of trial and testing is the field of character and conduct. A person's original treasure, which they provide or can provide with the help of religion, is a strong and pure character. This very thing exalts them to the station of 'Birr' and piety in individual life and guarantees them the companionship of the righteous, the good, the martyrs, and the truthful in collective life. For this reason, it became necessary to specifically emphasize that the Muslim remains alert to protect this treasure of theirs in all kinds of trials and all sorts of tribulations.
Covenant and the Fulfillment of Covenants: Another question can arise here: why were only two things related to character and conduct mentioned here—fulfilling covenants and patience? Other things could have been included in this list; why were they not mentioned? The answer is that these two things are, in reality, like the binding thread for all components related to character and morals. Within 'fulfilling covenants', all small and great rights and duties enter, whether they relate to the creation or the Creator, whether they come into existence through a written treaty or through some relationship, connection, kinship, and proximity, whether they are expressed and announced or are understood and accepted in every good society without being said. Allah and the Messenger, mother and father, wife and children, kith and kin, family and tribe, neighbors and neighborhood, teacher and student, servant and master, country and nation—we are bound to each of them under some outward or hidden covenant, and it is a mandatory demand of 'Birr' and piety that we become fulfillers of the rights of all these covenants. Thus, the original spirit of fulfilling covenants is the fulfillment of rights, and the fulfillment of rights encompasses all small and great duties of a human. Alongside this, the attribute of patience was gathered to clarify that every resistance that obstructs this path of fulfilling rights, the believer should face with resolve and steadfastness and should not be overcome by greed, discouragement, or fear in any condition. Three instances of patience are referenced. One is 'ba'sa'', by which the hardships of poverty and hunger are intended. Second is 'dharra'', which points to physical hardships. Third is 'ba's', by which the conditions of war are intended. If you reflect, you will realize that a person's resolve can fall into trial through these three paths. If a person succeeds in remaining steadfast on the stance of truth within these three conditions, who can dispute their attainment of the highest station of 'Birr' and piety? Thus, it was stated: 'ula'ika-lladhina sadaqu wa ula'ika humul-muttaqun' (those are the ones who have been true, and it is those who are the righteous). From this, the point automatically emerged that those who fulfill the right of loyalty to God merely through a few empty outward shows are neither true in their claim of faith nor are they righteous. One more point should be noted here: the qualifier 'idha 'ahadu' (when they make a covenant) attached to 'wal-mufuna bi-'ahdihim' also expresses the resolve and stability found within these lovers of loyalty. Its exact meaning is that when they happen to make a covenant, then no matter what happens, no matter what losses and pains they have to face because of it, they do not turn their backs but fulfill it by risking their lives. The attitude of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) in this matter holds the status of a bright chapter in the entire history of humanity. Especially the respect he showed for the treaty in the matter of Abu Jandal at the occasion of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah is something history can never forget.

Summary of Verses 178 to 179: Two Foundations for Establishing Peace and Justice: After clarifying the original reality of 'Birr' and piety, attention was turned toward those matters which are based on this very 'Birr' and piety, and which hold great importance in the sense that the peace, justice, protection, and survival of society depend upon them. If even a person with an average mind reflects, they can very easily reach the reality that the foundation of the stability of relationships between humans and humans rests primarily on two things. One, on the fact that every person respects the life of the other. Second, on the fact that every person respects the property of the other. For this reason, the law of the sanctity of life and the sanctity of property has always held superiority over all other laws. Under this principle, which is entirely based on human nature, the Quran also took up the law of respecting life first after establishing the foundation of 'Birr' and piety, and declared 'Qisas' (retribution) as the responsibility of the entire society. Meaning, if a person has been killed, it is not only the responsibility of their relatives and kin to track down their killer and punish them, but it is the responsibility of the entire society to pursue them and bring them to justice. Thus, the killing of one person is the killing of all, and their living is the life of all. This law of Qisas existed among the People of the Book as well as among the Arabs. But just as they had crushed the spirit of other laws, they had also ended the spirit of this law. The original spirit of this law is impartial justice and complete equality. Meaning, in this matter, the low and the high, the rich and the poor, the noble and the commoner, and the master and the slave should all be kept on the same level, and the law and the court should deal with each of them exactly the same. But this matter remained neither with the People of the Book nor with the Arabs; in fact, it is perhaps not misplaced to say that even today, despite this progress of civilization and culture, this concept of respecting life and equality is not found in any country or any law of the world that the Quran has presented.
Exegesis: The Meaning of Qisas: 'Qisas' is from 'qashash', the original meaning of which is to walk behind someone, following their footsteps. For example: 'And she said to his sister, "Follow him [qussihi]"; so she watched him from a distance while they perceived not' (Al-Qasas: 11). 'He said, "That is what we were seeking." So they returned, following their footprints [qashasha]' (Al-Kahf: 64). For this reason, a story is called 'qish-shah', because the one narrating the story follows the circumstances of the subject step by step. From this came 'Qisas', because the killer is also tracked down and pursued. Then 'Qisas' began to be used for the punishment in which the same treatment is meted out to the criminal that they themselves committed. There are two forms of this Qisas: one physical, the other financial, which is called 'Diyat' or blood money. The word 'Qisas' in its broad meaning encompasses both these forms because blood money is also, in reality, a form of retribution. The original law is 'a life for a life.' But for the welfare of the victim's heirs, Allah Almighty has granted such a concession in this law that if they wish, they can take blood money instead of a life. In 'kutiba 'alaykumul-qishashu fil-qatla', the use of 'ala' after 'kutiba' is proof that the subject of obligation and necessity is present here. 'Qatla' is the plural of 'qatil', meaning the slain; this word is used equally for masculine and feminine.
A Question and its Answer: A question will arise here: if the obligation of Qisas is proven by 'kutiba 'alaykum', then who is the addressee of this command? This question arises because it is established in Islam that the matter of Qisas is subject to a settlement. If the heirs of the slain wish, they can have the killer executed; if they wish, they can take blood money; if they wish, they can even forgive some of it. So when they can do all this, what is the meaning of saying "taking retribution is made obligatory upon you"?
The answer to this question is that the addressee of this command is the entire Islamic society as a collective, or in other words, the Islamic government. The duty is imposed upon it that if a murder occurs in its territory, it must track down the killers, arrest them, and implement the punishment according to the law. This responsibility was placed on the society or the government under the principle that a person who kills someone without any right is not just the killer of one person but the killer of all, because they have demolished that law of protecting life which provides the guarantee of the sanctity of life for everyone. For this reason, it became the responsibility of the entire society and the entire social system that all should pursue their retribution and not rest until they have revived this law of the sanctity of life and restored the guarantee of life for everyone. This reality is hinted at in Surah Al-Ma'idah like this: 'that whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land - it is as if he had slain mankind entirely. And whoever saves one - it is as if he had saved mankind entirely' (Al-Ma'idah: 32). If you reflect, you will realize that the original responsibility for Qisas should be upon the government, not on the heirs of the slain. This is because it is possible that a person is killed and has no guardian or heir; it is also possible that a person has some heirs but for some reason they have no interest in the matter of the victim's retribution. In fact, there is a significant possibility that the heirs' actual sympathy and interest, for some reason, might lie with the killer and their accomplices rather than the victim. Furthermore, the responsibilities of investigation and inquiry in such a matter, and then the implementation of limits (hudud), require vast authorities. For this reason, as far as the duty of taking retribution is concerned, Islam has imposed it upon the Islamic government; but in this regard, it has also imposed a restriction on the government that instead of deciding on its own, it should give the victim's guardians the authority to choose whatever treatment they prefer for the criminal within the limits of Islamic law. Whether they execute them or accept blood money from them. Giving the heirs this authority and implementing their choice will absolve the government of the duty imposed upon it by 'kutiba 'alaykumul-qishashu fil-qatla'.
The Wisdom of Considering the Victim's Heirs' Will in Qisas: The importance Islam has given to the will of the victim's guardians in the matter of Qisas is extremely wise from various angles. By giving the victim's heirs direct authority over the killer's life, first, a form of healing for their very large wound is created; second, if they adopt a lenient attitude in this situation, it is a direct favor from them upon the killer and their family, from which highly beneficial results can be expected; third, in the form of blood money, the victim's heirs—especially when they are poor—receive such help that can provide them great support. If the heirs were given no say in this, and all authority was handed over to the police and the court as in modern laws, they would be entirely deprived of all the benefits hinted at above. But despite the recognition of this right of theirs, the original party responsible for Qisas and its implementer is the government. For this reason, if it feels in a specific case that due to the heirs' coldness or their sympathy with the killers, the right of Qisas is not being fulfilled—thereby affecting the law of the sanctity of life—it will take appropriate action to save the law from this loss.
Observance of Equality in Qisas: "The free for the free, the slave for the slave, the woman for the female": This is the statement of the complete equality that must necessarily be observed in Qisas. Meaning, if a free person has killed another free person, then according to the law of 'a life for a life' (an-nafsu bin-nafsi), that free person alone will be killed in exchange for that free person, and in the case of blood money, the Diyat of one free person will be mandatory in exchange. It will not be according to the way of the pagan Arabs, where the victim's heirs, in their presumption of nobility and superiority, would demand that they will kill two or more free men from the killer's family in exchange for their one slain, or kill a man in exchange for a woman, or kill a free man in exchange for a slave, or in the case of blood money, collect a woman's Diyat equal to a man's Diyat, or take a slave's Diyat at the standard of a free man's Diyat. Similarly, the killer and their family and tribe members also have no right, in their presumption of nobility, lineage, and superiority, to claim that our slave is the equal (kufu) of others' free man or our one woman is equal to others' man, and therefore we will deal with the victim's heirs in physical or financial retribution while respecting this ratio. By announcing this complete equality, Islam ended all the aforementioned parities of the era of ignorance. The Jews had also established a distinction between the noble and the base, and between the Israelite and the non-Israelite in this matter. With this announcement, the foundation of this distinction also collapsed. On one hand, observe this law of Islam, which has existed in Islam's penal code for fourteen hundred years; on the other hand, look at this distinction between blacks and whites in America, the champion of justice and equality, which is being practiced in every field of life even today in this twentieth century.
'fa-man 'ufiya lahu min akhihi shay'un': Meaning, if some concession is given to the killer by the victim's heirs, they should value it. The form of this concession can only be that they agree to financial retribution instead of physical retribution; then it is the duty of the killer and their family members to pay the Diyat according to the 'Ma'ruf' (customary practice) with a spirit of favor-acknowledgment and gratitude, and the payment should be made with excellence and goodness. 'Ma'ruf' here refers to the custom and tradition of the Arabs which Islam gave the status of law in the matter of Diyat. The emphasis on payment with excellence and goodness was made because in Arabia, the payment of Diyat was generally not in cash but in the form of goods and property. Therefore, if the intentions of those making the payment were not good, they could play many tricks in it. It is very easily possible that the demand for Diyat is fulfilled in terms of the number of camels or goats or the quantity and volume of grain and dates, but in terms of reality and quality, its status is merely that of a formality. If this happens, it means no value was given to the favor of those who, having gained Shari authority over a person's life, forgave them and agreed to accept property from them. The response to their favor should only be favor. Meaning, the payment of Diyat should be made with such goodness, generosity, and open-heartedness that they do not have to suffer the grief that they committed an error or showed a lack of honor by accepting sheep and goats in exchange for the blood of a relative.
Under the command of Qisas, the mention of blood money (khun-baha) that appeared here without any prior context is primarily for the reason we hinted at above: the word 'Qisas' in its general meaning encompasses both physical retribution and financial retribution. The meaning of receiving some concession in it, as is clear from the words 'fa-man 'ufiya lahu min akhihi shay'un', is that the victim's guardians agree to take blood money according to custom instead of taking a life for a life. This permission for blood money is a concession and mercy from Allah Almighty. The original right of the sanctity of life was that a life should be taken for a life, but Allah Almighty has granted a concession in this purely out of His kindness; so this concession should be valued and no wrong advantage should be taken of it. 'fa-mani-'tada ba'da dhalika falahu 'adhabun alim': Meaning, those who open a path of oppression and excess after benefiting from this concession should remember that for them, the punishment of the Hereafter is painful, from which nothing will be able to deliver them. In this, there is a warning for the killer and their family members as well: after benefiting from this concession of Allah Almighty, it would be extreme ingratitude to make a scheme under its veil for some new oppression against the victim's family. For example, the killer and their relatives might plan to somehow save their life for now by satisfying the victim's heirs and then cause them further harm when an opportunity arises. Similarly, there is a warning for the victim's heirs too: they should not make a settlement for Diyat while holding the plan in their heart that we will take the Diyat from the killer for now and later, when an opportunity is found, we will also finish off their life. The settlement that has occurred under a concession granted by God must be respected by both parties with a sincere heart; whoever commits an excess after this settlement has occurred will deserve the wrath of Allah.

The Law is Superior to Emotions: This is an instruction to society that in the matter of Qisas, no negligence, no partiality, no turning a blind eye, and no misplaced mercy or compassion should be allowed to interfere. One who kills someone does not just kill one person but kills a law that is the guarantor of everyone's life protection. For this reason, they effectively kill everyone. Therefore, it is everyone's responsibility to restore this guarantee, in which everyone's life lies, by taking their retribution. Any person of the society who catches a killer, or tracks them down, or provides proof of their crime, and thus opens the path of Qisas from the victim, essentially gives life to that victim and simultaneously grants life to the entire society, because through this service of theirs, they revive the law which is life for everyone. The Quran hinted at this reality in Surah Al-Ma'idah like this, the reference to which has already passed above: 'that whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land - it is as if he had slain mankind entirely. And whoever saves one - it is as if he had saved mankind entirely' (Al-Ma'idah: 32). This removes the misunderstandings of those who, under the passion of misplaced compassion and partiality or an improper respect for nobility and wealth, sometimes declare sympathy for the killer instead of the victim as a rewarding act; whereas the true sympathy for everyone—whether poor or rich, noble or base, near or far, as in Surah An-Nisa verse 135—is to hand them over to God and His law, not to deliver them to Satan by rescuing them from God's law. But not everyone can understand this reality; only those who are people of reason can understand it. For this reason, the verse specifically addressed the 'people of understanding' (ulul-albab).
Are Penalties Contrary to Reason? One reason for specifically drawing the attention of the people of reason is that just as emotions sometimes obstruct the implementation of the law of Qisas, similarly, reason overawed and overcome by emotions also fails to estimate the original value and worth of this law. Especially in this era, a persistent philosophy has developed against all physical punishments, which is presented in the guise of rationality and philosophy, but if analyzed, it is clearly known that the original spirit within it is not of reason but of emotions. One group's view is that the crimes committed by criminals are primarily issued as a result of emotional imbalance, rational lack of equilibrium, and mental chaos and confusion; and these states are states of a person's illness in which they deserve reform, training, treatment, and medicine, not punishment. For this reason, according to this group, giving a killer the punishment of death is just like giving a patient some punishment instead of their treatment when they fall ill. According to this group, the treatment of such criminals—or in their own words, such patients—should be through education, training, and psychological and mental reform, not through the gallows and hanging. This theory has existed in the world in a specific group from the beginning, but it could never gain importance from a practical standpoint, nor perhaps will it ever. However, in this latter period, since people like Tolstoy and Mahatma Gandhi have advocated for it, the minds of many educated people are influenced by it. To remove the confusion of such people, it is necessary to point toward the wisdom of the law of Qisas, pointing toward which the Quran specifically addressed the people of reason and stated that within it is life. Obviously, this life is not in terms of the individual but in terms of the society. If a person is killed for the crime of murder, then outwardly, after one life, this second life is also effectively lost; but if viewed in terms of reality, a guarantee of life for the entire society is created by their execution. If retribution is not taken from them, then the mental defect they fell into, leading to the murder of an innocent, will become contagious in the entire society. There is a difference between illness and illness. The illnesses that cause dangerous crimes like murder, dacoity, theft, and adultery, etc., are like those illnesses in which, to save the entire body, a limb of the body sometimes has to be cut off and separated. Although cutting off and throwing away a limb seems like a cold-hearted act, a doctor has to adopt this cold-heartedness. If they do not adopt this cold-heartedness by forcing their nature, then in sympathy for one limb, they will have to deliver the patient's entire body to destruction. Society in its collective capacity resembles a body. In some limbs of this body too, sometimes the same kind of corruption and disorder arises whose treatment is not possible with ointments and bandages, but it becomes necessary to perform an operation on the diseased limb and separate it from the collective of the body. If it is thought that this limb is diseased and therefore deserves leniency and sympathy, the result of this leniency could be that one day this limb rots and decays the entire body. This is the nuance: the Holy Quran has described such punishments, which are of a severe nature, with the word 'Nakal'. 'Nakal' in Arabic refers to a punishment that serves as an example to others. Upon seeing which, others take heed and refrain from committing such a crime. In other words, this can also be said: by implementing such punishments, it is as if the entire society is given vaccinations so that it remains protected from the effects of contagious germs. Toward this same reality, the Quran has hinted here too with the words 'la'allakum tattaqun', the meaning of which is 'so that you may protect yourselves'—meaning protect yourselves from violating God's limits and from oppression and excess against each other.

Summary of Verses 180 to 182: After this law of the sanctity of life, attention was turned toward the law of the sanctity of property. These two subjects generally come together in both the Quran and Hadith, and in reason and nature too, there is a very close relationship between the two. Regarding the sanctity of property, the fundamental thing is that under a law, the rights of every person and, after them, their heirs, should be determined and protected, and second, that these rights should be respected. Among the Arabs, although the rights of parents and kith and kin were determined to some extent under 'Ma'ruf' (custom), just as corruptions arose in every aspect of their lives, disorder also appeared in this aspect, and the tendency among their powerful people to usurp the rights of weak heirs and rightful claimants intensified to such an extent that 'Ma'ruf' no longer held any value or worth among them. Surah Al-Fajr hints at this situation: 'And you consume inheritance, devouring [it] altogether' (Al-Fajr: 19). This situation demanded that the rights of kith and kin be determined through law and means for their protection be provided. But until the era of this Surah's revelation, society was not yet stable enough for that permanent law of inheritance distribution to be implemented which is in Surah An-Nisa; therefore, for the transitional period, the testators were instructed to make a will for parents and relatives according to custom, and the heirs were to fulfill this will. Then, when Allah Almighty sent down His own 'will' regarding the determination of the heirs' rights—which is mentioned in Surah An-Nisa—the will of the servants was abrogated, and only Allah Almighty's will remained. Then, the right of making a will for the servants remained only within a limited circle, the mention of which will come under the aforementioned verse of Surah An-Nisa. In this light, recite the following verses.
The Meaning of 'Wasiyyah' (Will): 'Kutiba 'alaykum' in the sense of making something obligatory is well-known in both the Quran and Arabic literature. The research of the word 'Wasiyyah' has passed in the previous pages: in Arabic, this word comes in the sense of instruction and guidance from an elder to juniors. Regardless of whether a person gives this instruction and guidance in their final hour or in general circumstances. For the instructions given by Allah Almighty to His servants, this word has also been used in the Quran. Here, since the word 'Wasiyyah' is in the sense of a verbal noun and is located at a distance from its verb, the observance of masculine and feminine was necessary neither in the verb nor in the subsequent pronouns. Two conditions are attached to the obligation of this will. One, that a person does it at the time when they see their death approaching, and second, when they are leaving some wealth behind. The first condition is mentioned with 'idha' (when) because the stage of death comes to everyone. The second is mentioned with 'in' (if) because having wealth is not necessary for everyone. This difference in the use of 'in' and 'idha' is not hidden from students of the Arabic language. Both these aspects hold great importance in a will. People who make a will during their active life sometimes fall into great complications, and people who avoid making a will while possessing wealth sometimes leave disputes behind them. 
The original meaning of 'Khayr' is a desired and liked thing; therefore, its use is for knowledge, reason, wisdom, justice, virtue, and goodness alike. Then, from here, it also began to be used for wealth because wealth is also a liked and desired thing. This word has been used in this sense in several places in the Quran; no reference is needed. By choosing this word for wealth, the Quran has effectively corrected the misunderstanding that was generally spread among people under the influence of monastic concepts—that wealth in itself is a filthy and impure thing and therefore it is not permissible for Godly people to be tainted by it. 
'Ma'ruf' literally means a recognized thing, meaning that which reason accepts, which stands up to justice, which good people recognize, and which is the practice and custom among the noble of society. This 'Ma'ruf' holds the status of Islamic law in many matters, and in this capacity, its reference has appeared in various places in the Quran. Its mention has already passed above in the series of Diyat. There are two types of law. One that is based on 'Ma'ruf', and the second that is based on the command of Allah Almighty. In a matter regarding which God's law does not exist, 'Ma'ruf' is authoritative; but in a chapter where God's law has been revealed, the authority of 'Ma'ruf' ends. This is because after the sun has risen, the need to obtain guidance from the stars no longer remains. 
This Command of Will was for the Transitional Period: The command for a will for parents and relatives given in this verse was under 'Ma'ruf' and was for that transitional period when Islamic society had not yet reached that stability for the final command of inheritance distribution to be given, which was revealed in Surah An-Nisa. Before the circumstances became favorable for the revelation of that command, this temporary command was revealed, and two benefits were intended from it. One, the immediate protection to an extent of the rights of those shareholders whose rights were being lost at the hands of the 'asabat' (agnate relatives). And second, to revive anew that 'Ma'ruf' which was authoritative among the noble Arabs since ancient times but had now gradually become buried under the dust of the era of ignorance, so that this 'Ma'ruf' could prepare the minds for the law that was to be revealed in this chapter. 
And regarding the will, it was stated: 'haqqan 'alal-muttaqin'. 'Haqqan' is for the emphasis of an omitted verb. Meaning: this is mandatory and necessary upon all believers who fear God; those who avoid it have hearts devoid of the fear of God. This means that in this transitional period, which passed before the original law of inheritance, its fulfillment was necessary for every Muslim. Its status was not merely that of a virtue and merit.

The Great Responsibility of Witnesses: Since the correct implementation of this law depended entirely on the honesty and integrity of the witnesses—as the rights of heirs had not yet been specifically determined in the Sharia—the great responsibility of the witnesses was clarified. If they make any changes or alterations to the will of the testator, the entire burden of that sin will be upon them. No responsibility for this will fall upon the testator or those who implement it. The reference to the attributes of 'All-Hearing' and 'All-Knowing' serves as a warning and threat to those who dare to make changes, reminding them that God hears and knows everything, and He will certainly punish this great crime.

The Meaning of 'Khawf': The original meaning of 'Khawf' is to suspect, imagine, expect, or apprehend. From this, it began to be used for the meaning of 'fear.' A poet from the Hamasa collection says: 'If I expected that by withholding my greeting, old age would turn away from me, I would have tried to stop it by refraining from my greeting.' In the verse under discussion, this word is used in the sense of apprehension, suspicion, and knowledge. The author of Al-Kashshaf hinted at this meaning but provided no evidence; we have provided the evidence here.
The Meaning of 'Janaf': The original meaning of 'Janaf' is to incline, but it is predominantly used for inclining away from goodness and truth toward evil and injustice. In the verse, it is used for undue partiality and improper bias.
The Meaning of 'Ithm': 'Ithm' originally contains the concept of 'delay' or 'falling behind.' Thus, 'Aathimah' is used for a she-camel that falls behind due to exhaustion. Later, this word was used for falling behind in fulfilling rights, whether they are the rights of God or of His servants. In this sense, it is the opposite of 'Birr' (righteousness/fulfillment of rights). The original meaning of 'Birr', as clarified under verse 177, is the fulfillment of rights. This word is also used alongside 'Udwan' (transgression) because sin regarding rights is of two types: one is of the nature of neglect and deprivation of rights ('Ithm'), and the other is of the nature of encroachment and excess ('Udwan'). In the verse under discussion, it is used with 'Janaf'. We clarified that 'Janaf' means partiality; in contrast, the exact meaning of 'Ithm' here would be the deprivation of rights. An unjust testator might be feared to commit one of these two things: either showing partiality to one heir or committing the deprivation of another's rights.
Prohibition of Alteration is Not Prohibition of Correction: In the previous verse, altering the testator's will was strictly forbidden. Now, in this verse, it is stated that this prohibition of alteration is not synonymous with a prohibition of correction (Islah). If a tendency toward partiality or deprivation of rights is felt in a testator, or if their will clearly contains such elements, then the effort by the witnesses to correct this partiality and deprivation is not considered the 'alteration' that was forbidden; rather, it is permissible. However, they do not have the right to make this correction arbitrarily; instead, they must adopt the path of compromise and reconciliation between the parties. If there is an opportunity to persuade the testator himself to adopt the path of justice, that path should be taken; otherwise, an effort should be made to bring about reconciliation among the heirs. This meaning is derived from the words 'fa-aslaha baynahum' (and settled the matter between them), as the clear meaning of these words is to bring about a reconciliation between them, not to perform an arbitrary correction.

Subject of Verses 183 to 187: Fasting for Self-Discipline: After the aforementioned laws regarding the sanctity of life and property, fasting and its related commands are now being described. According to our common jurisprudential (Fiqhi) order, fasting is included in the list of acts of worship, which might suggest it should have been mentioned alongside the verse discussing prayer and spending. However, the order of commands in the Wise Quran is not based on the order found in our books of Fiqh, but rather on the requirements of Sharia wisdom, social reform, purification of souls, and consideration of circumstances. Reflecting on these aspects reveals that after the laws of sanctity of life and property, the description of fasting brings forward the worship that Islam has appointed for self-control and the training of piety (Taqwa). This is so that humans can restrain the immoderate tendencies and motives of greed, provocation, avarice, revenge, desire, and agitation, and set the steed of their soul on the path of Taqwa. Fasting is a specific worship for producing patience and piety, and these are the qualities that save a person from encroachment and deprivation of rights, while urging them toward righteousness, excellence, truth, and justice. Thus, just as the command for fasting establishes the foundation of training for implementing the previous commands, it also provides the basis of patience for the upcoming commands regarding the prohibition of bribery, Hajj, and Jihad. In short, its placement in the sequence clarifies why fasting was made obligatory in Islam, what its goals and benefits are, how it affects life, and what its impacts are on collective life. In this light, recite the following verses.

Linguistic Research of the word 'Sawm': 'Sawm' and 'Siyam' are verbal nouns. The literal meaning of 'Sawm' is to abstain from something and to abandon it. 'Sama al-farsu sawman' means the horse did not eat fodder. A poet, Nabigha, says: 'Many fasting (hungry) and many non-fasting (satisfied) horses stood in the dust of the battlefield, while others were chewing their bits.'
Maulana Farahi (RA), in his linguistic research of the word 'Sawm' in his book 'Usul ash-Shara'i', states: 'The Arabs used to regularly train their horses and camels to become accustomed to hunger and thirst so they could endure more hardship in difficult times. Similarly, they trained their horses to face fierce winds. This is very useful in travel and war when encountering gusts of wind. The poet Jarir referenced both these points in one of his verses: "We remained in the place of the blowing heat as if we were standing with a fasting horse facing the wind." In this verse, he compared his and his companions' state to a person standing with his horse, training it to face hunger and fierce winds. It should be noted that the Arabs used things from their common experience for similes. In short, there are many verses regarding the "fasting" of horses.' From this comes 'Sa'im', meaning a person who abstains from food, drink, and marital relations. There are specific Shari limits and restrictions for this, the details of which are described in the Quran and Hadith.
Fasting is the Oldest Worship for Self-Discipline: 'Kama kutiba 'alal-ladhina min qablikum' (As it was prescribed for those before you): Meaning, this worship of fasting has not been made obligatory for the first time only upon you, but was also made obligatory for other nations before you. In divine Sharias, this has been a specific discipline for self-training from the beginning. The purpose of referencing this is to remove the hesitation of common temperaments by showing that this is not a new thing; it is an ancient inheritance of divine laws being transferred to you, and you are the most capable and deserving of adopting it and benefiting from it.
'La'allakum tattaqun' (So that you may become pious): The Purpose of Fasting: This describes the original goal of fasting. The foundation of the entire Sharia is on Taqwa (piety/God-consciousness). Taqwa is produced by the power and capability to control emotions and desires, and the best training for this power and capability is through fasting.

"A limited number of days": Meaning, this hardship of fasting has not been placed upon you for a long duration, but only a few counted days in the year have been specified for it. Just as the words 'as it was prescribed for those before you' in the previous verse came as a way of consoling hearts, the words 'ayyamam-ma'dudat' (a limited number of days) also appear as consolation: that this course of training in Taqwa and purification of the soul is only for a few days, so one should not be discouraged or lose heart. In describing worship that is heavy upon the soul, the Holy Quran has maintained this style of consoling the heart in many places. We have already hinted at some things regarding spending and zakat; more clear examples will follow.
The Meaning of 'Ayyamam-ma'dudat': What is meant by "a limited number of days"? One group of interpreters believes it refers to the three days of fasting each month. According to them, before the obligation of Ramadan, these three days of fasting were obligatory every month. The second group believes it refers to the fasts of Ramadan. They argue that in Islam, only the fasts of Ramadan are known as obligatory fasts. The three days of fasting the Prophet (PBUH) kept every month before the obligation of Ramadan were voluntary (Nafl), not obligatory. Imam Ibn Jarir, after quoting both views, gives his opinion in favor of the second group, and since we agree with his opinion, we quote it here. He says: 'In our view, the statement closest to the truth is that of those who say "a limited number of days" refers to the days of the month of Ramadan. This is because no reliable tradition proves that any fast other than Ramadan was made obligatory upon Muslims and then abrogated by Ramadan. The context of the verse itself is proof that the fasts made obligatory upon us are the fasts of Ramadan and no other. The words "The month of Ramadan in which was revealed the Quran" determine these days without any ambiguity. Therefore, those who claim other fasts were obligatory and then abrogated by Ramadan will be required to present a tradition that can serve as proof.' When viewed against the spiritual blessings of fasting, 29 or 30 days out of twelve months is not a long duration but truly just a few counted days; therefore, those seeking God's pleasure and self-reform do not consider this a long period but a very short and fleeting one. The Quran described it as 'ayyamam-ma'dudat' in view of its value and to console the heart.
A Wrong Interpretation: The meaning of 'wa 'alal-ladhina yutiqoonahu fidyatun ta'amu miskin' has generally been taken by people to mean that in the beginning, when the command for fasting was revealed, since the Arabs were not accustomed to this difficult worship, a concession was kept for their ease: that anyone who did not want to fast despite having the strength to do so could feed a poor person instead. Later, this permission was supposedly abrogated. But this interpretation does not seem correct in any way. First, what kind of 'obligation' would fasting be if there were open permission to fast or not fast and feed a poor person instead? If the nature of the initial command was such, the phrase 'fasting is prescribed for you' becomes almost unnecessary because its obligation would be entirely ineffective. Second, how strange it would be that on one hand, both the sick and the traveler are commanded to complete the number of their missed fasts by fasting on other days—as is clear from the words 'then an equal number of other days'—while on the other hand, there is freedom for anyone to choose not to fast despite being capable, just by feeding a poor person. This would mean the sick and traveler are bound to fast eventually, even if they must make them up later, while others are not required to fast under any circumstances, as a healthy resident could fulfill the requirement by feeding a poor person. Some, to avoid this difficulty, interpreted 'yutiqoonahu' as "those who can only fast with great difficulty." Taking this meaning resolves the above objections and provides a context for the phrase 'fasting is prescribed for you,' but it faces an even greater objection: does 'yutiqoonahu' have this meaning in the lexicon or is it just a self-invented meaning? In our view, the Arabic lexicon is entirely devoid of this meaning for this word. Some claim that a property of the 'If'al' pattern is 'salb-e-ma'khadh' (negation of the root), so 'itaqat' could mean 'not having strength.' We do not deny that negation of the root is among the properties of the 'If'al' pattern, but the properties of patterns, as scholars know, are based on usage (sama'i), not analogy (qiyasi). Therefore, the actual thing is the usage of the word. If the native speakers had used this word in the aforementioned sense and examples existed, then undoubtedly there would be no room for denial. But if no parallel for using this word in this sense exists in Arabic literature, the Quran, or Hadith, then merely on the assumption of a pattern's property, taking a word in a negative sense instead of positive is a great injustice to the Arabic language and a great tribulation in religion. If someone were to use this principle recklessly, they could easily change a large part of the religion from command to prohibition. Some less-learned people also say that saying "so-and-so has the strength for such-and-such" inherently means they have the strength only with difficulty. This is childish and requires no refutation. Even if we assume for a moment that the concept of difficulty exists within the meaning of having strength, the question is: does this strength make a person responsible for Shari hardships and religious commands, or does it absolve them? As far as Islamic Sharia is concerned, everyone knows that strength makes a person 'mukallaf' (accountable), not exempt. When you say "I have the strength for such-and-such," its clear meaning is that you are in the category of being accountable for it, regardless of whether you have the strength easily or with difficulty.
Furthermore, it is worth considering that if the intention was to say "those who can only fast with difficulty," there are dozens of well-known styles and words in Arabic that native speakers use; why did the Quran abandon them to use a word whose usage for this meaning is unknown to anyone? If a person says 'Inni utiqu hamla-s-silah', everyone will understand that he has the strength to carry weapons. No one will understand that he can only carry them with difficulty and therefore deserves to be exempt from Jihad. Similarly, suppose it is said 'lana taqatun bi-Jaluta wa junudihi'; its clear meaning can only be that we have the strength to face Goliath and his armies. If it could also mean "we do not have strength" or "we have strength with difficulty," then in the Quranic quote of the Israelites saying 'la taqata lana-l-yawma bi-Jaluta wa junudihi', the negative particle 'la' would have been absolutely unnecessary; their meaning would have been correctly conveyed in the positive form. In any case, those who took this meaning for 'yutiqoonahu' took a completely wrong meaning. But if this meaning is wrong, what is the interpretation of the verse when taking its well-known meaning?
The Real Problem and its Solution: The answer is that the problem in this segment is not in the word 'yutiqoonahu'—its meaning is the well-known and famous one, and there is no room for any other meaning. The real problem lies in the object pronoun of 'yutiqoonahu'—what is its referent? Generally, people take its referent to be 'Sawm' (fasting), which leads to all the problems mentioned above. But its referent is not 'Sawm' but 'Ta'am' (food), which is mentioned immediately after. This interpretation has been taken by some of our previous scholars, and I recall that Shah Waliullah's (RA) interpretation is also this. This interpretation is perfectly clear to us, but some might find it problematic that since the word 'Ta'am' has not been mentioned previously, bringing a pronoun for it before its mention is 'idmar qabla-dh-dhikr' (using a pronoun before the noun), which is considered a flaw in speech from which the Quran should be pure. There is no doubt that 'idmar qabla-dh-dhikr' is a flaw, but only when the referent is not prior in the speaker's intention. However, if the referent is prior in the speaker's intention and he is forced to delay the referent merely to avoid repetition or for some other requirement of eloquence, then in that case, it is not only not a flaw but a beauty of speech, and excellent examples of this exist in Arabic literature. In our view, the pronoun that our grammarians call 'dhamir ash-shan' is of this nature. In it too, the speaker brings the pronoun for a referent that is implicit in his mind. Here, the full statement was: 'wa 'alal-ladhina yutiqoona ta'ama miskinin fafidyatun ta'amu miskinin' (and upon those who can afford the food of a poor person, then the ransom is the feeding of a poor person). But in this form, the sentence becomes very heavy; therefore, the demand of the flow, brevity, and eloquence of the discourse was to omit 'ta'am miskin' in one place and put a pronoun in its place, and to make it explicit in the second place where its expression is indispensable, so that the speech remains pure from the flaw of unnecessary repetition. There is no doubt that this created the form of 'idmar qabla-dh-dhikr', but see that the thing for which the pronoun was originally brought is only delayed in the phrasing, not in the speaker's intention. After accepting this interpretation, the form of the issue that emerges is not that the fasts initially made obligatory allowed people to choose feeding a poor person instead if they didn't want to fast. Rather, the original form according to the Quran's words is that those who could not complete the fasts of Ramadan due to illness or travel were allowed to either make up the missed fasts on other days or, if they could afford it, pay a ransom by feeding a poor person for each missed fast. Thus, at that time, the compensation for missed fasts could also be done by feeding a poor person. Later, this permission was abrogated, as will be clear from the next verse; meaning, it was made mandatory to make up missed fasts only by fasting.
"And whoever volunteers good, it is better for him; and to fast is better for you" means that this ransom for a missed fast is the minimum demand from one who can afford it, which must be fulfilled. If someone feeds more than one poor person or does some other good to them, it is even better. Furthermore, the nature of this ransom is only that of a concession and relaxation; in the sight of God, it is much better for a person to complete these fasts on other days instead of paying the ransom. This was effectively a hint alongside the concession that this permission was temporary and provisional and was going to be abrogated. Allah Almighty prefers that the number of fasts be completed. Consequently, as hinted above and as follows, after some time the permission for ransom was abrogated, and the original command of 'then an equal number of other days' remained.

Context indicates that this verse was revealed some time after the previous verse. In it, one reality clarified is why Allah Almighty chose the month of Ramadan for fasting. The second is that the permission for ransom (fidyah) for fasts missed due to travel or illness was abrogated. Now, making up for the fasts was made mandatory through fasting itself.
The Wisdom of Choosing Ramadan for Fasting: The first reality is clarified by stating that this is the blessed month in which the revelation of the Quran for the guidance of the world began. Regarding this guidance, it is stated that it is both 'Guidance' (Huda) and contains 'Clear Proofs' (Bayyinat) of guidance and the 'Criterion' (Furqan). Meaning, alongside guiding to the straight path, it also contains those clear and decisive arguments for the guidance of reason and the distinction between truth and falsehood that will never become obsolete. The research of the word 'Huda' was given under verse 2 of this Surah, and 'Furqan' under verse 53. 'Bayyinat' refers to clear, heart-appealing proofs and arguments that remove every confusion. The Quran is not just a code for telling Halal and Haram, but also a never-ending treasure of the clear proofs of wisdom; therefore, it is sufficient for the guidance of the human intellect until the end of the world. The demand of gratitude for this great blessing was that Allah Almighty specified this very month for fasting, so that servants can become as close as possible to their Lord by being free from the desires of their soul and the temptations of Satan, and through their words and deeds, their outward and inward, and their day and night, they express and announce the reality that nothing in this world is greater to them than God and His command. A reflecting person can face no confusion in understanding that among all God's blessings, the greatest blessing is reason, and even greater than reason is the Quran, because reason itself obtains true guidance from the Quran. Without it, reason continues to wander in darkness even after applying all the telescopes and microscopes of science. Therefore, the month in which the world received this blessing deserved to be made a special month for the 'Takbir' (glorification) of God and gratitude to Him, so that the recognition of the value and greatness of this supreme blessing continues forever. For this gratitude and Takbir, Allah Almighty appointed the worship of fasting, which is the specific discipline for training in Taqwa, upon which the establishment and survival of the entire religion and Sharia depend, and for whose bearers the Quran was actually revealed as guidance—as He Himself clarified this reality in the very first verse of this Surah: 'This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those conscious of Allah (Muttaqin).' Thus, the sequence of this Quranic wisdom is that the true benefit of the Wise Quran is specific only to those who have the spirit of Taqwa, and the specific means for training in this Taqwa is the worship of fasting; therefore, the Kind and Wise Lord specified the month in which the Quran was revealed for fasting. In other words, the Quran is the spring for this world, and the month of Ramadan is the spring season, and the crop that this spring season nurtures is the crop of Taqwa. 
'fa-man shahida minkumush-shahra fal-yasumh. wa man kana maridhan aw 'ala safarin fa-'iddatum-min ayyamin ukhar': This is the actual predicate (khabar) of the preceding subject. Meaning: this month has been chosen for fasting due to its aforementioned characteristics, so whoever is present in this month should fast the entire month. The concept of being 'present' is clear from the subsequent words: that a person is not in a state of travel or illness. 'Fal-yasumhu' means to fast the entire month without any decrease or increase, and if one cannot complete the fasts due to illness or travel, they should complete this count on other days. From here, the words of the previous verse—'and upon those who can afford it is a ransom... and whoever volunteers good... and to fast is better for you'—were omitted, meaning the original command was amended by that much. For fasts missed during travel or illness, the permission for ransom that existed until now, as mentioned above, ended with the omission of those words.
The Wisdom of the Commands of Fasting: Further on, from 'yuridullahu bikumul-yusra' to the end of the verse, the wisdom and benefit of all the above commands are clarified in an ascending (sa'udi) order. Recall the points mentioned above: first, why was Ramadan specified for fasting? Second, the permission for ransom was abrogated, and now the number of fasts missed during travel and illness must be completed. Third, fasts can be postponed to other days in the state of travel and illness. The wisdom and cause of these three, starting from the bottom and moving upward, was stated thus: the permission to postpone fasts in travel and illness is given because Allah Almighty wants to create ease for you and does not want to put you in hardship; the permission for ransom was abrogated so that you complete the number of Ramadan fasts and are not deprived of the goodness and blessing hidden within it; and the month of Ramadan was specified for it so that you may glorify Allah's greatness and thank Him for this supreme blessing granted to you in the form of the Quran in this blessed month. Examples of this ascending order exist in Surah Al-Qasas verse 73 and Al-Anfal verse 11. In 'litukabbirullaha 'ala ma hadakum', 'Takbir' refers to that state of feeling and acknowledging God's greatness, majesty, and supreme glory which practically overcomes a fasting person during their fast, and due to which the servant abandons all their lawful desires solely in pursuit of their Lord's pleasure and satisfaction. This reality is also illuminated by the Hadith in Sahih Muslim: 'Every deed of the son of Adam is multiplied, a good deed by ten times its like up to seven hundred times. Allah Almighty said: Except for fasting, for it is for Me and I am the one who rewards for it; he leaves his desire and his food for My sake.' It should also be noted here that the concessions mentioned in Hadiths regarding pregnant women, nursing mothers, or the very elderly, etc., are all based on the principle of 'Allah intends for you ease and does not intend for you hardship' or on the same concession described for the sick and traveler. The Prophet (PBUH) extended the wisdom of the Wise Quran to those areas that fell within its sphere. Those who tried to derive commands for pregnant or nursing women from the word 'yutiqoonahu' have strained to join two completely unrelated things. We have already refuted this above.

Instruction to turn to God in doubts and difficulties: This verse is a preamble to the answers to questions that arose in people's minds or were uttered by their tongues regarding the sanctity of Ramadan and the commands and etiquettes of fasting after the revelation of the command to fast, which Allah Almighty then clarified. The Quran instructed such people not to make their doubts and objections a means of opposing God and His Sharia or a means of criticism and mockery, but to turn to God Himself to seek guidance. Whoever turns to God with sincerity and truthfulness for a real need, God certainly fulfills their quest, whether that need relates to their livelihood or the Hereafter, to mental and rational confusions, or to the benefits and wisdoms of the Sharia and its commands. The state of the hypocrites was that whenever they felt a difficulty in any matter of religion, instead of turning to Allah and the Messenger, they would make that matter a target of objection and mockery and start a campaign of whispering and mischief-making among the Muslims. The mention of this trait of theirs occurs in various places in the Quran. Especially in Surah Al-Mujadilah, some of its very important aspects are clarified. The Quran stated the preferred path of the believers is that they turn to God and the Messenger for their difficulties, and Allah Almighty removes their difficulties.
This preamble is a comprehensive one, suitable for various occasions, but here its connection, as mentioned, is specifically with the answers to a few questions regarding the month of Ramadan and its fasts. When these questions arose among the Muslims, the Quran clarified them and simultaneously encouraged them that when a question regarding God and His Sharia arises, one should turn to God for it; God is nearest and He solves everyone's difficulties. 'Wa idha sa'alaka 'ibadi 'anni fa-inni qarib.' "Question about Me" does not necessarily mean a question only about God's Essence and Attributes, but this question can encompass His Essence and Attributes, His likes and dislikes, and His laws and Sharia. Here, the context of the speech proves that the question relates to the commands that arose after the revelation of the original command regarding Ramadan and the etiquettes and conditions of fasting. From reflecting on the Quran, the reality emerges that the questions of inquirers are quoted with extreme brevity and summary; the original nature of the question becomes clear from the answer the Quran gives afterward. One reason for quoting the question in summary is to keep the discourse free from unnecessary length; another is that in answering most questions, the Quran's clarification has not remained limited only to the questions, but when its cloud of mercy has rained, it has irrigated both the dry and the moist. The requirement of this vastness and comprehensiveness of the answer was that the question be quoted in an ambiguous form so that the correspondence between the question and answer does not appear mismatched. Suitable opportunities for detailed discussion on this issue will come later; therefore, we limit ourselves to a hint here. 'Fa-inni qarib' (Indeed, I am near) is an expression of a reality. This is because proximity to or distance from God depends on the state of the servant's own heart. If the servant remains heedless and indifferent to God, nothing is further than Him; but if he remains attentive to God, keeps his heart filled with His remembrance, remains grateful for His blessings, and weeps and implores before Him for patience and steadfastness in His trials, then nothing is nearer to the servant than God. He is nearer than his jugular vein. This proximity is available to servants in every state and place, but specifically the era of a Prophet's advent—to which the hint is here—is a special era of proximity and connection to God. The Prophet is God's deputy and the servants' representative; God's angels are constantly coming to him; the chain of revelation remains established between him and God; the difficulties and questions the servants present before the Prophet are effectively presented before their Lord through his medium; and being an era of revelation, every moment there is the expectation that an answer to the question presented in His presence will be revealed. This reality is hinted at by this verse of Surah Al-Ma'idah: 'And if you ask about them when the Quran is being revealed, they will be made manifest to you' (Al-Ma'idah: 101). 'Ujibu da'wata-d-da'i idha da'an' (I respond to the invocation of the supplicant when he calls upon Me) is also the statement of a reality. When a servant calls upon his Lord, He responds to his call. Responding means the response of acceptance. It is not possible for a servant to call upon his Lord and for Him not to reach for his help, his cry for aid, and his redress; the only condition is that the servant calls with sincerity and supplication, and calls for that which is becoming to call for. If a servant asks his Lord for what should be asked, and asks in the way it should be asked, then that thing is certainly granted to him. If it is not granted immediately, it is preserved with God for his future or for his Hereafter; and if it is not received in the form he asked for, it is given to him in a better form or preserved for him. Regarding the acceptance of prayer, Allah Almighty has a specific Sunnah, the various components of which will be described in their respective places. There is no room for more detail here. 'Fal-yastajibu li wal-yu'minu bi' (So let them respond to Me and believe in Me) is the mandatory demand or result of the attributes of Allah Almighty described above. Meaning: when Allah Almighty is nearest to the servants and He hears their call and responds to it, then He alone has the right that all should say 'Labbaik' (here I am) to His call and believe in Him; then there is not even a minor reason of justification to turn away from Him toward anyone else. Especially, it is a great injustice to one's own soul that when the Lord is the embodiment of mercy and kindness to personally clarify the summaries of His Sharia, someone should make any of His commands a target of objections and doubts or fall into some betrayal or distress because of it.
Removal of a Doubt: From the context of this verse, it seems that the believers were also encouraged that if any hesitation arises in them regarding God and His Sharia, they should turn to God and His Messenger; Allah Almighty will remove their hesitation. Apparently, this seems contrary to the instruction given in 'Do not ask about things which, if they are shown to you, will distress you' and other verses of similar meaning. But this doubt is not correct. Where asking questions is prohibited, it refers to those types of unnecessary questions that the Jews used to ask their Prophet merely to corner him and to seek an escape from the Sharia. Those questions do not fall under this which are necessary for the clarification and explanation of the Sharia. Allah and the Messenger encouraged such questions, and their answers have increased the wealth of the religion; in fact, it is not wrong to say that Allah and the Messenger provided opportunities for such questions so that the wisdoms and benefits of the religion and the suitability of the Sharia for the needs of the servants become clear to the people.

Answers to Questions Regarding Fasting: After the above preamble, this is now the answer to the questions that arose in people's minds regarding the commands and etiquettes of fasting at that time. At its end, the words 'Thus does Allah make clear His verses' are witnesses that this verse was revealed as a clarification and explanation some time after the original command. Those familiar with the Quran's style of expression know that when some further explanation or detail of a command was revealed after the command itself, these words generally appeared, from which it is known that this command was revealed later as a clarification. This is the fulfillment of Allah Almighty's promise regarding the Quran mentioned in Surah Al-Qiyamah: 'Then indeed, upon Us is its clarification.'
'Uhilla lakum laylata-s-siyami-r-rafathu ila nisa'ikum': 'Rafath' originally means sexual talk, but here the preposition 'ila' after it creates the meaning of mixing and meeting with wives. Its being made 'lawful' does not mean that this thing was previously made Haram and later made Halal. That is not the case. Rather, since no such clarification existed in the initial command, many Muslims, out of caution and piety, understood that just as marital relations are not permitted during the day while fasting, they would not be permitted at night either. This idea might have been strengthened by the fact that among the People of the Book, the fast would restart immediately after breaking it, due to which they had to maintain the same restrictions at night as during the day. Since the Muslims had the fasting of the People of the Book before them as a practical example and it was referenced in the Quran, they voluntarily imposed this restriction upon themselves, abstaining from marital relations at night as they did during the day. But since there was no clear instruction in this matter until then, its nature was that of an ambiguous matter. Due to this ambiguity, some people, driven by the soul's prompting, would sometimes commit this act which was ambiguous even to their own conscience. The Sharia's instruction regarding ambiguous matters, as stated in the Hadith, is: 'Leave that which makes you doubt for that which does not make you doubt.' If, contrary to this, a person chooses the ambiguous side, it is a kind of betrayal of their own soul; therefore, the Quran described it as 'betraying yourselves.' But since this caution was contrary to the intent of the Sharia and the cautious Muslims had voluntarily imposed it upon themselves, Allah Almighty overlooked this 'betrayal' and gave explicit permission to establish marital relations with wives at night.
'Hunna libasul-lakum wa antum libasul-lahun': They are as a garment for you and you are as a garment for them. This points to the nature of the attachment husband and wife have for each other, and the purpose of this hint is to show that there is such a close-knit relationship between the two, and they are bound in such a bond of natural requirements, that there is no room to keep them separate from each other in any state. For this reason, the religion of nature has not imposed any such restriction on their mutual relationship that would build a wall between natural motives. If any limited type of restriction has been imposed in specific circumstances, it is only to the extent of the need for self-training, not exceeding it at all. The metaphor of a 'garment' for husband and wife is an extremely eloquent metaphor. We will point out some of its aspects here.
