The most prominent aspect of a garment is that it covers a person's body. It provides concealment for the defects of nakedness. Without it, a person would become naked and fall to the level of animals. Exactly in this way, husband and wife provide a veil for each other's sexual emotions and motives. The gender inclinations that arise within them have the means for their satisfaction and fulfillment within themselves; therefore, the occasion for them to become exposed and unveiled never arises. If this were not there, the agitation of emotions would create such a storm of sexual anarchy that nothing would remain hidden. The parts of the body that possess sexual attraction would push to be exposed, the tongue and pen would be overcome by the fever and delirium of obscenity, the heart would become a wanderer, and the gaze would become completely stray. If concealment for all these defects of our soul can be had, it can only be for the wife through the husband and for the husband through the wife. For this reason, the Holy Prophet (PBUH) declared marriage the most necessary thing for making the gaze modest. Regarding modesty (Haya), it is known that it is itself an internal garment—in fact, if you ask truthfully, this is the original garment; it is because of this garment of the interior that we adopt the garment of the exterior—and the help the husband gets from the wife and the wife from the husband in maintaining modesty is not obtained from anything else. Its second aspect is that it is an adornment (Zinat). This degree is after the covering of nakedness. Through a garment, a human adorns themselves with decoration, beauty, culture, and refinement, and steps into the field of civilization and progress. If you reflect, this same thing is obtained in a higher degree by the woman from the man and the man from the woman. Our sociologists say that humanity took its first step toward civilization and culture on the day the first man established his relationship with the first woman. This statement holds a very great reality within it. It is a fact that the desire for a home, the passion for elegance and decoration, and the zeal for acquiring wealth and property within a man were originally produced only from the relationship with a woman; later, other factors joined to increase it. Similarly, in a woman's adornment and decoration, her skill and refinement, and her zeal and absorption in housekeeping, the primary role is the desire to captivate the man. If either the woman or the man were deprived of this natural motive, all their aforementioned emotions would cool down. A man, if deprived of a wife, remains a traveler—in fact, somewhat of a nomad; similarly, a woman, if separated from or deprived of a husband, finds all her feelings dead and all her weapons rusted and blunt. It is this mutual link and connection of man and woman for whose sake we are blessed with all those livelinesses and springs of domestic life through which civilization and culture have flourished in the world. The third aspect of a garment is that it protects a person from the hardships of cold and heat and from many dangers of the enemy. Thus, it is stated in the Quran: 'And We taught him the fashioning of coats of armor to protect you from your [enemy in] battle.' From a moral perspective, exactly this is the case of the woman for the man and the man for the woman. The woman is a coat of mail and armor for the man, and the man is a coat of mail and armor for the woman. When these two are adorned and armed with their respective armor, the attacks of Satan are effective against neither of them; and if they are devoid of this garment, there remains the apprehension for both of them of being defeated by Satan. A gnostic said: make the wife a 'neck-amulet' in travel and residence so that you remain protected from the attacks of Satan. These three purposes of a garment are mentioned in the Holy Quran, and in all three regards, the woman holds the status of a garment for the man and the man for the woman. For this reason, Islam, which is the religion of nature, has respected this natural importance of their relationship and has not only not declared it contrary to Taqwa but, as known from the above detail, has declared it a helper of Taqwa in various regards. Consequently, the restriction that Muslims had voluntarily imposed upon themselves in this regard in the beginning due to misunderstanding or being influenced by the way of the People of the Book was removed through this verse.
'Alimallahu annakum kuntum takhtanuna anfusakum': This points to the fact that some people, holding the presumption that marital relations were not permissible at night in Ramadan just as they were not during the day, violated this; the Quran described this as 'betraying yourselves.' The reason is the same one we hinted at above: in ambiguous matters, a person should adopt the side of caution and then adhere to it. If they violate it, it means they violated what was in their reasoning or presumption a command of Sharia (even if it wasn't actually a command), and this is clearly a betrayal of one's own conscience. In our view, from here the point also emerges that a Mujtahid (jurist) should act upon their Ijtihad. If a thing is correct according to their Ijtihad, then its violation is not right for them unless the religion itself demands it. But since this restriction was contrary to the Divine intent, Allah Almighty took note of this betrayal but simultaneously forgave it and gave explicit permission for meeting with wives in the future. 'Wabtaghu ma kataballahu lakum': And seek what Allah has ordained for you—meaning children, which is the original purpose of marital life, seek them. And remember that this thing depends entirely on Divine decree, not on your choice or the intervention of anyone besides Allah. The purpose of referencing this is that the original goal of marital life is not just pleasure but the survival of the progeny, which is the very intent of Allah. If a person is only in pursuit of pleasure, then its stamp upon the human can be bad; but if the gaze is on the original goal, then this too enters into worship. In this era, the distortion of birth control is doing the exact opposite, uprooting the original purpose of marital life and giving pleasure the importance of the original goal. 'Kulu washrabu hatta yatabayyana lakumul-khaytul-abyadu minal-khaytil-aswadi minal-fajr': Eat and drink until the white thread of dawn becomes distinct to you from the black thread of night. This completely separates our fasts from the fasts of the People of the Book. Among them, there was no permission to wake up at night to eat and drink or for marital relations. Islam not only gave permission for this but emphasized it. From the Quran's words, it is also clearly known that this permission for eating and drinking is until the true dawn (Subh Sadiq) becomes well-distinct; this same point is supported by Hadiths and the practice of the Companions. For this reason, it is not correct in any way to declare one's own or others' fasts as doubtful merely due to a slight advancement or delay because of extremism in caution. These words of the Quran are so clear that it is surprising why some people in the era of the Companions faced difficulty in understanding their meaning. The tradition of 'Adi bin Hatim (RA) quoted in Tafseer books—that he tied two black and white threads to recognize the dawn—if fully reliable, should be attributed merely to that intensity of caution generally found in new converts to Islam. Such things should not be made a pretext to slander the understanding and insight of the Companions. 'Wa la tubashiruhunna wa antum 'akifuna fil-masajid.' 'Akafa' originally means to restrain oneself to something or to anchor oneself upon something. In religious terminology, it refers to secluding oneself from everything for the remembrance of God. This is called I'tikaf. From the Quran's words, it is clear that this worship has a special suitability with the month of Ramadan and the mosque. Accordingly, the practice and statements of the Prophet (PBUH) also support this. Since the purpose of I'tikaf is 'tabattul' (devotion) to Allah and complete single-mindedness of the heart is required, and staying in the mosque is among its requirements, marital relations are not permitted during it. 'Tilka hududullahi fala taqrabuha' means that the limits Allah has set for the soul's freedom must be monitored with full caution. So much so that to keep oneself safe from them, one should stay somewhat distant from them. This is because an animal that grazes very close to a sanctuary is in great danger of entering it. 'Kadhalika yubayyinullahu ayatihi lin-nasi la'allahum yattaqun.' We hinted above that when some further detail regarding a command was revealed after its initial revelation—either upon people's questions or merely due to the requirement of circumstances—this verse generally followed it. Some examples of this in this Surah are coming ahead; therefore, we do not feel much need to present parallels here. Since such details open further paths of Taqwa for those seeking it, it was stated 'la'allahum yattaqun', so that they may adopt Taqwa.
The Effect of Fasting on Human Capability: In this era, those influenced by the materialistic philosophy of life from the West raise the objection against fasting that it significantly reduces human capability and productivity, causing great loss to the individual and society. In our view, those making this objection overlook two fundamental realities. First, in the eyes of these people, whatever value and worth a human has is merely of their material existence. Their spiritual existence has no value or worth in their sight. According to them, just as a fat ox can plow more, a satisfied and full-bellied man can work more. These people are entirely unacquainted with the wisdom of Sayyidina Jesus: "Man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God." Similarly, these people are completely devoid of the reality to which our Prophet (PBUH) pointed: 'I spend the night in a state that a Provider feeds me and a Provider gives me drink.'
If a human is merely a collection of flesh and skin, then undoubtedly there is some weight in the objectors' objection. But if there is something called a 'soul' within a human, then the question is: is some food and strategy necessary for its freshness and strength as well or not? If necessary, are this same milk and butter, from which our bodies are nurtured, sufficient for it too, or is some other strategy and food required for it? Religion answers this question by saying that the essence of the soul within a human is not earthly but heavenly and divine; therefore, its food is obtained not from this earth but from the connection and link to God and from His word and inspiration. And its connection to God is nearest and strongest when it is somewhat free from the demands of the body (which serves only as its vehicle), its desires, and its emotions and inclinations. As long as it remains trapped in these base restrictions, it cannot fly in those heights which are its original playground according to its nature, and by flying in which those 'eagle-like' feats manifest that are deposited within its nature.
Fasting is the most effective means of granting the soul this freedom. The most prominent aspect of the training of the human soul through it is that the dominance of desires and lusts over the soul becomes weak, the human's power of restraint and willpower becomes strong, and thus the capability is produced within them that instead of following their desires like an obedient slave with hands tied, they, like a person of resolve and faith, put their desires and emotions behind the pleasure of their Lord and His commands. If you reflect, from here the reality will also become clear that the original treasure of power and strength within a human is not in their body but in their heart and soul. If the heart is weak and the soul is scattered, the state of bodies raised in extreme comfort and luxury is as if they are wooden logs that have been dressed in beautiful garments and stood up. The Quran described such people with the phrase 'khushubum-musannadah' (propped-up logs) and painted the picture of their fear and cowardice thus: 'they think every shout is against them.' Whatever danger appears in any corner of the world, their hearts start pounding that, for better or worse, this lightning is going to fall on our harvest. In contrast, those whose souls are awake, whose hearts are resolute, and whose spirits are high, show the feats of the 'arm of Haydar' (Ali RA) even while living on dry bread. The Quran pointed to this reality: 'How many a small company has overcome a large company by permission of Allah.' And the poet also pointed to this: 'I fear the living heart might die, for life is defined by your living.'
The second reality these objectors are neglecting is that the true value and worth of anything is not estimated by its temporary effects and results, but by the permanent and lasting effect it has on life, provided it is practiced correctly. It is possible that a medicine is extremely useful and proven for strengthening the heart and brain, with very lasting effects, but its immediate reaction manifests in the form of lethargy, body aches, or a hangover. Obviously, it is not correct in any way to use this immediate effect of the medicine as an argument to declare it a harmful or worthless medicine. Exactly this is the case with fasting. Its immediate effect—especially on the raw and unpracticed—certainly manifests in the form of fatigue and exhaustion, which also temporarily affects their capability. But the thing to look at is not this temporary effect, but that lasting effect which (provided it is practiced correctly) is imprinted on the human's outward and inward. For fasting, the word in Arabic is 'Sawm', as you read above. In the linguistic research of this word, we mentioned that initially this word was used for horses that, after reaching maturity, were prepared for war; and for this preparation, their fodder and grain were gradually reduced so that their bodies would become light for running and they would become well-capable of enduring the hardships of the battlefield and the toil of hunger and thirst. Obviously, this process was not performed on the horses so that their capability would decrease, but the goal was to increase their capability to the maximum so they would become fully useful for their purpose of creation. Although the immediate effect of this training on a well-fed horse is not good—it becomes weak and thin—the trainers keep in view that lasting effect which makes the horse capable of enduring every hardship and ease, turning it from a gluttonous animal tied to a post into a hard-working and life-risking soldier of the battlefield. This very 'Sawm' (fasting) which religion has prescribed for the outward and inward training of humans—the purpose is not to weaken their capability but to consolidate that capability as much as possible on the foundation of patience and Taqwa, so that the human becomes capable of Jihad against the opposing forces of truth, whether these forces be satanic or human. Those who keep an eye on the Quran and Hadith know well that two fundamental goals of fasting have been stated: Taqwa and patience. Taqwa is for a person to keep their soul bound to the limits of Allah in every stage of life and in every kind of circumstance. Patience is for them to face with full resolve and determination whatever difficulties and obstacles arise in this path from the outside or from their own interior, and not to surrender to them. This Jihad is a lifelong Jihad. In the month of Ramadan, every Muslim undergoes the immediate effect of this Jihad training in the form of exhaustion and weakness. But the thing to look at is not this immediate effect but its permanent effect. Its permanent effect, in the case of practicing it correctly, must certainly be that the human's dullness decreases, their soul becomes strong, their heart becomes vigorous, their willpower becomes firm, and their power of endurance increases, making them fully prepared for the Jihad of life and Jihad in the cause of Allah. Consider whether this is a decrease or an increase in human capability; in our view, those who possess these qualities are truly the 'flower of humanity.' Those who do not possess these qualities are not men, but merely 'cattle-raisers.'
Subject of the next verse, 188: We hinted above that both before and after the command for fasting, the prohibition of usurping the rights of relatives and the property and assets of others has been stated. This clarifies the position of fasting in the system of Sharia: that the original goal of this worship is to control greed, avarice, stinginess, and other diseases of this nature. Only by controlling them is that Taqwa produced within a human which makes them justice-loving and cautious in rights and transactions. Thus, alongside the instruction to avoid certain things, guidance was also provided for the strategy that can be most effective in saving the soul from them. Furthermore, if you reflect, this reality will also become clear: before the description of fasting, the instruction was given to make a will for the rightful claimants, to observe justice in that will, and then to implement that will with honesty; and after the description of fasting, the prohibition of 'buying' officials through bribery and using this as a means to usurp the rights of others was stated. The reason is that just as the entire value and worth of faith in Allah exists only as long as it is pure from the adulteration of Shirk—where Shirk mixes in, all its utility for life ends—similarly, the entire utility of the law exists only as long as honest officials are present for its implementation and society is pure from the disease of bribery; where bribery becomes common in society, the funeral of the law's utility is taken out. In light of this explanation, if you reflect, you will see that two aspects of the same command are mentioned here. One was mentioned before fasting, the second after it, and the mention of fasting was placed between the two so that from the structure of the discourse itself, it becomes clear that the person who wants to overcome these greeds and desires can climb this ascent by training their soul through fasting. In this light, now recite the next verse.

'Wa la ta'kulu amwalakum baynakum bil-batil': The Meaning of "Consuming Property Unjustly": 'Consuming property' does not refer merely to eating it but to its unlawful use and disposal. 'Batil' (falsehood/vanity) is the opposite of 'Haqq' (truth/right). Just as the word 'Haqq', as we clarified, comes in various meanings, its opposite also comes in various meanings. 'Batil' comes in the sense of 'vain' and 'purposeless.' For example: 'Our Lord, You did not create this purposelessly [batila]' (Al Imran: 191). Its second meaning is something that has no foundation in reason, nature, or Sharia. For example: 'And they disputed by [means of] falsehood [batil] to refute thereby the Truth' (Ghafir: 5). Similarly, 'Batil' refers to the method that is contrary to justice, fairness, Sharia, custom (Ma'ruf), and truth. Under this fall lies, betrayal, usurpation, bribery, interest (Riba), speculation, gambling, theft, and all those types of transactions that the Sharia has declared unlawful. Here the point is stated in summary. Its detail has come in other places and then further detail is in the Hadiths. The foundation of all transactions in Islam is on this principle.
'Wa tudlu biha ilal-hukkami lita'kulu fariqam-min amwalin-nasi': The Meaning of "Idla'": 'Idla'' originally means to lower a bucket into a well. For example, it is stated: 'fa-adla dalwahu' (and he lowered his bucket) (Yusuf). From here, the meaning of gaining access and proximity developed within it. Just as the bucket reaches the water through the rope, wealth can become a means of access to bribe-taking officials. It was stated: do not make wealth a 'rope' to officials to devour the property of others. This is because bribery is not a lawful means of acquiring wealth; rather, it is the path of 'Ithm'—meaning sin, deprivation of rights, and usurpation of rights. 'Wa antum ta'lamun' (while you know)—meaning its being a sin and deprivation of rights is known to you. In the 'Ma'ruf' of the entire world and in every religion and Sharia, its being a sin has been recognized. To reason as well, its being a sin is a self-evident matter. This sentence is conjoined to the first sentence, and since it is clarifying the first sentence, there was no need to repeat the negative particle 'la'. We have explained this style as much as necessary under verse 42 of this Surah.
Various Aspects of the Prohibition of Bribery: This verse sheds light on bribery from various angles. First, it is the greatest means of devouring the rights of others through unlawful methods. Consequently, after the prohibition of consuming others' property through unlawful methods, this specific thing was mentioned. The reason is clear: the utility of the law—which is the greatest means of protecting people's rights—depends entirely, as we hinted at the start of the section, on the rectitude and integrity of the officials. They are the original guardians of the law. For this reason, if they are made dishonest through any means, it means that rights are now for sale; whoever has money can buy them. Bribery is obviously the most effective weapon for making officials dishonest. Second, the most effective factor in the prevalence of bribery is society itself. When the tendency to usurp others' rights arises in people, they adopt the path of bribery to fulfill their purpose, and thus give the officials a 'taste for blood.' Then, when their mouths are bloodied with bribery, they become so addicted to it that they do not give people even their mandatory rights without taking a bribe. For this reason, Islam first stopped society itself from adopting this path: do not make your own watchmen into thieves through your own bad teaching. And so much caution was exercised in this matter that presenting gifts and presents to officials, and their acceptance by them, was also disliked—as is clear from Hadiths—because this too is a back door for bribery. Third, bribery being a sin is such a clear reality that everyone knows it. Reason is its witness, human nature is its observer, the world's 'Ma'ruf' is a proof for it, and all religions and creeds are united on its prohibition. Thus, it was stated: 'wa antum ta'lamun' (and you know it).
Subject of Verses 189 to 203: We have explained the aspect from which the prohibition of devouring people's property and using bribery as a means for it was mentioned incidentally alongside the description of fasting. Now, Hajj and Jihad are being described, the suitability of which with fasting requires no explanation. Hajj is also, in reality, a Jihad, and Jihad requires those qualities of patience and endurance that are best produced through fasting. In describing Hajj, the question people asked regarding the commands and etiquettes of those sacred months—which are specified for Hajj and Umrah and known as 'Ashhur-e-Hurum'—is quoted first. This question must have arisen in people's minds because when Allah Almighty declared Baitullah the Qibla of the Muslim Ummah and made its liberation from the control of the disbelievers necessary—as passed in the discussion of the Qibla (verses 142 to 160)—it followed that the stage of Jihad would have to be crossed for Hajj. Then, several questions arose regarding this Jihad. For example: what is the ruling if the turn for this Jihad comes during the sacred months? This question arose because war has always been prohibited in these sacred months; even in the era of ignorance, the Arabs used to respect them fully, and Islam also commanded their respect. Similarly, the question also arose: what will be the ruling if the turn for this war occurs right inside the Haram and the boundaries of the Haram? This question arose because, let alone war in the Haram, even disturbing any living creature within it was prohibited since ancient times. Similarly, the question of spending (infaq) regarding Jihad also came forward, because Jihad is not possible unless people are prepared to spend their wealth with full generosity in the path of God alongside their lives. Obviously, this spending is additional to the spending mentioned earlier. In this way, the issue of Hajj gathered within itself various questions regarding Jihad, the sacred months, and spending, etc., alongside the issues and commands of Hajj, due to the specific circumstances of the time. An outward observer, when seeing these different types of issues entangled with each other, might find a lack of connection in the discourse; but if a person reflects on this entire series while keeping in view the era in which these commands were revealed, they will clearly see the reality that all these points are connected links of a single chain. In this light, recite the following verses.

'Ahillah' is the plural of 'Hilal'. 'Hilal' refers to the moon at the start of the month, and it also denotes the month itself. Especially in the plural form, its usage for months is well-known. The definite article 'Al' on 'Ahillah' is proof that the question relates to some specific months, and looking at the context reveals that this question was about the 'Ashhur-e-Hurum' (Sacred Months) and their commands and etiquettes. Consequently, the answers given to this question in the following verses relate entirely to Hajj and the Sacred Months. Since the questions of inquirers are quoted in the Holy Quran with summary and brevity, as we hinted above, common interpreters presumed that this question related to the waxing and waning of the moon. But this idea is not correct. There are various reasons for its incorrectness, some of which we will mention.
First, this type of scientific and astronomical question is contrary to the taste and general temperament of the Arabs. The Arabs considered the sun and moon as God's creation and as subjugated and governed under His physical law; so what room was there for this irrational question as to why the moon waxes and wanes? They themselves could understand that this question could not corner the Prophet; he could very easily answer that it waxes and wanes by God's command and this is a very great proof of its being subjugated and governed. This answer had already been given in various styles and forms in the Makkan Surahs; in fact, those proofs were also before them which Prophet Abraham had derived from the rising and setting of the moon in favor of Tawheed. So what was the occasion for this type of question? Second, the context here proves that the question is not from the general Arabs or People of the Book, but from the Muslims. A question from the Muslims regarding the waxing and waning of the moon or sun is a completely far-fetched question. They could have asked about the commands and etiquettes of the months, but not a completely unnecessary and meaningless question. Third, if the question were about the waxing and waning of the moon, it would have been quoted as 'yas'alunaka 'anil-hilal' (they ask you about the moon). The words 'yas'alunaka 'anil-ahillah' would not have been used, because its well-known meaning, as we hinted above, would be that they ask you about the specific months. Fourth, the answers the Quran gave after quoting this question relate entirely, as will be clear from the following verses, to the commands and etiquettes of Hajj and the Sacred Months; there is not even a minor hint in them toward the cause of the moon's waxing and waning. If it were assumed that the Quran did not consider their question worthy of attention and therefore ignored it entirely to tell them some useful things about the months, then at least there should have been some hint here to the fact that people should not ask unnecessary questions, as such a warning was given to people in other places. In any case, in our view, this question has no connection to the moon and its phases; rather, as we submitted, it relates to those sacred months which had been sacred since the time of Prophet Abraham and in which fighting was considered Haram even in the era of ignorance. Regarding them, the question arose: after the Kaaba was declared the Qibla and its liberation from the disbelievers became necessary, what would be the limits and restrictions for observing their sanctity? The Quran quoted this question in summary and answered it in detail. We hinted under verse 186 that questions are generally quoted with extreme brevity in the Quran, and this is the demand of eloquence because the original nature of the question becomes well-clarified from the answer given afterward; so what is the need for lengthiness in quoting the question? This is the preferred style in the Arabic language. In other languages too, this is the well-known method of language experts. But it is a strange thing that due to this brevity in the Quran, people faced confusions in interpretation. Many people tried to determine the nature of the question from the summarized words of the question itself rather than from the answer, and thus they created a mismatch between the question and the answer. But this is not the fault of the Quran; it is the fault of the interpreters themselves.
'Qul hiya mawaqitu lin-nasi wal-hajj': This is one part of the answer to the question. The meaning is that these sacred months have been appointed for public welfare and specifically for the facilities of Hajj and Umrah. We explained in the discussion of the Qibla that the Sacred Months were not only of great importance to the Arabs from the viewpoint of worship, but their entire economic and commercial activity also depended on these months. During the era of ignorance, the Arabs spent the entire year fighting, due to which commercial movement in the country remained almost suspended. It was only the blessing of the Sacred Months that four whole months of the year passed in peace and security, and in these months, the people of the country would both be blessed with the bounties of Hajj and Umrah and reach the commercial markets of the country and abroad without any danger to conduct business. Especially for the commercial activities of the Quraysh, these months were like the months of spring. All of Arabia would head toward Makkah in these months, and this uncultivated valley would become the center of the entire country's trade. Alongside the spiritual blessings of the Kaaba and the Sacred Months, the Quran has mentioned their material blessings in various places and drawn the attention of the Quraysh to this great favor upon them. Toward this same reality, the words 'mawaqitu lin-nas' (measurements of time for the people) are hinting here. Meaning: various benefits and wisdoms for the people are hidden within these sacred months; therefore, their sanctity must be observed in every state. After mentioning this general benefit, its specific benefit, Hajj, was also mentioned: that these are the months in which people are blessed with the bounties of this Abrahamic Sunnah in peace and security. This aspect also specifically demands their prohibition [of fighting].
'Wa laysal-birru bi-an ta'tul-buyuta...': A Reformative Correction: This is a reformative correction, warning, and reminder alongside the mention of Hajj, similar to the correction and reformative warning and reminder that passed in verse 177 alongside the mention of the fundamental points of religion: that piety is not that you turn your face toward the East and West. Here it was declared: "Piety is not that you enter houses from their backs, but piety is the piety of the one who observes the respect of God's limits."
The Innovations of the Arabs regarding Hajj: It has been a common disease of nations that gradually people put the original commands and obligations of religion behind their backs and try to fill the void with innovations and rituals. This also happened to the Arabs. These people continued to perform Hajj in the era of ignorance, but by completely emptying it of its original spirit and making it a maze of rituals and delusions. Among others, they had invented this innovation regarding Hajj: after putting on the Ihram for Hajj, if they needed to enter their houses, or when they returned to their houses after Hajj, they would not enter through the doors from which they exited, but would enter from the back of the houses through some other path. The motive for this strange act must have been the delusion that since they exited through those doors laden with the burden of sins, it would be contrary to piety to enter through those same doors after becoming pure. This delusion was similar to the one they fell into regarding Tawaf. Many Arabs in the era of ignorance used to circumambulate the Kaaba naked. Their idea likely was: why should even a shred of clothing, which is among the things of adornment and decoration, remain attached to the body in this worship of asceticism and monasticism? The Quran refuted this innovation and stated: enter houses through their doors; this causes no difference in piety. However, if success in the Hereafter and God's pleasure are desired, then observe the guardianship of His limits and remain constantly fearful of Him. The original goal of Hajj is this very Taqwa.

Defensive War is Permissible in the Sacred Months: This is permission for the Muslims that if the turn for war arises in the context of Hajj, defensive war is permissible in the Sacred Months. However, exceeding limits is not liked by Allah. Meaning: neither is it permissible for you to initiate war yourselves in the Sacred Months, nor is it permissible to take any step beyond what is necessary for defense. However, you are fully authorized to defend; the sanctity of the Sacred Months or the Haram itself is not a barrier to this in any way; rather, it is the very demand of their sanctity. The detail of this nuance is coming in the following verses. This clarification alongside the mention of Hajj was necessary because until then, the Haram was under the control of the polytheists; therefore, the apprehension was very strong that if the Muslims went for Hajj, the disbelievers would stop them and the turn for war would arise. Especially when the polytheists had become well-aware during this interval that the Muslims had declared the Kaaba their Qibla and their claim was that they were the original heirs to the custodianship of this House built by Prophet Abraham. In such a state, it became necessary to give the Muslims those essential instructions regarding the commands and etiquettes of the Haram and the Sacred Months that could guide them in future potential situations. This reality should be kept in view: regarding the sanctity of the Haram and the Sacred Months, the feelings of the entire Arab nation were extremely sensitive. Fighting in them was the greatest sin in the eyes of everyone; therefore, the Muslims too could not be ready for any war in them—even if it were in defense—until the Quran gave permission for it.

Meaning: the Hajj of Baitullah is obligatory upon you, and as the original heirs of the religion of Abraham, it is your right too—in fact, you alone are its true rightful claimants; therefore, if the Quraysh obstruct this right and duty of yours, then face them and kill them wherever you encounter them. Even if the turn for this fighting occurs right inside the Haram and the boundaries of the Haram. And expel them from Makkah just as they expelled you, because the inheritance of Abraham and Ishmael (PBUT) cannot be obtained by anyone merely on the basis of lineage and descent; rather, according to the statement of Prophet Abraham (PBUH) himself, its true rightful claimants are those who are established upon his religion. You have attained this degree, not they; therefore, they are the ones who deserve to be expelled from this House, not you.
'wal-fitnatu ashaddu minal-qatl' (And Fitna is a greater crime than killing). 'Fitna' here means the effort to turn someone away from their religion through coercion and oppression. In English, this is called 'Persecution.' This word is used in this sense in various places in the Quran. For example: 'Indeed, those who have tortured [fatanu] the believing men and believing women and then have not repented will have the punishment of Hell...' (Al-Buruj: 10). '...for fear that Pharaoh and his establishment would persecute them [yaftinahum]' (Yunus: 83). 'Then, indeed your Lord, to those who emigrated after they had been compelled [futinu]...' (An-Nahl: 110). The reasoning for the permission given above to fight the polytheists of the Quraysh—even to the extent that if they fight in the boundaries of the Haram and the Sacred Months, it is permissible to kill them and evict them from Makkah—is stated here. The meaning of this short phrase is: although killing and fighting in the boundaries of the Haram and the Sacred Months is a very grave matter, the oppression and torture being inflicted upon God's servants in that House because they believed in Allah is even graver than this killing. To eliminate this graver Fitna, you are given permission that if the turn for war arises, you respond to the disbelievers in kind and kill them wherever they face you. This is contrary neither to the sanctity of the Haram nor to the sanctity of the Sacred Months. 'wa la tuqatiluhum 'indal-masjidil-harami hatta yuqatilukum fih' (And do not fight them at al-Masjid al-Haram until they fight you there). This is an emphasis that Muslims should not initiate war near the Sacred Mosque. However, if they are attacked by the disbelievers to stop them from the Sacred Mosque, they should give a crushing response. Respecting the Sacred Mosque is a shared responsibility; if the disbelievers cast aside its respect in their enmity for the Muslims, then they themselves no longer deserve any concession regarding its respect. This is, in reality, the punishment for their own actions. 'kadhalika jaza'ul-kafirin' (Such is the recompense of the disbelievers).

"But if they cease, then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." "Cease" (intahu) does not mean merely stopping the fight. Here, the reward for this ceasing is stated as "then Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." Obviously, if the disbelievers do not fight the Muslims, the most that can be their reward is that the Muslims also give them a respite and do not fight them at that moment; this cannot be the reward that Allah Almighty forgives all their disbelief and wickedness. Thus, 'ceasing' here refers to ceasing that enmity, opposition, and coercion (Persecution) which the Quraysh were guilty of, and through which they had expelled the Muslims from their homes, and simultaneously ceasing to block the Muslims from Baitullah, for which they no longer remained rightful claimants from any aspect.
If you keep in view the discussions in this Surah from the debate on the Qibla to this point, it cannot be hidden from you that this entire discussion does not relate to general disbelievers but specifically to the disbelievers of the Quraysh. Their dispute with the Muslims was not a temporary dispute over some partial matter; rather, it was primarily a dispute over the custodianship of Baitullah. The Quran's claim was that the true rightful claimants to the custodianship of this House built by Prophet Abraham are the believers, not the disbelievers and polytheists who have turned this House into a stronghold of Shirk and Kufr, completely contrary to its fundamental purposes. The Quran also claimed that according to Prophet Abraham's prayer and the Divine promise, the final Prophet through whom the purposes of this House were to be renewed and completed—Muhammad the Messenger of Allah (PBUH)—had completed that promise with his holy advent, and now it is mandatory that this House be liberated from the control of the disbelievers and polytheists and, being purified from all the pollutions of Kufr and Shirk, become the center of the religion of Abraham (Islam) and the Qibla of all believers. This claim has been presented in this entire Surah with such arguments and proofs and with such force and power that there is no room for any flexibility or leniency anywhere. In fact, in clear words, it can be said that liberating Baitullah from the control of the disbelievers and purifying it from all the taints of Kufr and Shirk to make it anew the center of monotheism, Islam, and the Muslim Ummah was the original objective of the Muhammadan messengership, and the achievement of this objective was, as it were, the final task of the Holy Prophet's (PBUH) sacred mission. In this light, if you reflect, the reality will come clearly forward that 'fa-in-intahu' does not mean only that the disbelievers of the Quraysh stop the war, but it means that they cease all those hostile and antagonistic acts they are committing today to resist this call of truth, and instead become its supporters and helpers. If they adopt this path, Allah Almighty will forgive all those crimes they have committed until now. This exact same point is stated in Surah Al-Anfal, addressing the disbelievers of the Quraysh: 'Say to those who have disbelieved [that] if they cease, what has previously occurred will be forgiven for them. But if they return [to hostility] - then the precedent of the former peoples has already occurred. And fight them until there is no fitnah and [until] the religion, all of it, is for Allah. And if they cease - then indeed, Allah is Seeing of what they do' (Al-Anfal: 38-39). This same reality is clarified in Surah At-Tawbah: that the disbelievers of the Quraysh have no share in the custodianship of Baitullah; this is specifically the right of the Muslims: 'It is not for the polytheists to maintain the mosques of Allah while witnessing against themselves with disbelief. For those, their deeds have become worthless, and in the Fire they will abide eternally. The mosques of Allah are only to be maintained by those who believe in Allah and the Last Day and establish prayer and give zakah and do not fear except Allah...' (At-Tawbah: 17-18). This is the specific aspect due to which, unlike general disbelievers, it was commanded for the disbelievers of the Quraysh that until they repent, establish prayer, and give zakat, there is no respite for them: 'And when the sacred months have passed, then kill the polytheists wherever you find them and capture them and besiege them and sit in wait for them at every place of ambush. But if they should repent, establish prayer, and give zakah, let them [go] on their way. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful' (At-Tawbah: 5). We limit ourselves to these brief indications here. God willing, we will discuss this issue of the disbelievers of the Quraysh in full detail in Surah At-Tawbah.

No Room for any Religion other than Islam in the Sacred Land: This verse further clarifies the subject of the previous verse: that this war with the disbelievers of the Quraysh must continue as long as any trace of Fitna remains in the Sacred Land and there is no room here for any religion other than the religion of Allah. And now this task must happen according to Prophet Abraham's prayer and Allah's promise, whether the disbelievers like it or not. This same point is stated in Surah As-Saff: 'It is He who as sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth to manifest it over all religion, although those who associate others with Allah dislike it.' To understand the correct context and original force of 'wa yakuna-d-dinu lillah' (and the religion becomes for Allah), it is necessary to briefly understand the Sunnah of Allah that He has preferred regarding His Messengers. That Sunnah is: when Allah sends His Messenger to a nation, that Messenger is the final and perfect proof of God for that nation, after which no further proof or argument is needed for them. If the nation still does not believe but persists in belying the Messenger and enmity toward the truth, it is destroyed. Regardless of whether it is destroyed through some Divine punishment or at the hands of the helpers and supporters of the truth and the companions of the Messenger, and regardless of whether this event manifests during the Messenger's life or after his death. 'I and My Messengers shall surely prevail,' 'Truth has come and falsehood has vanished; indeed falsehood is [by nature] ever-vanishing,' and other verses of this subject point toward this Sunnah of Allah, and a specific code is described in the Quran for its manifestation, the details of which will come in suitable places in this book. (Note that the Sunnah of Allah I hinted at here relates specifically to Messengers (Rasul), not to those Prophets (Nabi) who were only Prophets and not Messengers. The detailed discussion on this difference between Nabi and Rasul will also come in its place).
This verse points toward this Sunnah of Allah: that the completion of the objective of this final Messengership occurs at the point where no religion other than the true religion will remain in the Sacred Land. Consequently, on this basis, the Quran left only two paths before the disbelievers of Arabia—for whom the advent of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) was direct and who were occupying Baitullah completely unlawfully: either accept Islam or the sword. Unlike other disbelievers, there was no room for Jizya for them. Thus, when the requirement of the finality of proof was fulfilled, the Prophet (PBUH) led an army to Makkah, took control of Baitullah, purified it entirely from all pollutions of Kufr and Shirk, and announced 'Truth has come and falsehood has vanished.' Then, to permanently keep the Divine Haram pure from the dominance of Kufr and Shirk, it also became necessary to completely secure this entire region in which the Haram is located from non-Islamic control or intervention. Thus, the Prophet (PBUH) gave this instruction regarding the entire Arabian Peninsula: 'Two religions shall not coexist in it.' And in his final moments, he bequeathed the expulsion of the Jews and Christians from this land, which was carried out by Hazrat Umar (RA) in his time. This measure was necessary for the political protection of the center of Islam, and it is obligatory upon Muslims to always remain alert for the protection of this House and not allow the feet of any non-Islamic power to be established in this land.
The meaning of 'intahu' (they cease) in our view is what we hinted at above. Although the original meaning of 'udwan' is transgression and excess, here this word is used in the sense of mere 'action' (Action). In the Arabic language, sometimes certain words are used merely for 'mujanasat' (congruity) and harmony. Their meaning is determined by the context. For example, they say 'dannahu kama danu' (we recompensed them as they did to us). Obviously, 'danu' is brought here merely due to the similarity with 'danna', otherwise the context is for 'fa'alu' (they did) or some word of similar meaning. Or in the Quran: 'the recompense for an evil [sayyi'ah] is an evil [sayyi'ah] like it.' Everyone knows that the punishment for an evil is not an 'evil', but merely due to harmony with the preceding word, the punishment for the crime was also described as 'sayyi'ah'. Similarly, in the next verse: 'so whoever has transgressed [i'tada] against you, then transgress [i'tadu] against him' (meaning take action against him equal to his transgression). In this verse, the action taken in response to someone's transgression is also described with the word 'i'tida'', although in meaning it is merely 'action', used in this form only for harmony with what preceded it. According to this well-known style of the Arabic language, the word 'udwan' was used in the verse under discussion, but the meaning is merely that action which is taken as a retaliatory measure. The meaning is: if these people cease their actions and adopt the path of Islam, then no action will be taken against them based on their previous crimes; then action will only be taken against those who persist in their Kufr, Shirk, and their oppression and transgression.

The reasoning for the commands described above is stated here. The meaning is: fighting in the Sacred Months or within the boundaries of the Haram is a very great sin, but when the disbelievers do not observe its sanctity for you, then you too have the right that, as retribution (Qisas), you also deprive them of their sanctity. Every person's life is sacred in the Sharia, but when a person does not respect the life of another and kills them, then in retribution for that, they are also deprived of the right to the sanctity of life and are killed. Similarly, the respect for the Sacred Months and the boundaries of the Haram is recognized provided the disbelievers also observe their respect and do not make others a target of oppression and torture within them; but when their swords are unsheathed in this City of Security during these months, they deserve that in retribution, they too be deprived of the rights of peace and respect. Furthermore, it was stated that just as this Qisas of the Sacred Months is necessary, similarly, the Qisas of other sanctities is also required. Meaning: whatever sacred thing's rights of sanctity they deprive you of, you also have the right to deprive them of its right of sanctity in retribution. So, whatever kind of actions they take against you by ruining the sanctities of the Haram and the Sacred Months, you respond to them in kind. However, the limits of Taqwa must be observed. No transgression should be initiated from your side, and no action should exceed the necessary limit. The support and help of Allah Almighty are obtained only by those who fear Him in all kinds of circumstances.

Command of Spending (Infaq) for Jihad: This command of spending, as we hinted above, is for that Jihad which has been commanded here. Jihad demands the sacrifice of both life and wealth. For this reason, wherever the description of Jihad and fighting occurs in the Quran, the command for spending has also certainly occurred with it: 'strive in the cause of Allah with your wealth and your lives.' In 'wa la tulqu bi-aydikum ilat-tahlukah', the words 'bi-aydikum' (with your hands) bring to mind the image of a person jumping into a river or a pit with both hands spread out from above. Some Arab poets have also used this style. The meaning is: those who shrink from sacrificing life and wealth in the path of Allah might presume outwardly that they are saving themselves from dangers, but in reality, they are plunging themselves into the furnace of destruction. The original treasure of life and survival for a human lies in the sacrifice of life and wealth in the path of God, not in their [hoarding]... The Quran has made hints toward this reality in various places. In Surah At-Tawbah, while mentioning the hypocrites, it is stated: 'And they will soon swear by Allah, "If we could have, we would have gone out with you," destroying themselves [yuhlikuna anfusahum]; and Allah knows that indeed they are liars' (At-Tawbah: 42). In this verse, the words 'yuhlikuna anfusahum' point toward that very stinginess and cowardice from which the instruction to abstain was given in the phrase 'wa la tulqu bi-aydikum ilat-tahlukah'. From this, it is known that what those greedy for life and wealth consider success is, in the sight of Allah Almighty, death and destruction. 'Wa ahsinu' (and do good) is conjoined to 'anfiqu' (spend). Here, the meaning of this 'Ihsan' is to do a task with excellence. Meaning: spend in the path of Allah with generosity and a happy heart, and spend that wealth which is dear and beloved to you. The Quran has emphasized this 'Ihsan' in the matter of spending in various places: 'O you who have believed, spend from the good things which you have earned and from that which We have produced for you from the earth. And do not aim toward the defective therefrom, spending [from that] while you would not take it [yourself] except with closed eyes. And know that Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy' (Al-Baqarah: 267). As long as this zeal, passion, and caution for Allah Almighty are not included in spending, it does not attain the degree of 'Ihsan'. Allah Almighty is not in need of our wealth. He is independent and indifferent to all. However, we ourselves are in need of His generosity and kindness every moment. If He demands spending from us, it is not for Himself but for our own selves, so that in this way He may test our sincerity and, having accepted our 'shards of glass' (worthless offerings), return them to us one day in the form of an eternal and never-fading treasure.

The Nature of Umrah: Just as there are Sunnah and voluntary (Nafl) prayers alongside the obligatory prayers—which produce alertness and readiness for the original prayer and, if any deficiency remains in it, serve as a compensation for that lack—similarly, the nature of Umrah is like a rehearsal for Hajj. It produces readiness for Hajj in the temperament and, in some circumstances, serves as a compensation for its lack. According to its literal meaning as well, this word points toward the subject of construction, liveliness, and habitation, from which the point emerges that its objective is both the liveliness of Allah Almighty's House and the life and alertness of hearts. And these two things are inseparable.
In 'atimmu-l-hajja wal-'umrata lillah', the original force is on the word 'lillah' (for Allah). The meaning is: when you attain the blessing of Hajj and Umrah after crossing the stages of war and Jihad, then complete this Hajj and Umrah solely for Allah, the One without partner. The need for this emphasis and warning arose because the Arabs used to perform Hajj and Umrah even before Islam, but this Hajj and Umrah was not solely for the One God; rather, their false deities were also partners in it, whose idols they had installed right inside Baitullah and at other sites of Hajj rituals. Thus, when they went to the Haram for prayer or arrived there with the intention of Hajj and Umrah, their objective was not just the worship of Allah, but more than Allah, it was the pleasure and worship of those idols. They would worship them, present offerings to them, and perform sacrifices for them. Since these conditions existed in Makkah at the time of this verse's revelation, the Muslims were emphasized that when you perform Hajj and Umrah, do it only for Allah Almighty; let no taint of Shirk or Bid'ah be included in it. This reality was also stated in Surah Al-Kawthar: 'Indeed, We have granted you al-Kawthar. So pray to your Lord and sacrifice [to Him alone].' Maulana Farahi, in his Tafseer of Surah Al-Kawthar, has proven with clarification that 'Kawthar' here refers to the Kaaba, which is the metaphor in this world for the Fountain of Kawthar in the Hereafter, and the emphasis on prayer and sacrifice for your Lord alone was because before Islam, both prayer and sacrifice were mostly for other than Allah. Furthermore, there is another reason for the emphasis on 'lillah'. It is that Hajj and Umrah had become more of a means of trade than worship for the Arabs. For them, their status had remained that of commercial fairs, and those objectives for which Prophet Abraham (PBUH) had built this House had completely vanished from their sight with the passage of time. Consequently, under this commercial objective, they had invented the rule of 'Nasi'' to adjust the month of Hajj from the lunar to the solar calendar so that this month would fall in a suitable season for them from a commercial viewpoint. Here, through the word 'lillah', the Muslims were directed toward the original objective of Hajj and Umrah: that these acts of worship are appointed for seeking God's pleasure and training in Taqwa, not for holding fairs and business activities; therefore, unlike the disbelievers and polytheists, you make Allah your objective. The limited permission given to Muslims to obtain commercial benefits in this connection is mentioned later. 'fa-in uhsirtum famastaysara minal-hady': 'Ihsaar' means to be besieged or surrounded, and being surrounded here refers to being surrounded by the enemy. Further on, the words 'fa-idha amintum' (then when you are secure) also yield this same hint, and the circumstances of the time also support this, because at the time of these verses' revelation, Makkah was occupied by the polytheists of the Quraysh and they had not only expelled the Muslims from there but were not ready to allow them to come to Makkah again at any price; there was a strong apprehension that if the Muslims headed toward Makkah for Hajj or Umrah, they would obstruct them with full force. Consequently, it happened exactly like this. At the occasion of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, when the Prophet (PBUH) and his Companions intended Umrah, the disbelievers offered fierce resistance. This situation required that instructions be given to the Muslims beforehand for this potential danger. Thus, the instruction was given: if the enemy surrounds you and reaching Baitullah is not possible, then offer whatever sacrifice is available to you right there where you are surrounded. In accordance with this instruction, the Prophet offered the sacrifice at Hudaybiyyah and exited the Ihram. 'wa la tahliqu ru'usakum hatta yablughal-hadyu mahillah.' 'Mahill', as clarified by the author of Lisan al-Arab, is a noun of place/time from 'halla yahillu' and encompasses the meaning of both time and place. The meaning is: do not shave your heads until the sacrifice reaches its destination and the vow is fulfilled. Obviously, the forms of the sacrifice reaching its destination will be different in the two different states of security and 'Ihsaar' (being prevented), and evidence for both exists from the Prophet's (PBUH) action. At the occasion of Hudaybiyyah, he acted upon the form permitted in the case of compulsion, and later at the occasion of both Hajj and Umrah, he adopted the method for general circumstances. It should be remembered here that the security or 'Ihsaar' mentioned here originally relates to the enemy. Other obstructions that might arise due to illness or some other compulsion do not emerge from here originally but secondarily, and that relates to Ijtihad.
'fa-man kana minkum maridhan aw bihi adham-mir-ra'sihi fafidyatum-min siyamin aw sadaqatin aw nusuk': If someone is forced to shave their head before the sacrifice due to some illness or ailment, then in that case, there is an expiation (Kaffarah) upon them. Three forms of this expiation are mentioned in summary in the Quran: fasting, or charity, or sacrifice. The Prophet (PBUH) clarified this summary: either fast for three days, or feed six poor people, or at minimum offer the sacrifice of one goat.
'fa-idha amintum fa-man tamatta'a bil-'umrati ilal-hajji famastaysara minal-hady...': A Concession for 'Afaqi' (Distant) Pilgrims: This is a concession described for 'Afaqi' pilgrims—meaning those intending Hajj from outside the boundaries of the Haram. In the era of ignorance, performing both Hajj and Umrah in a single journey was considered a sin. This was fine for those living within the boundaries of the Haram, as it was not difficult for them to make separate journeys for Hajj and Umrah. But for pilgrims coming from afar, there was hardship in this; therefore, the Sharia granted them this concession that they can perform both Hajj and Umrah in a single journey. This is done by first performing Umrah and exiting the restrictions of Ihram, then putting on a new Ihram for Hajj during the dates of Hajj and performing the Hajj rituals. However, in this case, a sacrifice is mandatory for them. If a sacrifice is not available, then it is mandatory to fast for ten days: three days during the days of Hajj and seven days after returning from Hajj.
'wattaqullaha wa'lamu annallaha shadidul-'iqab': After the detail of the commands and instructions, attention was drawn toward the original spirit of the commands: that the original objective of all these commands is Taqwa. This is their essence, and it is from this that life is produced within them. If a human does not keep this thing in view, they neither fulfill their right nor obtain anything from them; rather, their entire life passes in associating false hopes with God and giving improper consolations to their soul, even though God's Sharia and moral laws are just as impartial in their results as His physical laws.

The context is proof that the word 'Hajj' here is used for both 'Hajj al-Akbar' and 'Hajj al-Asghar'—meaning both Hajj and Umrah. While discussing the word 'Qisas', we hinted at this specific nature of word usage. 'Ashhurum-ma'lumât' (well-known months) aims to point toward their being determined and limited, like 'ayyamam-ma'dudat' (a limited number of days), so that the one intending Hajj or Umrah does not become anxious at the thought of their restrictions. There are just a few known and determined months; so whoever resolves to perform Hajj or Umrah in them should uphold their restrictions and avoid obscenity, disobedience, and quarreling, and strive to earn as much goodness and Taqwa as possible. Although a human must certainly sacrifice their emotions and lusts in this Jihad, this is not something to be intimidated by. A human should remember that whatever smallest good they do remains in God's knowledge, and He will one day give a full reward for it. Here, three things are negated: 'Rafath', 'Fusooq', and 'Jidal'. 'Rafath' refers to sexual talk; the research of this word passed above. 'Fusooq' means disobedience to God, and 'Jidal' refers to mutual quarreling. Through the prohibition of these three things, all those doors of psychological motives are closed through which a human enters into sin. There are some specific reasons for the absolute prohibition of these things in Hajj. One reason is that in Islam, this worship is meant to acquaint a human with that final limit of world-abandonment and asceticism which is desired and liked in Islam and which is necessary for training and purification; beyond this begin the limits of monasticism, which Islam has forbidden. The second reason is that due to the restrictions of Ihram, the prompting within the soul for these things increases significantly. There is a weakness in humans that the desire for something they are prohibited from doubles within them, and Satan takes advantage of this weakness. The third reason is that because it is a state of travel, opportunities for these things arise frequently. If a person is not alert, they can fall into Fitna at every step.
A Specific Style of Language: In 'wa tazawwadu fa-inna khayraz-zadi-t-taqwa', the original structure of the discourse in our view is: 'tazawwadu [bit-taqwa] fa-inna khayraz-zadi-t-taqwa.' Meaning: when you set out for the journey of Hajj, set out taking the provision of Taqwa, because the best provision is Taqwa. In the first instance, under the requirement of brevity and eloquence, the word 'Taqwa' was omitted because its expression was necessary afterward; if it had been expressed in the first instance too, the flaw of repetition would have been created in the discourse, and the Holy Quran is pure from every flaw. Most people do not assume the word 'Taqwa' is omitted here. In their view, through the word 'tazawwadu', people are emphasized to set out for Hajj taking material provision. In their opinion, the reason for this emphasis was that many Arabs used to stand up for Hajj without any provision and thus caused trouble for themselves and became a cause of trouble for others. For this reason, they were instructed that when they set out for Hajj, they should do so after arranging provision for it. Although it is perfectly correct in its place that arranging provision for Hajj is prior—in fact, the reality is that the Sharia made Hajj obligatory only upon those who have the capability for it from every aspect—taking this meaning here would be entirely contrary to Arabic grammar. The reason is that in Arabic, when the word 'fa-inna' (for indeed) appears in the way it has appeared in this verse, it comes for the 'tawjih' (justification) and 'ta'lil' (explanation of cause) of what preceded it. If 'tazawwadu' meant material provision, then in its justification and explanation of cause afterward, its wisdom would also have been stated as to why arranging provision for this journey is necessary; but here, the wisdom of the provision of Taqwa was stated.
The context and occasion of this instruction also support the meaning we have hinted at. In the preceding segment, it was stated that whoever sets out with the resolve of Hajj should entirely abstain from sexual talk, sinful acts, and quarreling. The closest possible link to this subject can only be that for this sacred journey, a person should set out taking the provision of Taqwa instead of 'Rafath', 'Fusooq', and 'Jidal', because the best provision is the provision of Taqwa.

Meaning: the original objective of Hajj is Taqwa; therefore, the original provision for it should be that of Taqwa. However, there is no harm if a person also obtains some small or large commercial benefit from this journey. Here, 'fadhlam-mir-rabbikum' (bounty from your Lord) refers to commercial benefit. This word has been used for such economic benefits in other places in the Quran as well. The economic conquests a servant achieves while staying within God's limits and fulfilling His rights all enter into the 'Bounty of the Lord.' We hinted under the previous verse that in the era of ignorance, the gathering of Hajj had taken the nature of a very large commercial fair, due to which the original purpose of Hajj had been completely suppressed. The Quran clarified here that the original purpose of Hajj is worship, not trade. For this reason, take provision in this journey that is befitting of it, and that is Taqwa; but alongside the care for its original purpose, if someone also conducts some profitable business, no defect will occur in this worship. This is permissible. 'wadhkuruhu kama hadakum' (and remember Him as He has guided you): There is a command to spend the night in Mash'ar al-Haram (Muzdalifah) while returning from Arafat and to remember Allah there. Regarding this remembrance, the instruction was given that it should be in the way Allah has told and taught you—meaning in the form of glorification (Tasbih), declaration of Oneness (Tahlil), and worship—and not in the way you had adopted in the era of ignorance. Just as in this era people light lamps, make picnic programs, and hold poetry gatherings on occasions like Eid—to the extent that dance and music parties are also arranged in some places—similarly in the era of ignorance, people used to light fires in various places in Muzdalifah and hold gatherings for reciting panegyrics, storytelling, and boasting of ancestors. The Quran instructed them to perform Tasbih and Tahlil in place of these things, because originally the attendance at these sites is for this purpose. 'wa in kuntum min qablihi laminadh-dhallin' is stated as a way of mentioning favor and kindness. Just as in Surah Al-Jumu'ah: 'although they were before in clear error.' The meaning is: value the guidance being given to you regarding the commands and etiquettes of these sites, because until now you had made these sites places of play and sport, even though these sites are the manifestation-grounds of the lights of gnosis; but due to your ignorance, you considered the mines of jewels to be coal mines.

Context proves that the address here is specifically to the Quraysh. The meaning is: the restrictions that are upon others regarding the rites of Hajj are exactly the same upon you; therefore, just as all other people go to Arafat and return from there, you too should go to Arafat and return from there. The need for this instruction arose because the Quraysh, in the era of ignorance, did not consider attendance at Arafat necessary specifically for themselves during Hajj; they would only go as far as Muzdalifah and return from there. Their idea was that they were the priests and neighbors of Baitullah; therefore, it was not appropriate for them to step outside the boundaries of the Haram. They had established a distinction for themselves in worship. The Quran ended this self-created distinction of theirs and put everyone on one level.

After finishing the rites of Hajj, as we hinted above, a mood of recreation and interests used to overcome people, and gatherings of poetry and boasting were held; especially the days of staying in Mina had remained specified for these things. Poets and orators would describe the glories of their respective tribes and their respective forefathers in prose and verse and try to surpass each other with eloquence. The Quran corrected this absurdity as well and instructed to remain engaged in the remembrance of Allah with even more care and even more zeal and fervor in its place.
A Warning to the Seekers of the World: 'fa-minan-nasi may-yaqulu rabbana...' (And among the people is he who says, "Our Lord..."): This points to those people over every corner of whose heart and mind the love of the world is dominant, and due to this dominance, they keep their eye everywhere on that which is at the top of their heart. So much so that even if they are blessed with the opportunity for a great worship like Hajj, even in every instance and site of the acceptance of prayer within it, they pray to God only for the fulfillment of their worldly desires. Meanwhile, the column for their Hereafter is completely empty. How many people are there who perform Hajj only for some worldly purpose of theirs? Whichever site they understand to be a place where prayer is accepted, they present that same request which is dominant over their heart. They do not even like any mention of success in the Hereafter alongside it, lest this thing become a veil before God for their original desire. These are the types of people who tried to mold every aspect of religion into the molds of worldly interests, which distorted the appearance of religion; these were the types of people whose worldliness turned even a great worship like Hajj into the form of a commercial fair in the era of ignorance, as passed above; and this is the same tendency that in this era is trying to highlight Hajj merely as an annual congress. However, the original Abrahamic spirit of Hajj is migration toward Allah. Its worldly benefits are only incidental.

This points to those people whose minds are perfectly balanced regarding both the world and the Hereafter, and who ask their Lord for the good of both the world and the Hereafter. The mention of this group after the first group is to show that the quest of this group is liked in the eyes of Allah, and the believers should adopt this method. This prayer teaches that the servant should seek the good of both the world and the Hereafter from their Lord and leave the decision and choice of that good to Him; He alone knows in the best way what true good lies in for us. Especially, the being of 'good' in any worldly thing depends on whether that thing can become a means and resource for success in the Hereafter, and knowing this aspect of a thing is the work of Allah Almighty alone. For this reason, it is better for the servant to leave this matter to Allah Almighty and not present any specific proposal of their own. However, they should constantly seek refuge from the punishment of the Fire. This is a very severe thing; the servant's greatest success is that Allah Almighty keeps them in protection from Hell.

'ula'ika lahum nasibum-mimma kasabu' (Those will have a share of what they have earned): Regarding the first group, who only seek the world, it was stated: "they have no share in the Hereafter." But regarding this second group, it was declared that they will receive a share of their earning, and this share will be according to the principle Allah Almighty has appointed for the reward of the good deeds of His righteous servants. 'wallahu sari'ul-hisab' (And Allah is swift in account). This is suitable for both threat and consolation, and it has been used in both contexts in the Holy Quran. Those who remain intoxicated in their evil deeds, considering the punishment and reward of the Hereafter as a very distant thing—thinking why should they embitter their luxury now by worrying about something so far away—are reminded by these words: the account and reckoning you consider a very distant thing today, when it comes upon you, you will realize that not even a morning and evening passed upon it. Similarly, those who consider God's good promises as 'promises of tomorrow' and presume that an infinite duration remains for their manifestation are consoled by these words: be at peace, there will be no delay in the fulfillment of God's promises; when you receive the reward, you will feel that your wages were given to you before your sweat dried. Context proves that here it is not in the context of a threat but in the context of consolation. Here, this point should also be kept in view: in these matters, all importance lies in the feeling a human will have at the time of reward and punishment. If the feeling at the time of reward and punishment is that the distance between the deed and the reward has now completely vanished, then this distance is entirely negligible. Then the correct path is for the criminal to keep their punishment before them and the believer their reward. Neither should the former be deceived by the respite, nor the latter be impatient with the delay. And if someone, out of their lack of understanding, gives importance to this distance, they should keep this reality in view: 'Whoever dies, their Resurrection has stood up.' For the believer, as soon as the eyes close, the manifestation of the states of their good deeds begins, and for the disbeliever, those of their evil deeds. Then what distance remained between the deed and the reward? On one side the human laid down the burden of life, on the other side reward and punishment are standing.

The Gathering of Hajj is a Reminder of the Gathering of the Day of Resurrection: The words 'ayyamam-ma'dudat' (a limited number of days) appeared here for the same purpose they appeared in the mention of fasting, and they refer to the 'Ayyam-e-Tashreeq'—meaning the days of staying in Mina. The objective is: these are just a few counted days, so add as much as you can to the treasure of God's remembrance in them. Do not try to run away from there by considering this short duration heavy and burdensome. However, permission is given that whoever is in haste may return on the 12th of Dhul-Hijjah; otherwise, they may obtain the reward of staying until the 13th. Both forms can be adopted. But it should be noted that the cause of this haste should not be a feeling of the burden and lengthiness of faith, but some real necessity should be its driver. The need for this warning arose because many people, as soon as they return from Arafat, want to finish the remaining rites as quickly as possible to reach their respective homes. This is natural to an extent, but it should not contain the quality of disgust and anxiety. If there is any effect of this, it is a matter far from Taqwa, and a human should remember that one day they must face God, and on that day, no one will be able to move from His presence without His command. The words 'wa'lamu annakum ilayhi tuhsharun' (and know that unto Him you will be gathered) point toward this reality. This gathering of Hajj is a reminder of the gathering of the Day of Resurrection; therefore, in this metaphor, there should be no heedlessness of that reality.
Subject of Verses 204 to 214: Above, in the series of describing Hajj, the point that came at the end—that some people make Hajj merely a means for the fulfillment of their worldly desires, their hearts being entirely empty of the quest for the Hereafter—caused the discourse to turn toward the mention of the hypocrites. This is because those who are so world-seeking that they try to build only their world even in the prayers of Hajj can only be hypocrites. Consequently, in suitability with their mention, a commentary on the character of the hypocrites occurred in a few verses, and simultaneously, the character that true believers should have was mentioned, and they were given some necessary warnings at appropriate occasions so that the hypocritical way of the hypocrites does not become a stumbling block for them. In this light, recite the following verses.

Hypocrites are Weak in Character and Champions of Speech: This points to the hypocrites. Those whose characters are weak are generally champions of speech. They try to place veils over their practical weaknesses with their glibness and pleasant talk, and by taking advantage of the addressee's good intentions and noble nature, they try to satisfy them regarding their conduct. Among the hypocrites of Madinah, there was a group of such people. These people were well-to-do, ease-loving, comfort-seeking, and glib-tongued. Good appearances, clean clothes, skilled in social etiquettes, but hollow in heart and thieves in action. When they attended the presence of the Holy Prophet (PBUH), they would weave grand tales in support of Islam, but when they left there, all their running and striving would be in the path of opposing Islam. The picture of these very people is painted in Surah Al-Munafiqun in these words: 'And when you see them, their forms please you, and if they speak, you listen to their speech. [They are] as if they were propped-up logs [khushubum-musannadah] - they think every shout is against them. They are the enemy, so beware of them. May Allah destroy them; how are they deluded?' (Al-Munafiqun: 4). Meaning: their well-nurtured bodies and polished appearances outwardly entice the heart, and their smooth talk which they do to please you is drenched in the zeal of supporting Islam, which is why it seems attractive to you and you listen to their talk; but in reality, they are completely hollow from within. In their chests, there are neither hearts, nor faith, nor Islam. They are exactly like hollow wooden logs that have been dressed in clothes and sat up against walls. Due to being deprived of the wealth of faith, they are cowardly to the extreme; therefore, they see every danger as coming upon themselves, and they try to hide this cowardice behind the veil of their smooth talk. To divert your attention, they point fingers at others as being a danger to Islam, but the true danger to Islam is from their own hypocrisy and their cowardice; therefore, be fully alert toward them.
This exact same point is stated in the verse under discussion: as far as their talk is concerned, it is very captivating to the heart, but all this talk is gold-plated, and this artificial luster of the plating is temporary. In this world, they can certainly try to deceive people with these false pearls, but soon that day is coming when the distinction between the liar and the truthful, and the genuine and the fake, will be made, and this deceptive mask on their face will be removed. 'wa yush-hidullaha 'ala ma fi qalbihi' (and he calls Allah to witness as to what is in his heart). Calling Allah to witness means swearing by Allah. It is a characteristic of the hypocrite that they swear an oath at every step to prove themselves reliable.
