Since they do not have the proof of character, they try to present an oath as evidence at every step. A liar, due to their psychological weakness, thinks that the addressee will not believe their statement until they reassure them by swearing an oath. A righteous person of character relies on their action, and when they are questioned, they defend themselves with the proof of their action alone; but a hypocrite, since they lack the support of action, makes an oath their shield when they are questioned and tries to escape people's grasp with its support. In Surah Al-Munafiqun, this character of the hypocrites is also pointed out: 'When the hypocrites come to you, [O Muhammad], they say, "We testify that you are the Messenger of Allah." And Allah knows that you are His Messenger, and Allah testifies that the hypocrites are liars. They have taken their oaths as a cover, so they averted [people] from the way of Allah. Indeed, it was evil that they were doing' (Al-Munafiqun: 1-2).
'wa huwa aladdu-l-khisham'. 'Khisham' is the plural of 'khasm' (opponent), and 'aladd' means 'most severe in enmity.' The meaning is: outwardly their talk before you is very smooth, but within their hearts, an extremely severe type of malice and envy against you and Islam is filled. Toward this reality, Surah Al-Munafiqun points with the words: 'They are the enemy, so beware of them.'

Meaning: before you, their talk is very captivating, but after leaving your presence, all their running and striving is in the path of 'Fasad fil-Ardh' (corruption in the land). 'Fasad fil-Ardh', as we clarified under verse 11, refers to the resistance and opposition to that call of God's worship and obedience which the Prophet (PBUH) was giving. The entire peace and justice of the earth depend on the fact that God's servants enter into God's worship and His obedience, as is stated further on: 'Enter into Islam completely and do not follow the footsteps of Satan.' After entering into this worship and obedience, all paths for Satan's interventions and corruption are closed. Otherwise, the entire human race remains a target for Satan's mischief-making, and he constantly fans the fire of malice and enmity, which causes the destruction of both 'crops and progeny' (harath wa nasl). The Arabs had well-experienced this situation in the era of ignorance before Islam; therefore, it was not difficult for them to estimate what a great mercy and blessing that call of 'Silm' (peace/submission) and obedience was which the Quran presented to bring God's creation out of this furnace of destruction, and then what great enemies of humanity those people were who were opposing this call and wanted the world to remain burning in this furnace. 'wallahu la yuhibbu-l-fasad' (And Allah does not love corruption). Meaning: no matter how much smooth talk they do and how much they claim friendship with Islam and the Prophet, how can they obtain any status in the eyes of Allah when they are fanning through their conduct that corruption whose result is the destruction of all humanity? When Allah Almighty created this world, He liked its welfare and well-being; He does not like corruption and the corrupters within it.

Another characteristic of such false claimants of friendship with Islam and religiosity is that when they are questioned for some act contrary to Islam and their attention is drawn toward repentance and reform, their arrogance is deeply wounded. Due to their weakness, lack of confidence, and sense of inferiority, they think that if they once admit their weakness, their entire prestige will end; for this reason, they do not allow any bend in their stiff-neckedness. This specific aspect of the hypocrites is pointed out in Surah Al-Munafiqun in these words: 'And when it is said to them, "Come, the Messenger of Allah will ask forgiveness for you," they turn their heads aside and you see them evading while they are arrogant. It is all the same for them whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them; never will Allah forgive them. Indeed, Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people' (Al-Munafiqun: 5-6). These verses of Surah An-Nisa also point toward this reality: 'And when it is said to them, "Come to what Allah has revealed and to the Messenger," you see the hypocrites turning away from you in aversion. So how [will it be] when disaster strikes them because of what their hands have put forth and then they come to you swearing by Allah, "We intended nothing but good-will and conciliation." Those are the ones of whom Allah knows what is in their hearts, so turn away from them but admonish them and speak to them a far-reaching word. And We did not send any messenger except to be obeyed by permission of Allah. And if, when they wronged themselves, they had come to you, [O Muhammad], and asked forgiveness of Allah and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah Accepting of repentance and Merciful' (An-Nisa: 61-64).
'fa-hasbuhu jahannam' (then sufficient for him is Hell): This segment generally appears in places where the intention is to show that those given respite in the world despite these grave mischiefs—this respite is not a concession for them, but is given only because the Hell prepared for such people ahead is going to make up for all the lack; in its presence, there is no need for any punishment for them in this world. 'wa la-bi'sal-mihad' (and wretched is the resting place).

Encouragement for the Sincere: 'Shara yashri' means to sell. This points to the sincere believers who have sacrificed everything for the sake of seeking God's pleasure and satisfaction. Their mention here is from two aspects. One, so that those hypocrites mentioned in the previous verses feel a sense of shame—that not everyone is an interest-seeker and opportunist like you, but right before your eyes are those servants of Allah who have pledged to sacrifice their body, soul, and wealth in the path of God and consider the purpose of their life nothing but His pleasure. Second, so that those believers are encouraged who, unlike those hypocrites, were living for Allah alone and dying for Allah alone. Alongside the mention of the hypocrites, Allah Almighty pointed toward them to show that His life-risking and loyal servants are also present, and they alone are worthy of His kindness and mercy. In 'wallahu ra'ufum-bil-'ibad', there is a hint that although the covenant of 'buying and selling' with Allah Almighty is very difficult, and sacrificing one's entire life for seeking Allah's pleasure is a great Jihad whose demands are very testing of patience, Allah Almighty is very Kind to His servants; He does not place a burden upon them beyond their capacity, and if some forgetfulness or error occurs in the demands of this covenant, He forgives it; He has kept the paths of repentance and reform open for slips and shortcomings.

'Silm' means obedience, and it refers to the obedience of Allah and the Messenger. Some have taken its meaning as 'Islam.' But this difference is merely outward, because the original reality of Islam is the obedience of Allah and the Messenger. This word also comes as the opposite of 'Harb' (war), in which case its meaning is peace and security; the spirit of Islam exists in this meaning too, because the original path to peace and security is the obedience of Allah and the Messenger. 'Kaffah' means 'as a whole/collectively' and it is in the state of condition (Hal) here. It is used in this same form in other places in the Quran.
Invitation to the Hypocrites for Sincere Obedience: Although the address is general in terms of words—meaning to all Muslims—the context proves that the focus of the speech is toward those hypocrites mentioned in the previous verses. Addressing them, it is being said: like the true and firm believers, you too enter into the obedience of Allah and the Messenger completely. The reason for this instruction is that the loyalty of these hypocrites was divided. On one hand, they were claimants of faith in the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and professed support for Islam, and on the other hand, they also had dealings and conspiracies with the opponents of Islam. The Quran has hinted at this way of theirs in various places. For example, in Surah Muhammad, these very people are pointed to: 'That is because they said to those who disliked what Allah sent down, "We will obey you in part of the matter." And Allah knows what they conceal' (Muhammad: 26). Obviously, 'those who disliked' here can only refer to the leaders of the Jews and polytheists. The following verses of Surah An-Nisa also point toward this same way of theirs: 'Have you not seen those who claim to have believed in what was revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and what was revealed before you? They wish to refer legislation to Taghut, while they were commanded to reject it; and Satan wishes to lead them far astray. And when it is said to them, "Come to what Allah has revealed and to the Messenger," you see the hypocrites turning away from you in aversion' (An-Nisa: 60-61). It is established in its place that 'Taghut' here refers to the courts of the Jews. Since it was very easily possible to obtain judgments contrary to justice and fairness in those courts by giving bribes, etc., and the Jewish scholars had, through their cutting and tailoring, made many commands of the Sharia according to their desires, the hypocrites wanted to take many of their matters to those very courts; and when they were told that the demand of faith and Islam is to present their matters before the Quran and the Prophet (PBUH), they would try to adopt evasion through various excuses. This division of loyalty is contrary to faith and Islam—in fact, in its reality, it is Shirk. It is here that devils find an extremely wide path to mislead humans, as hinted in the aforementioned verse of Surah An-Nisa. For this reason, the Quran instructed to close the door of this Fitna and commanded that everyone enter into the obedience of Allah and His Messenger without any exception and without any reservation. This path of complete obedience is the path of peace and justice, and for those who walk on this path is success and felicity. Those who want to carve out some path apart from this and are desirous of maintaining relations with both Kufr and Islam simultaneously are following the footsteps of Satan, and Satan is a clear enemy of man. This is because he has given an open ultimatum to block his path and mislead him since the first day.

'Bayyinat' (clear proofs) refers to those warnings and threats that are described in great detail in the Quran to make one aware of Satan's tricks and his Fitnas, and also to that clear and decisive guidance revealed to describe the requirements of faith and Islam. The meaning is: if after the guidance and warnings as bright as the sun, you still (the address is to the hypocrites) follow the footsteps of your eternal and open enemy, then understand well that you cannot escape God's grasp in any way. God is Mighty and Wise.
Through the reference to the attribute 'Aziz' (Mighty), two realities are intended to be pointed to. One, to the reality that God is not some weak and powerless being, but He is Dominant and Powerful; so those who follow Satan despite His warnings, He will certainly seize them in that punishment which He has decreed for the followers of Satan and of which He has given prior notice. Second, to the point that those who, even after these clear instructions, abandon the path of truth to adopt the following of Satan will not harm God at all, but will only harm themselves, because God is 'Aziz'—meaning superior to every benefit and loss. Similarly, the attribute 'Hakim' (Wise) here highlights two realities. One, that the Creator of this world is Wise, and the self-evident demand of His being Wise is that He distinguishes between those who remain firm on His guidance and those who deviate from it in terms of their end; if He were to make no distinction between them but leave both to their state or drive both with the same stick, it would mean He is not Wise but a 'player', and this world is not a wise and purposeful machinery but the play and sport of some player. Second, that whatever delay or speed occurs in the manifestation of the results of evil and goodness is all based on wisdom; sometimes Allah Almighty gives respite to the followers of Satan, and sometimes the people of truth are put into some trial; from this, neither should the people of falsehood become arrogant nor the people of truth become despondent. Rather, one should have the certainty that both that respite and this trial are based on the wisdom of the Wise and Knowing God, and under this wisdom, His laws and their results are absolutely certain and inevitable; not a hair's breadth of difference is possible in them.

Just as 'nazhara yanzhuru' means to see, it also means to wait. The meaning is: after these 'Bayyinat' (clear proofs) and these warnings and threats, those who could not be smoothed onto the straight path but remained wandering behind Satan—now, under the Sunnah of Allah, nothing remains for the finality of proof (itmam al-hujjah) upon them. If they are still waiting for something, it means they are now desirous that Allah Almighty, Jalla Shânuhu, should show them His Majesty in such a way that His punishment is hidden with it in the clouds, and in its retinue are the overriding armies of His angels, and this struggle of truth and falsehood be finally decided. But this decision is not for the Prophet to make; rather, it relates directly to Allah Almighty alone. He alone knows when the decision for which nation should happen and how it should happen. From this verse, it is known that the faith and guidance which is authoritative is that which is the result of hearing and understanding the Divine verses, not that which waits for the manifestation and observation of Divine Majesty and Godly Wrath. The group that waits for this thing is only waiting for the manifestation of its own wretchedness, because it wants to believe after seeing the realities with its eyes; whereas what Allah Almighty requires is that a human use their reason and accept the guidance of the Prophets and Messengers sent for this purpose.

"Ayat Bayyinat" (Clear Signs) refers to the manifest miracles given to the Children of Israel. The purpose of referencing them is to show that the path of faith and guidance opens for those who use their reason and understanding; those who do not use their reason remain in their hesitation and lack of conviction even after seeing all the miracles of the world. Look at the Children of Israel: how many miracles did they see with their own eyes? Aside from the miracles manifested through the hands of Prophet Moses (PBUH) in Egypt, the sea dried up for them, Mount Tur was split, twelve springs gushed from a dry rock, and a table of Manna and Salwa was spread for them in a waterless desert. In short, miracles appeared at every step, yet the lack of conviction that had gripped them from day one remained. How then can you expect from those following in their footsteps that their eyes will open if a miracle appears before them according to their demand? This is wrong; their eyes will remain closed even after seeing the greatest miracles. A special aspect of referencing the history of the Children of Israel is that the hypocrites whose condition is being commented upon here mostly belonged to the group of the Children of Israel; therefore, holding up the mirror of their own past history is an extremely eloquent critique.
'Wa may-yubaddil ni'matallahi mim-ba'di ma ja'at-hu fa-innallaha shadidul-'iqab': 'Ni'mat Allah' here refers to Allah's guidance and Sharia. 'Changing' it means that instead of valuing it and making it a means of faith and guidance, they devalue it and make it a means of disbelief. In some places, the nature of this change is clarified. For example: 'Have you not seen those who exchanged the favor of Allah for disbelief...' (Ibrahim: 28). The meaning is that those who, after receiving this great blessing of Allah, wait for the manifestation of some sign that would forcibly bow their necks before the truth instead of benefiting from it, are in reality exchanging guidance for misguidance and blessing for punishment. Such people, according to the Sunnah of Allah, face God's severe retribution.

This points to the optical illusion (deception of sight) due to which the people of falsehood continue to live happily in their falsehoods. When they are informed by the Prophet and his companions of the evil end of this heedlessness, they mock them and demand punishment just to corner them, as hinted in the previous verse. The illusion is that in this worldly life, both truth and falsehood, and disbelief and faith, are given respite. If someone adopts the path of goodness and obedience, it does not mean they become superior to the law of trial (Ibtila); rather, in some cases, their trial becomes harder according to their faith. Similarly, if someone wants to live a life of disbelief and disobedience, the Sunnah of Allah is not to immediately send angels to seize them, but in most cases, they are given respite after respite so that their audacity increases day by day. This illusion is described here with the word 'Zuyyina' (made pleasing). Meaning, this deception of worldly life has been so embedded in their sight that they are not ready to look away from this aspect and see it from any other angle. Obviously, Satan has performed this 'beautification' (tazyin) of this specific aspect of life in their eyes, as clarified in other places in the Quran. And it is also evident that man's worship of the immediate (worldliness) and following of lusts provided Satan the opportunity for this beautification. When the believers remind those trapped in this illusion of some seizure or punishment in this world or the Hereafter for their deeds and beliefs, they laugh at them and mock them, saying: "Tell us, is your condition better or ours? If our condition is better—and obviously it is far better than yours—then why shouldn't we believe our way is also correct?" Then, when they see that despite all their intoxication, no such seizure is occurring as the believers warn them about, their satisfaction with their path increases further and they become even bolder in their mockery. 'wal-ladhinat-taqaw fawqahum yawmal-qiyamah': Meaning, in this world, the situation is indeed such that the outward-worshippers can mock the people of faith and piety because the machinery of this world is not running on the law of reward and punishment but under the Sunnah of trial. But after this life, another life is coming which will be the manifestation of the recompense of deeds; on that day, those believers who were not trapped in this worldly illusion but lived a life of Taqwa will be superior. It only says they will be 'superior' (fawqahum), without stating where those who mocked them will be. This is because that was already determined and they had been informed through the warnings of the Prophet and the believers; therefore, its expression was not needed. However, to clarify the superiority of the believers, it was stated: "And Allah gives provision to whom He wills without account." 'Provision' (Rizq) is an expression for Allah Almighty's favor (Fadhl), and regarding this favor, it was stated it will be 'without account'—meaning all scales of expectations, estimates, analogies, and presumptions will fail to measure it. The parable of this reality is presented in a very effective manner in some Hadiths, the details of which will come elsewhere.

Encouragement for the Believers: Above, the mockery and ridicule of the disbelievers and hypocrites against the believers was mentioned; now, in this verse, the believers are encouraged: "Be at peace, you alone are on the stance of truth; the reality that decides between differences and disputes is with you alone; and within this pitch-black darkness of disbelief and misguidance, you alone are the ones blessed with guidance to the straight path." These disbelievers and hypocrites who are opposing you and mocking you—all this is a miracle of the mutual stubbornness and enmity in which they have always been trapped. Due to this entrapment, they have become such enemies of God's guidance that they neither want to find it themselves nor let anyone else find it. So, despite their hostile activities, you remain firm on your stance. After this period of trial—which is under the Sunnah of Allah—passes, success and victory are for you alone.
'Kanan-nasu ummataw-wahidah': In our view, 'Kana' here is 'Tammah' (complete) in the sense of permanence, as in 'Kanallahu 'aliman hakima'. The meaning is that as far as Allah Almighty is concerned, He gave people only one religion and made them one Ummah, as stated: 'Indeed, the religion in the sight of Allah is Islam.' From the beginning, Allah's religion has been Islam: 'the fitrah of Allah upon which He has created mankind.' This is the religion of nature upon which He created people and this is the religion He sent through His prophets and messengers for people's guidance; He neither liked any religion other than Islam nor intended to make any Ummah other than the Muslim Ummah. With Him, religion is only Islam and the Ummah is only the Muslim Ummah. After 'Kanan-nasu ummataw-wahidah', the word 'fakhtalafu' (but they differed) is omitted. This omission is unfolded further on, where it says: '...that He might judge between the people concerning that in which they differed.' This omission is according to Arabic style to avoid repetition. The meaning is: Allah gave only one religion and made one Ummah, but people differed in this religion and consequently fell into destruction and sectarianism. So Allah sent His prophets to inform people of the evil consequences of differing in religion and to give glad tidings of success and salvation to those who remain firm on the truth. Allah granted these prophets Books; these Books were sent down with 'Truth' (Al-Haqq)—meaning a decisive word—so that all the disputes created in the true religion could be settled and the truth highlighted anew. But the nations granted this truth, after understanding it in the light of very clear proofs, differed in this truth themselves merely due to mutual stubbornness. Thus, despite repeated clarification of the truth from Allah Almighty, the difference remained, and it remained at the hands of those very people who were made custodians of this truth. Now, Allah Almighty has again opened the path of truth in this dispute and difference through this Quran for the believers—meaning those who believe in the Prophet of the Final Age (PBUH). And it is Allah who, according to the requirements of His will and wisdom, guides whom He wills to the straight path. Within this final segment of the verse, there is also a hint toward the great responsibility that falls upon this Ummah regarding the trust of the true religion: that after finding this truth, you too should not become those who create such differences in it as others did before you. And there is also a hint that this game is no easy game; rather, it is a very life-risking task. The hereditary monopolists of religiosity and truth-worship—whose entire business success depends on the truth remaining lost—will not leave you easily but will pursue you like the thorns of a bush.

The Touchstone of Trial for Truth-Bearers: This points toward that Sunnah of Allah by whose touchstone every group is tested that rises as the bearer of the original truth. The meaning is: do not be frightened by this opposition and mockery of the hypocrites and disbelievers; this is only the beginning of this path of love; far more difficult places come ahead. You too must pass through all those stages that the truth-bearers who rose before you had to pass through. Those who stepped onto this path before you faced such calamities and hardships and were so shaken by trials that the Messenger and his companions all cried out: "When is the help of Allah?" 'Hatta yaqula' (until he says) is in the sense of 'Hal' (present state) in our view, and its purpose is to paint the picture of the situation. The style of 'Mata nasrullah' (When is Allah's help?) expresses that plea which is like the final knock on the door of hope. It was stated that the door of Divine help opens with the key of this very knock: 'Unquestionably, the help of Allah is near.'
(Thirsty traveler, do not be afraid; now you have reached the fountain of eternity).
Subject of the next verse, 215 to 221:
The preceding subject, as clarified, arose as a digression (Iltifat) from the subject of Hajj, providing a timely warning to the people of hypocrisy and a pertinent reminder to the people of faith. The original sequence of discourse related to Hajj, Jihad, and spending (Infaq); therefore, after the conclusion of that incidental subject, the sequence returned, and answers were given to the questions that arose among the people during this time regarding the aforementioned things. These questions, as will be clear from the following details, relate to the very issues discussed above. However, the question regarding wine and gambling might seem disjointed at this point to those unacquainted with the cultural and social conditions of Arabia at the time these verses were revealed. This question actually arose here in connection with the 'spending' (Infaq) mentioned above. While explaining the verses, we will show that in the society of the pagan Arabs, while wine and gambling had many harmful aspects, they also had some beneficial aspects. Among the generous and ambitious people of Arabia, there was a tradition that during famine—especially in winter—they would gather in various places, drink wine heavily, and in the intoxication of the wine, they would slaughter whichever camel or she-camel they wished. Then they would pay the owner whatever price they demanded and gamble over its meat; each person would distribute whatever meat they won among the poor and destitute who had already gathered at the site upon hearing news of such an event. This tradition was very beloved in Arabia. When the cold northern winds blew and a famine-like state arose in the country, those who organized or participated in such events were considered great generous donors and held great respect in society. Conversely, those who stayed away from this were called by a specific word, 'Baram', meaning 'miserly.' Arab poets mention this gambling and wine with great fanfare in their panegyrics (Qasa'id). I wanted to quote references from the works of some famous poets here, but since general readers do not benefit much from such purely academic things, I ignore them. In short, this was the aspect of gambling and wine due to which, in the society of the pagan Arabs, they were counted among the traits of generosity and the motives for serving creation and sympathizing with the poor. Consequently, when the Quran emphasized spending and sympathy for the poor, a question arose in some people's minds: when Islam emphasizes spending wealth for sympathy and assistance to the poor and orphans so much, what then is the harm in this gambling and wine which become a means of helping the poor during famine? The Quran quoted this question here and answered it: there is no doubt that from these things, society does obtain some benefits in certain regards, but the material and moral losses they cause to both the individual and society are far greater than their benefits; therefore, Islam declared them Haram. This question is exactly like the question raised by those today who organize dance and music parties, or show cinema shows, or arrange displays and matches of film stars to collect funds for the aid of victims of famine, earthquake, and flood. If these people are also reproached for adopting these evil paths (even if adopted for a good purpose), they say: "When we are doing this work for the service of humanity, what is the harm in it?" In reality, these people, like the pagan Arabs, see only those aspects of their follies which in their eyes are apparently for public benefit; their gaze does not go toward the horrific losses these cause to the entire society. We limit ourselves to a hint here; the necessary detail will come under the exegesis of the verses. Similarly, in this context, a question also arose regarding orphans. Above, in the series of spending, orphans were referenced alongside parents and relatives as being deserving of this spending. Regarding them, the question arose: if someone includes the affairs of an orphan in their family—for whom they are responsible—with their own, and marries the orphan's mother, is there any harm in it? Here, the Quran clarified some aspects of this question as well. Now, in this light, recite the following verses, and they will appear as perfectly connected links.

In this Surah, the command for spending (Infaq) and zakat has been appearing repeatedly from the beginning. Especially in verse 95, in the context of the Jihad for the liberation of Baitullah, spending was urged with great emphasis. We hinted there that although the address is general in terms of words, the focus of the speech is actually toward those Muslims who were weak in sacrificing life and wealth. It is a rule that if a person has a weakness regarding something and is not finding the courage to do it, they ask questions repeatedly to hide this weakness, and thus they want to give the impression that as far as the actual task is concerned, they are present with heart and soul, but what can they do since the core matter has not yet entered their understanding? This is the secret why very few questions were asked by the true and firm Muslims; they were mostly asked by those who were weak-hearted and miserly and wanted to hide this weakness behind a veil of questions. These were the types of people who, in response to the command of spending, raised this question which is referenced and answered in the verse under discussion. This question itself expresses that they are being weighed down by the demands of spending and do not understand at what limit these demands will stop. Consequently, the Quran answered by keeping the mindset of the inquirers in view, and this answer has two parts. One part is that those spending in the path of God should keep this reality in view: whatever they spend, no part of it goes into God's pocket; He is not in need of anyone's wealth or goods. Rather, what He takes with one hand, He returns to us with the other; our own parents, our own kith and kin, our own orphans, our own poor, and our own travelers benefit from them. It is as if whatever we spend in the path of God, we spend not on the service of someone else but on our own service. The only difference is that the nature of this spending is that of a collective system whose benefit reaches everyone as a whole. The Prophet (PBUH) clarified this reality with the words: 'It is taken from their wealthy and returned to their poor.' And the verse of Surah Saba also points toward this: 'Say, "Whatever payment I might have asked of you - it is yours."' The second aspect of this answer is that a human should be at peace regarding whatever good they do: every single grain of it is in God's knowledge. Nothing is outside His knowledge, nor is He one to forget anything. When He knows everything, the mandatory result is that He will also give a full reward for it. Then, when a reward is to be received for everything—and that too from ten times to seven hundred times—why should a human hesitate to invest capital in such a profitable business? 'And they do not spend any expenditure, small or large, or cross a valley but that it is registered for them that Allah may reward them for the best of what they were doing' (At-Tawbah: 121).
Although this answer was extremely clear and comprehensive, it seems that even after this, some people—likely due to the same weakness we hinted at above—continued to ask questions. Thus, it comes later: 'And they ask you what they should spend. Say, "The excess [al-'afw]."' This answer determined the final limit of spending: since this spending is part of the Jihad for the protection and survival of the Ummah, the demand of religion in it is that a human should be ready to spend in the path of God whatever they can save from their indispensable needs. Maulana Farahi (RA) views this verse from a slightly different angle. His interpretation is that since this spending was for the Jihad commanded to liberate the Kaaba from polytheistic control, it absorbed all the Muslims' attention. They became so absorbed in the preparations for this Jihad that they did not have the attention they should have had toward the other recipients of spending—parents, relatives, orphans, the poor, etc. For this reason, a question arose among the people as to what the amount of spending should be. In response, it was stated that the first rightful claimants of whatever is spent in the path of God are those deserving ones who were mentioned; then, whatever further is spent, all of it will remain in Allah's knowledge and He will give the full reward for it. Here, the amount was not specified so that people would use their own reason and establish a balance between different religious needs. It seems that even after this, some doubt regarding the amount remained in some people's minds, so they asked again. In their answer, this clarification was made: spend whatever is in excess of the [needs of the] deserving ones. Since the deserving ones had been mentioned above, this short answer was sufficient.

Jihad of Wealth and Jihad of Life: It has passed in detail above that this spending and this fighting are both matters of the same series because Jihad is performed with both life and wealth. The previous verse removed the hesitation that arose in some minds regarding financial sacrifice; now, in this verse, the hesitation regarding the sacrifice of life is being removed. To remove this hesitation, a fundamental reality clarified is that a human cannot determine the path of success, felicity, ascent, and perfection for themselves; rather, only God can determine it, Who created them. This is because He alone has knowledge of the implications of human nature and its capabilities, and the methods and rules by which they can highlight all their capabilities. If the entire matter of deciding good and evil for themselves were left to man's own desire and his own reason, it is not surprising that in following his base desires, he would remove from his life's program one by one all those things that are the means of his ascent, perfection, and spiritual and moral evolution, because not a single one of those things is pleasing to his soul; rather, each is more heavy upon the soul than the last. Similarly, there was also the apprehension that he would gather within himself all those things that take him to the 'lowest of the low' (asfala safilin) because all those things are extremely easy and delicious for the soul. It is a strange mystery of human nature that the things desired by his soul are those that degrade him, and the things that elevate him are generally very heavy upon his soul. For this reason, Allah Almighty took upon Himself the responsibility of showing the path of his success and guided him through His messengers and books. The nature of the matter of war and Jihad is exactly this. If its outward aspect is looked at, what could be more horrific? But sometimes this horrific thing has to be made most beloved because if it is avoided, all human values will be completely destroyed.
When it is said that "Allah knows and you do not know," it does not mean that a human cannot determine the aspect of good and evil in any matter at all; rather, it means that a human faces many misconceptions on this path, which is why they sometimes stumble. It is the glory of Allah alone that His knowledge encompasses the manifest and the hidden, and the past and the future; therefore, He has personally revealed the entire program of life for man's guidance. And this program is so comprehensive that it guarantees success and progress in both this world and the Hereafter.

Further Questions and Answers regarding the Sacred Months: Above, in verses 190-195, it was hinted that if the polytheists obstruct your intention for Hajj and the turn for war arises due to their obstruction, then fight them and kill them, even if this war has to be fought in the Sacred Months or right within the boundaries of the Haram. Although this was clear, the traditions of the Arabs since the era of ignorance regarding the Sacred Months were so strict, and they considered war and bloodshed in them such a great sin, that this point could not easily enter many people's hearts. Since emotions play a large role in such matters, opponents also get a great opportunity for propaganda. Consequently, a question was asked by the people regarding this, and the Quran answered this question in detail.
Retribution (Qisas) for the Sanctity of the Sacred Months: Just as the Quran expressed the reality in the previous verses that killing and bloodshed in the Sacred Months is a grave sin, it expressed that reality here too: 'Qul qitalun fihi kabir' (Say, "Fighting therein is great [sin]"). But simultaneously, it also clarified the reality that: preventing people from the path of Allah, disbelief in Allah, depriving God's servants of the Sacred Mosque, and forcing those who are most deserving of this Sacred Mosque by their deeds and beliefs to emigrate from here—these are even greater crimes than fighting in the Sacred Months. Therefore, if to block these even graver crimes, one has to fight in the Sacred Months, this will be the retribution (Qisas) for the sanctity of the Sacred Months and it is not a sin but a very great virtue. Then, specifically, 'Fitna' was referenced: 'wal-fitnatu akbaru minal-qatl'—that this Fitna found in Makkah is a far graver crime than killing. We have explained the research of Fitna above: it refers to those cold-hearted tortures and hardships that the disbelievers and polytheists were inflicting upon Muslims to turn them away from Islam. It was stated that this Fitna is a greater sin than killing. Then, when this Fitna exists today right in the 'City of Security' and the 'Sacred City', and neither the respect for the Sacred Land is stopping the oppressors from it nor the respect for the Sacred Months is a barrier, then why should fighting in the Sacred Months for the defense of the oppressed be a sin? Then, to clarify the gravity of this Fitna, it was stated that the oppressions the disbelievers and polytheists are committing to turn Muslims away from the true religion are not merely individual events that might have occurred under some temporary passion; rather, to turn Muslims away from the religion, these people are making plans to trigger a series of bloody wars, and they (if it were in their power) are not ones to catch their breath until they succeed in turning you away from Islam. Will fighting in the Sacred Months still remain a sin even for countering such a hard and severe Fitna?
Up to here was the answer to the original question. After this, in connection with the mention of apostasy (irtidad), a timely warning was also given to the Muslims: if anyone among you turns away from their religion, overawed by their oppression and torture, and dies in that state, then all their deeds will become worthless in both this world and the Hereafter, and they will fall into Hell, in which they will remain forever. For becoming 'worthless', the words 'habitat a'maluhum' were used. 'Deeds' (A'mal) refers to those deeds that are apparently good, and 'Habt' refers to them becoming completely resultless and ineffective. Meaning: after this disbelief, the works of Islam they performed will also be completely ruined.
There is a special point to consider in this verse. Regarding the deeds becoming worthless, it was stated they will become worthless in both this world and the Hereafter: 'habitat a'maluhum fid-dunya wal-akhirah'. The deeds of apostates becoming worthless in the Hereafter is clear; however, the question arises: what will be the form of their deeds becoming worthless in this world? In our view, the answer is that a person who becomes an apostate is deprived of all civil rights in the Islamic state; the state no longer holds the responsibility for the protection of their life and property. Consequently, the law of Islamic penalties regarding the punishment of apostates is based on this very principle.

The Station of the Steadfast: After stating the end of those who become apostates out of fear of the disbelievers' oppression and torture, the station of those people was also stated who, despite all these cruelties of the disbelievers, will remain firm on their faith and play the gambles of migration (Hijrah) and Jihad. Regarding these people, it was stated that they are indeed worthy of hoping for their Lord's mercy. Context proves that the word 'amanu' (they believed) is used here in its perfect sense; for this reason, we have translated it as "remained firm on their faith." At that time, migration and Jihad were both simultaneously mandatory upon Muslims: the command for Jihad had been given for the liberation of Baitullah and the uprooting of Fitna, and the command for migration had been given for the help of the Prophet (PBUH) and the protection of faith. Both these battles were very hard; therefore, both these things were mentioned. This also contained guidance for the Muslims: the answer to the disbelievers' cruelties is not apostasy but migration and Jihad. Regarding those who can play these gambles, it was stated that they can be hopeful of Allah's mercy. Meaning: even after passing through all these stages, no one should have absolute certainty [of salvation based on deeds alone], because no one will attain salvation through their deeds; rather, whoever attains salvation will do so through God's forgiveness and His kindness. Thus, it was stated next: 'wallahu ghafurur-rahim' (And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful).

The Nature of the Question regarding Gambling and Wine: In the preamble to the section above, we hinted that this question regarding wine and gambling was asked by some people keeping in view those benefits which existed in them based on the traditions of the specific Arab society of that time. We have explained that in the society of the pagan Arabs, the nature of gambling and wine was completely different from current gambling and current drinking. For us, gambling is pure risk-taking and ruin, and wine-drinking is pure luxury; but in the era of ignorance, they had some aspects of sympathy for creation due to which the people counted them among virtues, not vices. From this angle, a question arose here in the series of spending and Jihad regarding them: one path of spending is that in times of famine, wealthy people drink wine and gamble, and whatever they win, they distribute among the poor; so what is the Quran's decree regarding this? The Quran answered: true, there are some aspects of benefit in them, but the losses they cause to society are far greater than their benefits; therefore, from the viewpoint of moral welfare, they are unlawful. Thus, the Quran here stated this temperament of the Islamic Sharia: things whose harm is greater than their benefit are prohibited in this Sharia.
A Misconception: Some people have thought that the benefits and profits of gambling and wine acknowledged by the Quran here are their material and medical benefits. But this idea is wrong. First, the Quran has no direct connection to the physical or material benefits of things or actions; and even if it did from some aspect, what thing in the world is so bad or impure that it does not have some aspect of benefit alongside its harm? Then what was the specificity of gambling and wine that the Quran acknowledged their benefits? Why did the Quran not express the aspects of benefit found in theft, adultery, interest, or swine, etc.?
Reasons for the Misconception: In our view, there are three reasons for this entire misconception. One is that people compared this gambling and wine exactly to the gambling and wine prevalent in our society; therefore, they could not even imagine the existence of any moral or human value within them. Second is that people's gaze is generally very limited regarding the literature, traditions, and 'Ma'ruf and Munkar' of the pagan Arabs; therefore, the gaze hardly reaches such hints of the Quran. Third is that these people do not fully fulfill the right of reflecting on the Quran's words; they just fly away with whatever point appears superficially. See, by using the word 'Ithm' (sin) as the opposite of 'Naf'' (benefit) here in the verse, the Quran made it perfectly clear that the subject under discussion here is not their material and medical benefits but their moral benefits. This is because the word 'Ithm' is not used for medical harms but for moral corruptions and sins; if the question were about the medical benefit and loss of wine, the word 'Dharar' (harm) would have been used as the opposite of 'Naf'', not 'Ithm'.
This verse clarified this temperament of the Islamic Sharia: things that are harmful from a moral perspective—even if some benefit apparently reaches or can be delivered to humanity through them—are still mandatory to avoid in Islam due to the dominance of the aspect of their harm. For example, it is possible that in some place people start a lottery so that they can build a magnificent mosque from its proceeds, or organize a benefit show of film stars so that by selling its tickets they can help Muslims in a disaster-stricken area. Outwardly, these works are of virtue and service to creation, but Islam has not kept this virtue as permissible because the evil that is nurtured under the veil of this virtue is far greater than this virtue.
'Wa yas'alunaka madha yunfiqun. Qulil-'afwa. Kadhalika yubayyinullahu lakumul-ayati la'allakum tatafakkarun':
The Wisdom of Graduality in the Answer: I have already explained the cause of this repeated question regarding the same thing: these questions were from those weak types of people who were feeling hardship in spending. It was in consideration of this very weakness of theirs that the Quran gave them the answer stage by grade so that it would not weigh too heavily upon them. If a full dose cannot be given at once to a patient who shrinks from medicine, the demand of wisdom is that it be given in two or three portions. Consequently, to those asking repeatedly about spending, the Quran gave this third and final, decisive answer. If this answer had been given the very first time, it is not surprising that it would have become a trial for the faith of the weaker types of people.
This answer is extremely brief yet simultaneously extremely clear and certain. It stated: 'Qulil-'afwa' (Say, "The excess"). 'Excess' (Al-'Afw) obviously refers to what remains beyond a person's own indispensable needs and those of their wife and children. It should be noted that the spending under discussion here is not that which is mandatory upon every Muslim for general deserving ones in the form of obligatory charities and zakat; rather, this is the spending related to Jihad, the exaltation of the Word of Allah, and the protection and defense of the Millah. For these objectives, the final limit of the responsibility of spending that falls upon a Muslim has been stated: if the need arises for the protection and defense of the Millah, then sacrifice in this Jihad everything you can save beyond your indispensable needs. In national life, such states and events do occur when everything must be sacrificed for the nation and religion, and every dignified nation of the world, whether disbeliever or believer, is forced to play this gamble; Islam has desired that we remain ready for this sacrifice and life-risking devotion of our own will.
'Kadhalika yubayyinullahu lakumul-ayati la'allakum tatafakkarun': This is a commentary on the entire preceding question and answer. We have submitted that all these questions relate to those very issues mentioned in the series of Hajj regarding the Sacred Months, Jihad, and spending. When some further questions arose later regarding these same issues, He clarified them and pointed out, as a mention of favor, that this is the explanation of the above summaries and the fulfillment of Allah Almighty's promise made in Surah Al-Qiyamah: 'Indeed, upon Us is its collection and its recitation. So when We have recited it, then follow its recitation. Then indeed, upon Us is its clarification.' Here, the benefit of this clarification is stated as 'la'allakum tatafakkarun' (so that you may reflect). The Holy Quran adopted this method in describing issues: it did not present every aspect of every matter all at once but left some of their aspects summarized until questions arose regarding them in minds and He clarified them gradually. This is purely so that people obtain the training of reflection and thought, and so that people do not remain merely 'blind followers of the line' in matters of religion but use their own reason to reach its secrets, nuances, benefits, and wisdoms.
A Wise Addition: Apparently, this verse ends at 'la'allakum tatafakkarun', but after this, the addition of 'fid-dunya wal-akhirah' (in this world and the Hereafter) exists. Reflecting reveals that this addition is extremely valuable. If you reflect on all the questions above, it will be known that these questions arose purely because people generally do not have that balance in their sight which can correctly weigh the benefits and wisdoms of both religion and the world. The result of this lack of balance is that if the inclination is toward religiosity, people turn religion into pure monasticism—to the extent that war and Jihad, in whatever state, are declared contrary to Taqwa. And if the inclination is toward worldliness, they will try to declare things like gambling and wine as virtues merely on the basis of the thought that, after all, they too have some aspects of benefit. The path the Quran has adopted for the training of human thought removes this lack of balance and makes it capable of correctly recognizing the rights of both the world and the Hereafter.
A Wrong Deduction of Socialism from the word "Afw": People influenced by socialist theories have tried to derive the conclusion from the word "Afw" that an Islamic government can take possession of income in excess of indispensable needs for collective purposes. But this idea is not correct. First, what is said here relates not to the government but to general individuals—that they should be ready for sacrifice to this extent of their own free will. Second, this matter, as has been clarified, does not relate to general circumstances but to emergency circumstances when the question of the Millah's protection stands before them. In such circumstances, first, individuals themselves become ready to make all kinds of sacrifices, and if the government feels the need to impose some restriction, they do not consider it an evil either. However, the true inclination of Islam is that individuals be trained in such a way that the courage to do maximum good is produced within them along with the freedom of will and choice. In the eyes of Islam, the value of this freedom is far greater than the value of forced and restricted goodness.

The Responsibility of Muslim Society regarding Orphans: This question regarding orphans also arose in connection with the social service and cooperation instructed in verse 215. When Islam placed the responsibility of orphans—especially family orphans—upon every person alongside their parents and relatives, stating that if they are needy and without resources, spend on them, and if they have wealth, try to monitor and nurture it with full caution (as much as possible without compensation), then a question arose among the people: if a person, for the sake of administrative ease, includes an orphan's wealth or business with their own wealth and business, and from the aspect of protecting their rights, marries the mother of such an orphan, is there any harm in it? This question arose specifically because the precautions Islam emphasized regarding the rights of orphans and the protection of their wealth are very strict. It is clearly stated in the Quran: do not even go near the wealth of orphans except for a good purpose; there is also the warning that those who devour the wealth of orphans are filling their bellies with the fire of Hell. A pious person, keeping these warnings in view, could not have the audacity to unify the affairs of orphans and their own affairs without the Quran's permission.
In response to this question, the fundamental thing the Quran stated was: 'Qul islahul-lahum khayr' (Say, "Improvement for them is best"). The original objective is that the welfare of the orphans remains in view from every aspect; whatever is for their betterment is what is better. If, according to your circumstances, it is better to keep their affairs separate while looking after them, then do so; and if you feel that by including them with yours you can perform this duty in a better way, then you are given permission for that too. After all, they are your own brothers, so why should separation be made mandatory? But along with this permission, this warning was also issued: 'wallahu ya'lamul-mufsida minal-muslih' (And Allah knows the corrupter from the reformer). Meaning: everyone should remember that it cannot remain hidden from Allah Almighty whose objective in this partnership is the welfare of the orphan and whose objective is to devour their wealth under this veil. If someone takes wrong advantage of this permission, they should remember that God is Mighty and Wise; no one will be able to escape His grasp. Simultaneously, attention was drawn toward this favor: it is merely the kindness of Allah Almighty that He gave you permission for this type of partnership for your ease, otherwise it was entirely possible that He would have stopped you from this partnership and simultaneously commanded you to protect the orphan's wealth or property. If that had happened, we would have fallen into a great hardship. But Allah Almighty showed great grace upon you by opening a path of service and virtue for you without putting you in any hardship; so every one of you should be grateful for this favor of God and not try to take any wrong advantage of this permission.
The Meaning of 'Anat': "'Anat" means trouble and hardship, and "I'nat" means putting into hardship. From this, the temperament of the Islamic Sharia is known: this Sharia has not opened paths for putting into hardship but for saving from hardships.

Prohibition of Marriage with Polytheistic Women: One aspect of the partnership permitted above for the welfare of orphans is: if the guardian of an orphan, from the viewpoint of protecting the orphan's rights, considers it appropriate to marry the orphan's mother—so that in this way the maintenance and protection of a widow's chastity is arranged, and an alert eye also enters his home for the monitoring of the orphan's rights—what is the ruling? The Quran answered that this benefit is valuable in itself and, keeping it in view, the mothers of orphans can be married, but the condition is that they must be believers (Mu'minat). This condition was placed because at that time, the situation was that there were many such orphans whose mothers had not entered Islam; therefore, the instruction was given that for the sake of this benefit, marriage with polytheistic women is nonetheless not permitted because of the apprehensions of other corruptions arising, which are pointed to further on.
The Usage of 'Mushrikin' and 'Mushrikat' as Terminology: It should be noted that the words 'Mushrikin' (polytheistic men) and 'Mushrikat' (polytheistic women) are used in the Quran as a title or proper noun specifically for the polytheists of Arabia. Other nations in which Shirk is found—whether they are from the People of the Book or similar to the People of the Book—are not directly under this word; therefore, the detail of their rulings will come in its place.
Islamic Standard for Liking and Disliking: Here, regarding the polytheistic men and women of the Ishmaelites, it was clarified that neither is it permissible for you to take their women in marriage nor is it permissible to give your girls to them. Along with this prohibition, this clarification was also made with great emphasis: a Muslim slave-girl holds preference over a free polytheistic woman even if she seems very attractive to you; similarly, a believing slave holds preference over a free polytheist even if he seems very pleasing to you. Then the reason was given: in Islam, the standard for liking and disliking is neither outward shape and form, nor lineage and descent, nor freedom and slavery, but faith and righteous action. For this reason, your relationships and ties are no longer bound to castes and brotherhoods but have become subordinate to creed and action. A beautiful princess of the Quraysh is worth two cents to you if she is not adorned with the jewelry of faith, and a pitch-black slave-girl from the coasts of Africa is a houri of Paradise for you if her heart is illuminated with the beauty of faith and Islam. Similarly, it is permissible for you to place your daughter's hand in the hand of the son of a slave if he possesses the wealth of faith, and to refuse to give your daughter even to a majestic chief of the Quraysh if he is deprived of faith and Islam.
The Effects of Relationships on Life: Then its philosophy was stated: the effects of relationships on life are not superficial and cursory but very deep. If a person gives no importance to beliefs and actions in these things, and only keeps beauty, or wealth, or family, or expediency before them, it is possible that at their own expense they nurture a calamity in their home that destroys the seed of faith and Islam not only for themselves but for their future generations. History is full of practical examples of the great changes marriage relationships have made in religion, traditions, and civilization. From a study of the history of the Children of Israel, it is known that countless creedal misguidances spread among them through the women they brought in marriage from other idol-worshipping nations. Similarly, among us, the marriages the Mughal emperors performed with Hindu Rajas under political expediencies brought their beliefs, delusions, rituals, and methods of worship into their homes along with their daughters. Even today, those bent on ending the distinguishing marks and theories of nations and religions consider mutual marriages the most effective prescription for it. For this reason, a Muslim should not be heedless and negligent in this matter but should always keep in view this great reality: the Lord in Whom he believes calls toward forgiveness and Paradise, and those deprived of this faith are callers toward Hell, whether they be women or men.
Since this verse was also revealed as a clarification of the above discussions, it was stated at the end: 'wa yubayyinu ayatihi lin-nasi la'allahum yatadhakkarun' (And Allah makes clear His verses for the people that they may take heed).
Regarding the points said about orphans in the two verses above, there are also hints toward them in Surah An-Nisa. We quote the relevant verses so that by keeping both before us, every aspect becomes clear: 
'And give to the orphans their properties and do not substitute the defective [of your own] for the good [of theirs]. And do not consume their properties into your own. Indeed, that is ever a great sin. And if you fear that you will not justly deal with the orphan girls, then marry those that please you of [other] women, two or three or four. But if you fear that you will not be just, then [marry only] one or those your right hand possesses. That is more suitable that you may not incline [to injustice]. And give the women [upon marriage] their [bridal] gifts graciously. But if they give up willingly to you anything of it, then consume it with satisfaction and ease. And do not give the weak-minded your property, which Allah has made a means of support for you, but feed them from it and clothe them and speak to them words of appropriate kindness. And test the orphans [in their abilities] until they reach marriageable age. Then if you perceive in them sound judgment, release their property to them. And do not consume it excessively and quickly, [anticipating] that they will grow up. And whoever, [among guardians], is self-sufficient should refrain [from taking a fee]; and whoever is poor - let him consume according to what is acceptable. Then when you release their property to them, bring witnesses upon them. And sufficient is Allah as Accountant' (An-Nisa: 2-6).
Subject of Verses 222 to 231: Above you saw how from Hajj, the issues of Jihad; from Jihad, spending; from spending, gambling and wine; and simultaneously the issues of sympathy for orphans arose one after another. Similarly, the issue of marriage with the mothers of orphans, on one hand, created the occasion for stating some timely issues related to divorce and marriage, and on the other hand, created a very favorable atmosphere for the revelation of certain commands and instructions for the protection of women's rights. The method of the Holy Quran is that when suitable circumstances arise for stating one point, the discourse rains down in a vast circle like rain. Consequently, a very important part of the related issues has been described here. These issues began with the answer to a question regarding menstruation. The importance of this specific question in this series is that this very thing, as will be clear later, sets the boundaries for many issues of marriage and divorce. For this reason, before the original issues, it was necessary to know the Sharia's commands and instructions regarding this thing itself. Now, in this light, observe the following verses.

Rulings of Menstruation: This question regarding menstruation did not arise only from the aspect of whether intimacy with a woman is permissible during this time; intimacy during this time has not only been prohibited in all heavenly religions, but the pagan Arabs also considered it unlawful, and it is mentioned from various angles in their poetry. However, there were many other issues related to it in which great excess and neglect were found—for example, what are the limits of avoiding a woman during this time, what are the etiquettes and conditions of purification for it, and what is its importance in matters of divorce and waiting periods (Iddah), etc.? Especially the latter question was of great importance because its effects fell upon marriage, divorce, Iddah, and inheritance—almost all family issues. For this reason, in this discussion of marriage and divorce, the Quran took up this question first and answered it.
The Limit of Separation: The command given to stay separate (i'tizal) from women during this time—its correct limit is clarified by the subsequent words: 'wa la taqrabuhunna hatta yathurna fa-idha tatat-taharna fa'tuhunna min haythu amarakumullah' (and do not approach them until they are pure. And when they have purified themselves, then come to them from where Allah has ordained for you). This shows that this separation is required only to the extent of the specific marital relationship. It does not mean making the woman a complete untouchable, as in other religions. This point has also been clarified by Hadiths and the practice of the Prophet (PBUH).
In this verse, two words are used: 'yathurna' (they are pure) and 'tatat-taharna' (they have purified themselves). 'Tuhr' means the state of the woman's impurity has ended and the bleeding has stopped. 'Tatat-tahur' means the woman has attained a state of purity by bathing. In the verse, 'Tuhr' is made a condition for intimacy with the woman, and it is simultaneously stated that when they have purified themselves, then come to them. From this, the point emerges that since the original cause for the prohibition of intimacy is the blood, this restriction is lifted after its cessation; however, the correct method is that when the woman has attained purity by bathing, then meet with her.
All Self-Evident Truths of Nature are Components of the Sharia: From 'fa'tuhunna min haythu amarakumullah' (then come to them from where Allah has commanded you), the reality is clarified that all self-evident truths of nature are included in Allah's commands, and from this aspect, they are components of the Divine Sharia, whether they have been explicitly commanded by God in words or not. For example, although it has not been commanded anywhere that a morsel must be put in the mouth and not in the nose or eye, it is nonetheless God's command because the Creator (Fatir) made our nature thus; if someone violates this, they in reality violate a clear—rather, a most clear—command of God, and for this, they will be deserving of punishment with God. We have described it as 'most clear' instead of just 'clear' because Allah Almighty left such matters to our nature solely because nature, due to their clarity, was not in need of any guidance regarding them. Exactly this is the case with the site of intimacy; if someone proves their blindness in this, they are even lower than animals because animals commit no error in this even though they are unacquainted with any Quran or Book.
The Reality of Repentance and Purification: If you reflect on the reality of 'Tawbah' (repentance) and 'Tatat-tahur' (purification), it will be known that 'Tawbah' is the name for purifying one's interior from sins, and 'Tatat-tahur' is for purifying one's exterior from impurities and filth. In this regard, the reality of both is one, and both these traits of a believer are very beloved to Allah Almighty; conversely, those deprived of them are odious to Allah Almighty. In the context in which this point has appeared here, it teaches that those who do not abstain from intimacy during a woman's period of impurity or who exceed the limits of nature in the matter of satisfying lust are extremely odious to Allah. Clarification of this exists in the Hadiths.

'Harath' in Arabic means tilth or field, whether it be of the nature of orchards or other crops.
The Metaphor of Tilth for Women: In the metaphor of tilth for women, one simple aspect is that just as for a field, the code made by nature is that sowing is done in the correct season and at the appropriate time, and seeds are sown within the field, not thrown outside the field—no farmer violates this code—similarly for women, this code of nature is that lust should not be satisfied with them during the days of menstruation or in any improper site, because the time of menstruation is a time of the woman's 'bath' and unreadiness, and intimacy in an improper site is a cause of hurt and waste. For this reason, its commission is not permissible for any person of sound nature. From this aspect, this verse became, as it were, a further explanation of the previous verse.
The Limits of Freedom and Restriction: In 'fa'tu harthakum anna shi'tum' (so come to your tilth however you wish), there is a hint toward two things simultaneously. One, toward that freedom, lack of formality, and autonomy which the owner of an orchard or field possesses regarding their orchard or field. Second, toward that restriction, responsibility, and caution which an orchardist or farmer observes regarding their orchard or field. The word 'Harath' points toward this second thing, and the words 'anna shi'tum' point toward the first thing. That freedom and this restriction—these two things together determine the attitude a husband should adopt regarding his wife.
Everyone knows that the entire peace and joy of marital life lies in the parties' satisfaction that there is no restriction, no bond, and no monitoring over the freedoms of their privacy except for a few broad limits of nature. There is great ecstasy and great intoxication in this feeling of freedom. When a human enters this garden of their pleasure and joy, nature wants them to be intoxicated with this feeling, but simultaneously nature has placed this reality before them: that this is not a jungle but their own garden, and this is not a wasteland but their own tilth; therefore, they may come into it a hundred times and come in whatever glory, whatever moment, whatever direction, and whatever aspect they wish, but they must remember the garden being a garden and the tilth being a tilth; let there be no heedlessness of this reality in any of their comings. Regarding their tilth, every farmer's heartfelt desire is that they consistently obtain an extremely good crop from it, that it be plowed at appropriate times, that it receive fertilizer and water according to need, that it remain protected from seasonal calamities, that future rodents, beasts, birds, enemies, and thieves cannot harm it, that when they see it, its freshness and lushness make them happy, and when the time comes, it fills their lap with its fruits and flowers.
The Absurdity of the Theory of Family Planning: The Quran gathered all these points in the metaphor of tilth for women, and this metaphor has cut the very root of the theory of those who run family planning schemes. This is because regarding a field, the guidance as to how to obtain the maximum and best production from it can be considered rational, but it is completely illogical to teach people lessons that they should sow the maximum seeds but obtain the minimum crop. This kind of irrational logic can only occur to the ignorant.
'wa qaddimu li-anfusikum' (and send forward for yourselves) means that just as the purpose of an earthly field is that you arrange your future livelihood from it, similarly the original goal of the woman's field is that you can secure your place in the future of the human race and play your part in its establishment and survival. After 'fa'tu harthakum anna shi'tum', the addition of these words placed this reality in extremely clear terms: the original goal of union with a woman is the survival of the progeny; pleasure is only its incidental benefit. Therefore, every method that wastes this objective or harms it—even if the demands of pleasure are fulfilled by it—is completely contrary to the nature created by the Creator and its requirements.
It should be remembered here that just as a human secures a place for themselves within the human race through their children, similarly through them—if they can train them well—they also consistently increase their capital in the Hereafter, because the goodness of a righteous child is a 'continuous charity' (khayr-e-jariyah) whose series continues even after the person's death. Proof of this exists in the Hadiths. Both these aspects are present in 'qaddimu li-anfusikum'.
Some interpreters have interpreted this differently, but in our view, only this interpretation is correct. Quranic parallels support this. In this same Surah, it is stated elsewhere: '...so now have relations with them and seek that which Allah has decreed for you' (Al-Baqarah: 187). The interpretation of this verse passed above.
'wattaqullaha wa'lamu annakum mulaquh' (and fear Allah and know that you will meet Him) means that although you can break these laws of nature and these limits of Allah today in both privacy and public and you have been given respite for it, remember that one day you must face God, whose eyes are watching you everywhere and from whose grasp no one will be able to save you. Along with this threat, glad tidings were also given to those believers who, despite all the temptations of the soul, remember this matter: that one day they must be present before their Lord. 'wa bash-shiril-mu'minin' (and give good tidings to the believers).

The Meaning of Making Allah a Target for Oaths: 'Urdhah' means a target or mark. Making Allah a target for oaths means that unnecessary and meaningless oaths are taken in His name, or oaths that are against goodness, Taqwa, and the objective of reform. Using God's great name for meaningless oaths is exactly like someone firing a cannon to kill a mosquito; and using His holy name for oaths against goodness and Taqwa is effectively cutting the root of goodness and Taqwa—which is the fountainhead of all goodness and all virtue—using His own name.
In the Arabic language, in some circumstances, a 'mudhaf' (possessive noun) is omitted before 'an', and in some instances, the negative particle 'la' is omitted after it. This omission is understood from the context. Here, there is a clear clue that 'la' is omitted after 'an'. Examples of this are many in the Quran. The teacher-imam has collected its evidences in his book 'Al-Asalib'.
The Meaning of 'Birr', 'Taqwa', and 'Islah': The three words 'Birr', 'Taqwa', and 'Islah' have gathered all types of goodness and virtue here. "Birr" encompasses all those virtues related to parents, relatives, the poor, orphans, and other rights of servants ('huquq al-'ibad'); "Taqwa" encompasses those virtues related to the rights of Allah; and "Islah" refers to those virtues related to society.
This verse is a preamble to the issues described next. Further on, 'Ila' is mentioned, followed by some important issues related to marriage and divorce. 'Ila' refers to the oath a person takes not to have marital relations with their wife. Since an oath is the means of confidence and stability in all social, societal, and political matters and treaties, and through it the highest objectives of civilization are fulfilled, therefore, before the issues of 'Ila' and marriage/divorce, to clarify the importance of the oath itself, it was stated: it is never permissible to use God's name for such oaths that are contrary to goodness, Taqwa, and reform. Taking an oath by God means making Him a witness; if someone takes an oath in His name for the opposition of goodness, truth, or justice, it means they want to make God a witness against God Himself and in favor of Satan. All such oaths are synonymous with mocking God; therefore, such oaths are not permissible in the first place, but if someone happens to take one, Islam has commanded to break it.
At the end, in the reference to 'Sami'' and 'Alim', there is a tone of threat: those who make God's holy name a target for the practice of oppression in this way should not forget that God is All-Hearing and All-Knowing; He will not remain without punishing such audacious ones.

Intentional and Unintentional Oaths: Meaning, only those oaths are exempt from accountability which are uttered by the tongue completely unintentionally, which relate not to the heart but merely to the tongue, which are not taken keeping some benefit or loss in view but merely drop from the tongue during speech as a filler (sukhan-takiya). But the oaths that are the result of the heart's aim and intention and the soul's deliberation, and which will have some near or distant effect on the person's own or others' rights and interests—if God's name is used wrongly in them, Allah Almighty will certainly hold them accountable for those oaths.
Although "idle" (laghw) oaths are kept exempt from accountability because God is Forgiving and Forbearing, describing them with the word "laghw" clarified that it is mandatory for reliable and serious people to abstain from them as well. Among the attributes of noble and civilized people described in the Quran, it is specifically mentioned that they abstain from idle (laghw) things.

Exegesis of Verses 226 to 227: Rulings of 'Ila': After the aforementioned preamble regarding oaths, a Shari issue is now being described in which the oath holds the status of the primary factor. This is the issue of 'Ila'. "Ila" is in the 'If'al' pattern from 'ala ya'lu'. The original literal meaning of 'ala ya'lu' is to fall short or be deficient in a matter, and 'Ila' means to take an oath to abandon something. This is a terminology of the pagan Arabs whose meaning is to take an oath not to have a marital relationship with the wife. Since the subject of abandonment is present within this word itself, there was no need to join any other word with it to convey the meaning of severing ties.
Since this type of oath is contrary to marital objectives and contrary to 'Birr' and Taqwa—as the wife is left completely suspended because of it—Islam appointed a limit of four months for those who take such an oath: within this time, they must either restore marital relations with the wife or, if the decision is to divorce, then divorce her. Regarding those who adopt the first form, it was stated that Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. Meaning: although their oath was based on a deprivation of rights, and it is not permissible to make an oath a shield for any deprivation of rights, after the reform of the state, Allah Almighty will forgive this shortcoming. Although no expiation (kaffarah) is mentioned here for breaking this oath, given the general code the Quran described in another place regarding the breaking of oaths, there is no reason for this situation to be exempt. For this reason, we consider the opinion of those jurists who favor expiation in this case too as stronger.
Regarding the second group, it was stated that if they have decided on divorce, they can adopt this path, but the limits and restrictions Allah has appointed in this matter must be fully monitored. Allah hears and knows everything.
A question arises here: what will happen if the aforementioned period of four months passes and during this time a person neither returns nor divorces? One group of jurists answers this question by saying that as soon as the four-month period passes, one divorce will occur automatically; according to some, this will be an irrevocable (ba'in) divorce, and according to others, a revocable (raj'i) one. The second group says that upon the passing of the four-month period, the matter will be presented in the judge's (qadi) court, and he will order the husband to either return or divorce. What emerges from the Quran's words is that upon the passing of four months, the husband must nonetheless make a decision to either return or divorce. If he does neither of these two things, the woman will obtain a divorce from such a husband through the court.
It is also apparent from the Quran's words that the woman will have this right to obtain a divorce only in the case where the husband took the oath not to meet the wife based on malice and hatred and intends to keep her suspended. If this is not the case but rather due to some other temporary and provisional benefit—whether required by health or with the intention of reprimand—someone has severed the specific marital relationship, then this situation does not fall under this ruling even if the duration of this severance exceeds four months.

The Meaning of 'Quru'': "Quru'" is the plural of 'qur''. Linguists have differed in determining its meaning. Some have taken its meaning as menstruation (hayd) and some as purity (tuhr). From as much as we have reflected on its original root and its derivatives, our inclination is toward the fact that its original meaning is indeed menstruation; but since every menstruation is necessarily followed by purity, in common parlance it is also used to denote purity—just as the word 'night' is used for the day attached to it or the word 'day' for the night attached to it.
