Examples of this type of usage can be found in every language.
The outward clue of the issue described here also points to the fact that 'quru'' refers to menstruation. This is because the original wisdom of the waiting period (tawaqquf) instructed for divorced women in the verse—as is clear from the verse itself—is to determine that they are not pregnant. Obviously, the decision of being pregnant or not is primarily made through menstruation, not purity. For this reason, taking it in the sense of menstruation is closer. Due to this difference in meaning, a disagreement occurred between the Hanafis and Shafi'is in determining the duration of the Iddah, which is mentioned in the books of Fiqh.
The Wisdom of the Divorce Period: Since the original objective of this period of three menstruations is to determine whether the woman is pregnant or not—because many important matters depend on this—it was made a mandatory demand of these divorced women's faith and Islam that if they feel anything like pregnancy, they should not try to hide it; otherwise, it will severely harm all those benefits which the Sharia has kept in view in this command for the woman, the man, and the child in the womb. During this period, the husband has the right that if he seeks reconciliation and restoration of relations, he can perform 'mura-ja'at' (return). In the Sharia, the relationship of husband and wife is given great importance; its breaking is tolerated only when no possibility of reconciliation remains. For this reason, this period was kept, in which among other benefits is the benefit that if the cause of divorce was some temporary anger, the parties can reconsider their matter with a calm heart and peace of mind. But along with this right of return for the husband, the Quran also attached the condition that this must only be with the intention of reform—meaning to live the marital life with pleasantness and love—and must absolutely not be to distress and harass the woman; otherwise, it will be an extremely oppressive use of this right which causes God's displeasure.
Husband and Wife Both Have Rights: Following this, the rights of both the woman and the man over each other were clarified in very comprehensive words: husbands should not think that rights belong only to them and wives have no right at all; rather, just as there are duties and responsibilities upon them regarding husbands, similarly according to custom (Ma'ruf), there are also rights for them upon husbands, and these duties and these rights are perfectly balanced. It is the duty of every husband to consider the wife's rights alongside the demand for his own rights; the union of husband and wife and the pleasantness of their marital life depend on this consideration.
The Man is the 'Qawwam' (Maintainer) of the Home: However, the fact is that from the viewpoint of the survival and stability of the family system, Islam has given the man one degree of preference over the woman. This preference means, as is clear from the clarifications of other places in the Quran, that Islam has made the man, not the woman, the 'Qawwam' (maintainer) and guardian of the family. Just as the administration of a state is in need of the leadership of a head, similarly on a small scale, the administration of a home is in need of the 'Qawwam-ship' of a maintainer, and for this 'Qawwam-ship', the man is suitable according to his nature and his capabilities, not the woman. The Quran has provided the reasoning for the man's preference elsewhere; therefore, that discussion will be more appropriate in its own place. The thing to which we wish to draw attention here is these words of the Quran: 'wa lir-rijali 'alayhinna darajah'. Their meaning is obviously that "men have a degree of preference over them." In the presence of these clear words of the Quran, there seems to be no room for a Muslim to believe in the theory of the equality of man and woman that has been imported to us from the West. The Quran does accept that the woman has rights of the same weight as her responsibilities, but it does not accept that the woman and man are perfectly equal from every aspect; rather, in clear words, it gives the man one degree of preference over the woman. It should also be noted that the statement 'wa lahunna mithlul-ladhi 'alayhinna bil-ma'ruf' also absolutely does not mean that the rights of woman and man are equal; rather, its clear meaning is that just as there are responsibilities upon the woman, there are also rights for her. By making this principle of 'wa lir-rijali 'alayhinna darajah' the foundation, the Quran gave the position of 'Qawwam-ship' and leadership in the family to the man, as we hinted above, and then it based all family laws and commands upon this. If an attempt is made to understand and mold Islam's family laws on the basis of the Western theory of equality—which claims to keep both woman and man in the same degree from every aspect—by demolishing this foundation, the result of this attempt can be nothing other than the entire religion becoming distorted.
Explanation of 'Aziz' and 'Hakim': At the end of the verse is a reference to two attributes of God: Aziz and Hakim. God is 'Aziz' (Mighty), therefore He alone has the right to command; and He is 'Hakim' (Wise), therefore whatever command He has given is based entirely on wisdom. The task of the servants is to obey His commands without hesitation. If they oppose His commands, they will challenge His honor and pride and invite His punishment; and if they fall into the obsession of being wiser and more cognizant of benefit than God, they will ruin their own law and system with their own hands.

The Correct Method of Divorce: Now the correct method of divorce is stated: since the foundation of all social life is on the pure relationship of marriage, if the turn for its breaking comes due to some compulsion, it should not be that a person breaks this sacred relationship in a single jolt. Rather, just as there is an instruction for the divorced woman to wait for three menstruations, there is an instruction for the one giving divorce to give divorce in two separate periods of purity (tuhr) in two instances, and then in the third period of purity, either perform 'mura-ja'at' (return) with the wife if he wishes to return, or release her if his final decision is to release her. In the case of return, he is instructed to adhere to 'Ma'ruf' (custom)—meaning the purpose of this return should be to keep her as a wife in the way a noble, civilized, and God-fearing man keeps a wife and which is the practice among good people; the purpose should not be to keep the wife suspended and to cause her pain. In the case of releasing her, he is instructed to 'Ihsan' (excellence)—that although she no longer has any right as a wife, the glory of a man's manhood and chivalry is that he releases her beautifully by giving her something according to his means, with whom he has had links of affection and love and who is also from the 'weaker sex.'
Just as there are many benefits in the divorced woman's waiting for three menstruations, there are many blessings for the ones giving divorce in giving divorce according to the aforementioned sequence, from which those people are deprived who, in a state of anger and passion, do not follow this instruction of the Sharia and give three or more divorces in a single breath. Such people generally regret their action for the rest of their lives, but their regret is completely useless. The Sharia stated this method precisely because the marital bond is an extremely important bond; its remaining joined as much as possible is desired, not its breaking. For this reason, any decision regarding it should not be in anger or haste but should be after thinking and with a calm heart, and this is possible only when the aforementioned instruction is followed.
'wa la yahillu lakum an ta'khudhu mimma ataytumuhunna shay'a' (And it is not lawful for you to take anything of what you have given them): It is apparent that things of the nature of maintenance and dowry (mahr) etc. cannot be intended because these things are the woman's right; the question of taking them back or returning them does not even arise. Therefore, it necessarily refers to those things given as gifts, etc. Regarding these things, it was stated that after the divorce has occurred, it is not becoming for the man to sit down and calculate them. The reason for this prohibition, as we hinted above, is that this type of meanness is contrary to the chivalry and high-mindedness that should be in a man. Consequently, regarding women, the Quran has drawn men's attention toward this chivalry in more than one place, especially in the case of the severance of relations. For example: '...and do not distress them in order to take back part of what you have given them...' (An-Nisa: 19). Elsewhere: 'And how could you take it while you have gone in unto each other and they have taken from you a solemn covenant?' (An-Nisa: 21). And further on in this Surah Al-Baqarah, this verse is coming addressing men: '...and to forego is nearer to Taqwa. And do not forget graciousness between you' (Al-Baqarah: 237).
Rulings of 'Khula'': Following this, the form that is exempt from this prohibition is described: this is the form when the wife also has such a disagreement with the husband that it is clearly seen that the limits and restrictions necessary for maintaining marital life cannot be observed by the parties; then there is no harm in the wife giving some property or amount as a ransom (fidyah) to obtain release from such a husband. In the terminology of Sharia, this is called 'Khula''. In this case, since the dominant benefit is the woman's, the permission to take this compensation was given despite her being the weaker element.
Two things become prominent regarding this 'Khula'' from the Quran's words. 1. One is that if the husband and wife cannot settle the matter between themselves, the woman must necessarily take this matter to court, and the court will decide both the 'Khula'' and the compensation. The proof for this is found in 'fa-in khiftum alla yuqima hududallah' (then if you fear that they will not keep the limits of Allah...). In this, the address 'khiftum' (you fear) is obviously to the Islamic society as a collective, and the society's intervention in transactions and disputes is possible only through the medium of the court. 2. Second is that the woman has the right to demand 'Khula'' or the annulment of marriage only in the case where it can be proven that in the marital life, the man is not capable of establishing the limits of Allah (Hudud Allah) or does not want to establish them, and it is impossible or difficult for the woman to remain upon the limits of Allah without their establishment. The proof for this is found in 'alla yuqima hududallah'. If the disagreement is merely of a matter of taste and superficial nature which can be endured, then in such a case, the woman should not rise with a demand for 'Khula'' or annulment. If the path to using this right were opened for the woman even over small things, it would shake the joints of the family system, whereas this very system of the family has been made the foundation of the political system in Islam. Due to this importance of the matter, in the case of a demand for 'Khula'' or annulment, the court will see whether the state of the matter is truly such that maintenance is impossible or difficult for the parties, or if it is merely a desire to 'change shoulders' or change taste, under which the woman has dragged the man to court.
'tilka hududullahi...': This relates to all those commands and instructions described from verse 222 to here. It stated: these are God's boundaries regarding your marital life. Just as you make boundaries around your plots and your pastures and do not want anyone to break these boundaries—and if someone interferes in these limits, you consider it an interference in your ownership and a challenge to your honor and pride—similarly, God too has established these boundaries around His prohibitions. You are free outside them, but you are not permitted to interfere within them. If someone has the audacity to break or cross these boundaries, then let them remember that those are the wrongdoers. Meaning: whatever comes before them in this world or the Hereafter as a result of it, its entire responsibility is upon themselves, not on God, and through it they will wrong only their own souls and not harm God at all. God's laws are based entirely on the demands of human nature and the servants' own benefits; therefore, those who break them scatter the shreds of their own nature and their own benefits with their own hands.

A Wise Restriction: If someone wants to marry that woman again after giving the final [third] divorce, the ruling for it is described: until that woman marries another husband and he divorces her, this woman cannot be lawful for her first husband. Just as the previous command was given so that the decision of divorce is not made in anger or haste, similarly the purpose of this restriction is to save divorce from being made an easy game. If the freedom to marry that woman remained for the one giving divorce even after the divorce, many people would not understand the true importance of divorce. But when this restriction was placed—that the divorced wife can be obtained again only in the case where she becomes someone else's wife and he leaves her for some reason and the woman agrees to marry him [the first husband]—then effectively a whole 'seven-labors' (haft-khwan) became an obstacle in between. Obviously, after this restriction has come forward, whoever gives divorce will think a hundred times before doing so, and the intent of Islam is that whoever gives divorce should give it after thinking well, keeping all results in view far ahead.
The word 'Nikah' in the sense of the Marriage Contract: In 'hatta tankiha zawjan ghayrahu', the word 'Nikah' in our view is in the sense of the marriage contract ('aqd-e-nikah) itself. Those who have taken it in the sense of sexual intercourse (wat'i) have performed an unnecessary affectation. Aside from the fact that taking this meaning does not achieve the purpose they want to achieve, the way this word is used here is refusing this meaning. Here, the subject (fa'il) of 'tankiha' is obviously the woman; if its meaning were taken as 'wat'i', the translation would be "until that woman performs wat'i with another husband." Performing 'wat'i' is the man's task, not the woman's. And if the translation were "until she has wat'i performed by another husband," where will the proof for this rare meaning be brought from?
The Original Purpose of Nikah: The fact is that the word 'Nikah' is a well-known terminology of the Islamic Sharia which applies to that marital treaty between a woman and a man which is made with the intention of lifelong maintenance for living as husband and wife. If this intention is not found within a marriage, then in reality it is not a marriage but a conspiracy that a woman and a man have made together. The room the Sharia has kept for divorce alongside marriage is not a part of the original scheme but a forced remedy for the occurrence of some sudden disaster. For this reason, the original nature of marriage is that it comes into action with the intention of lifelong union. If a marriage is explicitly only for a determined and specific duration, it is called 'Mut'ah', and 'Mut'ah' is strictly Haram in Islam. Similarly, if someone marries a woman with the intention that after this marriage, by giving divorce, he will provide a pretext for this woman to become lawful for her first husband, then in the terminology of Sharia this is 'Halala', and this too is Haram in Islam just like 'Mut'ah'. The person who performs this vile act for someone's purpose-fulfillment is in reality playing the role of a pimp or, as stated in the Hadith, a "borrowed bull," and the curse of Allah is upon the one who does this and the one for whom it is done.
The Difference between 'Mut'ah' and 'Halala': However, alongside this commonality between 'Mut'ah' and 'Halala', there is also a slight difference. It is that 'Mut'ah' is explicitly for a determined duration; therefore, a jurist (faqih) can clearly apply the ruling that this marriage was not contracted. But the nature of 'Halala' is that of a hidden conspiracy; no outward proof exists for the fact that in the name of marriage, this was a mockery made with Allah's Sharia. For this reason, with Allah, this marriage and this divorce will all be void, but a jurist who is forced to give a fatwa by keeping only the outward circumstances in view cannot say that this type of marriage was not contracted at all. Consequently, on this basis, some jurists accept its contraction, and this statement of theirs seems strong to me.
An Unauthorized Point: As for the point that such a woman will be lawful for her first husband only in the case where her second husband divorces her after 'wat'i' (intercourse), at least for this 'wat'i', no proof emerges from the Quran. The baselessness of the argument given from the word 'tankiha' is perfectly clear, as we hinted above. Then, the thing to see is also this: what reduction in the gravity and accursedness of 'Halala' will occur by the act of 'wat'i' having taken place? If a marriage has occurred under the conspiracy of 'Halala', what difference does it make to its nature whether the divorce was given before 'wat'i' or after 'wat'i'? If given without 'wat'i', it is pimping; and if given after 'wat'i', consider such a person in the words of the Hadith a "borrowed bull"; in any case, in both forms, this drama of marriage and divorce was a mockery with the Divine Sharia. The teaching given to us in this verse is that the woman should in reality enter the bond of marriage with another husband with the intention of lifelong maintenance, and this second husband should divorce her under some such compulsion under which a person divorces his wife. If this form occurs, then undoubtedly this woman can marry her first husband. But if some conspiracy has a part in this marriage and divorce, then this marriage and divorce and all its participants are accursed and under wrath with Allah, regardless of whether all this happened after 'wat'i' or without 'wat'i'.
This issue actually arose on the basis of a Hadith; the argument for it from the Quran is merely a 'post-facto' point. But in our view, the argument derived from the Hadith is also extremely weak. By gathering the different paths of the Hadith, the result that emerges is, as we have seen, perfectly in accord with the Quran. If we had not fixed a specific limit for jurisprudential discussions in this book, we would have discussed this Hadith in detail to show what the original reality stated was and what people have made of it; but this discussion is outside our scope.
Further on it was stated that after receiving a divorce from the second husband, there is no harm in the two former spouses being linked in the bond of marriage again, provided they expect that they will be able to establish the limits of Allah. The need for this warning arose because marriage and divorce are not child's play after all. Whenever they come into action, they should come into action only with true intentions and sincere desires for reconciliation.
Finally, it was stated that Allah has well-clarified His appointed boundaries for the people, so that those who seek the knowledge of Divine boundaries value them and avoid the consequences of their violation. We have translated 'ya'lamun' as "those who seek knowledge." The reason is that reflecting on the instances of verb usage in Arabic reveals that just as a verb comes for its outward or initial meaning, or for its perfect and real meaning, it also comes for the 'intention of the action' and the 'seeking of the action', and the distinction between them is made by the occasion of the speech and the context.

The End of Mocking the Divine Sharia:
The duration of waiting for a divorced woman appointed by the Sharia was told in verse 228, and the correct method of divorce was told in verse 229, and it was also clarified that in the third period of purity, either restore marital relations with the wife according to custom or, if this is not acceptable, then release her with excellence and goodness. Now, in this verse, further clarification was made as to what the Sharia's intent is by "retaining according to custom." This intent was clarified such that this retaining must absolutely not be with the intention that the wife remains a prisoner in your grasp of oppression and you can inflict pain upon her according to your desire. After saying the point from the positive aspect above, its clarification from the negative aspect was also made because oppressive people could use the husband's right of divorce and the right of return after divorce for this oppression, even though this is open 'I'tida''—meaning transgression of Allah's limits and making His Sharia a joke. It was stated that those who commit such audacity apparently make a woman a target of oppression, but in reality, they commit the greatest oppression against their own souls because the punishment for jumping over Allah's limits and making His Sharia a joke is very severe.
Finally, it was stated: remember this favor of Allah that He blessed you with the position of a chosen Ummah, sent His Prophet among you for your guidance, and sent down His Book upon you—which is a collection of both law and wisdom—to inform you of good and evil and right and wrong. After receiving such great blessings from Allah, if this is how you fulfilled their right—that you broke God's limits and made His Sharia a joke—then think what the end of such people can be! Then it was stated: fear Allah and know well that He is aware of your every matter; meaning He does give respite to people despite their mischiefs, but when He seizes, no one will be able to escape His grasp.
Here, this point should also be noted: making the Sharia a joke does not only mean that it is openly mocked; rather, an extremely grave form of it is also that from an outward perspective, a task is done such that no objection can be raised against it, but in terms of objective and intent, that task is completely contrary to the Sharia's objective. For example, if someone performs 'mura-ja'at' (return) with his wife in the third period of purity, he holds the right to it according to the Sharia; but if his objective in this is to distress the wife, then it means he opposed Allah under the veil of Allah's verses. Obviously, this is an open joke with Allah and His Sharia.
Subject of the next verses, 232 to 237: Further instructions in the same series as the subject of marriage and divorce described above are being stated next.

The Meaning of 'Adhl': "'Adhl" means to create an obstacle and place hurdles. In 'azwajahunna', 'husbands' refers to their future husbands with whom they desire to marry in the future.
A Divorced Woman should not be Obstructed: A woman who has completed her Iddah after receiving a divorce is free to marry wherever she likes. In her intention, neither the husband who gave the divorce nor his family members should create any obstacle, whether this obstacle is of the nature of an open prohibition or of the nature of internal conspiracy and manipulation. In some families and brotherhoods, this ignorance is found: if a woman has been married among them, then even after she is divorced or her husband dies, these people do not tolerate that such a woman marries elsewhere; they consider it an insult to themselves and place all sorts of hurdles in her path. So much so that because of this, sometimes extremely grave incidents of murder and corruption occur. Just as this ignorance is found in our country, it was also found in Arabia. The Quran stopped this: the one who has divorced a woman no longer has any right to become an obstacle in her path; she may marry wherever and with whomever her matter is settled. If the matter is settled according to custom (Ma'ruf), no one has the right to object to it.
'According to custom' (bil-Ma'ruf) here refers to that practice and custom of the noble Arabs which Islam had purified from bad customs and made a part of the Islamic Sharia, and in many matters, He either instructed people to act upon them or gave them the freedom to act upon them. Here, the condition of 'Ma'ruf' attached to settling the matter aims at this: it is necessary for both the woman and the man that in settling the matter, they do not do anything that is against the traditions of noble families and due to which there is an apprehension of a stain upon the honor and reputation of the former husband, the future husband, or the woman's own family.
It was stated that these advices are being given to those who believe in Allah and the Last Day; meaning, for those in whom faith in God and the Hereafter exists, it is a mandatory demand of their faith that they act upon these advices. Then it was stated that this is a more pure and clean way for you. Meaning: if an obstacle is created in the path of marriage according to the woman's wish, then there are apprehensions of many evils spreading in the family and then in society. It is from here that secret links, then adultery, then many back doors of abduction and escape are created, and one day the 'noses' of all those are cut off who, in the presumption of keeping their nose high, try to stand obstacles of absurd customs against natural emotions. Finally, it was stated: "Allah knows and you do not know"—meaning your knowledge and your sight are very limited; it is very difficult for you to understand all the ups and downs of life; therefore, act upon what you are commanded by God.

Issues related to Breastfeeding (Radha'ah): In this verse, many issues related to breastfeeding have been stated together, which are in order:
1. A divorced woman has the responsibility to suckle her child for two full years if the husband who gave the divorce wants the woman to complete this period of breastfeeding.
2. During this period, the responsibility for the divorced woman's food and clothing is upon the child's father, and in this matter, custom (Ma'ruf) will be considered—meaning the parties will decide what should be given as maintenance by keeping in view the husband's status, the woman's needs, and the local conditions.
3. A burden beyond strength will not be placed on either of the parties; neither will an attempt be made to harm the mother on the pretext of the child, nor will an improper pressure be placed on the father by taking cover behind the child.
4. If the child's father has died, his heir will hold exactly the same position regarding the aforementioned responsibilities and rights.
5. If the woman and man decide to wean the child within the two-year period through mutual consent and consultation, they can do so.
6. If the father or the child's heirs want to have the child suckled by some other woman instead of the child's mother, they are authorized to do so, provided that the agreement made with the child's mother regarding giving and taking is fulfilled.
At the end is the warning: fear Allah and know that whatever you do, you do before God; nothing remains hidden from Him.
The aforementioned matters are for the woman, the man, and the responsible persons of the related families to settle themselves in general circumstances; but if any dispute arises, the councils (panchayats) and courts will decide by keeping these principles in view.

The Iddah (Waiting Period) of a Widow: If a woman's husband dies, the Iddah of such a woman is four months and ten days (and in the case of being pregnant, the delivery of the child). Compared to a general divorcee, this increase in the widow's Iddah is for various benefits such as 'istibra al-rahm' (ensuring the womb is clear), the woman's ease, and mourning, etc. Being the weaker party, tender-hearted, and intensely sensitive, the woman feels the shock of the husband's death more, and in the state of widowhood, she is also in much greater need of sympathy. For this reason, her period of Iddah was kept longer so that alongside the shock of the husband's death, she does not immediately have to suffer the shock of leaving the husband's threshold. Consequently, under this benefit, further on as a further explanation of this same verse, a temporary instruction also occurred: 'And those of you who die and leave wives should bequeath for their wives a provision for a year without driving them out. But if they leave [of their own accord], then there is no blame upon you for what they do with themselves in an acceptable manner. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise' (Al-Baqarah: 240).
After completing the aforementioned Iddah, they are free to take whatever step they consider appropriate in their matter according to custom. After that, there is no blame on the guardians nor is there any blame on them, provided they have not committed any violation of 'Ma'ruf'. The meaning is that one should not unnecessarily make each other a target of taunt and blame by giving non-Shari customs the status of Sharia. Neither should the husband's heirs and the woman's guardians be taunted that the woman had not even finished her full mourning for her husband when they became tired of her, nor should the woman be taunted that the husband's shroud had not even become soiled before she rose to celebrate a wedding. One should only adhere to the limits God has appointed and remember that Allah is aware of every deed of His servants.
The condition of adhering to 'Ma'ruf' (custom) placed for the woman also implies that in the matter of marriage, 'kufu' (compatibility) should also be considered so that no harm reaches the dignity of the related families.

Respect for Emotions in Islamic Society: In Islamic society, the consideration and respect for each other's emotions is of great importance. For this reason, it was prohibited that if someone passes away, it is not permissible for anyone to start 'extending the swings' of marriage (courting) with his widow during her period of Iddah. This is contrary to the emotions that should be within a sensitive and pained brother for his deceased brother, and it is also against the consideration a noble man should have for the emotions of a grieving widow. The society of Muslims is a society of 'merciful among themselves,' not a herd of animals. It was stated that if someone is a seeker of marriage with a widow, they can indeed utter some word as a hint or intend marriage in their heart, but it is not permissible to make a secret promise or contract of marriage. The matter should remain limited to condolence and sympathy, which is well-known for such circumstances; if in the course of this sympathy some word is leaked that gives a hint, there is no harm in it.
'Alimallahu annakum satadhkurunahunna' is an incidental phrase (jumlah mu'taridhah), and its purpose is a warning: do not hold the presumption regarding the hidden intentions of the hearts that they remain hidden from God. God well knows that you will express this intention, so if you express it, do not do so in a way that it takes the form of a promise, contract, or covenant; rather, let it be in that style which is liked for such circumstances and in accord with custom.
The word 'Kitab' (Book), as we have clarified elsewhere, is also used in the Quran for a specific Shari law. Here it refers to that law of the Iddah of four months and ten days which has been described above for a widow. Describing a specific law with the word 'Kitab' clarifies its importance. It was stated: do not resolve upon the marriage contract until the law's duration is complete.
At the end, He referred to His attribute of Knowledge, upon the remembrance of which the correct respect for God's laws is based. And simultaneously He stated: fear God, do not be deceived by His respite; He is Forgiving and Forbearing, which is why He overlooks, but nothing is outside His knowledge.

A Right upon the People of Excellence (Ihsan): In this verse, 'la junaha 'alaykum' (there is no blame upon you) relates to an omitted phrase. The full point is: if the situation is that a person divorces his wedded wife in a state where he has not yet established a marital relationship with her nor has he fixed a dowry (mahr) for her, then in such a case, there is no sin upon him regarding the dowry. Rather, instead of the dowry, he should release her by giving her something according to custom. 'In accord with custom' (bil-Ma'ruf) means that no limit is fixed for it; rather, it depends on a person's standard of living. A poor person should give according to his capacity, and a wealthy person according to his capacity. Yes, it is certain that for those who desire to build and beautify their lives and want to be included in the group of the 'Muhsinin' (people of excellence), this is a 'right' (obligation).

This describes a situation different from the one above. It is that the dowry is fixed, but the divorce was given before the meeting (consummation). In such a case, half of the fixed dowry must be given. However, if the woman waives her right, that is a different matter, or if the man waives his right—meaning he pays the full dowry instead of half. Although a motive for the woman to waive the dowry exists—as the husband gave divorce before the meeting—the Quran urged the man that his chivalry, masculine high-mindedness, and his rank and status demand that he should not be a seeker of the woman's waiver of his right, but should himself step forward in this field of sacrifice. For this sacrifice, the Quran urged the man here from three aspects. First, that God has blessed the man with the excellence that just as he holds the authority to tie the knot of marriage, he is also authorized to loosen it. Second, that sacrifice and selflessness, which are among the highest attributes of Taqwa, are more befitting of the 'stronger sex' compared to the 'weaker sex.' Third, that the degree of preference God has blessed the man with over the woman according to his capabilities—and due to which He made him the 'Qawwam' (maintainer) and head of the woman—is a very great excellence which the man should not forget when dealing with a woman; the natural demand of this excellence is that the man becomes a giver to the woman, not a taker from her.
A Warning for the Social Thinkers of this Era: Here, in the words 'bi-yadihi 'uqdatun-nikah' (in whose hand is the marriage knot), there is another nuance which the social thinkers and reformers of this era should specifically keep in sight. It is that just as the marriage knot is tied by the man's acceptance, it is untied by his divorce; effectively, the Sharia has kept this source-control originally in the man's authority. Therefore, the tendency to give the woman equal authority to the man in the matter of divorce—which is an imitation of the West and is increasing in our Muslim countries—is completely against the Sharia and will result in the entire fabric of the family system being scattered.
Subject of Verses 238 to 242: The chapter of commands and laws that began from verse 163 with the mention of Tawheed and then prayer and zakat now ends with these verses. The arrangement of this group of verses is such that one verse—which holds the status of the actual conclusion of the chapter—relates to guarding the prayers in all kinds of circumstances, fear and security alike; and in two verses are some incidental instructions regarding the widow and the divorcee, which were mentioned in the verses above and were revealed later. These two verses were attached to the conclusion of the chapter so that from their arrangement in the discourse itself, it becomes clear that these verses were revealed as clarifications after the original commands. Consequently, the segment 'kadhalika yubayyinullahu lakum ayatihi' was attached to them to point toward them being explanatory verses, so that the seeker of the discourse's structure faces no difficulty in understanding the connection of the speech.
Prayer is a Fortress for the Entire Religion: Effectively, the actual concluding verse of the chapter is the one: 'hafizhu 'alas-salawati was-salatil-wusta'. Now look at the start of this chapter: you will find that at its beginning, after the mention of Tawheed, the first thing that comes in the series of Sharia commands in verse 177 is prayer and simultaneously zakat. Look here and it will be known that the conclusion of this chapter also occurred on the mention of prayer. This is a hint that the importance prayer holds in this religion is not held by anything else. The establishment and survival of the entire Sharia depend on its establishment and survival. Allah Almighty has given it the status of a fortress and a fence for the establishment and protection of the Sharia. The person who guards it effectively guards the entire Sharia, and the person who creates loopholes in it, as reported from Hazrat Umar (RA), loses the rest of the religion to an even greater degree.
Here, this point should also be noted: the prayer mentioned at the start of the chapter is the well-known five-time prayer of circumstances of peace and tranquility; and here, besides the prayer of peace and tranquility, the prayer of fear and danger is also mentioned. This is a gradual evolution in the description of the commands of prayer along with the change of circumstances. At the time the verses at the start of the chapter were revealed, circumstances of war and Jihad did not exist; but after the change of Qibla, you read that the commands of war and Jihad were described in great detail—in fact, the reality is that the original sequence of discourse that was running was that of Jihad and spending; other issues, as we hinted above, arose incidentally. This change of circumstances required that alongside the prayer of peace, the prayer of fear and danger also be mentioned. Consequently, the mention of the first form of prayer was made with the word 'iqamat-e-salat' (establishing prayer), and the mention of this second state of prayer was made with the words 'muhafizhat 'alas-salawat' (guarding the prayers). The difference in the intensity of care manifest in these two styles of expression cannot be hidden from the people of vision.
The point that prayer is like a fortress and city-wall for the entire religion, although it cannot be hidden from those who reflect on the Quran—as its evidences and parallels are many in the Quran—nonetheless, a general reader might have the doubt that we have used affectation in joining the connection of the speech. For this reason, we reference Surah Al-Mu'minun, in which an extremely clear example of this connection of speech exists. It is stated: 'Certainly will the believers have succeeded: They who are during their prayer humbly submissive... And they who turn away from ill speech... And they who are observant of zakah... And they who guard their private parts... Except from their wives or those their right hands possess, for indeed, they will not be blamed... But whoever seeks beyond that, then those are the transgressors... And they who are to their trusts and their promises attentive... And they who carefully maintain [yuhafizhun] their prayers' (Al-Mu'minun: 1-9).
Reflecting on these verses reveals that the points described here began with prayer, and then after describing a few fundamental points of religion and morals, their conclusion also occurred on prayer. Furthermore, with the first prayer, 'khushu'' (humility) is mentioned—which is the original spirit of prayer—and with this second prayer, 'muhafizhat' (guarding) is referenced—which is a comprehensive expression for all its outward care and from which the hint also emerges that in reality, it is the guarding of prayers that guarantees the protection of other matters of religion.
Exactly the same pattern is in the following verses of Surah Al-Ma'arij: 'Indeed, mankind was created anxious: When evil touches him, impatient, And when good touches him, withholding [of charity], Except the observers of prayer - Those who are constant in their prayer... And those within whose wealth is a known right For the petitioner and the deprived... And those who believe in the Day of Recompense... And those who are fearful of the punishment of their Lord - Indeed, the punishment of their Lord is not that from which one is safe... And those who guard their private parts... Except from their wives or those their right hands possess, for indeed, they will not be blamed... But whoever seeks beyond that, then those are the transgressors... And those who are to their trusts and promises attentive... And those who are in their testimonies upright... And those who [carefully] maintain [yuhafizhun] their prayer' (Al-Ma'arij: 19-34).
Here too, see: the beginning is from prayer and the conclusion is on prayer. Just as a city-wall takes the entire city into its protection, prayer takes all other virtues into its protection, and the purpose of this, as stated above, is to point toward the reality that the guardian of the entire religion is prayer. Whoever guarded it guarded the entire religion, and whoever wasted it wasted the entire religion.
On this very principle, in Surah Al-Baqarah too, this entire chapter related to commands and laws has been surrounded from both front and back by the command of prayer. In this light, now recite the following verses.

Guarding the Prayer: Guarding the prayer includes the monitoring and care of all those things related to its requirements, conditions, etiquettes, and pillars. While explaining 'iqamat-e-salat', we mentioned these things at the start of the book. Here, the word 'muhafizhat' (guarding) in place of 'iqamat' (establishing) points toward a new aspect: that even in difficult and perilous circumstances, it should be guarded by facing all kinds of dangers. Consequently, the mention of 'salat al-khawf' (the prayer of fear) is in the next verse, from which it is clear that even under the shade of swords, the thing a believer does not forget is this.
(Though I remained a captive to the tyrannies of the times... yet I did not remain heedless of your remembrance).
The Meaning of 'Salat al-Wusta': The literal meaning of 'as-salat al-wusta' is the middle prayer, and the style of speech is a clear testimony that this is the mention of the specific after the general. As for the question of what is meant by this specific one, interpreters have differed greatly in its answer. The opinion of most people is that it refers to the Asr prayer. Our own inclination is also toward this statement. In our division of night and day, this prayer holds the status of a prayer situated on the border of both night and day. One could say the Fajr prayer is also situated on a border, but the border on which the Asr prayer is situated is perilous even in general circumstances, and if the circumstances are of war, then it becomes extremely perilous. In general circumstances, it is clearly seen that since at the time of Asr all the day's activities are entering their final stage, it is a time of great 'scramble' for world-seekers: the traveler wants to reach the destination before nightfall, the shopkeeper is in the zeal of making some earning before closing the shop, the employee gets caught up in finishing their assigned duty—to the extent that even players in the fields are so immersed in the plans of their final move and their final game that no awareness of anything else remains.
Now, by analogy to this, consider that if, God forbid, circumstances of war arise, then how much this scramble can increase, especially in that part of the day in which the Asr prayer is situated. For this reason, the Quran commanded the monitoring of general prayers and simultaneously gave special emphasis for the monitoring of the Asr prayer.
As for the question: if the intention was the Asr prayer, why was it not described clearly with the word 'Asr'? The answer is that through the use of this word ['Wusta'], that delicate location of this prayer comes before us due to which it is in need of special monitoring.
It is also worth remembering that this is the prayer regarding which two of the Prophets (PBUT) faced a trial. One was Prophet Solomon (PBUH) at the occasion of a military parade, and the second was our Prophet (PBUH) at the occasion of the Battle of the Trench (Ahzab).
'Qunut' means humility and self-abasement. Its mention here is proof that this etiquette of prayer is also included in the command to guard the prayer.

The Meaning of 'Salat al-Khawf': 'Rijal' is the plural of 'rajil' (walking/pedestrian) and 'rukban' is the plural of 'rakib' (rider). It stated: if the enemy has created a state of danger and it is not possible to perform the prayer with all its conditions and etiquettes, then perform the prayer in whatever state you are—riding or walking. This is the 'guarding' of prayer in circumstances of danger. In another place in the Quran, that form has also been told which can be adopted for establishing congregational prayer in circumstances of danger if it is possible.
Then it stated: when circumstances of peace are available, then remember Allah in the way He has taught you. "Remember Allah" refers to the performance of prayer. The word 'Zikr' (remembrance) has been used for prayer in various places in the Quran. Since the original reality of prayer is indeed remembrance, sometimes its form is described by the original reality so that while adopting the form, the person's eye remains on the original spirit and does not remain stuck only on the form.
The Prophet's (PBUH) Teaching is Exactly Allah's Teaching:
From 'kama 'allamakum ma lam takunu ta'lamun' (as He taught you that which you did not know), this point comes forward very clearly: the Prophet's (PBUH) teaching is exactly Allah Almighty's teaching. This is because the command for prayer occurred in the Quran, but the method of performing it was not told anywhere; this thing became known to the Ummah only through the Prophet's teaching, yet despite this, He said: "as He taught." Now the question is: if the Prophet's teaching is not exactly Allah's teaching, then what is that thing which Allah Almighty described here as His teaching?
While clarifying the verse 'wa yu'allimuhumul-kitaba wal-hikmah', we explained that the concept regarding the Prophet (PBUH) that he came only to recite the Quran is fundamentally wrong. Along with reciting the Quran, you were also commissioned by Allah Almighty for this task: to read and teach the Quran to the people and to thoroughly clarify its implications, hints, wisdoms, and secrets. Since you were commissioned for this task by Allah Almighty Himself, whatever you told and taught the Ummah as a teacher is all under your prophetic duty. It is surprising that in the presence of these clear verses, some people raise baseless arguments regarding the times of prayer and its units (rak'ahs), etc.
The words 'ma lam takunu ta'lamun' are for the expression of favor and kindness. It was a very great grace of Allah Almighty upon the unlettered Arabs that He opened to them those secrets of religion and Sharia which had not been opened to them, nor to those before them, nor to anyone else. Obviously, the only becoming response to this favor and kindness from their side is to value it and not devalue it like the Children of Israel.

The word 'Wasiyyah' is the object (maf'ul) of an omitted verb. 'Mata'an' is the object of 'Wasiyyah', and 'ghayra ikhraj' in our view is in the state of condition (Hal) from 'li-azwajihim'. In the translation, we have tried to clarify this structure of the discourse. If it has not been clarified, attribute it to our shortcoming. In any case, this composition of the speech is what is correct in our view.
A Temporary Command of Will for the Widow: Above in verse 234, the Iddah of widows was described; regarding them, this further instruction was revealed later as a further explanation of the previous verse: that husbands leaving behind widows should bequeath for their widows one year's maintenance and permission for residence in their homes. If during this time the widow leaves the home of her own will and takes some step regarding her second marriage or her residence according to custom, she has the right to it. The heirs of the deceased do not have the right to violate the will.
This instruction for a will occurred because until the time of these verses' revelation, the law of inheritance had not yet been revealed. Consequently, at the start of this same chapter (verse 180), the instruction for a will for parents and relatives has already passed, and we explained there that this command was given temporarily for the time until the inheritance law of Surah An-Nisa was revealed. Under this same code, this instruction regarding widows also occurred: that a will for one year's maintenance and residence be made for them. Obviously, later when the law of inheritance was implemented and the share of the widow or widows was determined in the Sharia like the other heirs of the testator, then just as the aforementioned instruction for a will regarding parents and other heirs was abrogated, it was also abrogated for widows, and its place was taken by the permanent law of inheritance.
If this verse were alongside the previous verse—i.e., verse 234 in which the widow's Iddah was mentioned—no one would have faced trouble in understanding its arrangement; but in that case, it would not have been clear that this verse was revealed after the first command as an explanation of that command. However, this thing is necessary for understanding the graduality of commands and their wisdoms. For this very wisdom, this verse and the verse alongside it, as we hinted above, were placed at the conclusion of the chapter, indicating that these are explanations revealed later.
The attributes 'Aziz' and 'Hakim' point toward God's right of legislation and the wisdom of His law, and also toward the consequences of its violation. In Islam, the foundation of the entire religion, Sharia, and all commands and prohibitions is on God's attributes alone. For this reason, they should nowhere be considered merely for 'decoration' but should everywhere be reflected upon as the foundation of Islam's philosophy of law and philosophy of morals.

The Status of Rights Based on Attributes: Following the instruction in verse 236 to provide for divorced women when releasing them, this is a reminder that characterizes this as a 'right' upon the God-fearing (Muttaqin). Rights based on character and conduct may sometimes fall outside the grasp of worldly law, but with God, these rights serve as the standard for those attributes. If something is declared a right upon the believers, the doers of good, or the pious, it is possible that Islamic law in this world may not seize those who violate it, but this does not mean the violation will have no consequence in the Hereafter. In the Hereafter, a person's faith, excellence (Ihsan), or piety will be judged as weighty or weightless based on the fulfillment or non-fulfillment of these very rights.

The phrase 'Thus Allah makes clear His verses...' is an expression of favor. As clarified elsewhere, this highlights the nature of these verses as further explanations. We have noted that this phrase generally follows verses that provide additional detail or were revealed after people sought further clarification on original commands. Students of the Quran's structure can find valuable guidance in these markers and should keep them in mind.

Subject of Verses 243 to 253: Let us refresh the sequence of the discourse. As mentioned earlier, the primary subject was Jihad and spending (Infaq) in relation to the House of Allah. However, the discussion on spending led to questions about the welfare of orphans and marriage with their mothers, leading to a section on marriage and divorce. Now, the original subject of Jihad and spending resumes.

Summary and Structure of Upcoming Subjects: The upcoming content first references an event concerning the Children of Israel: despite their large numbers, they fled their homeland out of fear of their enemies, thereby choosing moral and political death. Referencing this aims to warn Muslims that their migration (Hijrah) from Makkah to Madinah was not a flight from death or the enemy, but a flight from disbelief and persecution (Fitna). Its true purpose was not merely saving lives but organizing for the help of God's religion and Jihad in His path.

Following this preamble, Muslims are urged toward Jihad and spending. Simultaneously, the war fought by the Children of Israel is mentioned in some detail—a narrative that had been distorted among them but is presented here for the same purpose Muslims are being urged to Jihad: the Children of Israel fought that war for the liberation of their Qibla, just as Muslims were rising for the liberation of theirs.

The trials and tribulations the Children of Israel faced during various stages of that war were highly instructive. It was necessary to relate this history to the Muslims, who were preparing to cross similar stages, so they might learn from it and protect themselves from the pitfalls that could arise.

Subsequently, addressing the Prophet (PBUH), it is stated that the purpose of this history is not mere storytelling; rather, similar situations will confront him, and these events will confirm his prophethood. Despite seeing their own history mirrored in his experience, the Children of Israel would remain obstinate in their opposition. Therefore, there is no need to worry about their opposition; God will grant victory. In this light, recite the following verses.

The address 'Alam tara' (Have you not seen) is not necessarily for a singular person. As explained by the teacher-imam in the Tafseer of Surah Al-Fil, it generally addresses a collective, speaking to every individual within that group. The event referenced is either something the group witnessed or something so famous that the audience is assumed to be aware of it, or the speaker is so confident in the event's truth that it is beyond doubt.

The Meaning of 'Life and Death': As noted under verse 56, 'death' in the Quran is used for the end of physical life, but also for sleep, unconsciousness, and moral or spiritual death. Examples include: 'Allah takes the souls at the time of their death [sleep]' (Az-Zumar); 'Then We raised you after your death [unconsciousness] so that you might be grateful'; 'Indeed, you cannot make the dead hear...' (An-Naml: 80), referring to the dead of heart; and 'Is he who was dead and We gave him life and made for him a light...' (Al-An'am: 122), referring to spiritual life.

Similarly, 'life' ranges from material existence to waking from sleep and attaining moral/spiritual life. A clear example is in Al-Anfal: 'Respond to Allah and to the Messenger when he calls you to that which gives you life' (Al-Anfal: 24).

The Event of 'Those who left their homes': This verse refers to a period in the history of the Children of Israel mentioned in the Book of Samuel. During the early period of the Prophet Samuel, the Israelites were in severe disarray. Though they numbered over three hundred thousand, their religious and moral state was wretched due to innovations and polytheism, and their political state was abysmal due to a lack of social organization. They were so terrified by surrounding enemies, particularly the Philistines, that they lacked the courage to resist. The Philistines massacred them and captured the Ark of the Covenant, which served as their Qibla and was carried at the front of all their worship and expeditions. Out of fear, the Israelites abandoned their cities from Ekron to Gath. This 'death' of fear and cowardice lasted twenty years. Then, Prophet Samuel began the work of reform and renewal, calling them to repent from Shirk and unite. Allah granted success to his call, and after twenty years of 'deadness,' spiritual and political life was revived. They became capable of standing against the Philistines and reclaiming the cities they had fled.

This narrative is extensive in the Book of Samuel (Chapters 4-7). When they chose a life of fear, Allah consigned them to the spiritual and moral death expressed by 'Mutu' (Die). This followed the Sunnah of Allah: 'When they deviated, Allah caused their hearts to deviate.' When the call for renewal arose and they resolved to adopt a new life of faith, Allah revived them, expressed here as 'thumma ahyahum' (then He gave them life). Allah's dealing with nations follows this principle: if a nation prefers disgrace, God consigns them to it; if they seek elevation and show the courage required for it, God grants them honor as a test.

The Purpose of the Narrative: The goal is to urge Muslims, especially the weak-hearted, toward Jihad and spending in Allah's path. This serves as a preamble to the following verses. As noted, this Israelite event concerned a war for their Qibla, just as the Muslims' struggle concerned the liberation of theirs. Both paths—life and death—were open to the Muslims. If they feared death, nothing could save them from the 'death' of disgrace and hypocrisy. If they rose for life, Allah would bless them with a dignified life of faith in this world and eternal success in the Hereafter.

Two Motives for Jihad: Following the historical preamble, this urges Muslims to fight, highlighting two motives. First, this war is 'in the path of Allah,' not for the self or Satan; therefore, the servant has Allah's companionship at every step. Second, Allah is All-Hearing and All-Knowing; your sacrifices, prayers, and the enemy's plots are all in His knowledge. There is no fear that your efforts will go unnoticed. Referencing these attributes implies that since He hears and knows, He will surely help you and grant a full reward for your devotion.

Describing Spending as a 'Loan': After the call to sacrifice life comes the call to sacrifice wealth, using a highly effective style. The question 'Who is it that will lend to Allah...' is inspiring. Describing spending in Allah's path as 'lending' is a great favor from the Generous Lord; the wealth He gave to the servants, He now treats as a loan to Himself when they spend it in His path, making its return mandatory upon Himself. Even more soul-stirring is that He asks for this loan to multiply the servants' 'shards of glass' (worthless offerings) and return them as an eternal treasure. This is not because God's treasury is lacking—He is independent of all—but His generosity has opened this path for servants to earn profit, turning one into seven hundred times the reward.

Meaning of 'Qard Hasan' (A Goodly Loan): The only condition is that it be a 'goodly loan.' From other parts of the Quran, this means: it should not be given with a constricted heart just to get it over with, but with generosity; not for show (Riya), but for God's pleasure; not for worldly gain, but for the Hereafter; and not from low-quality or unlawful wealth, but from beloved and pure earnings.

Finally, the core point is stated: prosperity and hardship do not depend on human strategy but are in God's authority. If one hides wealth from God, they hide it from the One who has the power to take back what He gave. 'And to Him you will be returned' reminds those who hide from God today that they must face Him tomorrow, and that the worldly life they are being stingy for is fleeting compared to the Hereafter.

The word 'Mala' originally means 'to fill.' It evolved to refer to the nobles and chiefs of a nation because they 'fill' the assemblies, councils, and courts. 

Meaning of 'Malik' (King/Leader): 'Malik' refers to one possessing authority and power. This can be absolute, like a tyrant, or limited, like a constitutional monarch or a military commander. The context here indicates the latter, as the Israelite nobles asked their Prophet (Samuel) to appoint one so they could fight under him. Biblical details show he performed his duties under the Prophet's guidance. The Quran uses this term in both praise and blame. It condemned the king who argued with Abraham, but praised David and Solomon. This shows that in a political system, the spirit matters more than the form; if the spirit is subordinate to God's law, it is praiseworthy, whether it is a monarchy or a democracy.

Subject of Verse 246: Just as the previous verse used an event of moral life and death to invite Muslims to sacrifice, this verse and the following ones use Israelite history to teach social and political lessons. The Israelites were surrounded by enemies (Philistines, Moabites, etc.). Samuel was old, and his sons were not leading as expected. The people asked for a commander to lead them in Jihad. Samuel knew their real weakness was a lack of resolve, not a lack of a leader. He warned them that they might refuse to fight once it was ordained. They protested, claiming they were ready because they had been driven from their homes. Samuel's assessment proved correct: when a leader was appointed and Jihad ordained, most turned away. They first objected to the chosen leader and then failed the first test of discipline. 'And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers' implies He will deal with them according to His knowledge of their true nature.

Meaning of 'Ba'atha': It means to raise or send, and here it implies appointing to a mission. Since the selection was made by Samuel according to God's instruction, the word 'ba'atha' is used.

Talut and Saul: 'Talut' is the name of the appointee. In the Torah, he is called Saul. His extraordinary height is mentioned in both: 'when he stood among the people, he was head and shoulders taller than any of them.' It is possible 'Talut' (meaning 'the tall one') was a title. The Quran often corrects or clarifies Biblical narratives; in cases of difference, the Quranic account is preferred for its logical consistency and purpose.

Israelites' Objection: Instead of accepting him, they objected that he was not wealthy. Talut was from the tribe of Benjamin, the smallest tribe, and from its humblest family. Saul himself acknowledged this humility in the Bible. The Israelite tribes, proud of their status and wealth, looked down on him. 

Response to the Objection: Prophet Samuel replied that the choice was God's. Humans measure leadership by wealth and numbers, but God measures by knowledge and physical/practical strength. Talut possessed the 'breadth of knowledge' and 'strength of body' required for leadership. Authority is a gift from God, given according to His wisdom. 'Wasi'un 'Alim' (All-Encompassing, All-Knowing) implies that while humans have narrow views, God's decisions are based on His infinite power and knowledge.

The Reality of the 'Tabut' (Ark): 'Tabut' means a chest or ark. Here it refers to the Ark of the Covenant. From the Exodus until the building of Solomon's Temple, this Ark served as the Israelites' Qibla. They kept it in a special place in the Tabernacle and turned toward it in prayer. It was their source of 'Sakinah' (tranquility and morale) during national calamities and wars. It contained the Torah and relics of Moses and Aaron.

The Return of the Ark: We mentioned its capture by the Philistines and the Israelites' mourning over the loss of their 'glory.' Reclaiming it was their biggest issue. Samuel made its return a sign of Talut's divine appointment. The Philistines, plagued by disasters after capturing it, eventually placed it on a cart pulled by two cows and sent it back toward Israelite territory. The cart, moving without a driver or guard and leaving its calves behind, reached its destination—an event that could only happen through the guidance of 'Malā'ikah' (angels). 

Differences between Torah and Quran: The Quran places the return of the Ark at the time of Talut's appointment as a sign of his kingship. The Torah claims it was returned much earlier because it brought plagues upon the Philistines. The Quranic account is preferred because: 1. It is presented as 'Truth' (bil-Haqq) and a proof of Muhammad's (PBUH) messengership, correcting previous distortions. 2. It is logically consistent; the return of the Qibla is a fitting sign for a new era of victory under a chosen leader. 3. The Torah's own accounts are contradictory, mentioning the nation 'lamenting after the Lord' for twenty years even while the Ark was supposedly back in their territory. The Quran clarifies the true aspects of these events.

'Fasala' means to set out or depart. Talut set out with his army to face the Philistines led by Jalut (Goliath). The battle formation described in the Bible (two armies on opposite hills with a valley between) mirrors the formation at the Battle of Badr. This suggests that God showed Muslims the map of Badr through the story of Talut. 

The Test of Obedience: Before facing the enemy, Talut tested his army's discipline. He announced that God would test them at a river: whoever drank freely would not be his companion; only those who abstained or took a single handful were true followers. Most failed and drank their fill. This test was necessary to sift the sincere from the weak-hearted before the heat of battle. 

'They said: We have no strength today against Jalut and his hosts.' This was the cry of those who failed the test and collapsed at the river. The mention of Jalut's name shows how much his reputation terrified them. 

Victory depends on Faith, not Numbers: 'Those who were certain they would meet Allah' encouraged their discouraged companions. True courage comes from the belief that those killed in God's path are not dead but meet their Lord. They reminded the army that small groups have often overcome large ones by Allah's leave. Success requires patience and resolve, not just numerical superiority. Jonathan, Talut's son, likely uttered the famous biblical phrase: 'there is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few.'

Differences in the Test: The Torah mentions a similar test involving honey and a fast, but it portrays the entire army, including Jonathan, as failing or being misled. The Quranic account is more purposeful: it shows the test occurred *before* the encounter to sift the army, and it confirms that a faithful group remained steadfast, leading to victory. The Quranic version is a meaningful lesson, whereas the biblical version is a disjointed narrative.

Jalut (Goliath) was the Philistine commander, a giant and expert warrior who terrified the Israelites. 

The Beginning of David's (Dawud) Life: This is the same David whom Allah exalted to prophethood and from whom Solomon was born. His beginning was humble—a shepherd—but his end was magnificent. He was part of Talut's army. One tradition says he was already a shield-bearer; another says he arrived just to bring food to his brothers and saw Goliath's challenge. Moved by honor, he asked for permission to fight. Talut was hesitant due to David's youth, but David spoke of killing lions and bears to protect his sheep. Talut armed him, but David felt restricted by the heavy armor. He took only his sling and some stones. Goliath mocked him, but David replied with faith. David struck Goliath's forehead with a stone from his sling, killing him instantly. This led to the Philistine rout and the beginning of David's rise in Israelite history.

'So they defeated them by Allah's permission' expresses the reality that victory or defeat depends on Allah, not just numbers or resources. This doesn't negate using means, but negates relying solely on them. David, though not yet a prophet, knew this, telling Goliath: 'the battle is the LORD's.' This is the same truth found in the verse: 'You did not throw when you threw, but Allah threw.'

'And Allah gave him kingship and wisdom and taught him what He willed.' This describes the rewards David received: he became Talut's son-in-law and eventually the King of Israel. He was granted the wisdom manifested in the Psalms (Zabur). This combination of kingship and wisdom is the true 'Caliphate' on earth. David, Solomon, Abu Bakr, and Umar were 'dervish kings' whose crowns were adorned with wisdom, not just gold. The phrase 'taught him what He willed' (mimma yasha') implies a Sunnah of Allah—He teaches such servants what He deems fit for them.

Philosophy of Jihad: 'And if it were not for Allah checking [defending] some people by means of others...' explains that without Jihad and the struggle of the righteous to cleanse the earth of corruption, the world would be filled with evil. This is also mentioned in Surah Al-Hajj (verse 40). This refutes the monastic view that war is contrary to piety. Jihad by the righteous is for establishing justice and is a great favor from God to the inhabitants of the earth.

Confirmation of the Prophet's (PBUH) Messengership: This verse and the next are a digression (Iltifat) addressing the Prophet (PBUH). It states that the Israelites had made their history meaningless, but God has related it to him with 'Truth' (Haqq), including its proper lessons and wisdoms. This is a bright proof of his messengership: he could not have known these ancient stories with such accuracy and logical sequence except through Divine revelation. If the People of the Book were fair, this alone would suffice as proof, but their blind prejudice prevents them from accepting any messenger other than their own. God has granted different excellences to different messengers, but the People of the Book have turned this into a source of sectarianism and conflict. Muslims should remain patient; evil is given respite in this world by God's wisdom.

'Tilka' refers to the messengers mentioned in the previous verse. 

The Correct Attitude toward Messengers: This verse explains the correct attitude nations should have had toward God's messengers. Instead, they adopted a wrong attitude, building walls of prejudice and falling into mutual conflict. This explains why the People of the Book were so intensely opposing the Prophet (PBUH). 

Allah has blessed every messenger with some specific excellence. For example, He spoke directly to Moses, and He gave clear miracles to Jesus and supported him with the Holy Spirit (Gabriel). This is the correct perspective. However, the followers of these prophets often claimed that all excellences belonged *only* to their specific prophet, viewing the recognition of any excellence in another as contrary to faith. This narrow-mindedness caused each nation to shut itself in a shell, depriving itself of the blessings of other prophets and the final guidance of the Quran. 

Sunnah of Allah regarding Guidance: The second part of the verse points to the Sunnah of Allah regarding guidance and misguidance: He does not use compulsion (jabr). If He did, no one could choose disbelief. Instead, He gave humans the freedom of choice. Those who choose faith are rewarded; those who choose disbelief face the consequences. 'But Allah does what He intends' implies that this lack of compulsion is based on His wisdom. This was to console the Prophet (PBUH): his responsibility is only to deliver the truth clearly; the choice to accept or reject lies with the people.

The Logic and Ease of Spending: This provides further detail to the call for spending in verse 245. 'From what We have provided you' contains both the logic and the ease of spending: God is not asking for something that is yours, but for a portion of what He Himself gave you. 

It then states that the only eternal benefit of worldly wealth is to spend it in God's path today, converting it into an eternal treasure. On the Day that is coming, only the good deeds earned in this world will be of use. In this world, trade, friendships, and intercessions might work, but in that world, those paths are closed; it is a world where only the results of faith and righteous deeds manifest. 

'Al-Kafirun' (the disbelievers) here refers to those who deny the coming of such a Day. Those who deny the Hereafter cannot sacrifice the 'cash' of today for the 'credit' of tomorrow. Though they think they are being realistic and beneficial to themselves, they are actually the greatest wrongdoers against their own souls.

Meaning of 'Qayyum': It is an intensive form meaning the Being who is self-subsisting and the means for the subsistence of all others. 

'Sinah' means drowsiness and 'Nawm' means sleep. Negating both means Allah is entirely free from all effects of heedlessness. 

'What is before them and what is behind them' means Allah's knowledge encompasses their past and future. In contrast, others only know what God wills them to know. 

Meaning of 'Kursi': Lexically, it refers to a foundation or a seat/throne. Since a throne is the center of a ruler's power, 'Kursi' is used to denote authority. 'His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth' means His authority encompasses every corner of the universe. 

'Ada ya'udu' means for something to be so heavy that it is difficult to manage. 'And their preservation tires Him not' means looking after the universe is not a burden for God; He needs no helper.

Ayat al-Kursi is a Great Verse of Tawheed: The previous verse urged spending before the Day when there is no intercession; this verse provides the theological detail. The rejection of false intercession and Shirk led to this magnificent description of pure Tawheed. 

First, it affirms that Allah is the only Deity. Then it affirms the attributes necessary for Divinity (Al-Hayy, Al-Qayyum). One who is not alive cannot give life, and one who is not self-subsisting cannot sustain the universe. Since God possesses these attributes, associating partners with Him is illogical. It negates all deities that are themselves dependent on the Ever-Living. 

Negating sleep and drowsiness is a requirement of being Al-Qayyum. Sleep is a precursor to death and a sign of weakness; one overcome by sleep cannot sustain the world. Since He is always awake and monitoring His creation, He needs no one else in its administration.

Reality of Intercession (Shafa'ah): 'To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and earth.' This was accepted by the audience, as denying it meant denying God's divinity. From this, the Quran refutes the false concept of intercession held by polytheists and the People of the Book. 'Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?' Since all are His subjects, no one can speak without His leave. This ends the idea that certain 'partners' have such influence that God *must* accept their intercession. Angels and prophets do not precede Him in speech; they only obey. 

Furthermore, intercession requires knowing more than God, but 'He knows what is before them and what is behind them.' No one can encompass His knowledge except what He reveals. This limited knowledge of others destroys the polytheistic concept of intercession. True intercession is only for whom He is pleased with and is based on truth. 

'His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth.' He is not like worldly kings who need deputies to manage distant provinces. He possesses infinite knowledge and power. Managing the entire universe is easier for Him than for us to look after our own courtyard. 

Finally, He is 'Al-Ali' (the Most High) and 'Al-Azim' (the Most Magnificent). Do not measure His attributes by human scales; that is where Shirk begins. Believe in what He says about Himself and avoid the delusions of analogy and anthropomorphism.

Linguistic Research of 'Taghut': It is from the root 'tagha', meaning to exceed limits. 'Taghut' refers to any being that exits the boundaries of servitude to God and any deity worshipped besides Allah. The Quran uses it to denote Satan, false courts (like those of the Jews who distorted Sharia), and anything contrary to the Divine Book and Sunnah. 

Meaning of 'La ikraha fid-din' (No compulsion in religion): This clarifies the Sunnah of Allah mentioned in verse 253. The Messenger's duty is to make the truth distinct from falsehood. Once the truth is clear, the responsibility lies with the people. Allah has not used 'natural compulsion' to force people into guidance; He gave them free will. Those who believe are rewarded; those who disbelieve are punished. This is mentioned in Surah An-Nahl (verses 35-37). 

Negation of Natural Compulsion, Not Legal Compulsion: This verse negates 'natural compulsion'—God doesn't force the heart. It does *not* negate 'legal compulsion' for those within the system. If this meant no legal accountability, there would be no penalties (Hudud) for adultery, theft, or rebellion. Islam has a complete system of laws. While no one is forced to *enter* Islam, once they are in, they are required to follow its limits. In secular systems, religion is private, but in Islam, the government is God's government, and the state implements His laws. Apostasy is punished not as a forced conversion, but as a rebellion against God's government and law. 

Similarly, Jihad is not for forced conversion but for uprooting 'Fitna' (persecution) and 'Fasad' (corruption). Non-Muslims can live under Islamic rule with protected rights (except for the specific case of the pagan Ishmaelites, explained under verses 192-193). 

'Right course has become clear from error' explains the lack of compulsion: God has done His part by clarifying the truth. Now the choice is yours. But remember, the 'unbreakable rope' is only for those who turn to Allah. The reference to 'All-Hearing, All-Knowing' implies that those who hold onto Allah hold onto a Being who is always present and aware, unlike false deities who are unaware of their own worshippers.

Subject of Verses 257-260: These verses further explain the Sunnah of Allah regarding guidance and misguidance through three historical examples. One shows how people fall into Satan's trap, and two show how Allah helps those seeking truth, granting them the light of faith and tranquility of heart.

'Wali' means protector, ally, and supporter. 'Light' (Nur) refers to intellectual, spiritual, and moral radiance. It is singular because the source of truth is One (Allah) and truth is unified. 'Darknesses' (Zulumat) is plural because the paths of error are many and discordant. 

The core point is that guidance depends on whether a servant holds onto their Lord or someone else. If they hold onto God, He becomes their Wali, gradually leading them out of the darkness of lusts, disbelief, and hypocrisy into the light of perfect faith. If they turn away, they fall into the hands of Satan and his progeny, who lead them into the pit of misguidance. As the proverb says, 'An empty house is occupied by a demon.' A heart empty of faith becomes a base for Satan. This is also mentioned in Surah Al-An'am (verse 122), Al-A'raf (verse 175), and Az-Zukhruf (verse 36).

The 'one who argued with Abraham' is generally identified as Nimrod. He was a contemporary king who, like many ancient 'God-Kings' in Egypt or China, claimed to be a manifestation of a deity (likely the Sun god) to consolidate political and religious power. 

'Because Allah had given him kingship': He fell into the delusion of divinity because of the power God granted him. Instead of being grateful, he became arrogant. Power is a test; those who view it as their own right rather than God's favor often try to set up their own 'throne of divinity.' 

Prophetic Method of Debate: Abraham first presented the most obvious proof: 'My Lord is the one who gives life and causes death.' Nimrod's response was absurdly literal: 'I [too] give life and cause death,' implying he could execute or pardon someone.
