Abraham did not get bogged down in correcting this foolishness; prophets are callers, not mere debaters. He moved to a proof that left no room for argument: 'Allah brings the sun from the east, so bring it from the west.' Nimrod was speechless. This was particularly effective because Nimrod claimed to be the avatar of the Sun god. 

'And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people': This summarizes the principle. 'Wrongdoers' (Zalimin) are those who misuse God's gifts, act with arrogance like Iblis, and set themselves up as lords. Such people find ways to argue even against the clearest truth. Even when silenced like Nimrod, they do not accept the truth.

The particle 'Aw' (or) is for categorization. After the example of those whose Wali is Taghut, this is the example of those whose Wali is Allah. 

A Believer Seeking Certainty: Unlike the view in Al-Kashshaf, this refers to a believer seeking 'Haqq al-Yaqin' (absolute certainty/tranquility of heart). His question 'How will Allah bring this to life after its death?' was not one of denial but of wonder and a quest for reality. This is a spiritual discipline every seeker of truth undergoes. 

Who was this person? It is difficult to say. Some suggest Khidr or Ezra (Uzair), but their scriptures don't mention this. However, a similar vision is found in the Book of Ezekiel (Chapter 37), where he sees a valley of dry bones and God brings them to life. 

Differences between Quran and Torah: The differences are of summary vs. detail. The Quranic account is the preserved word of God and is preferred. The Torah doesn't mention the 100-year sleep of the observer himself or the donkey's revival, but these were likely part of the vision that the Israelites lost or omitted. 

'And it was falling on its roofs' describes a ruined town where the high points (minarets, roofs) fall first. The question 'How?' was a request for the highest level of certainty. Allah granted him a 'vision of the kingdom' (similar to the Mi'raj) to settle his heart regarding the resurrection and his mission to revive the Children of Israel. 

'How long have you remained?' The answer 'A day or part of a day' vs. 'A hundred years' shows that in the realm of Barzakh, time loses its worldly perception. On the Day of Resurrection, the long interval will seem like a single morning or evening. 

'And look at your donkey... and We will make you a sign for the people.' An omitted phrase 'see how We revive it' is implied. The return of the Ark and this vision were signs for the Israelites—who were hopeless about their future—that God could revive them from the 'death' of slavery and disgrace. 

Why did the food not spoil while the donkey rotted? This showed the observer that God's command is the ultimate factor; He can cause a thing to rot or keep it beyond the laws of nature (like the People of the Cave). 

'When it became clear to him, he said: I know that Allah is over all things competent.' This 'I know' refers to 'Ilm al-Yaqin' (certainty of knowledge). He saw the dry bones of Israel being revived and gained full confidence in his mission.

The Reality of 'Itminan' (Tranquility): It means for something to be so firmly settled that there is no fear of it leaning or wavering. In faith, it refers to a heart that remains steadfast regardless of changing circumstances. This is also called 'Sharh al-Sadr' (opening of the chest). Prophets, though possessing faith from day one, still seek this higher station of certainty. 

'Fa-surhunna': 'Sawr' means to incline or lean. 'Surhunna' means to make the birds lean toward you or become tamed by you. 

Abraham's Request was for 'Sharh al-Sadr': Just as the previous believer expressed a spiritual longing, Abraham (PBUH) desired to witness the process of resurrection. This was not a lack of belief, but a desire for the 'vision' that settles the heart. Even our Prophet (PBUH) was taught to pray: 'My Lord, increase me in knowledge.' Absolute thirst is only quenched in the Hereafter, but God does not deprive sincere seekers of 'Itminan' in this world. 

Abraham was told to take four birds, tame them so he could recognize them, then cut them into pieces and place portions on various hills. When called, they came running to him. 

Four birds were used to show their gathering from all four directions, mirroring how all creation will rush to their Lord at the blast of the trumpet. Taming them ensured he knew they were the *same* birds he had cut up, proving that the resurrected life retains the memories and identities of the worldly life. 

Though a specific word for 'cutting into pieces' isn't used, the word 'juz'an' (a portion) makes it clear. If he had just placed whole live birds on hills, it would not have addressed the question of how the *dead* are revived. The miracle lies in the components of a thing gathering from different places after being destroyed to receive new life. 

This was a private vision for Abraham's training and 'Itminan', not a public miracle for his nation. Prophets are given such visions to prepare them for the heavy burden of their mission. 

Finally, the reference to 'Aziz' and 'Hakim' reminds us that resurrection is certain because God is both Powerful (to do it) and Wise (to require it).

Subject of Verses 261 to 274: The incidental discussion on guidance and misguidance (triggered by 'no compulsion') has ended. The discourse returns to the original subject: spending (Infaq). The blessings, characteristics, and pitfalls of spending are now described in detail.

Meaning of 'Fi Sabilillah': This is a Quranic term encompassing all works done for the welfare of Islam and Muslims. Depending on circumstances, some works are more important than others, but any work done for God's pleasure under Sharia guidance is 'in the path of Allah.'

Parable of Spending: This illustrates the multiplication of the reward for wealth spent in God's path. Just as one grain produces seven ears, each with a hundred grains, one good deed can earn a seven-hundred-fold reward in the Hereafter. Hadiths clarify that the reward ranges from ten to seven hundred times, depending on the nature of the deed, the time it was done, and the internal state of the doer. A deed done in hardship with a sincere heart earns more than one done in ease with half-heartedness. 'Allah multiplies for whom He wills' points to His system of justice and wisdom. 

'Wasi'un 'Alim' (All-Encompassing, All-Knowing) clarifies that while humans might be amazed by such vast rewards, God's capacity is infinite, and He is aware of every small, large, hidden, or open deed. There is no room for anxiety when the Giver's treasury is limitless and His knowledge is perfect.

The word 'Adha' refers to anything that causes pain or distress, whether physical or emotional. Here, it refers to the taunts, insults, and humiliation often shown by mean-spirited people toward those they have helped. 

The True Deservers of Reward: The great reward mentioned in the previous verse is for those who, after spending, do not follow it up with reminders of their generosity ('Mann') or any form of injury ('Adha'). These two are aspects of the same corrupt character. Mean people want the recipient to remain a lifelong debtor or slave. If this desire isn't met, they target them with taunts. Such people have no reward.

'No fear shall be upon them, nor shall they grieve' is a description of Paradise—a place with no future anxiety and no past regret. This also hints that these spenders deserve Paradise because they did not grieve over what they spent, nor did they fear Satan's threats of future poverty. In return, God gives them a seven-hundred-fold reward and a home of absolute peace.

Though only 'Adha' (injury) is mentioned here, both 'Mann' and 'Adha' are intended, as they are two sides of the same coin. 

A Kind Word is Better than Charity followed by Injury: Instructing people to speak kindly to the needy and overlook their behavior is a recurring theme in the Quran (see verse 83). If a person has wealth but lacks forbearance (Hilm), they cannot fulfill the right of spending. They may even earn sin instead of reward. Wealth often creates a sense of superiority, leading people to look down on the poor or snap at them. Sometimes the needy person's behavior is unpleasant, which irritates the wealthy. All these flaws arise when wealth is not accompanied by the attribute of forgiveness. This is mentioned in Al Imran (verse 134).

Demands of the Attributes 'Ghani' and 'Halim': Allah is 'Ghani' (Self-Sufficient) and 'Halim' (Forbearing). Despite human shortcomings, He continues to provide. If He withheld His grace for every mistake, no one would receive anything. He wants His servants to reflect these attributes: those He has made 'wealthy' (Ghani) should also possess 'forbearance' (Hilm). 

Furthermore, as stated in a Hadith Qudsi, the hand stretched out by the poor is effectively the hand of God. Those who humiliate that hand should remember they are rejecting the One from whom they received everything. If He still overlooks their conduct, it is only because He is Forbearing; otherwise, such ingratitude would warrant the withdrawal of all blessings.

Meaning of 'Wabil': A heavy downpour or torrential rain. 

Meaning of 'Sald': Something hard and smooth. 'Ard sald' is land where nothing grows. 'Ra's sald' is a bald head.

The Scenario in the Parable: On mountains, soil sometimes settles on a large, smooth rock, forming a thick layer. Farmers might sow crops there. However, a heavy rain can wash the entire layer of soil and the crops down into the valley, leaving only the bare, hard rock. 

It is stated that those who spend and then follow it with reminders of generosity or injury find their charity nullified. Despite being believers, their act is wasted just like the act of a hypocrite who spends only for show and does not believe in Allah or the Last Day. This shows that 'Mann' and 'Adha' are as destructive to charity as disbelief itself.

The parable compares such a person to a farmer who sows on a thin layer of soil over a smooth rock. A heavy rain washes everything away, leaving the rock as bare as a bald head. Just as the farmer's labor is wasted, the spender's charity is ruined; they cannot save any part of it.

Different Forms of Guidance: The 'guidance' mentioned in 'And Allah does not guide...' refers to the guidance of the ultimate goal. As noted at the start of the Tafseer, there are several types of guidance: natural instinct, success in worldly efforts, the guidance of prophets, and finally, the guidance in the Hereafter toward the fruits of one's labor. Here, it means that the ungrateful and disbelieving will not find success in their deeds; all their efforts will be washed away.

Meaning of 'Tathbitan min anfusihim': 'Tathbit' means to strengthen, anchor, or consolidate. They spend their wealth not only for God's pleasure but also to train their own souls to become firm in obeying religious commands.

Spending is a Discipline for Self-Training: Just as fasting trains the soul in Taqwa, spending is a discipline to overcome the self and ease the path to God. This implies: 1. Like all worship, the true benefit of spending returns to the servant, not God. 2. The highest spending is that done during famine, despite poverty, ignoring personal needs, from beloved wealth, and for those one might personally dislike. This provides the true training to keep the soul firm on the truth. The more one practices acting against their base desires, the easier it becomes to attain proximity to God.

'Rabwah' refers to a high, elevated piece of land. Usage shows it is also flat and smooth. High, flat land has pleasant weather and is protected from floods. Because it is flat, it doesn't suffer the erosion that sloped lands do. The good climate ensures it is fruitful; if the weather is perfect, it yields double; if not, even a light drizzle suffices due to the quality of the land.

Parable of Sincere Spending: This is the opposite of the previous group. These people spend for God's pleasure and self-training. They have planted their 'garden' on high, fertile ground. Heavy rain doubles its yield, and even without heavy rain, the mist is enough. 

'And Allah is Seeing of what you do' provides consolation: God is not unaware of the discipline and sacrifice His righteous servants undergo. He sees everything and will grant a full reward for their labor and selflessness.

Arab Taste regarding Gardens: 'A garden of palm trees and grapevines with rivers flowing beneath it... having all kinds of fruits' is the image of a perfect garden according to the Arabs. Their ideal garden had dates along the edges, grapes in the middle, plots for various crops, was situated on a height, and had a river with irrigation channels running through it. This is also described in Surah Al-Kahf (verses 32-33). The dates provided fruit, protection from heat and wind, and beauty. Grapes and other fruits were in the middle, with various crops in designated sections. 'All kinds of fruits' (thamarat) includes grains as well as fruits.

Meaning of 'I'sar': 'I'sarun fihi nar' refers to a whirlwind or tornado containing fire. This is not our common fire, but the 'Samum' (scorching wind) often found in whirlwinds, which shrivels and burns any garden it hits.

This parable further explains the fate of those who ruin their spending through hypocrisy, reminders of generosity, or injury. It shows that such spenders will see the heartbreaking destruction of the 'garden of their hopes' at the very moment they need it most—when the doors of action and effort have closed.

The parable describes a man who planted a magnificent garden of grapes and dates, irrigated by a river, containing all types of fruits and grains. The owner became old, and his children were small and helpless. Suddenly, a fiery whirlwind destroyed the entire garden. This is the state in the Hereafter of those who do not protect their spending from destructive pitfalls. The lightning that destroys their harvest is hidden in their own sleeves and manifests exactly when they have no chance to recover what they lost.

Meaning of 'Tayyibat': 'From the good things [tayyibat] you have earned' signifies two things. First, spend wealth that was earned through pure and lawful means, not wrong or doubtful ones. Second, the wealth itself should be of good quality, not worthless or defective. Neither God's pleasure nor the 'Tathbit' (strengthening) of the soul can be achieved through ill-gotten or low-quality wealth.

The separate mention of 'what We have produced for you from the earth' shows that 'what you have earned' refers to trade and other professions. Land produce is mentioned separately because its Zakat system is distinct from other forms of wealth.

Spending of 'Khabith' (Defective) Wealth is Not Accepted: 'And do not aim toward the defective [khabith] therefrom' is a strong emphasis, like 'do not go near.' It means do not even *intend* to give bad wealth. It defines 'bad wealth' as that which you would be willing to give in God's path but would only accept for yourself with closed eyes and a forced heart. To offer God what you wouldn't like for yourself—when everything was given by Him—is extreme meanness. Far from gaining God's pleasure or training the soul, it risks increasing one's distance from Him.

Explanation of 'Ghani' and 'Hamid': God is 'Ghani' (Self-Sufficient) and 'Hamid' (Praiseworthy). He does not need anyone's wealth. He asks for spending only to test the servants' loyalty—to see how they give back what He gave them. 'Hamid' means He is the source of all praise; despite being independent, He is of praiseworthy attributes, His grace is constant, and all benefit from Him, both the good and the bad.

Meaning of 'Fahsha'': It refers to open indecency and immorality, such as adultery, sodomy, and public nakedness. The word 'amr' (command) can also mean to suggest or advise. 

Obstacles in the Path of Spending: This points to the resistance from Satan and his progeny. When a person intends to spend on a good cause, Satan and his agents try to divert them in two ways. First, they frighten them with imaginary future risks—claiming that hardships lie ahead and they should hold onto their money. Second, they lure them into luxury, drinking, and wasteful entertainment (Israf and Tabdhir) so that no resources remain for a high purpose. Satan's Fitna is severe; those trapped in it become so drowned in their intoxication that they are incapable of fulfilling the rights of God or His servants. Thus, in Surah Al-Isra, alongside the command to spend, there is a specific warning against Satan's tactic: 'And give the relative his right, and [also] the poor and the traveler, and do not spend wastefully. Indeed, the wasteful are brothers of the devils, and ever is Satan to his Lord ungrateful' (Al-Isra: 26-27). The same subject appears in Al Imran (verses 134-135).

'And Allah promises you forgiveness from Him and bounty...': Forgiveness is the opposite of 'Fahsha'', and bounty is the opposite of 'poverty.' Satan suggests wastefulness and lust to lead you to Hell, but Allah invites you to spend in His path to lead you to forgiveness and Paradise. Satan frightens you with the ghost of poverty, but Allah promises His boundless favor in both worlds. Allah is All-Encompassing and aware of every deed; He has no lack of resources to give, and He is aware of every penny you give in His path.

Spending is the Key to the Treasure of Wisdom: We explained 'Wisdom' (Hikmah) under verse 151. Its essence is the maturity of faith and action based on deep insight. One who possesses it does not collect treasures in this fleeting world but with God. They do not fear Satan's threats but rely on God's promises. They seek the treasure of wisdom instead of worldly shards of glass. This treasure is so great that no worldly wealth compares to it. Allah gives it to those who prove themselves worthy of it, as His will is never contrary to wisdom. His Sunnah is that those who spend their wealth for God's forgiveness and favor, instead of being infatuated with worldly pleasures, find their hearts filled with the jewels of wisdom.

Finally, it is stated that not everyone can understand this; only the 'people of understanding' (ulul-albab)—those whose reason is illuminated by the light of wisdom—can. To spend one's earnings for the successes of an unseen world while leaving behind the 'cash' pleasures of this world requires the courage of those blessed with wisdom.

'Nadhr' (Vow) means to pledge that if a certain wish is fulfilled, one will perform a specific act of worship or charity. In Islam, as clear from Hadiths, making vows is not encouraged. This is because it is a covenant with the Lord, and every covenant, large or small, must be fulfilled if it is not contrary to Sharia, as one will be questioned about it. We will explain in Surah Al-Ma'idah that the foundation of all Sharia and morals is the covenant; therefore, Islam tolerates no laxity here.

'And indeed, Allah knows it' is the response to the condition. Whoever spends in God's path or makes a vow should be at peace that God knows it well. 'Knowing' implies the result: since He knows, He will certainly reward it according to His promise. This is a common style in Arabic and the Quran.

'And for the wrongdoers there are no helpers.' 'Wrongdoers' here refers to those who wrong their own souls—those who make worldly wealth their deity and do not spend in God's path, or those who ruin their spending through hypocrisy, reminders of generosity, and injury. Such people are unfortunate; they rely on their wealth and false deities instead of sacrifice and God's mercy. They will find no helper with God.

Levels of Open and Secret Spending: Since the discussion here concerns general voluntary charity (Sadaqat) rather than obligatory charity (Zakat), which is given openly, the question arises whether it is better to give secretly or openly. The Quran answers that giving openly is good because it has beneficial aspects, such as encouraging others to spend—especially when motivating people for a collective campaign. However, when such circumstances do not exist, it is better to give to the poor secretly to remain safe from the trial of hypocrisy and show, and to preserve the dignity of self-respecting needy people. Here, 'He will remove from you [your sins]' is conjoined to the concept of 'it is better for you.' Meaning: this secret spending will earn a greater reward and will be more effective in shedding sins from your mantle. Finally, it states that the issue of secret or open is for your consideration; nothing is hidden from God. Whatever you do, wherever you do it, God is aware of everything.

An Imperative Sentence in the Style of a Statement: In this verse, there is no specific word requiring linguistic research, but there is some hesitation regarding the style of 'wa ma tunfiqun...'. I repeatedly feel that this is an imperative (insha'iyah) sentence in the style of a statement (khabariyah). This method is well-known in the Arabic language. There are many parallels in the Quran where a command or prohibition is put in a declarative style when the addressee needs to be instructed with compassion and special attention. I have reflected this in the translation. Some previous scholars have also adopted this, but I am not entirely certain of it; let the people of knowledge keep it in mind.

Reflecting on the entire verse reveals that it simultaneously addresses the Prophet (PBUH) by way of digression (Iltifat) and the general Muslims.

Addressing the Prophet (PBUH), he is reminded of the Sunnah of Allah regarding guidance and misguidance, which has been mentioned in several places in this Surah and elsewhere in the Quran in various styles. That Sunnah is: the Prophet's responsibility regarding people's guidance is only to thoroughly inform them of Allah's teachings and instructions. Once he has done this, his duty is fulfilled. The responsibility is not upon him that people must accept these teachings. Granting the success to accept them is Allah's work, and He grants this success to whom He wills. There is a code appointed by Allah for this 'willing', which we explained under verses 256-260.

The purpose of pointing to this Sunnah is: you have detailed the matters regarding spending on behalf of Allah. Your duty is done. Now it is the people's job to accept or reject them. Do not be distressed over this. If people do not value them, they will suffer the consequences themselves.

Giving to God is Saving for Oneself: Addressing the Muslims as a final warning, it is stated: whatever wealth you spend in the path of God, do not think you are giving it to someone else; rather, you are saving it for yourselves, which will return to you one day multiplied up to seven hundred times. The only condition is that your spending must not be for any purpose other than Allah's pleasure. Whatever you spend for Allah's pleasure will be repaid to you in full; not the slightest bit will be decreased.

Before 'lil-fuqara'', a subject (mubtada') is omitted. Meaning: this charity and spending being invited is for those poor who have such-and-such qualities. The reason for omitting the subject is, first, that the context itself is proof of it; second, it serves as an eloquent hint toward the concealment of charity. It is as if the verse pointed its finger toward the needy but left the specific task for which it pointed to the understanding of the addressee. Thus, alongside the concealment of charity, the dignity of the self-respecting needy—to which the verse points—is also preserved.

'uhshiru fi sabilillah' means some religious objective has prevented them from the struggle of earning a livelihood. These people cannot travel here and there for trade, etc. 'Dharban fil-ardh' means to travel, as stated: 'and others traveling through the land seeking of the bounty of Allah.'

'yahsabuhumul-jahilu aghniya'a minat-ta'affuf': 'Jahil' here means the uninformed, and 'ta'affuf' means self-respect/restraint. Meaning: due to their self-respect, these people neither stretch out a hand of begging to anyone nor express their poverty and hunger; therefore, one who is uninformed of their circumstances considers them wealthy/self-sufficient.

'ta'rifuhum bi-simahum la yas'alunan-nasa ilhafa': 'Sima' means sign and appearance, as stated: 'Their mark is on their faces from the trace of prostration.' 'Ilhaf' means to beg persistently or clingingly. In 'they do not ask people persistently,' the original objective is the negation of asking altogether; the qualifier 'persistently' is attached only to paint the general condition of beggars and express its repulsiveness. For example: 'do not kill your children for fear of poverty'—the prohibition is actually against killing; the qualifier 'fear of poverty' is merely to highlight its repulsiveness. Or: 'do not consume usury doubled and multiplied'—the prohibition is against usury itself; the qualifier 'doubled and multiplied' is to highlight its loathsomeness. Or: 'do not compel your slave-girls to prostitution if they desire chastity'—the objective is the absolute prohibition of coercion; the qualifier 'if they desire chastity' is to express the repulsiveness of the act.

Similarly, in 'they do not ask people persistently,' the objective is the negation of their asking. The qualifier 'persistently' is merely to express the general state of beggars: how could people so self-respecting that the uninformed think them wealthy commit an act like that of beggars and mendicants? Consequently, due to this self-respect and 'veiling' of theirs, the Quran gave spenders their identification: try to find them by recognizing them only from their appearance and reach them yourself; do not expect that like common beggars, these people will run after you.

At the time of the Quran's revelation, the best referents of this verse were the 'Ashab al-Suffah', and there is no doubt that the characteristics described for them in Hadiths correspond exactly to these Quranic hints. In these verses, the Quran has shown the model that believing poor should have and the method that believing wealthy should adopt regarding these poor. Let today's poor and today's wealthy see their faces in this mirror.

Finally, it stated: "whatever you spend, Allah is aware of it"—meaning, searching for such self-respecting supplicants and fulfilling their needs quietly will remain hidden from the creation but not from the Creator; He is aware of your every spending and will give a full reward.

In this verse, note the suitability and contrast between night and day, and secret and open. All times are encompassed, and both states are gathered. Furthermore, the zeal for spending that should be within believers is automatically overflowing from the style of the discourse. Additionally, the reward for spending with God is also fully described. This verse holds the status of the conclusion of the chapter regarding spending.

Subject of Verses 275 to 283: After the subject of spending (Infaq), the prohibition of usury (Riba) is now described. Since usury relates to loans, the necessary instructions regarding the transaction of loans are also given in this series. Pledging (Rahan) is also a related matter, so its ruling is described alongside. 

It requires no explanation that usury is the absolute opposite of spending. The motive for spending is high-mindedness, sympathy, generosity, sacrifice, and mercy; the motive for usury is cowardice, selfishness, cold-heartedness, and the desire to exploit others' difficulties. Spending wants to support the needy, and usury wants to suck the blood of the fallen. The two hold the relationship of opposites, and the principle of nature is that the reality of a thing is not well-clarified until its opposite is described. Based on this principle, the Quran has maintained this method in describing most things: mentioning opposites together. For example, the description of believers is followed by that of disbelievers. The mention of Paradise is often followed by Hell. This has become a characteristic of the Quran's structure. On this principle, the Quran has often mentioned either miserliness or usury alongside spending. Here, it comes after spending. In Surah Al Imran, it is before the description of spending. But the objective in both places is the same: that the darkness of one may unveil the light of the other, and the beauty of one may unveil the ugliness of the other. From this structure of discourse, certain aspects of wisdom are revealed that cannot be revealed in any other way. In this light, recite the next verse.

The Meaning of the word "Riba": 'Raba yarbu riban' means to increase and grow. From this comes 'Riba', which refers to that specific addition which a lender collects from their debtor over the original amount merely in exchange for the respite (time). In both the era of ignorance and Islam, this terminology has been well-known for the aforementioned meaning. Its forms have varied, but its original reality is this: the lender collects profit on their given money from the debtor at a fixed rate solely on the basis of the right that they allowed them to use the money for a specific duration. The fact that the loan was given to a poor person or a wealthy king has no role in determining its reality; nor does it make a difference whether the loan was given for a funeral, a welfare scheme, or an individual or collective project of trade, agriculture, or industry. In the meaning of the terminology of Riba recognized in both the era of ignorance and Islam, these outward differences do not cause a hair's breadth of difference. The idea of those who think that the purpose of the loan or the status of the debtor changes the customary status of Riba is completely false. Further on, you will see that the Quran's own words completely refute this idea.

Linguistic Research of "Khabt": The word 'takhabbat' is from 'khabt al-layl', which means to wander in the darkness of the night. 'Khabit al-layl' refers to a person who has no awareness of right or left, just wandering aimlessly. From this, the idiom 'yakhbitu khabta 'ashwa'' arose for those who are entirely deprived of insight and wander around like a blind buffalo. From this came 'takhabbatahu-sh-shaytan', meaning 'massahu-sh-shaytanu bi-khabalin aw junun' (Satan touched him with madness or insanity).

'Mass' originally means to touch. It is predominantly used for the reaching and afflicting of some evil, calamity, or pain. We have translated it as 'contagion/touch' (chhoot) according to the context, which is both literal and meaningful. Everything in the world happens by God's permission, but the actions for which Satan has been given respite are sometimes attributed to him. As in Prophet Job's prayer: 'Indeed, Satan has touched me with distress and punishment.' Evil spirits do not affect righteous servants more than causing some pain or trial, but for those whose souls are themselves evil—just as their heart is in Satan's fist, sometimes Satan's dominance extends over their reason and brain, and they appear outwardly completely mad, tearing clothes, foaming at the mouth, and wandering aimlessly with disheveled hair.

Above, you read regarding the spenders that Allah Almighty trains them, grants them depth and resolve in both knowledge and action, blesses their spending, becomes their Protector to bring them out of darkness into light, grants them the never-fading treasure of wisdom, and above all, will bless them in the Hereafter with the eternal joy of 'no fear shall be upon them nor shall they grieve.'

Now, this is the description of those with the opposite character: the usurers. Regarding them, it was stated that when they rise on the Day of Resurrection, they will rise exactly like a person possessed by a Jinn or ghost, making them completely 'makhbut al-hawas' (disoriented/insane). This simile was not explained further because all the explanation is present in its summary. Just as the Quran in one place uses the simile 'heads of devils' which causes a shiver just by hearing the words, similarly the words 'takhabbatahu-sh-shaytanu minal-mass' bring forward such a picture of horror and distress that it requires no explanation.

Further on, it stated that their condition will be such because they used to raise the objection against the prohibition of usury that "trade is just like usury"; so why did Allah make trade Halal and usury Haram? Since this point can only be said by someone who is completely disoriented, whose reason is destroyed, and whom Satan has made mad with his touch, therefore, under the law of similarity between deed and reward—as stated in the Quran: 'Whoever is blind in this [world] will be blind in the Hereafter'—such a person will rise on the Day of Resurrection like the mad and insane.

The Context of 'wa ahallallahu-l-bay'a wa harrama-r-riba': Here, their statement was referenced and then ignored; it was not refuted. This is because the absurdity of this statement is so clear that it requires no refutation. This point can only be said by someone who has gone completely mad, and there is no need to refute anything said by the mad. Our interpreters have generally taken the segment 'wa ahallallahu-l-bay'a wa harrama-r-riba' as the refutation of their statement and interpreted it from that angle. Although there is a place for this—because Allah's making something Halal or Haram is in itself a great proof that its permission or prohibition is perfectly rational from the viewpoint of reason, nature, livelihood, and the Hereafter—this proof is reliable only for the believer; it cannot benefit the denier. For this reason, I repeatedly feel that this segment is not separate but falls under 'qalu innama-l-bay'u mithlu-r-riba'. Meaning: they say trade is just like usury; when a trader takes profit on his capital, why should a capitalist be deemed a criminal if he obtains profit on his capital? Why then did Allah make trade Halal and usury Haram? Although this sentence is not in the form of a question, the same question or objection is hidden within it as in the first segment.

The Difference between a Trader's and a Usurer's Capital: From this objection, it became perfectly clear that the generation of madmen who compare usury to trade is not new in the world but very old. The Quran did not consider this analogy worthy of attention. The reason is that it is self-evidently void and a proof of the analogizer's mental defect. A trader invests his capital in the trade of goods that people demand; he makes those goods obtainable for people by enduring labor, trouble, and risks—goods which they either could not easily obtain through their own efforts or could only obtain at a much higher price than what the trader provided. Then the trader presents his capital and goods for competition in the open market, and the market's ebb and flow determines the rate of profit; it is possible he becomes completely bankrupt due to this ebb and flow, and it is possible he earns some profit. Similarly, his hands are tied in this matter: once he sells a one-rupee item for one rupee and two or four annas, he cannot earn even a penny of profit from that rupee again until that rupee passes through all the risks and fluctuations and enters the field again to earn entitlement by serving society.

Pray tell, what is the comparison between a trader's brave, honorable, and service-oriented capital and a usurer's cold-hearted, cowardly, dishonorable, and ego-enemy capital, which is not ready to endure even a single risk but sits on the head to share the profit? The usurer's capital appears in two guises, and in both, it is equally cowardly and bloodthirsty. One is in the form of usurious business done by moneylenders, Pathans, and Jews; in this form, there is no room for anyone to deny its bloodthirstiness because once a moneylender finds someone trapped in his net, even if not a scrap of flesh remains on the victim's body, he will come at every deadline to cut his 'pound of flesh.' And after a lifetime of this cutting and trimming, it often happens that not only does the moneylender's original capital remain loaded on the debtor, but it becomes many times the original and, like a parasitic vine, envelops the victim's home, household goods, and wife and children, uprooting entire families. Tell me, which trader's capital has the power to inflict this oppression on someone? The most he can do is face all the market fluctuations and, if he finds an opportunity, sell his one-rupee item for one-and-a-half or two rupees, and that too only once.

The second guise of the usurer's capital is that in which it rises in the name of patronizing welfare works, collective projects, and national schemes. In this era, many simple-minded people consider it very innocent in this guise, but if you reflect, you will realize that its evil nature of cowardice and selfishness is just as present in this garment as in the first guise. Understand this with an example. Suppose the country's government wants to implement a large project to save some part of the country from the destruction of floods or some other danger, and for this, it takes loans from people at a rate of five or seven percent. It is possible that the government's project, after spending millions of rupees, is destroyed by some earthly or heavenly calamity. Now tell me: the country as a whole suffered a severe type of loss, but the usurers who gave loans for it—not only is their capital safe, but with a fixed rate, its interest is also increasing it moment by moment. After all, what form of national service is this? If you reflect, you will realize that there is no difference in reality between the first case and this second case. In the first case, the usurer is only concerned with his capital remaining safe and a pound of flesh of profit reaching him without any blemish at every season, even if seven generations of the debtor die of starvation. And in this case too, the thing before him is that his original capital remains spotlessly safe and the fixed interest continues to be added to his account, regardless of whether the nation's corpse goes to Hell or Paradise.

In contrast, a trader's capital first puts itself in danger for the service of the nation and country. If it suffers a loss due to the change of circumstances, market fluctuations, or some other cause, it bears that loss upon itself. There is not a moment's possibility for it to sit hidden in an ambush like the usurer's capital and continue collecting its profit from others. Even if it obtains the most favorable circumstances, nonetheless the market's decision is final for its profit rate, and thus whatever it manages to obtain is in reality its legitimate 'right of labor' (haqq al-mihnat). This is why Allah Almighty described the trader's profit as His favor (Fadhl) and likened the usurer's capital—as we will clarify in the Tafseer of Surah Ar-Rum—to a bull that grows fat by grazing in others' pastures.

Warning for the Usurers: 'fa-man ja'ahu maw'izhatum...' (So whoever has received an admonition...): The word 'maw'izhah' is dominated by the aspect of rebuke and warning; therefore, keeping its original spirit in view, we have translated it as "warning." Since its femininity is non-literal, a masculine verb was used for it based on the word's meaning. The words 'mir-rabbihi' (from his Lord) have created a special force within this warning. Meaning: this warning is not some airy talk but a warning from the Master and Lord of all; therefore, it should not be considered light. 'falahu ma salaf' (then for him is what has passed): meaning, after this warning, whoever stops usurious transactions will not be asked to return the interest they previously took. If the implementation of this law affected the past, it would create insoluble difficulties. But along with this, He also stated: 'wa amruhu ilallah' (and his matter is with Allah). Meaning: the Islamic government will not seize such a person for their previous usury, but this does not mean they have also received forgiveness from God; rather, their matter is referred to God. The reason for referring it to God is that merely stopping the taking of usury is not enough to escape the grasp of the Hereafter; it is also necessary that every trace of usury leaves the person's heart. This hints that such people should not sit back unafraid of the Hereafter but should try to make amends for their previous oppressions as much as possible, because these oppressions relate to the 'rights of servants' (huquq al-'ibad), and the matter of the rights of servants holds great importance with God.

'wa man 'ada fa-ula'ika as-habun-nari hum fiha khalidun' (And whoever returns - those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide eternally therein). Meaning: those who conduct usurious business even after this clear warning are people of Hell and will remain in Hell forever. The reason for the punishment of eternal Hell for such people is, in our view, that this attitude of theirs is a very clear proof that they are not ready to accept this command of God. If disobedience to Allah Almighty's commands occurs due to the dominance of emotions, the path of reform through repentance is open. It is also possible that despite negligence in repentance, Allah Almighty forgives someone or releases them after punishing them according to their sins. But when a person insists on violating a command even after an extremely clear reminder and warning, it means they are a denier of that command of God. If they claim faith alongside this, it is merely a hypocritical act which is not hidden from the Knower of the Unseen, who is well-aware of all the secrets of the hearts; therefore, Allah Almighty will deal with such people as they deserve. Meaning, He will plunge them into that eternal punishment of Hell which is appointed for the disbelievers and hypocrites.

Encompassing Sin Causes Eternal Punishment: It should also be remembered here that there is a difference between committing a crime and making a crime one's 'covering and bedding' (way of life). A crime that becomes a way of life does not affect only one aspect of the criminal's life but every aspect comes into its grip. Their outward, their inward, their creed, their action, and their faith and Islam all accept the stamp of this crime. The Quran described this state with the word 'ihatah' (encompassing) and stated regarding such people that with Allah Almighty, for them is the eternal punishment of the Fire. For example: 'Yes, whoever earns an evil deed and his sin has encompassed him - those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide eternally therein' (Al-Baqarah: 81).

'Mahq' means to decrease and erase. From this came 'mahaqallahu-sh-shay'', the meaning of which is that Allah erased the blessing (barakah) of that thing. 'Irba'' means to increase and make more. The meaning is: the usurer thinks that capital increases through usury and decreases through charity, but the reality is exactly the opposite. Allah Almighty decreases usury and increases charities. This decreasing and increasing is not in terms of the limited concept of worldly life but in terms of the collective life of both this world and the Hereafter. When the morning of the afterlife life dawns, the usurer will see that while millions of rupees were deposited in the worldly bank, there is not a single penny in God's bank; only regret and remorse are his capital. In contrast, when the one who spent in God's path opens his eyes in that life, he will see that in exchange for his 'shards of glass', mountains of jewels possessing eternal value are built here. In another place in the Quran, there is a clarification that this decreasing and increasing relates to the afterlife life. For example: 'And whatever you give for usury to increase within the wealth of people will not increase with Allah. But what you give in zakah, desiring the countenance of Allah - those are the multipliers' (Ar-Rum: 39).

This is also supported by Hadiths. Consider the translation of one Hadith: "It is narrated from Abu Hurairah (RA) that the Prophet (PBUH) said: Allah Almighty accepts charity and takes it with His right hand, then He nurtures it for you just as one of you nurtures his foal, until your given morsel becomes like Mount Uhud with God." Although there is no goodness or blessing in usurious capital even in this worldly life, the verse relates to the results and fruits of the Hereafter.

The Cause of Usury's Lack of Blessing: 'Kaffar' here means the ungrateful, and 'Athim' means, as we explained elsewhere, the one who deprives others of their rights. This segment explains why Allah Almighty will erase the usurer's capital. It stated: the reason is that he is ungrateful and a depriver of the rights of God's servants, and Allah can never love those who are ungrateful to Him and destroyers of His creation's rights. Allah Almighty gives someone more wealth than their need so that they become a grateful servant of God and, just as Allah has done favor upon them, they do favor upon others; but when they obtain wealth and fall into the worship of that wealth itself, and instead of making it a support for others, they make it a means of sucking their blood and depriving them of their rights, then such a hard-hearted and ungrateful person deserves that their wealth with Allah be only a cause of burden and loss, and nothing but deprivation falls into their lap.

The great reward of the believers is mentioned, and the context proves that the reference here is to those believers in whose charities the blessing was hinted at in the previous verse. The explanation of all components of this verse has passed in various places. There is no need to repeat it here.

The Final Blow to Usurious Business: In this verse, addressing the believers directly, the instruction was given to remain fearful of Allah and to completely forego the portion of usury still remaining due from debtors, and this was made a mandatory demand of faith. The verses revealed regarding usury before this were of the nature of advice and admonition, and their foundation was on the fact that in heavenly religions and the world's 'Ma'ruf', its status has always been that of a Haram or at least a loathsome thing. The Arabs were not unacquainted with this reality. For this reason, the Quran began clarifying its oppressiveness from the Makkan period itself; thus, from this aspect, its mention occurred in Surah Ar-Rum, which is a Makkan Surah. But since its nature was that of a vast economic corruption whose reform was not possible without the entire system of the country being practically under the authority of Islam, the final blow was struck at the occasion of the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hujjat al-Wada'). These verses were revealed at that occasion and were given a place here in the arrangement due to the suitability of the subject.

Ultimatum to the Usurers: Its nature is entirely that of an ultimatum: meaning, those who do not accept this command now should be warned of war from Allah and His Messenger. The tone regarding usury in these verses is exactly the same tone found in the Holy Prophet's (PBUH) sermon of the Farewell Pilgrimage regarding usury, which gives an idea of the era of these verses' revelation. From this verse, it emerges explicitly that in the Islamic system, the status of those conducting usurious business is that of rebels and corrupters, against whom military action can also be taken if necessary. Detailed discussion on this will come in Surah Al-Ma'idah.

After this ultimatum, those having usurious businesses were given permission only for this: they can take back their original principal amounts from the debtors. Neither should they seek a path to deprive the debtor of their rights by joining some usury amount to it through some trick, nor is it permissible for the debtors to take advantage of this reform and try to suppress the moneylenders' original principal amounts as well. This warning was necessary because when an important economic reform comes into action, great stir is created in the affected classes. Those who apprehend loss from this reform adopt such strategies and tricks by which they might save themselves from loss, and those who expect to benefit do not want to suffice with the amount of benefit the law is bringing them but want to strike further beyond the limits of the law. To stop this chaos and struggle, the Quran first prepared minds for it long before, as clarified above; then, when the final command was given, it was accompanied by the instruction: 'la tazhlimuna wa la tuzhlamun' (you do not wrong and you are not wronged). It was the blessing of this instruction that this great economic reform in Arabia came into action without any class struggle. Neither did any calamity fall upon the moneylenders nor did any harm reach the debtors; rather, both benefited equally from the blessing of this reform. If there were no fear of the point exceeding its limits, we would show here what prices other nations of the world had to pay for such reforms.

Exegesis of Verses 280-281: Along with the permission given to moneylenders above to take back their original principal from debtors, they were given this instruction: if the debtor is in hardship, give them respite until they attain ease (maysarah); and if in such a state you even forgive the principal, that is much better, and its reward is boundless.

A Claim of the Supporters of Usury and its Answer: In this era, some less-learned people claim that the usury prevalent in Arabia before the era of the Quran's revelation was only 'moneylending usury' (private/predatory). Poor and destitute people were forced to take loans from moneylenders to obtain their indispensable necessities of life, and these moneylenders collected heavy usury from these victims. It is this usury that the Quran declared as Riba and prohibited here. As for these commercial and business loans prevalent in this era, they were neither the custom in that era nor did the Quran discuss their prohibition or loathsomeness.

Commercial Loans were also subject to Usury in Arabia: An extremely clear answer to these people is present within this very verse. When the Quran gives this command—that if the debtor is in hardship (dhu 'usrah), give them respite until ease (maysarah) is attained—this verse has effectively announced that in that era, there were also wealthy and well-to-do people among the borrowers. In fact, if you fulfill the true right of the style of expression here, the point emerges that the transaction of lending and borrowing occurred mostly among the wealthy. However, there was also the possibility that some debtor fell into hardship such that even returning the moneylender's principal was becoming impossible for them; regarding him, the instruction was given that the moneylender give him respite until his financial state recovers, and if he forgives the principal too, it is better. The hint of this meaning emerges from the words of the verse because it stated: 'in kana dhu 'usratin fa-nazhiratun ila maysarah' (if the debtor is in hardship, then a respite until ease). In the Arabic language, the use of 'in' (if) is not for general and habitual circumstances but generally for describing rare and exceptional circumstances. For general circumstances, Arabic uses 'idha' (when). In this light, reflecting on the words of the verse reveals clearly that in that era, debtors were generally 'dhu maysarah' (prosperous), but occasionally such a situation would arise where the debtor was poor or became poor after taking the loan; so the instruction for this concession was given regarding him.

Although everything I am writing in this Tafseer is the grace of the teacher (RA), I have also found his own words specifically regarding this verse, which came from his pen at least fifty or sixty years ago when these hair-splittings of objectors regarding usury had not arisen. At that time, Maulana, with a completely impartial mind and solely from the words of the Quran, formed this opinion on this issue: "'wa in kana dhu 'usratin fa-nazhiratun ila maysarah wa an tasaddaqu khayrul-lakum'. From these words, it appears that they used to take usury from the prosperous as well. Furthermore, the Quraysh were traders and usurious trade was prevalent among them. Therefore, I see no specific difference between their circumstances and the circumstances of the people of our time regarding usury. And Allah knows best what is correct."

Obviously, wealthy people would not have been turning to moneylenders for their indispensable necessities of life; rather, they would have been taking loans for their commercial purposes. Then what is the difference between their loans and the loans of this era taken for commercial and business purposes?

The Meaning of 'Imla'' and 'Imlal': There is no specific linguistic or grammatical difficulty in this verse. The meaning of the word 'Imlal' is the same as 'Imla'', meaning to dictate. The Quran used both these words and used them in exactly the same meaning. From as much as I have reflected on the roots and derivatives of both, my inclination is toward the fact that in the meaning of dictating, the original word is indeed 'Imla'', but due to phonetic similarity, 'Imlal' also began to be used in this meaning; otherwise, in the root of 'Imlal' itself, there is not much room for the subject of writing or dictating. Examples of words transferring from one root to another based on phonetic similarity are abundant in the Arabic language, but there is no room for us to dig deeper into such details.

The Meaning of 'Fusuqum-bikum': Regarding the word 'bikum' appearing with 'fusuq' at the end of the verse, the connection of 'bi' with 'fusuq' might feel somewhat alien to some people. Such people should remember this style of the Arabic language: when there is an alienation between a prepositional link (silah) and its related word, some word is assumed to be omitted that is compatible with that link. Reflecting here reveals that after 'fusuq', some word with the meaning of 'becoming mandatory' and 'sticking' is omitted. The meaning would be: if you did such-and-such, it will be the commission of such a 'fisq' (wickedness) from your side that will stick to you, and it will be difficult to get rid of it.

Instructions to Save Borrowers and Lenders from Loss and Dispute:
Here, after ending the series of usurious loans with a single stroke, the following instructions were given to keep lenders and debtors safe from dispute and loss:
(a) When any loan transaction is for a specific duration, its document should be written down.
(b) This document should not contain any fraud or manipulation in the presence of both parties, and whoever has the skill of writing should not refuse this service. The skill of writing is a blessing from Allah; gratitude for this blessing is that a person helps people when needed. The need for this advice arose because literate people were few in that era. Official arrangements for writing and registering documents had neither come into action then, nor was it so easy for them to come into action.
(c) The responsibility for having the document written will be upon the borrower. He will acknowledge in the document that I am the debtor of so-and-so for such an amount, and like the scribe, it is his responsibility to observe Taqwa in this acknowledgment and never try to make any kind of decrease for the rightful owner.
(d) If this person is weak-minded or infirm or does not have the capability of having a document written, then whoever is his guardian or representative should act in his place and have the document written with justice and truth.
(e) Two men's testimony will be recorded on it, regarding whom one instruction is that they be "min rijalikum" (from your men), which yields two points simultaneously. One, that they be Muslims. Second, that they be from people of your social circle and connection so that the parties know them. The second instruction is that they be "mimman tardhawn" (from those you approve)—meaning of pleasing character and action, reliable, trustworthy, and honest.
(f) If two men of the aforementioned qualities are not available, then one man and two women can be chosen for it. The condition of two women is so that if a slip occurs from one, the reminder and warning of the other can block it. This difference is not from the aspect of belittling the woman, but rather based on her temperamental characteristics and her circumstances and occupations, this responsibility is a heavy one for her; therefore, the Sharia has also arranged support for her in carrying it. This subject will be discussed with all its details in Surah An-Nisa.
(g) Those who have been included among the witnesses of a document are not permitted to avoid testifying when called upon. This is because the testimony of truth is a great social service and also a part of the official duty of this Ummah from the aspect of being 'Witnesses of Allah' (Shuhada' Allah).
(h) Whether the loan transaction is small or large, if it is for a duration and not of a hand-to-hand nature, one should not feel burdened in bringing it into writing. Those who avoid it considering it a trouble often fall into such disputes due to negligence whose results turn out to be far-reaching.
(i) The aforementioned instructions are, in the sight of Allah, closer to truth and justice, more likely to keep the testimony correct, and more effective in saving from doubt and dispute; therefore, their observance is necessary for social reform and welfare.
(j) For hand-to-hand transactions, the restriction of writing and recording is not mandatory.
(k) However, if some important buying and selling has occurred, witnesses should be made for it so that if a dispute arises, its settlement can be made.
(l) In the event of a dispute arising, an attempt to harm the scribe or the witness is not permissible for any party. The scribe and the witness perform an important collective and civil service. For this reason, the result of trying to harm them without cause will be that reliable and cautious people will begin to avoid the responsibilities of testimony and writing, and it will become difficult for people to find any reasonable witness except professional witnesses.
In this era, the reason why reliable and serious people run away from the responsibilities of testimony, etc., is precisely this: when a matter takes a disputed form, the witnesses face wretchedness. These poor people become targets of defamation, abduction, and loss of wealth and property—even murder. The Quran stopped this type of mischief: those who commit such acts should remember that this is no small disobedience that will be easily forgiven; rather, it is a 'fisq' (wickedness) that will stick to them, and it will be difficult to escape its evil consequences. This is an attempt to demolish the foundation of that 'Shahadat' (bearing witness) which is the original goal of this Ummah's advent.
At the end of the verse, it stated: fear Allah. God does not seize immediately for disobediences. But when He seizes, no one can escape Him. Then He stated that this is "Allah teaching you," in which is entirely your own benefit, and He is the one aware of all matters; therefore, He alone holds the right to give education and guidance.

The Meaning of 'Rihanum-Maqbudhah': 'Rihan' is the plural of 'Rahan' (pledge/collateral). It refers to that object which is placed in the possession of the lender as a guarantee for their loan. 'farihanum-maqbudhah' is exactly the same type of sentence as 'fa-sabrun jamil' in Surah Yusuf. It can be treated as a subject (mubtada') with its predicate (khabar) omitted, or as a predicate with its subject omitted. The meaning is: if the situation of writing and testimony is missing, a loan transaction can also be done by placing something in possession as a pledge.
'amina fulanan' means that so-and-so considers himself safe from danger on the part of so-and-so, is secure from him, and trusts him.
The Reality of 'athimun qalbuh': 'athimun qalbuh' (his heart is sinful). This points toward the reality that some sins are such that their effect remains limited only to the human's outward limbs; their nature is merely like dust on the surface—for example, idle oaths taken without purpose. Such sins either fall away on their own through a person's daily minor good deeds or are corrected with minor attention. Other sins are those whose impulse rises from the depths of the heart. The effects of such sins also infect the heart. This type of sin is dangerous. It indicates the corruption of the heart. If caution is not exercised or an effort for reform is not made immediately after commission, there is an apprehension of it taking root. Above, we discussed the 'encompassing sin' and the 'sticking wickedness'; keep that in sight. Here, it is told that concealing testimony is a sin of this nature. The reason, as we hinted above, is that 'Shahadat 'alan-nas' (bearing witness over mankind) is that original official duty for which Allah Almighty has commissioned this Ummah; therefore, every shortcoming in this series carries far-reaching consequences.
Rulings of Pledging (Rahan): In this verse, the teaching is given that if the need for a loan transaction arises during travel and arranging for writing and testimony is not possible, and the lender is not willing to give the loan without some guarantee, then this form can also be adopted: that some object be placed in their possession as a pledge. But this form is only for the time until a situation of confidence and trust is created for the lender. When such circumstances arise that the things required for mutual trust are provided—for example, the journey ended and they arrived at residence, a scribe and witness were found for writing the document, the loan transaction was confirmed in the presence of their own people, and no reasonable cause remains for the lender not to trust without a pledge—then he should return the pledged item to him and, if he wishes for his satisfaction, adopt the form instructed above. Here, the pledged wealth is described as an 'amanat' (trust), which clarifies that the pledge is with the lender as a trust, the protection of which is necessary and from which any kind of benefit-taking is unlawful.
It is reported regarding Mujahid and Dhahhak in the Tafseers that these gentlemen considered pledging to be specific to travel; this opinion of theirs seems strong to me. From the Quran's words, the point emerges clearly that when reasons and causes for trust exist, no reason remains for maintaining possession over the pledge; this trust must be returned to those who placed the trust. Especially when the transaction is between a Muslim and a Muslim, then this thing is not only against Islamic brotherhood and chivalry but is also a kind of meanness. When a person can obtain the guarantees of a document and testimony, it is an extremely ugly thing for him to keep the debtor's house, or his field, or his garden, or his horse, or his goat, or the jewelry and clothes worn by his wife and children in his possession as a guarantee for his loan.
Explanation of the Hadith regarding Pledging: We do not deny the tradition from which it is known that the Holy Prophet (PBUH) pledged his armor with a Jew in exchange for some barley. But the most that emerges from this is that if a Muslim, due to some severe necessity, has to take a loan from a moneylender or a Jew and he is not ready to transact on any form except a pledge, then this transaction can be done with him. And if stretched a lot, the point can also be derived that it can be transacted this way with a narrow-hearted Muslim as well in a state of compulsion. But for general Muslims, when a clear, reliable form in accord with the demands of Islamic brotherhood and chivalry has been described for mutual transaction, then in its presence, how can it be declared as preferred that they transact loans on pledges even without any compulsion? This point is entirely against this verse of the Quran; as for the Hadith, deriving an argument from it for the general permissibility of pledging is not correct in any way. First, this transaction, as we hinted, occurred with a Jew; second, the state of the event is clearly testifying that it occurred in a state of great necessity.
In the verse under discussion, commentators have generally interpreted 'amanat' (trust) as the loan which someone gives to another merely on trust without a pledge. But the description of a loan as a 'trust' is wrong in our view from various angles. In reality, since these gentlemen are not ready to accept that the pledge should be returned after the journey ends and a form of trust and confidence is created, they had to perform this affectation in the interpretation of 'trust'. But in the interpretation we have given, the place for both the verse and the Hadith has been determined separately; therefore, neither the need to fall into this affectation remained nor the need to collect arguments in its refutation remained.
Subject of Verses 284 to 286: Now this great Surah has ended. The following verses are as a conclusion. In this conclusion, first is the warning: whatever is in the heavens and the earth is all God's property and in His authority. He is aware of all the manifest and hidden of the servants. He will take account of everything and, by His absolute authority, will forgive whom He deems worthy of forgiveness and punish whom He finds deserving of punishment; no one has the audacity to interfere in His will and decision.
This is that fundamental reality of religion whose correct awareness makes one capable of the trust entrusted to the Muslim Ummah in this Surah, and this is the reality by forgetting which the Jews and Christians were deprived of the blessings of this trust. Due to this importance, just as its reminder was given in various places in this Surah, it was also given at the conclusion.
Following this, it stated that Allah's Messenger and His believing servants have accepted that which was sent down to them. These were the ones worthy of believing in it. Unlike the Jews and Christians, they were not trapped in any prejudice regarding God's prophets and messengers—to believe in some and reject others; therefore, Allah opened the path of guidance for them and they attained their goal. As for those trapped in the snares of prejudices, Allah has no concern for them. They may wander in whichever valley they wish. They will see their end themselves.
Following this, that great prayer appears which is the 'voice of the state' of every individual of this Ummah. From its every word drips the awareness of the heavy responsibility placed upon this Ummah; that acknowledgment is also prominent which is the spirit of faith; the plea to be saved from those things that were a cause of stumbling for previous nations is also visible; and the request for assistance in the difficulties apprehended in the path of fulfilling duties and for overlooking the slips that are feared is also there. Now, in this light, recite the following verses.

"Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth belongs to Allah." This sentence simultaneously holds three meanings. One, that everything is God's property; second, that everything is in His authority and disposal; third, that ultimately the place of return for everything is God alone. I have not understood any such translation in Urdu that could carry all three of these meanings at once.
The Meaning of the Accounting of the Hidden Thoughts of the Heart: 'aw tukhfuhu yuhasibkum bihillah'. Accounting for the hidden thoughts of the heart does not mean that the thoughts and whispers that keep passing through the heart will also be accounted for; rather, it refers only to those resolves which exist in the heart with firm intention but could not manifest or come into action due to some compulsion or resistance. For example, if a person holds a firm intention in their heart to murder someone, then even if their intention could not be carried out due to some fear or compulsion, they will be seized for this intention with Allah Almighty.
God's Will is with His Wisdom: In 'fayaghfiru limay-yasha'u...' and other verses of this type, as we have clarified repeatedly in this book, the original force is on the point that there is no one to resist God's will, and the purpose of this is the negation of Shirk. The purpose is not that there is no code or rule at all for this will of God. Every will of God is with His wisdom. Consequently, this matter of forgiveness and punishment will also come into action under that code of wisdom which He has appointed for it. However, it is certain that there is no room for any intervention in it for anyone else.
The Twofold Connection of the Verse: Reflecting on the context and occasion of this entire verse reveals that from one aspect, it is connected to what preceded it, and from another aspect, it is an extremely comprehensive and effective preamble to the conclusion of the Surah. In the previous verse, it was stated: do not conceal testimony; whoever conceals testimony, their heart becomes tainted with sin. "And Allah is aware of what you do." Now, if you attach this subject to it—that "whatever is in the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah, Allah is the Accountant of all your manifest and hidden, then He will forgive whom He wills and punish whom He wills"—then the point has become fully reasoned as well as complete. This was the verse's connection to what preceded it.
Its connection to what follows, as we hinted above, is that this is a verse of Tawheed. Just as prayer holds the greatest importance in the chapter of commands and laws, similarly Tawheed holds the status of the foundation of religion in the chapter of beliefs. Consequently, you will recall that where the chapter of teaching the Sharia for the Ummah began in this Surah, the statement of Tawheed occurred first, followed by prayer. Now at the conclusion, to teach the Ummah complete surrender and entrustment, the reminder of Tawheed was given again, and the style of the reminder is more of warning than education. The objective is that the Ummah understands the burdens of this trust and remains ready for accountability only before Allah, the One without partner, instead of relying on false supports.

Indifference toward those who do not believe: Here, the purpose of informing about the faith of the Messenger and the Muslims is not merely to report an event, but to express indifference toward the opposition of the Quran's opponents, especially the Jews. You will recall that the Surah began with the point that while there is no room for doubt in the Quran being a Divine Book, only those people will believe in it who possess God-fearingness, reality-perception, and truth-seeking. Those trapped in sectarianism, prejudice, and the presumption of their own superiority will remain deprived of believing in this Book. Now here at the conclusion, this announcement was made that the Prophet and those who believed with him have made clear who the people were who were blessed with this faith. It is as if all the butter within the milk was taken out and placed in front, and pointed to with a finger to show that this was the butter within this milk which has emerged. Now what remains is whey; God has no concern for it.
In the matter of obedience to the law, the Prophet and the follower are equal: Here, this point should also be noted: among those believing in the Divine Book, the first one mentioned is the person of the Messenger himself. This shows that in Islam, in the matter of obedience and submission to the law, the Prophet is on the same level as the general believers. Worldly kings are superior to the law they give their subjects, but in God's law, the bringer of the law himself not only falls under it but must be the first to step forward and place its collar around his neck, saying 'ana awwalul-mu'minin' (I am the first of the believers) and 'ana awwalul-muslimin' (I am the first of the Muslims). This is such a testimony to the truthfulness of these Prophets that only a stubborn person could belie it.
General Faith in all Prophets and all Scriptures: The components of faith mentioned in 'kullun amana billahi...' have been discussed by us under verse 177 of this Surah. The reality of faith in Allah, the angels, the books, and the prophets should be understood from there. We explained the need for faith in the angels in detail there. However, one point is necessary to hint at here too. It is that there, the word 'Kitab' (Book) is used. Here, instead of 'Kitab', the word 'Kutub' is used, which is the plural of 'Kitab'. This clarifies that just as it is necessary for us to believe in all the Messengers, it is also necessary to believe in all the heavenly Books. The People of the Book express faith only in their own Book and only in the Prophet or those Prophets whom they consider their own. In contrast, this Ummah holds faith in all the Prophets and all the Books of Allah. As far as general faith is concerned, we make no distinction between the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and other Prophets, or between the Quran and other heavenly Books. However, since the teaching of other Prophets and their scriptures has not remained preserved, and those scriptures and those Prophets themselves gave news that their Sharia is not perfect—that the perfect Sharia will be received by the world through the Quran and Muhammad (PBUH)—therefore, we hold not just general but detailed faith in the Quran and the Seal of the Prophets (PBUH), and we invite the world to this same detailed faith.
The Nuance of Eloquence in the Shift of Style: 'la nufarriqu bayna ahadim-mir-rusulih' (We make no distinction between any of His messengers). We have explained this segment under verse 135 of this Surah while clarifying the declaration of the 'Ummat-e-Wasat'. However, the sudden shift in style within it—meaning the speech moving from the third-person (absent) form to the first-person (speaker) form—is to be noted. In the preceding segment, the point was said on behalf of Allah Almighty, but this sentence has emerged directly from the Ummah in the form of acknowledgment and expression. The nuance of eloquence hidden in this is that the entire Ummah acknowledges and expresses the faith and creed of the Muslims described in the preceding segment: that we are not trapped in any prejudice regarding Allah's Messengers; all these Prophets are links of the same 'Golden Chain' (Silsilat al-Dhahab); therefore, we do not act like the Jews and Christians, believing in some and rejecting others. Since the word 'ahad' (one/any) comes in a collective sense—like 'lastunna ka-ahadim-minan-nisa''—therefore, the use of the word 'bayna' (between) with it is correct.
'wa qalu sami'na wa ata'na'. The Meaning of Hearing and Obeying: The word 'sam'' (hearing) here is not in the sense of merely hearing but in the sense of believing and accepting; in this meaning, this word is used in various places in the Quran. In Urdu too, the word 'sunna' (to hear) is used in this sense. In this, the word 'sami'na' expresses the heart's acceptance and the word 'ata'na' expresses practical obedience, and this is the original reality of faith and Islam. This also contains a subtle critique of the Jews' 'sami'na wa 'asayna' (we heard and we disobeyed).
'ghufranaka rabbana wa ilaykal-mashir'. A Benefit of Omitting the Verb: 'Ghufranaka' is the object of an omitted verb. In such instances, the omission of the verb expresses the supplicant's distress, which is an extremely effective recommendation for the acceptance of the prayer.
The Connection of the Prayer to the Acknowledgment of Hearing and Obeying: The immediate flow of the prayer from the tongue after the acknowledgment of hearing and obeying shows that this acknowledgment is the acknowledgment of a great responsibility; it is stepping into the 'slaughterhouse of love'; great trials are to be encountered in it, and at every step are apprehensions of slips, shortcomings, and stumbles. The awareness of this reality directed them toward seeking forgiveness immediately after the acknowledgment of 'sami'na wa ata'na'. This is because when the path is hard and the accounting is to be for every hidden and manifest—as passed in the previous verse—and punishment and mercy are all in Allah's hand, then every support besides the support of His forgiveness is reality-less. In 'wa ilaykal-mashir' (and to You is the destination) is complete surrender: meaning, besides You, there is no one who can be a place of return and refuge from any aspect. This also contains a subtle critique of the Jews and Christians who, relying on their forefathers and partners and intercessors, deemed themselves absolved of the responsibilities of hearing and obeying. But to this Ummah, this reality is clear: all must return to Allah, be presented before Him, and be held accountable before Him.

'la yukallifullahu nafsan illa wus'aha laha ma kasabat wa 'alayha maktasabat' (Allah does not charge a soul except [with that within] its capacity. It will have [the consequence of] what it has gained, and it will bear [the consequence of] what it has earned). This is an incidental phrase (jumlah mu'taridhah) in the middle of the prayer, and the purpose of bringing it is to express the important reality that this responsibility of hearing and obeying placed upon this Ummah is indeed a heavy responsibility, but alongside the feeling of its being heavy, this aspect of Allah Almighty's favor and mercy should also be remembered: that He does not place a burden upon servants beyond their strength. Every person is accountable only to the extent that strength has been granted to them; there is no accountability for what is outside their limits of choice and possibility. The Sharia has respected this matter in its own commands and laws and has given concessions to servants from this aspect in cases of compulsion. For this reason, neither does Allah like that servants put themselves into some 'unbearable hardship' (taklif ma la yutaq), nor is it permissible for anyone else to place such a burden upon them which they cannot carry.

It should also be remembered here that it comes in Hadiths that when the Holy Prophet (PBUH) took the pledge of hearing and obeying from people, he would personally remind them and have them add the condition "to the extent of my capability." This was the Prophet's fulfillment of this very verse. The reduction and glad tiding within this for the believers is perfectly clear, especially at this occasion when the responsibilities of a great Sharia are being placed upon them.

'laha ma kasabat wa 'alayha maktasabat' (It will have what it earned and it will suffer what it did): Since this point is an aspect of the point that passed above, it was joined with it and not separated. The meaning is: whatever benefit or harm reaches a person will reach them only through their own action, not through anything else. What they sow, they shall reap, and what they do, they shall pay for. Neither will the credit of another's good deeds be given to them, nor will another's evils fall into their account. Nor will anyone else become the bearer of their burden. Allah Almighty has placed responsibility upon every soul by measuring it with the scale of its strength and its choice. For this reason, every person's success and failure is tied to this responsibility. 'Every soul is a pledge for what it has earned.'
